title: A Geometry-Informed Computer Vision Method for Detecting and Examining Overtaking Vehicles From A Bicycle
comment: 18 pages, 6 figures, in preparation for journal submission
summary: Instrumented bicycle studies have produced direct field evidence on vehicle passing behavior, but extracting overtaking events from continuous rear-facing video has remained dependent on manual, frame-by-frame annotation. This bottleneck constrains sample sizes and limits naturalistic cycling safety research. We present a geometry-informed computer vision pipeline that automates overtaking event detection from a single bicycle-mounted camera without multi-sensor configurations or explicit camera calibration. The system combines RT-DETR object detection with ByteTrack multi-object tracking through a three-stage geometric validation module enforcing bearing angle trend, apparent size growth, and spatial confirmation criteria derived from perspective projection principles. Validated on 315 manually annotated real-world overtaking events from urban roads in Ann Arbor, Michigan, the pipeline achieved 97.8% recall with zero false positives. The system identified overtaking intentions a mean of 2.44 seconds before vehicle passage, with 84.1% of events exceeding the 1.5-second human reaction time threshold, demonstrating feasibility for active cyclist warning. Lateral passing distance measurements from 96 events revealed 33.3% of passes below the 5-foot (152.4 cm) threshold, consistent with non-compliance rates in prior field and self-reported studies. A preliminary calibration-free lateral distance estimation approach using bounding box geometric features achieved mean absolute errors of 13-14 cm under leave-one-out cross-validation, sufficient to distinguish close passes from standard passes for safety categorization. By automating event isolation from consumer-grade footage, the system removes the primary annotation bottleneck of instrumented bicycle research and provides a scalable foundation for vehicle-bicycle interaction analysis across larger datasets and diverse urban environments.

AI.tldr: A geometry-informed CV pipeline combining RT-DETR and ByteTrack with a three-stage geometric validation automates overtaking event detection from a single bicycle camera, achieving high recall and zero false positives on 315 events, with calibration-free lateral distance estimation and early intention detection, enabling scalable, annotation-free analysis.
AI.motivation: To overcome manual frame-by-frame annotation bottlenecks in extracting overtaking events from rear-view video, enabling larger naturalistic cycling safety studies.
AI.method: Integrates RT-DETR object detection and ByteTrack MOT, plus a three-stage geometric validation: bearing angle trend, apparent size growth, and spatial confirmation from perspective projection; calibration-free lateral distance estimation from bounding box features; evaluated on 315 annotated events from Ann Arbor with leave-one-out cross-validation.
AI.result: 97.8% recall with zero false positives; overtaking intentions identifiable on average 2.44 seconds before vehicle passage; 84.1% of events exceed 1.5 s reaction-time threshold; 33.3% of lateral passes below 5 ft threshold; MAE 13-14 cm for calibration-free distance estimation; validated as scalable and reducing annotation bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: Automates event isolation from consumer-grade footage, enabling scalable vehicle-bicycle interaction analysis across larger datasets and diverse urban environments.

title: Verifiable Foundation Models for Robot Safety
summary: Deploying foundation models for robot control raises a central challenge: the expressive power that enables rich, multimodal perception also makes these models opaque and difficult to analyze formally, rendering them intractable for existing verification tools. In this paper, we present FEARL (Foundation-Enabled Assured Robot Learning), a framework that addresses this tension through a modular architectural decomposition. FEARL separates the policy into a large Controller (C) responsible for high-dimensional perception and task reasoning, and a small Safety module (S) that receives low-dimensional observations from dedicated safety sensors together with a bounded context embedding from C and produces the final action. Since many robot safety requirements, such as collision avoidance and workspace boundary constraints, can be expressed over these safety sensor observations, formal verification can be applied to S rather than to the full foundation-model backbone. This makes formal analysis tractable with existing tools while preserving the Controller's expressive power for task reasoning. To show that the decomposed policy remains capable of solving diverse tasks, we evaluate FEARL on three simulated robotic domains using multiple Controller backbones and training procedures, including pretrained off-the-shelf vision-language-action models. We further transfer the learned policy from one of our simulated tasks to a physical robot, suggesting that the low-dimensional safety interface supports practical sim-to-real transfer.

AI.tldr: FEARL decomposes a foundation-model–based robot controller into a large, expressive Controller (C) and a small Safety module (S) that is verified formally, enabling tractable safety guarantees while preserving task performance; demonstrated across three simulated domains with sim-to-real transfer.
AI.motivation: Foundation-models enable rich multimodal perception and reasoning but are opaque and hard to verify formally. Verifying the full backbone is intractable; safety-critical robotics require guarantees. A modular separation aims to localize verification to a small, observable safety interface.
AI.method: Split policy into C (perception/task reasoning) and S (safety module). S observes low-dimensional safety sensors plus a bounded context embedding from C and outputs the final action. Formal verification targets S for safety properties expressible over safety observations; this yields tractable verification using existing tools. Evaluated across three simulated robotic domains with multiple C backbones and training procedures, including pretrained vision-language-action models. Demonstrated sim-to-real transfer by deploying learned policy on a physical robot.
AI.result: Formal verification is applied to the Safety module S rather than the full foundation-model backbone, enabling tractable safety guarantees while preserving the Controller's expressive power. FEARL achieves task performance across three simulated domains and transfers from simulation to a real robot, suggesting practical viability of the safety-interface approach.
AI.conclusion: A modular C–S decomposition makes formal analysis feasible for foundation-model–driven robot controllers without sacrificing expressiveness. The safety-centric interface enables safety guarantees and supports sim-to-real deployment, indicating a promising direction for safe, scalable foundation-model robotics.

title: Sol Video Inference Engine: Agent-Native Full-Stack Acceleration Framework for Efficient Video Generation
summary: Modern video diffusion models achieve higher generation quality through scaling, but this also increases inference cost. Although many acceleration methods have been proposed, a central challenge is that the most effective acceleration strategy is highly instance-specific: a recipe that works well for one combination of model, hardware, and inference configuration often does not transfer to another. Different models vary in architecture, numerical sensitivity, and attention concentration patterns. Inference settings differ in spatial and temporal resolution and video duration, while hardware platforms differ in memory hierarchy, supported numerical formats, and kernel throughput. These factors create a large tuning space, making manual performance engineering costly. We present Sol Video Inference Engine, an agentic, native, training-free acceleration framework for video diffusion models. It organizes five broadly applicable techniques, cache, sparse attention, token pruning, quantization, and kernel fusion, into an agentic acceleration stack for instance-specific optimization. For a concrete deployment target defined by a model, hardware platform, and serving configuration, parallel skill agents optimize the implementation of each technique, an agent integrator composes them into a global acceleration stack, and a human validator provides feedback on generation quality. We instantiate this workflow on three video models with different sizes and architectures: 64B Cosmos3-Super, 22B LTX-2.3, and 2B SANA-Video. With little human effort, the full stack achieves more than 2x end-to-end acceleration while maintaining near-lossless VBench quality, demonstrating the effectiveness of the agent framework for video diffusion acceleration.

AI.tldr: A training-free, agent-based acceleration framework (Sol Video Inference Engine) that optimizes five techniques for video diffusion models, achieving ~2x speedups with near-lossless quality across multiple models.
AI.motivation: Inference cost grows with model/hardware scaling; acceleration is highly instance-specific and manual tuning is costly. A generalizable, automated framework for video diffusion is needed.
AI.method: Five techniques (cache, sparse attention, token pruning, quantization, kernel fusion) organized in an agentic stack. Skill agents optimize each technique for a given model, hardware, and config; an agent integrator composes them into a global acceleration stack; a human validator supervises generation quality. Demonstrated on three models: 64B Cosmos3-Super, 22B LTX-2.3, 2B SANA-Video.
AI.result: Achieves more than 2x end-to-end acceleration with near-lossless VBench quality; validates the effectiveness of the agent framework for video diffusion acceleration.
AI.conclusion: The agent-based Sol Video Inference Engine is effective for instance-specific, training-free acceleration of video diffusion models, with modest human oversight and broad applicability.

title: Engineering Reliable Autonomous Systems: Challenges and Solutions
summary:
Engineering reliable autonomous systems is an important and growing topic in computer science. As autonomous systems become more prevalent, easy-to-use techniques for building them reliably are increasingly important.
  This workshop report captures and expands on the discussions at the Lorentz Center Workshop "Engineering Reliable Autonomous Systems" (ERAS), held from 10 to 14 June 2024. The workshop was co-organised by the organisers of the Workshop on Formal Methods for Autonomous Systems (FMAS) and the Workshop on Agents and Robots for reliable Engineered Autonomy (AREA). It brought together members of the FMAS and AREA communities, industry practitioners, and representatives from sectors where autonomous systems pose distinctive engineering challenges.
  The workshop focused on three main research topics: techniques for verification and validation of autonomous systems; engineering real-world autonomous systems; and software architectures for safe autonomous systems. Its main outcome is a catalogue of challenges in these areas and, most importantly, a pathway to solutions. Some challenges can already be tackled by techniques that are well known in academia but have not yet become regularly used in practice. Other challenges remain unresolved and require further research. This roadmap is intended to support future research and industrial collaboration.

AI.tldr: A workshop report presenting a roadmap of challenges and solutions for engineering reliable autonomous systems, spanning verification/validation, real-world engineering, and safe software architectures, with a pathway to industrial–academic collaboration.
AI.motivation: As autonomous systems proliferate, there is a need for accessible, reliable engineering techniques and to bridge the gap between academic research and practical deployment.
AI.method: Synthesis of discussions from the Lorentz Center Workshop; compilation of a catalogue of challenges; identification of challenges solvable with known techniques; and a roadmap to address unresolved problems through future research and collaboration.
AI.result: A catalogue of challenges in verification/validation, real-world engineering, and software architectures for safe autonomous systems; a roadmap outlining pathways to solutions; recognition that some challenges are tractable with well-known techniques not yet adopted in practice; others require further research.
AI.conclusion: The roadmap is intended to guide future research and industrial collaboration toward reliably engineered autonomous systems.

title: Listening makes Vision Clear for VLMs
summary: Recent work typically assesses vision--language consistency using attention distributions of answer-side tokens. However, we observe that highest attention regions are not always consistent with the intended semantic token. This probably stems from decoding drift, where language priors from previously generated answer tokens accumulate and mismatch with visual attention. Besides the priors from previous answer tokens, we find that structural tokens, e.g., modality boundary markers, may encompass the entire context and generate high attention to areas unrelated to the target. To avoid these distortions and provide consistency evaluation for large VLMs, we adopt prompt-side semantics and propose Prompt-Vision Token Activation Map (PV-TAM). PV-TAM further incorporates a filter to remove systematic bias induced by modality boundary markers. Unlike traditional methods that evaluate overlap solely through masks while ignoring activation intensity, our metrics leverage the peak distribution of attention to measure the alignment between prompts and visual regions. In experiments, PV-TAM consistently improves both attention-based and IoU-style localization metrics over answer-side baselines on various datasets.

AI.tldr: Prompt-Vision Token Activation Map (PV-TAM) uses prompt-side attention peaks and a bias-filter to evaluate vision-language alignment in large VLMs, addressing decoding drift and modality-marker bias; it improves attention-based and IoU-style localization metrics over answer-side baselines.
AI.motivation: Traditional answer-side attention analyses can drift due to language priors from previously generated tokens and modality boundary markers, leading to misalignment between visual regions and semantic tokens. A robust evaluation should center on prompt semantics.
AI.method: Introduce PV-TAM that maps activation aligned with prompt tokens, applies a filter to remove systematic bias from modality boundaries, and uses attention peak distributions rather than coarse overlap, evaluating alignment between prompts and visual regions.
AI.result: PV-TAM consistently improves both attention-based and IoU-style localization metrics across datasets compared to answer-side baselines.
AI.conclusion: PV-TAM offers a robust, bias-aware, prompt-centered mechanism for assessing vision-language consistency in large VLMs, mitigating decoding drift and boundary-marker biases.

title: Enforcing Human-like Kinematics in Dexterous Piano Playing via Adversarial Posture Regularization
summary: Reinforcement learning can train bimanual dexterous hands to play piano in physics simulation with high note accuracy, but for high-DoF dexterous hands, relying solely on task rewards or IK inversion often leads to unnatural postures and joint overextension. We propose \textit{Adversarial Posture Regularization (APR)}. It avoids expensive, song-aligned expert demonstration data and instead uses a small amount of casual human playing data. By matching the distribution of the posture of the policy with the human prior through an adversarial objective, APR encourages more human-like hand shapes. Meanwhile, we collect and release unstructured hand motion data of piano playing using a consumer-grade Meta Quest 3, and retarget the key motion information to the Shadow Hand. Finally, we achieve significantly better performance than prior methods on all three human-likeness metrics (cPSI, BSE, and FAC) as well as in visual quality. Project repository: https://github.com/APRProject/APRPianist.

AI.tldr: APR uses an adversarial posture regularization to align a high-DoF robotic hand's postures with human playing data, improving human-likeness without heavy expert demonstrations by leveraging casual human data and unstructured Quest 3 motion capture retargeted to a Shadow Hand; achieves superior human-likeness metrics (cPSI, BSE, FAC) and visual quality.
AI.motivation: High-DoF dexterous hands trained with only task rewards or IK tend to adopt unnatural postures; obtaining extensive expert demonstrations is costly. A data-efficient method that promotes human-like hand shapes is desirable, using casual human data and consumer motion data.
AI.method: Adversarial Posture Regularization (APR): match the policy's posture distribution to a human prior via an adversarial loss. Collect unstructured hand motion data from a consumer device (Meta Quest 3), retarget to the Shadow Hand, and optimize the policy to align posture distributions without requiring aligned expert demonstrations.
AI.result: The approach achieves significantly better performance than prior methods on three human-likeness metrics (cPSI, BSE, FAC) and in visual quality on piano playing with a Shadow Hand.
AI.conclusion: APR provides a data-efficient path to generate human-like hand postures in high-DoF dexterous hands by leveraging casual human data and retargeted motion capture, reducing reliance on expensive demonstration data while improving perceptual and metric-based human-likeness.

title: From Spatial to Spectral: An Efficient, Frequency-Guided Feature Representation Learner for Small Object Detection
summary: Efficient small object detection is bottlenecked by the inherent feature scarcity of tiny targets, which is further aggravated by operations of spatial-domain detectors that indiscriminately discard critical high-frequency details. Recovering these fragile cues within the spatial domain is notoriously difficult, as it often requires computationally expensive architectural upscaling that inadvertently amplifies background noise. To bridge this gap, we propose a paradigm \textbf{shift from spatial to spectral} feature processing, introducing a holistic solution with the following novelty: (1) A versatile \textbf{Frequency-Guided Feature Representation framework} that generalizes across diverse detector architectures (both CNN and Transformer-based), offering a robust alternative to spatial-only feature extraction; (2) The unified \textbf{Decompose--Enhance--Reconstruct (DER)} operator, instantiated via three \textbf{lightweight, plug-and-play} modules -- Wavelet-Difference Gate (WDG), Log-Gabor Enhancer (LGE), and Frequency-Driven Head (FDHead) -- to systematically inject frequency-aware modulation into the backbone, neck, and head. This mechanism decouples feature modeling from resolution reduction, capturing discriminative high-frequency components to enable accurate localization with significantly reduced parameter redundancy; (3) Extensive validation on multi-domain benchmarks (VisDrone2019, UAVDT, TinyPerson, DOTAv1) demonstrating consistent gains. Notably, our proposed \textbf{DERNet} series outperforms YOLOv11 models under the same scale while requiring \textbf{only 1/6 of the parameters}, backed by rigorous spectral diagnostics and error decomposition analysis.

AI.tldr: Proposes DERNet, a frequency-guided feature representation for small object detection that shifts processing from spatial to spectral domains. It introduces a plug-and-play Derivative Operator (DER) with three modules (Wavelet-Difference Gate, Log-Gabor Enhancer, Frequency-Driven Head) to inject spectral cues into backbone/neck/head, achieving strong gains across CNN and Transformer detectors with far fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Small object detection is hampered by scarce high-frequency details and by spatial-domain processing that discards useful high-frequency information. Conventional upsampling/additive noise leads to inefficiency. A spectral-domain approach could recover discriminative cues more efficiently and generically across architectures.
AI.method: Introduce Frequency-Guided Feature Representation framework that generalizes across detector architectures (CNN/Transformer). Propose Decompose–Enhance–Reconstruct (DER) operator instantiated via three lightweight modules: Wavelet-Difference Gate (WDG), Log-Gabor Enhancer (LGE), and Frequency-Driven Head (FDHead). The modules inject frequency-aware modulation into backbone, neck, and head, decoupling feature modeling from resolution reduction and emphasizing high-frequency components for localization.
AI.result: Extensive validation on VisDrone2019, UAVDT, TinyPerson, DOTAv1 showing consistent gains. DERNet outperforms YOLOv11 models at the same scale while using only about 1/6 of the parameters, supported by spectral diagnostics and error decomposition analysis.
AI.conclusion: A spectral-domain paradigm provides robust, architecture-agnostic improvements for small object detection. The DER framework yields parameter-efficient gains and broad applicability across detectors, with empirical evidence across diverse benchmarks.

title: Topological Online Learning for Displacement-based Formation Control
summary: This paper addresses the problem of robust formation control by introducing Topological Online Learning for Displacement-based (TOLD) formation control, a real-time edge-level adaptation framework. Unlike conventional node-level robust controllers that regulate individual robot inputs without modifying the interaction topology, TOLD updates the interaction topology weights online to directly minimize formation distortion. Two strategies are proposed under the TOLD formation control framework: Online Gradient Flow (OGF) with unconstrained weights and Online Exponential Gradient Flow (OExpGF) with non-negative convex weights. Theoretical analysis establishes that, for single-integrator agents over directed graphs, OExpGF guarantees asymptotic consensus, while OGF ensures bounded formation distortion. Simulations with twelve robots under intermittent disturbances show 1.2%-33.14% median cumulative Root Mean Distortion Error reduction when augmenting TOLD with node-level controllers. Hardware experiments with Crazyflie 2.0 quadrotors demonstrate over 62% (OGF) and 31.4% (OExpGF) reduction in median formation distortion compared to fixed-weight consensus.

AI.tldr: TOLD: a real-time, edge-weight online learning framework for displacement-based formation control that adapts topology weights to minimize distortion. Two strategies—OGF (unconstrained weights) and OExpGF (non-negative convex weights)—yield asymptotic consensus (OExpGF) or bounded distortion (OGF) on single-integrator directed graphs, with substantial empirical gains in simulations and hardware experiments.
AI.motivation: Limitations of traditional node-level robust controllers: they adjust inputs without changing interaction topology. By online updating topology weights, TOLD directly minimizes formation distortion under disturbances, improving robustness and performance.
AI.method: Propose Topological Online Learning for Displacement-based (TOLD) formation control. Implement two online weight-update strategies: Online Gradient Flow (OGF) with unconstrained weights and Online Exponential Gradient Flow (OExpGF) with non-negative convex weights. Theoretically analyze single-integrator agents on directed graphs: OExpGF guarantees asymptotic consensus; OGF bounds formation distortion. Validate via simulations (12 robots under intermittent disturbances) and hardware experiments (Crazyflie 2.0 quadrotors).
AI.result: Simulation results show median cumulative Root Mean Distortion Error reduction between 1.2% and 33.14% when augmenting with node-level controllers. Hardware experiments report median formation distortion reductions of over 62% (OGF) and 31.4% (OExpGF) compared to fixed-weight consensus.
AI.conclusion: Online topology adaptation via TOLD enhances formation robustness. OExpGF ensures asymptotic consensus, OGF ensures bounded distortion, and practical gains are demonstrated in both simulations and real hardware.

title: Systematic Exploration of 4-Expert Heterogeneous Mixture-of-Experts via Automated Pipeline Search
summary: We present an automated large-scale search pipeline for heterogeneous 4-Expert Mixture-of-Experts (MoE4) architectures within the LEMUR neural network dataset ecosystem. Building on a hand-crafted heterogeneous MoE reference model, we replace manual design with a deterministic code-assembly generator that systematically combines base architecture families drawn from the LEMUR database into MoE4 ensembles, each governed by a convolutional gating network with temperature scaling, mixup augmentation, and cosine-annealed learning rate scheduling. Over a 28-day campaign on an NVIDIA RTX 4090, the pipeline generated 4,463 candidate models across 197 batches, of which 1,021 were evaluated successfully. A critical finding emerged from the campaign: due to alphabetical enumeration via itertools.combinations, the entire explored search space (4.8% of the theoretical 23,751 possible 4-family combinations) is anchored to a single family, AirNet. We characterise this coverage bias precisely, identify the root cause in the generator, and propose a stratified random sampling fix. Within the AirNet anchored scope, ShuffleNet and MobileNetV3 consistently co-produce the highest-accuracy ensembles (mean accuracy up to 0.632), while FractalNet and MNASNet are identified as low-yield families warranting exclusion in future campaigns. The pipeline, analysis artefacts, and corrected generator are released as part of the open-source NNGPT project at https://github.com/ABrain-One/nn-gpt

AI.tldr: Deterministic code-assembly generator assembles heterogeneous 4-Expert MoE architectures from LEMUR families, reveals a bias anchored to AirNet due to alphabetical enumeration; within AirNet, ShuffleNet and MobileNetV3 yield highest accuracy; some families like FractalNet and MNASNet are low-yield; corrected generator released under NNGPT.
AI.motivation: Automate large-scale exploration of heterogeneous MoE4 architectures in the LEMUR ecosystem, replacing manual design with a reproducible generator to enable scalable exploration and bias diagnosis.
AI.method: A deterministic code-assembly generator combines base architecture families into MoE4 ensembles; uses a convolutional gating network with temperature scaling, mixup augmentation, cosine-annealed learning rate scheduling; 28-day campaign on an RTX 4090; generated 4,463 candidates across 197 batches; 1,021 evaluated; discovered coverage bias due to itertools.combinations enumerating alphabetically, anchoring to AirNet; diagnosed root cause in generator; proposed stratified random sampling fix.
AI.result: Found coverage bias anchored to AirNet; within this anchored scope, ShuffleNet and MobileNetV3 co-produce highest-accuracy ensembles with mean accuracy up to 0.632; FractalNet and MNASNet identified as low-yield; pipeline and artifacts released; 4,463 generated, 1,021 evaluated; corrected generator available.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a corrected generator to ensure balanced exploration; open-source release (NNGPT) with pipeline, artifacts, and fix; improves reliability of MoE4 space exploration.

title: RIFT-Bench: Dynamic Red-teaming For Agentic AI Systems
summary: Agentic AI systems powered by large language models (LLMs) are rapidly evolving into autonomous decision-making systems, exposing attack vectors beyond those of traditional LLM vulnerabilities. Existing security evaluations are often tied to specific implementations or domains, limiting unified comparison across heterogeneous systems. To address this gap, we introduce RIFT-Bench, a graph representation-driven methodology for dynamic red-teaming that enables unified evaluations across diverse agentic architectures. Building on a novel hierarchical representation, RIFT-Bench operates in two automated phases: Discovery, which extracts system structure, and Scanning, which deploys adaptive adversarial attacks and produces a comprehensive evaluation report. It evaluates the examined system itself, leveraging a broad set of dynamically adaptable adversarial probes across diverse attack vectors and objectives. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed evaluation pipeline across 45 agentic systems spanning a diverse range of implementations, showing that the approach generalizes effectively to heterogeneous agentic architectures. Beyond systems and attacks, RIFT-Bench also supports direct evaluation of mitigation strategies. These key capabilities make RIFT-Bench a scalable foundation for security evaluation of agentic AI systems.

AI.tldr: RIFT-Bench introduces a graph-based, two-phase red-teaming framework to evaluate security of diverse agentic AI systems, enabling unified cross-architecture assessments and mitigation evaluation.
AI.motivation: Security evaluation of agentic AI is fragmented by implementation and domain; a unified, scalable approach is needed to compare and improve defenses across heterogeneous architectures.
AI.method: RIFT-Bench uses a hierarchical graph representation and operates in two automated phases: (1) Discovery to extract system structure, (2) Scanning to deploy adaptive adversarial probes and generate a comprehensive evaluation report; it evaluates the target system with a broad set of dynamically adaptable probes across multiple attack vectors and objectives; it also supports mitigation evaluation and is validated across 45 agentic systems.
AI.result: The pipeline generalizes across heterogeneous agentic architectures and provides scalable, unified security evaluations; demonstrated on 45 systems, with capability to assess mitigation strategies.
AI.conclusion: RIFT-Bench offers a scalable, architecture-agnostic foundation for security evaluation of agentic AI systems, enabling cross-system comparisons and informed mitigation design; further benchmarking and diverse real-world deployments would strengthen validation.

title: ABACUS: Adapting Unified Foundation Model for Bridging Image Count Understanding and Generation
summary: ABACUS is a unified vision-language model that handles object counting, crowd counting, referring-expression counting, and count-faithful image generation without any benchmark-specific training required. Our model is built on existing 3B-parameter unified foundation model and is adapted for object localization tasks using three key innovations: density-aware adaptive zooming with objectness maps for spatial grounding; a boundary-aware count policy via GRPO to eliminate crop-boundary errors; and a cycle-consistent GRPO strategy where the understanding branch self-critiques generated outputs, closing the understanding-generation gap without any external annotations. ABACUS achieves state-of-the-art results across seven benchmarks, outperforming both task-specific specialists and larger generalist models.

AI.tldr: ABACUS offers a unified 3B vision-language model for counting tasks (object, crowd, referring-expression) and count-faithful image generation, achieving SOTA without benchmark-specific training via density-aware zooming, boundary-aware GRPO, and cycle-consistent GRPO.
AI.motivation: Resolve the need for a single model capable of diverse counting tasks across diverse images without task-specific fine-tuning, while mitigating crop-boundary errors and grounding challenges.
AI.method: Three innovations: density-aware adaptive zooming with objectness maps for spatial grounding; boundary-aware count policy via GRPO to reduce crop-boundary errors; cycle-consistent GRPO where the understanding branch critiques generated outputs to close the understanding-generation gap, all atop a 3B unified foundation.
AI.result: State-of-the-art across seven benchmarks, surpassing task-specific specialists and larger generalist models on counting and count-faithful generation without benchmark-specific training.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of a truly unified, training-free counting framework with robust grounding and cross-task generalization, suggesting wide applicability to counting-centric V&L problems.

title: Sim-to-Real Betting on the E-Process: Bringing "simulators" to anytime-valid confidence sequences
summary: This note describes an integration of the sim-to-real performance estimate with betting (from Chen et al.) and the safe anytime-valid inference (from Ramdas et al.). Using the scaled simulators. The method produces efficient, reliable certificates for the mean estimate, an approach that is especially valuable in robot performance testing. This note gives a primary, self-contained account of the construction; preliminaries of the respective methods are kept at a minimum, and one shall refer to the original works for full detail. Some synthetic examples demonstrating the proposed algorithm can be found at https://github.com/ISUSAIL/Bet4Sim2Real-EProcess.

AI.tldr: A note that merges sim-to-real performance estimates with betting and anytime-valid inference to yield efficient, time-uniform certificates for mean estimates using scaled simulators.
AI.motivation: Address uncertainty in sim-to-real transfer and provide reliable, sequential confidence guarantees for robot performance testing by leveraging Chen et al.'s betting framework and Ramdas et al.'s anytime-valid inference.
AI.method: Integrate scaled simulators with a betting-based performance estimate and safe anytime-valid inference to construct time-uniform certificates for the mean; present a self-contained construction with minimal prerequisites.
AI.result: Produces efficient, reliable certificates for the mean estimate; demonstrated via synthetic examples available on a GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: Offers a self-contained account of the construction, pointing to the original works for full details; applicable to robot performance testing where robust mean-estimate certificates are valuable.

title: Weight-Space Geometry of Offline Reasoning Training
summary: Offline reinforcement-learning losses (RFT, RIFT, DFT, Offline GRPO, DPO) are widely used to distill reasoning from large teachers into smaller students, and are typically compared on downstream accuracy alone. We ask whether they are mechanistically distinct or converge to a similar weight update. Training six methods (SFT, RFT, DFT, RIFT, Offline GRPO, DPO) on identical math rollouts from a single base model (Qwen3-4B) with attention-only LoRA, we analyze the resulting deltas via cosine similarity, principal-angle subspace analysis, linear mode connectivity, and CKA. We observe: (i) SFT, RFT, and RIFT have nearly colinear weight deltas (cosine >= 0.97, top-1 principal angle ~7 deg median over 144 modules) and comparable GSM8K accuracy (87-88%, n=1319; pairwise McNemar p >= 0.15); (ii) DFT diverges further in direction than any reward-weighted method despite using the same data; (iii) Offline GRPO adds a substantial component orthogonal to the SFT direction (~67% globally, up to ~86% in late layers) while staying in the SFT loss basin; (iv) DPO sits in a near-orthogonal subspace, shows a mode-connectivity barrier, and collapses late-layer CKA to ~0.46. DPO also reaches the highest accuracy in our protocol on both GSM8K (93.5%, McNemar p < 10^-9 vs. each other method) and AIME26 (30.0% vs. 3.3-10.0%); its training uses a 10x smaller learning rate than the others (the standard convention), so the update-norm and accuracy gaps reflect loss-function and optimizer choices jointly, and a learning-rate-matched DPO comparison is left for future work.

AI.tldr: Offline RL losses largely align in parameter-space directions for SFT, RFT, and RIFT, while DFT diverges; Offline GRPO and DPO occupy distinct subspaces with DPO showing near-orthogonal updates and a mode-connectivity barrier. DPO achieves the best GSM8K and AIME26 performance under the tested LR regime, though LR setting confounds the comparison.
AI.motivation: To determine whether common offline RL losses are mechanistically distinct in weight space beyond downstream accuracy by analyzing the geometry of their updates under identical data and model settings.
AI.method: Train six methods (SFT, RFT, DFT, RIFT, Offline GRPO, DPO) on identical math rollouts from Qwen3-4B using attention-only LoRA. Compare weight deltas via cosine similarity, principal-angle subspace analysis, linear mode connectivity, and centered kernel alignment (CKA). Assess performance on GSM8K and AIME26.
AI.result: (i) SFT, RFT, RIFT yield nearly colinear updates (cosine >= 0.97; top-1 median principal angle ~7° across 144 modules); GSM8K accuracy similar (87–88%, n=1319); McNemar p >= 0.15. (ii) DFT diverges in direction despite same data. (iii) Offline GRPO adds substantial orthogonal component (~67% globally; up to ~86% in late layers) while remaining in the SFT loss basin. (iv) DPO is near-orthogonal, exhibits a mode-connectivity barrier, and late-layer CKA ~0.46. DPO achieves highest GSM8K (93.5%, McNemar p < 1e-9 vs others) and AIME26 (30.0% vs 3.3–10.0%); uses a 10x smaller learning rate; LR-matched comparisons remain for future work.
AI.conclusion: Most offline RL losses generate similar weight-space directions (SFT/ RFT/ RIFT). DFT, Offline GRPO, and especially DPO occupy distinct subspaces and display different optimization dynamics; DPO’s superior accuracy may be driven by both loss choice and significantly smaller learning rate, warranting LR-controlled future studies.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Drive: Rule-Grounded Faithful Reasoning for Driving VLAs
summary: Driving VLA models incorporating Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning are attractive because they leverage pretrained VLM representations and expose intermediate decisions in natural language, yet current rationales often lack the step-by-step decision semantics needed to keep the rationale causally connected to the planned motion. We introduce Neuro-Symbolic Drive, a neuro-symbolic driving framework that supervises a driving VLA with rule-grounded reasoning traces extracted directly from classical rule-based planners. Our key observation is that rule-based planners are symbolic AI systems that already function as executable reasoning engines: they reason about active safety constraints, search over candidate maneuvers, and select a final trajectory. We instrument these planners in simulation to capture both the executed trajectory and the internal decision trace at each rule-evaluation step. Each trace is serialized into structured rule-grounded reasoning and paired with the trajectory to fine-tune Qwen3.5-4B as a driving VLA. Because these traces are derived directly from the planner states that determine the action, they ensure reasoning is structurally coupled to motion generation by construction, rather than by post-hoc alignment. On our simulator-generated benchmark, detailed rule-grounded reasoning reduces ADE@3s from 0.47 to 0.26 and miss rate from 8.30% to 6.40% under three-camera perception, and from 0.54 to 0.26 and 10.13% to 5.99% under eight-camera perception. Neuro-Symbolic Drive thus converts neuro-symbolic planning logic into structured supervision. Code base: https://github.com/XiangboGaoBarry/Neural-Symbolic-Drive.

AI.tldr: Neuro-Symbolic Drive couples VLM-based driving agents with symbolic, rule-grounded planning traces to provide structured, causally connected reasoning for motion planning; demonstrates improved ADE@3s and miss rate in simulator benchmarks by supervising a driving VLA with traces derived from classical planners.
AI.motivation: Address the disconnection between natural-language chain-of-thoughts and actual motion planning; leverage executable, symbolic planners to provide step-by-step, causally linked reasoning traces for driving VLMs.
AI.method: Instrument rule-based planners in simulation to extract executed trajectories and internal decision traces at each rule-evaluation; serialize traces into structured reasoning; pair traces with trajectories to fine-tune Qwen3.5-4B as a driving VLA; evaluate on simulator-generated benchmarks under varying perception (3-camera, 8-camera).
AI.result: Detailed rule-grounded reasoning reduces ADE@3s from 0.47 to 0.26 and miss rate from 8.30% to 6.40% (3-camera); and ADE@3s from 0.54 to 0.26 and miss rate from 10.13% to 5.99% (8-camera).
AI.conclusion: Neuro-Symbolic Drive converts neuro-symbolic planning logic into structured supervision for driving VLAs, demonstrating improved safety and accuracy; code available at the linked repository.

title: HANCLIP: A Family of Hyperbolic Angular Negation Vision Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are typically pre-trained on large-scale image-text datasets to capture semantic correspondences between visual content and natural language. However, they remain surprisingly brittle to negation: models often rely on shallow word co-occurrence and are easily distracted by misleading or irrelevant textual cues, even when their overall retrieval or classification performance is strong. Moreover, directly finetuning on negation data can interfere with previously acquired knowledge, causing noticeable degradation on standard vision-language benchmarks. To tackle these issues, this work introduces HANCLIP (Hyperbolic + Angular + Negation), a family of VLMs that explicitly restructures the embedding space to encode "what an image is not" alongside "what it is." HANCLIP is trained on a compact set of 20,000 image-text quadruplets and combines a hyperbolic formulation, which models hierarchical semantic relations and asymmetries, with an angular triplet objective that drives systematic separation between negated descriptions and their corresponding positives. This geometry-aware design strengthens negation sensitivity while preserving the global structure of pretrained representations, rather than overwriting them. Extensive experiments across multiple vision-language tasks show that HANCLIP delivers consistent gains on the negation-focused NegBench benchmark, while maintaining competitive or improved performance on standard classification and image-text retrieval benchmarks. The framework is model-agnostic and can be plugged into CLIP, LongCLIP, SmartCLIP, and HiMo-CLIP without large-scale retraining, demonstrating that a carefully designed geometric objective can substantially extend the reasoning capabilities of existing VLMs using only modest additional data.

AI.tldr: HANCLIP introduces hyperbolic + angular geometry to CLIP-like embeddings to explicitly encode negation, trained on a compact 20k quadruplet dataset, yielding improved negation handling with minimal retraining while maintaining standard task performance.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models are brittle to negation and can be misled by irrelevant textual cues; fine-tuning on negation data can disrupt previously learned knowledge. A geometry-aware embedding space that separates negated descriptions from positives aims to enhance negation reasoning without degrading overall performance.
AI.method: Propose HANCLIP, a plug-in framework that uses hyperbolic embeddings to capture hierarchical/ asymmetric relations and an angular triplet objective to separate negated descriptions from their positives. Trained on about 20k image-text quadruplets; designed to preserve global structure of pretrained representations and be compatible with CLIP-family models without large-scale retraining.
AI.result: Experiments show consistent gains on the negation-focused NegBench benchmark, while maintaining or improving performance on standard vision-language tasks such as image-text retrieval and classification. The approach is model-agnostic and can be integrated into CLIP, LongCLIP, SmartCLIP, and HiMo-CLIP with modest data and training.
AI.conclusion: A geometry-aware objective can substantially extend the negation reasoning capabilities of existing VLMs while preserving their global embedding structure and requiring only modest additional data and minimal retraining.

title: TurboMPC: Fast, Scalable, and Differentiable Model Predictive Control on the GPU
summary: Robotics increasingly relies on GPUs for parallel simulation, large-scale learning, and neural-network inference. For model predictive control (MPC) to scale with this paradigm, solvers must run efficiently on this hardware while remaining fast, differentiable, and compatible with expressive MPC formulations used in robotics. We present TurboMPC, a differentiable MPC solver that runs entirely on the GPU and supports state and control inequality constraints, implicit integrators, cross-time-coupled costs, and slack variables. TurboMPC combines sequential quadratic programming (SQP), an alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) inner solver, implicit differentiation, and a co-designed JAX-CUDA implementation for efficiency and ease of use. In simulation, we validate TurboMPC on constrained planning, humanoid imitation learning, and reinforcement learning with neural-network cost function tasks, achieving up to $15\times$ and $58\times$ speedups over state-of-the-art CPU and GPU differentiable solvers, respectively. We deploy TurboMPC on a full-scale car for minimum-time racing and find that batched, GPU-accelerated tuning of MPC parameters via Bayesian optimization yields significantly faster driving than a hand-tuned baseline. TurboMPC also scales to planning horizons of over $8000$ knot points while maintaining control of the vehicle. We open-source TurboMPC at: https://github.com/ToyotaResearchInstitute/turbompc

AI.tldr: TurboMPC is a GPU-only, differentiable MPC solver that combines an outer SQP loop with an inner ADMM solver, leveraging implicit differentiation and a JAX-CUDA implementation. It supports state/control inequality constraints, implicit integrators, cross-time costs, and slack variables, scales to very long horizons, and delivers large speedups over CPU and GPU differentiable solvers. Open-sourced.
AI.motivation: Robotics MPC demands fast, differentiable optimization on GPUs to exploit parallelism for large-scale learning, neural-network costs, and expressive MPC formulations. Existing differentiable solvers often struggle with constraints, implicit dynamics, and end-to-end differentiability within a unified GPU stack.
AI.method: TurboMPC integrates an outer sequential quadratic programming (SQP) framework with an inner solver based on the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM). It leverages implicit differentiation to obtain gradients w.r.t. problem data, and is co-designed with JAX-CUDA for efficient GPU execution and easy integration with differentiable learning frameworks. It supports state and control inequality constraints, implicit integrators, cross-time-coupled costs, and slack variables. The implementation is batched and scales to long planning horizons ( > 8000 knot points ), and includes Bayesian optimization for tuning MPC parameters.
AI.result: In simulation, TurboMPC achieves up to 15x speedups over state-of-the-art CPU differentiable solvers and up to 58x speedups over GPU differentiable solvers. It is validated on constrained planning, humanoid imitation learning, and reinforcement learning with neural-network cost functions. It is deployed on a full-scale car for minimum-time racing, with batched GPU-tuned MPC parameters yielding faster driving than a hand-tuned baseline. It scales to horizons exceeding 8000 knot points while maintaining vehicle control. The project is open-sourced at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: TurboMPC demonstrates that differentiable MPC can run efficiently entirely on GPUs, enabling long-horizon, expressive MPC formulations for robotics, with substantial speedups and practical deployment potential. The work highlights a practical pathway for integrating MPC with learning pipelines and Bayesian hyperparameter tuning.

title: A Survey on Federated Causal Discovery and Inference
summary: Causal reasoning, which encompasses the discovery of causal structures and the inference of causal effects, is fundamental to data-driven decision making. In practice, data for reliable causal analysis are often distributed across institutions and cannot be centralized due to privacy regulations or communication constraints. Federated learning (FL) addresses this by enabling collaborative analysis without raw data sharing, giving rise to the rapidly growing field of federated causal discovery (FCD) and inference (FCI). However, the interdisciplinary nature of this field and the absence of a comprehensive survey present barriers to entry for researchers. This paper bridges that gap by providing a systematic review through multi-dimensional taxonomies. Grounded in the three core design decisions underlying any FCD solution, namely how structures are learned, how data are partitioned, and what structural knowledge each party obtains, we organize FCD along three axes: methodological paradigm, federation topology, and structural scope. We further examine key practical dimensions, including temporal dynamics, data heterogeneity, missing data, and non-identical variable sets. For FCI, we categorize methods by target estimand (average versus individualized/conditional treatment effects) and by estimation strategy, from classical weighting methods to modern deep generative architectures. Unlike prior works that treat FCD and FCI separately, we formalize their connection as complementary stages of a unified federated causal reasoning pipeline, where FCD supplies the structural knowledge required for valid effect estimation in FCI. Finally, we highlight their shared concerns regarding privacy, communication efficiency, theoretical guarantees, and application domains, and conclude by identifying open challenges for future research.

AI.tldr: A systematic survey of federated causal discovery (FCD) and inference (FCI) with multi-axis taxonomies and a unified pipeline linking structure learning and effect estimation, addressing privacy, efficiency, and heterogeneity, and outlining future challenges.
AI.motivation: Causal analysis often relies on data distributed across institutions; privacy and communication constraints impede centralized analyses, creating a need for a coherent survey that bridges FCD and FCI.
AI.method: Proposes three core design axes for organization: how structures are learned, data partitioning, and what structural knowledge is shared; adds practical dimensions (temporal dynamics, data heterogeneity, missing data, non-identical variable sets); categorizes FCI by estimand and estimation strategy; formalizes FCD and FCI as a unified federated causal reasoning pipeline; discusses privacy, communication, theoretical guarantees, and applications; identifies open challenges.
AI.result: Provides a comprehensive taxonomy and a formalized union of FCD and FCI into a federated causal reasoning framework, clarifying methodological choices and practical considerations, and delivering a consolidated view to guide future research.
AI.conclusion: Emphasizes shared concerns over privacy, communication efficiency, and theoretical guarantees; outlines open challenges and directions for future work across taxonomy, data heterogeneity, and dynamic/real-world settings.

title: Critique of Agent Model
summary: What is an agent? What constitutes agency? With the rise of Large Language Model (LLM) systems marketed as ``coding agents'', ``AI co-scientists'', and other ``agentic" tools that promise to drive up productivity, and at the same time, ``existential" concerns such as AI escaping human control with destructive power under a speculative ``machine agency" against humans, it has become essential to clarify where automation ends and agency begins, both for building capable systems and for understanding whether and what to fear. Drawing on Descartes' grounding of agency in independent thought, and on portrayals of autonomous beings in science fiction, we survey the current landscape of AI agents, and analyze agent architectures along five dimensions: goal, identity, decision-making, self-regulation, and learning. Specifically, we argue that genuine agency requires these structures to be \emph{internalized within the system itself} rather than assembled through external scaffolding. This distinction between \emph{agentic} systems, whose competence resides in engineered workflows, and \emph{agentive} systems, whose capabilities (including social interaction) arise endogenously, defines the boundary between systems designed for prescribed tasks, and those capable of operating in the open world with true autonomy. Building on this analysis, we propose the Goal-Identity-Configurator (GIC) architecture for a general-purpose agent model, combining hierarchical goal decomposition, identity evolution, simulative reasoning grounded in a separately trained world model, learned self-regulation, and self-directed learning from both real and simulated experience. Furthermore, we share insight on the auditability, controllability, and safety of agentive systems that possess greater autonomy and ``agency", but remain under human oversight.

AI.tldr: Distinguishes agentic vs agentive AI, develops a five-dimension internalization framework, and proposes the Goal-Identity-Configurator (GIC) architecture for general-purpose agents, with safety/auditability under human oversight.
AI.motivation: Clarify what constitutes agency in AI amid emerging coding agents and autonomous systems, addressing both practical capabilities and existential fears by grounding agency in internal structures. Build a coherent, philosophically informed model to guide design and governance.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis inspired by Descartes and sci‑fi depictions of autonomous beings; survey of current AI agents; formalization along five dimensions (goal, identity, decision-making, self-regulation, learning); proposal of the GIC architecture; discussion of auditability and safety.
AI.result: A cohesive framework that differentiates agentic versus agentive systems, with a blueprint (GIC) for a general-purpose agent model integrating hierarchical goals, evolving identity, world-model grounded simulational reasoning, self-regulation, and self-directed learning; pragmatic safety and oversight guidelines.
AI.conclusion: Agency requires internalized structures rather than external scaffolding; agentive systems can exhibit autonomy under human oversight, but true autonomy benefits from internalized cognition and learning mechanisms; the GIC model offers a concrete path for building capable, auditable, and safely governed agents.

title: Mind the Heads: Topological Representation Alignment for Multimodal LLMs
summary: Representation alignment has emerged as an effective approach to improve Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) by regularizing their internal representations toward those of an external vision encoder. However, existing methods typically align a fixed layer of the language backbone, overlooking the fine-grained structure of Transformer models. In this work, we propose Head-Wise Representation Alignment (HeRA), a method that enforces cross-modal alignment at the level of individual attention heads. Our approach is grounded in the Platonic Representation Hypothesis, focusing on preserving the topological structure of representations (i.e., their local neighborhood relationships) across modalities. Following the Mutual K-Nearest Neighbor (MKNN) alignment metric, we introduce a contrastive objective that acts as a differentiable proxy for matching local structures. HeRA applies this objective during multimodal training to specific attention heads in the LLM, selected by their alignment score according to the MKNN metric. Counterintuitively, we find that aligning the least aligned heads yields the largest gains. Extensive evaluations across multiple MLLMs and 18 benchmarks demonstrate that HeRA consistently improves performance on challenging vision-centric tasks and serves as an effective regularizer against visual hallucinations by naturally curbing the over-reliance on linguistic priors. Our code is publicly released.

AI.tldr: Head-Wise Representation Alignment (HeRA) aligns cross-modal representations at the level of individual attention heads using an MKNN-based contrastive objective, selecting heads by their alignment score and finding that aligning the least-aligned heads yields the largest gains. Evaluations on multiple MLLMs and 18 benchmarks show consistent improvements on vision-centric tasks and reduced visual hallucinations; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the coarse, fixed-layer alignment in prior works by exploiting the fine-grained structure of Transformer models. Grounded in the Platonic Representation Hypothesis to preserve local neighborhood structure across modalities.
AI.method: Compute an MKNN-based alignment score for each attention head during multimodal training. Select heads with the best (or worst, per findings) alignment scores and apply a differentiable contrastive objective that preserves local representation structures across modalities. Train MLLMs end-to-end with head-wise regularization integrated into the multimodal objective.
AI.result: Improved performance on vision-centric tasks across various MLLMs and 18 benchmarks; acts as a regularizer to reduce visual hallucinations by limiting over-reliance on linguistic priors; code publicly released.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained, head-level representation alignment provides consistent gains and serves as an effective regularizer for MLLMs, suggesting the importance of preserving local cross-modal structure rather than solely aligning fixed layers.

title: SPACE: Enabling Learning from Cross-Robot Data Toward Generalist Policies
summary: In robot learning, scaling training datasets across diverse embodiments and environments has become a dominant paradigm for learning generalizable robot policies. These policies are commonly trained via behavior cloning to imitate actions from pre-collected demonstrations. However, since robot actions are tied to the dynamics of the data collection robot, different robots may require different actions to achieve the same motion. This discrepancy hinders both policy training and deployment across diverse robots. To address this, we propose using Cartesian state delta as a universal action representation across robots, and introduce State Prediction and Adaptive Command Execution (SPACE) framework. SPACE handles robot dynamics variation at three levels: across different embodiments, across hardware units of the same embodiment, and within a single robot during operation. It consists of two components: (i) a Cartesian state delta policy that predicts geometric end-effector displacement, and (ii) Action Adapter, which converts the predicted Cartesian state delta into robot-specific control commands. Experiments show that SPACE substantially outperforms policies that directly predict control commands when learning from data collected across different embodiments and across hardware units of the same embodiment. SPACE also remains robust under dynamics shifts at deployment, including changes in control frequency, object weight, and controller gains. The project page is available at http://haeone.site/space-website/.

AI.tldr: SPACE: a universal action representation using Cartesian state delta with an Action Adapter to map to robot-specific commands, enabling cross-embodiment, cross-hardware, and deployment-time robustness; outperforms direct control-command policies.
AI.motivation: Behavior cloning policies are tied to the data-collection robot dynamics; transferring across embodiments and hardware is hard due to robot-specific action semantics, hindering generalization and deployment.
AI.method: SPACE comprises two components: (i) a Cartesian state delta policy that predicts geometric end-effector displacement, and (ii) an Action Adapter that converts the predicted Cartesian delta into robot-specific control commands. It addresses dynamics variation across embodiments, across hardware units, and within a single robot during operation.
AI.result: Experiments show SPACE substantially outperforms policies that directly predict control commands when learning from data collected across different embodiments and across hardware units of the same embodiment. SPACE remains robust under deployment dynamics shifts such as changes in control frequency, object weight, and controller gains.
AI.conclusion: A universal Cartesian delta action representation coupled with an adapter enables better generalization and robustness to dynamics across diverse robots, reducing the coupling between policy learning and robot-specific dynamics.

title: Low-power analogue neural networks with trainable nonlinear connections for continuous control
comment: Preprint. Further verification of all simulations is ongoing. Any resulting corrections will be incorporated in a revised version
summary: Physical neural networks promise low-power machine learning by computing directly with analogue device physics, but most architectures force nonlinear device responses to act as scalar weights. Inspired by Kolmogorov-Arnold networks, we place trainable nonlinear functions on the connections, making each physical connection a learnable computational element. Realising these functions as analogue band-pass filters on field-programmable analogue arrays, we find that the benefit is task-dependent and follows from the smoothness of the physical basis: the networks represent smooth, continuously valued targets, including robotic kinematics, continuous control, and photovoltaic maximum-power-point tracking, with far fewer nodes and connections than multilayer perceptrons, but offer no parameter-efficiency advantage on classification-like decision boundaries. Trained networks transfer to hardware across approximately 35,000 connections with quantified fidelity, and a dedicated CMOS implementation is projected to operate at approximately 30 microwatts. A memristive realisation reproduces the same behaviour in simulation, indicating that the advantage comes from placing trainable nonlinearity on connections, rather than from a particular device.

AI.tldr: Trainable nonlinearities on connections in physical neural networks (Kolmogorov-Arnold-inspired) outperform conventional scalar-weighted nets on smooth-function tasks, enabling efficient analogue hardware implementations, but provide limited parameter-efficiency for classification.
AI.motivation: To exploit analogue device physics by moving the nonlinearity from neuron-like units to the connections, enabling learnable, smooth mappings that better capture continuous control and function approximation tasks while aiming for low-power hardware realization.
AI.method: Implement trainable nonlinear functions as analogue band-pass filters realized on field-programmable analogue arrays (FPAA). Train these connection-wise nonlinearities; evaluate on continuous tasks (robotic kinematics, continuous control, PV maximum-power-point tracking); assess transfer to hardware (~35k connections) and project CMOS energy (~30 μW); corroborate with memristive simulations.
AI.result: Networks represent smooth, continuously valued targets with far fewer nodes/connections than MLPs; task dependence observed: benefits align with smoothness of targets; no parameter-efficiency advantage for classification boundaries; hardware transfer feasible across ~35k connections with quantified fidelity; CMOS implementation projected at ~30 μW; memristive realization reproduces the same behavior in simulation, suggesting the advantage stems from connection-wise nonlinearities rather than device specifics.
AI.conclusion: Placing trainable nonlinearities on connections enables efficient learning for smooth-function tasks and supports energy-efficient analogue hardware; benefits are task-dependent and do not translate into classification efficiency; device-agnostic implementation (memristive) reinforces the core principle that connection-based nonlinearity—not the particular device—drives performance.

title: Safe and Generalizable Hierarchical Multi-Agent RL via Constraint Manifold Control
summary: Multi-agent systems are widely used in safety-critical applications that require coordinated behavior under strict safety constraints. Existing approaches face a fundamental trade-off: learning-based methods achieve strong empirical performance but lack theoretical safety guarantees, while control-theoretic methods enforce safety but often lead to overly conservative and inefficient behaviors. We propose a hierarchical multi-agent reinforcement learning framework that enforces hard safety constraints under mild assumptions at low level via a constraint manifold, while enabling effective coordination through high-level policy learning. Our approach provides theoretical safety guarantees in the multi-agent setting and yields stationary learning dynamics, thereby enabling stable and efficient training. Empirically, our method achieves competitive performance while maintaining nearly perfect safety rates, and generalizes effectively to varying numbers of agents and obstacles.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical MARL with a low-level constraint manifold enforces hard safety, while high-level policies coordinate agents; provides theoretical safety guarantees, stationary learning dynamics, and competitive performance with near-perfect safety and good generalization across varying agent counts and obstacles.
AI.motivation: Safely coordinating multiple agents is crucial in safety-critical domains. Learning-based methods excel empirically but lack guarantees; control-theoretic methods ensure safety but can be overly conservative. A hierarchical approach aims to combine strengths while providing formal safety assurances.
AI.method: A two-tier framework: a low-level safety layer enforces hard safety constraints via a constraint manifold (under mild assumptions), and a high-level policy learns coordination among agents. The paper claims theoretical safety guarantees and stationary learning dynamics, facilitating stable, efficient training.
AI.result: The framework achieves theoretical safety guarantees and stationary learning dynamics. Empirically, it shows competitive performance with near-perfect safety rates and strong generalization to different numbers of agents and obstacle configurations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical MARL approach effectively balances safety and efficiency, delivering formal guarantees and robust scalability. It is well-suited for safety-critical multi-agent tasks, though practical deployment will require careful specification of the constraint manifold and assumptions.

title: REALM: A Unified Red-Teaming Benchmark for Physical-World VLMs
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used as perception-reasoning backbones for embodied intelligence in safety-critical physical systems, where perception or reasoning errors can lead to unsafe decisions or actions. Although many red-teaming methods have been developed to probe VLM vulnerabilities, their evaluation remains fragmented across datasets, metrics, and threat models, making direct comparison difficult and obscuring whether observed differences arise from stronger attacks, more vulnerable models, or incompatible evaluation settings. Existing chatbot-centric red-teaming benchmarks mainly standardize jailbreak and content-safety evaluation, but they do not systematically capture physically grounded functional failures or cover red-teaming methods that target physical-world VLMs. This raises the key challenge of comparing diverse attack methods under a unified protocol while targeting the same scenario-specific failures. We introduce REALM, to our knowledge the first unified red-teaming benchmark for physical-world VLMs. REALM integrates 12 red-teaming methods, 3 model-agnostic defenses, and 13 VLMs under a practical black-box threat model with shared datasets and metrics. To align adversarial objectives across attack families, REALM introduces an agentic target-generation pipeline that constructs shared, scenario-specific, and physically grounded attack objectives for each scene, enabling fair comparison of diverse red-teaming methods under aligned adversarial goals. Our evaluation shows that text and typographic injection attacks induce the most failures, multimodal co-optimization yields the strongest visual-perturbation transfer, single-pass attacks approach iterative methods at much lower cost, and model scale alone does not confer adversarial robustness. Code is available at https://github.com/UCF-ML-Research/REALM.

AI.tldr: REALM unifies red-teaming for physical-world VLMs
AI.motivation: Addresses fragmentation in evaluation across datasets, metrics, and threat models for vision-language models in safety-critical, physical-world settings; aims to enable fair, apples-to-apples comparisons of diverse attacks.
AI.method: A unified benchmark that integrates 12 red-teaming methods, 3 model-agnostic defenses, and 13 VLMs under a black-box threat model with shared datasets and metrics. Introduces an agentic target-generation pipeline to produce scenario-specific, physically grounded attack objectives for each scene, aligning adversarial goals across attack families.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows text and typographic injections cause the most failures; multimodal co-optimization yields strong visual perturbation transfer; single-pass attacks approach iterative methods at lower cost; model scale alone does not confer robustness.
AI.conclusion: REALM provides a comprehensive, comparable evaluation protocol for physical-world VLM red-teaming and enables fair comparisons across attack methods, defenses, and models; code is publicly available.

title: MinInter: Minimizing Trajectory Interpolation During Data Augmentation for Imitation Learning
summary: Imitation learning enables robots to acquire complex manipulation skills from demonstrations, but its effectiveness is limited by the cost of collecting high-quality data. Trajectory-level data augmentation methods alleviate this challenge by recombining expert demonstrations under varied initial states. However, such methods typically insert interpolations or other non-expert transition segments between disjoint parts, and such non-expert segments could reduce the quality of the generated data. This paper introduces Minimizing Interpolation (MinInter), an effective trajectory selection method that, for each sampled initial configuration, chooses the source demonstration requiring the least interpolation to form a complete trajectory. By explicitly minimizing interpolations during data generation, MinInter produces higher-quality synthetic demonstrations while remaining compatible with existing data generation frameworks. Experiments on 12 manipulation tasks with 26 variants from the MimicGen benchmark show that MinInter consistently improves both data generation success rates and policy success rates, with the largest gains on contact-rich, long-horizon and high-variance settings. Compared to the recent SkillGen framework, MinInter achieves higher policy success rates despite its conceptual simplicity, underscoring the value of interpolation minimization for data augmentation.

AI.tldr: MinInter selects source demonstrations that require the least interpolation to complete a trajectory during data augmentation, improving data quality and policy performance in imitation learning across 12 tasks and outperforming SkillGen.
AI.motivation: Explicitly minimize non-expert interpolations in trajectory-augmentation to improve data quality and policy success, especially for long-horizon, contact-rich, and high-variance tasks.
AI.method: For each sampled initial configuration, choose the source demonstration that minimizes the amount of interpolation needed to form a full trajectory, then generate synthetic data within existing frameworks.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in data-generation success and policy success across 12 MimicGen manipulation tasks (26 variants); largest gains in challenging settings; outperforming SkillGen in policy success.
AI.conclusion: Interpolation minimization is a simple, effective augmentation strategy that yields higher-quality synthetic demonstrations and can be integrated with current data-generation pipelines.

title: Synergizing Physically Constrained MCMC and Chemical-Informed Gaussian Processes for Reaction Network Discovery
summary: Extracting interpretable governing equations from sparse, noisy chemical time-series data remains difficult because discrete reaction topology and continuous kinetic parameters are tightly coupled. We present PC-MCMC-CIGP, a reproducible gray-box workflow that combines spike-and-slab topology sampling, hard conservation and thermodynamic screening, and a Chemical-Informed Gaussian Process (CIGP) residual model for parameter calibration and experimental design. The methodological contribution is not a new MCMC or GP family in isolation; rather, it is the integration of these components into a physically constrained workflow with explicit uncertainty-aware acquisition choices. On the H2 + Br2 benchmark, the constrained sampler distinguishes elementary radical pathways from deceptive phenomenological fits in our experiments. On styrene epoxidation, the CIGP optimization loop improves final yield by 12.5% over the reported GP-BO baseline. A new 10-seed acquisition study shows that EI, GWU, PC-EI, uncertainty sampling, discrepancy hunting, and random search have different trade-offs: PC-EI substantially reduces low-yield BO suggestions, while EI-style criteria give the strongest final-yield performance.

AI.tldr: A reproducible gray-box workflow (PC-MCMC-CIGP) that integrates spike-and-slab topology sampling, hard conservation/thermodynamic screening, and a Chemical-Informed Gaussian Process residual model to infer interpretable governing equations from sparse, noisy chemical time-series data; demonstrates improved path discrimination and yield, with nuanced acquisition trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Disentangle discrete reaction topology from continuous kinetic parameters in sparse, noisy chemical time-series data and enable uncertainty-aware experimental design for learning governing equations.
AI.method: Integrates (i) spike-and-slab topology sampling to explore network structure, (ii) hard conservation and thermodynamic screening to enforce physical plausibility, and (iii) a Chemical-Informed Gaussian Process (CIGP) residual model for parameter calibration. The workflow is framed as a gray-box inference system with uncertainty-aware acquisition strategies. An empirical study uses a 10-seed acquisition design to compare EI, GWU, PC-EI, uncertainty sampling, discrepancy hunting, and random search.
AI.result: On the H2 + Br2 benchmark, the constrained sampler distinguishes elementary radical pathways from deceptive phenomenological fits. On styrene epoxidation, the CIGP loop improves final yield by 12.5% over the GP-BO baseline. The 10-seed acquisition study reveals trade-offs: PC-EI reduces low-yield BO suggestions, while EI-style criteria yield the strongest final yield.
AI.conclusion: Integrating topology sampling, physical constraints, and physics-informed GP residuals into a single, uncertainty-aware gray-box workflow enhances interpretability and optimization in chemical kinetics and informs acquisition strategies for data-scarce problems. The approach shows practical gains in yield and improved discrimination of true reaction pathways, suggesting broad applicability to data-constrained, physics-informed system identification in chemistry.

title: Reinforcement Learning Towards Broadly and Persistently Beneficial Models
summary: As AI systems are deployed across increasingly diverse and high-stakes settings, model alignment must generalize beyond the tasks and domains seen during training. This is especially important for reinforcement learning (RL), which can introduce unexpected misalignment through reward hacking, deception, or other unintended strategies. We study whether RL on beneficial behavior, instantiated in realistic domains, can produce broad and persistent alignment generalization beyond the training distribution. We construct a dataset of realistic situations designed to measure and train beneficial traits, such as truthfulness, fairness, risk awareness, and corrigibility, spanning varied domains, including health, science, and education. We then train models with RL on this dataset and evaluate them on more than 50 independent benchmarks of alignment and beneficial behavior. Compared to a compute-matched baseline, beneficial trait RL improves performance on over 80% of these out-of-distribution benchmarks. We observe substantial out-of-distribution alignment transfer: a beneficial-behavior RL intervention entirely limited to one domain, health, produces broad improvements on non-health alignment evaluations, including reduced reward hacking, deception, and general misalignment. Finally, we study alignment persistence: whether behavior remains robustly aligned under attempts to steer models towards misalignment. Models trained with beneficial trait RL show improved persistence, including greater resistance to adversarial prompting and harmful finetuning; further work is required to isolate the sources of these effects. These results suggest that RL to reinforce beneficial behavior in realistic domains can produce models that are more robustly aligned with human flourishing.

AI.tldr: RL trained on datasets of beneficial behavior yields broad, out-of-distribution alignment and persistence, outperforming baselines across many benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of aligning RL agents beyond training tasks/domains by focusing on beneficial behaviors in realistic domains to promote general alignment with human flourishing.
AI.method: Create a dataset of beneficial traits (truthfulness, fairness, risk awareness, corrigibility) across health, science, education; train RL agents on this dataset; evaluate on 50+ independent alignment benchmarks; compare with compute-matched baselines; test transfer and persistence under misalignment attempts.
AI.result: Beneficial-trait RL improves performance on about 80% of out-of-distribution benchmarks; demonstrates substantial cross-domain alignment transfer from domain-specific training; showed enhanced persistence against adversarial prompting and harmful fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcing beneficial behavior via RL in realistic domains can yield models that are more robustly aligned with human flourishing; further work needed to identify the sources of observed robustness and persistence.

title: The Professor: Multi-Teacher Unsupervised Prompt Distillation for Vision-Language Models
summary: Prompt distillation compresses large vision-language models (VLMs) such as CLIP into lightweight student models by matching teacher predictions on unlabeled domain images. PromptKD (CVPR 2024) established this paradigm with a single PromptSRC-finetuned ViT-L/14 teacher and a ViT-B/16 student. We propose TheProfessor, a multi-teacher extension that distills from a fixed two-teacher ensemble: a domain-finetuned PromptSRC ViT-L/14 teacher and a zero-shot EVA-CLIP-L/14 teacher whose logits are pre-computed per dataset.
