We evaluate single-teacher PromptKD, equal-probability ensembling, and confidence-weighted ensembling on four base-to-novel datasets: Caltech-101, DTD, UCF101, and EuroSAT. In a 12-run single-seed sweep, confidence-weighted ensembling improves average HM from 87.52 to 89.28 (+1.77 points), while equal averaging improves average HM to 88.88 (+1.37 points). Gains are dataset dependent: they are negligible on Caltech-101 (+0.16 HM for confidence weighting), modest on UCF101 (+0.62), and largest on domain-shifted EuroSAT (+5.78). These results update our earlier Caltech-only analysis and show that multi-teacher prompt distillation is most useful when the second teacher contributes complementary supervision under domain shift.

AI.tldr: TheProfessor extends PromptKD with a fixed two-teacher ensemble (PromptSRC domain-tuned ViT-L/14 + EVA-CLIP-L/14 precomputed logits) for multi-teacher prompt distillation; improves HM on several datasets, especially under domain shift; gains are dataset-dependent and build on prior Caltech-only analysis.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of single-teacher prompt distillation by leveraging complementary supervision from a second teacher, particularly when domain shift occurs, thereby improving student performance.
AI.method: Introduce TheProfessor, a two-teacher ensemble where distillation uses a fixed pair of teachers: a domain-finetuned PromptSRC ViT-L/14 and a zero-shot EVA-CLIP-L/14 with per-dataset precomputed logits. Compare single-teacher PromptKD, equal-probability ensembling, and confidence-weighted ensembling on four base-to-novel datasets (Caltech-101, DTD, UCF101, EuroSAT) across 12 runs with a single seed.
AI.result: Confidence-weighted ensembling improves average HM from 87.52 to 89.28 (+1.77). Equal averaging yields 88.88 (+1.37). Gains are dataset-dependent: Caltech-101 +0.16, UCF101 +0.62, EuroSAT +5.78; EuroSAT shows the largest boost under domain shift.
AI.conclusion: Multi-teacher prompt distillation provides complementary supervision that is most beneficial under domain shift; TheProfessor extends PromptKD and updates prior Caltech-only analyses by demonstrating benefits when a second teacher contributes valuable external signals.

title: DynaWM: Dynamics-Aware Distillation with World Model and Momentum Targets for Smooth Locomotion over Continuous Stairs
comment: Comments: 8 pages, 7 figures, accepted by IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS)
summary: Recent advances in control have enabled bipedal-wheeled robots to traverse slopes and single-step obstacles, yet long staircase traversal remains challenging as current teacher-student frameworks suffer from weakened dynamics-aware representations and incomplete terrain geometry encoding. To bridge this gap, we propose DynaWM, a dynamics-aware representation learning framework. To enhance terrain encoding capability and enable transparent assessment, we introduce a world model as a regularizer to enforce forward-dynamics awareness, preserving comprehensive terrain geometry while facilitating hierarchical encoding visualization. To stabilize knowledge transfer, we employ a momentum target encoder to provide consistent distillation targets, preventing dimensional collapse from non-stationary teacher updates. Evaluation of the learned representations through Principal Component Analysis (PCA) visualization and quantitative metrics reveals that our encoder hierarchically captures terrain geometry with higher terrain encoding capability, leading to enhanced terrain adaptability and motion smoothness. Experimental results in simulation and real hardware demonstrate that our method achieves superior terrain adaptability and motion smoothness, enabling bipedal-wheeled robots to overcome diverse continuous stairs, as shown in Fig. 1.

AI.tldr: DynaWM introduces a dynamics-aware representation learning framework for bipedal-wheeled robots to improve terrain encoding and long staircase traversal, using a world-model regularizer and a momentum target encoder to stabilize knowledge transfer; empirical evaluation via PCA and metrics shows hierarchical terrain geometry capture and improved adaptability and motion smoothness in simulation and hardware.
AI.motivation: Current teacher-student frameworks suffer from weakened dynamics-aware representations and incomplete terrain geometry encoding, hindering long staircase traversal for bipedal-wheeled robots. A dynamics-aware, interpretable representation with stable distillation could enhance terrain understanding and locomotion quality.
AI.method: Introduce DynaWM: a dynamics-aware representation learning framework. Use a world model as a regularizer to enforce forward-dynamics awareness while preserving comprehensive terrain geometry and enabling hierarchical encoding visualization. Employ a momentum target encoder to stabilize knowledge transfer and prevent dimensional collapse due to non-stationary teacher updates. Evaluate representations with PCA visualization and quantitative metrics.
AI.result: The encoder hierarchically captures terrain geometry with higher terrain encoding capability, improving terrain adaptability and motion smoothness. In simulation and real hardware, DynaWM achieves superior terrain adaptability and motion smoothness, enabling bipedal-wheeled robots to traverse diverse continuous stairs (as illustrated in Fig. 1).
AI.conclusion: DynaWM provides a dynamics-aware, interpretable representation learning approach that enhances terrain encoding and transfer in bipedal-wheeled locomotion, enabling robust traversal of challenging terrains like continuous stairs, with demonstrated benefits in both simulation and real hardware.

title: Exploring Dualistic Meta-Learning to Enhance Domain Generalization in Open Set Scenarios
summary: Domain generalization learns from multiple source domains to generalize to unseen target domains. However, it often neglects the realistic case of label mismatch between source and target. Open set domain generalization is then proposed to recognize unseen classes in unseen domains. A simple approach trains one-vs-all classifiers to separate each class and detect outliers as unknown. Yet, the imbalance between few positive samples and many negative samples skews the decision boundary towards the positive ones, leading the model to over-reject out-of-distribution data, even from known classes in unseen domains. In this paper, we propose a novel meta-learning stategy called dualistic MEta-learning with joint DomaIn-Class matching (MEDIC), which considers implicit gradient matching towards inter-domain and inter-class task splits simultaneously to find optimal boundaries balanced for both domains and classes. Experimental results show that MEDIC not only outperforms prior methods in open set scenarios, but also maintains competitive close set generalization ability.

AI.tldr: MEDIC is a meta-learning approach for open-set domain generalization that balances inter-domain and inter-class boundaries via implicit gradient matching, addressing label mismatch and class-imbalance.
AI.motivation: Open-set DG faces label mismatch and severe class imbalance when distinguishing unseen classes; standard one-vs-all strategies bias the boundary toward known positives, causing over-rejection of unknowns and even in-distribution data.
AI.method: Proposes dualistic meta-learning with joint Domain-Class matching (MEDIC). It uses implicit gradient matching across inter-domain and inter-class task splits to find boundaries balanced for both domain and class variations.
AI.result: Empirical results show MEDIC outperforms existing open-set DG methods and maintains competitive closed-set generalization.
AI.conclusion: MEDIC provides a principled meta-learning mechanism to balance domain and class decision boundaries under label mismatch, improving open-set DG while preserving in-distribution generalization.

title: Can Language Model Agents be Helpful Circuit Explainers in Mechanistic Interpretability?
summary: Mechanistic interpretability has made substantial progress in automatically localizing circuits, but explaining what localized components do remains labor-intensive and difficult to standardize. In this work, we study whether language model (LM) agents can assist with this explanation problem once a circuit has already been identified. We introduce AgenticInterpBench, a benchmark for circuit explanation built from 84 semi-synthetic transformer circuits with 163 component-level annotations. We propose HyVE (Hypothesize, Validate, Explain), an agentic explainer that analyzes each component through an iterative loop of observation, hypothesis generation, and causal validation, eventually producing a component-level explanation and a circuit-level task description. Across four LM backbones, HyVE recovers useful component- and task-level explanations, but no backbone is uniformly best. Our analysis shows that strong backbones usually form observation-grounded hypotheses, while failures more often arise later in the validation loop, through incomplete validation plans, code execution errors, or unresolved hypotheses. A case study on an arithmetic circuit in Llama-3-8B shows that the same formulation can extend beyond semi-synthetic benchmarks to naturally trained models. Overall, LM agents are promising circuit explainers, but reliable validation remains the key obstacle.

AI.tldr: AgenticInterpBench and HyVE enable language-model-based circuit explanations: an iterative Observation–Hypothesis–Validation loop produces component- and circuit-level explanations for semi-synthetic transformer circuits, but reliable validation remains the main bottleneck; a case study on Llama-3-8B suggests applicability to naturally trained models.
AI.motivation: Mechanistic interpretability progress localizes circuits but struggles to standardize explanations. The work investigates whether LM agents can automate or aid the explanation process after circuits are identified.
AI.method: Introduce AgenticInterpBench (84 semi-synthetic transformer circuits, 163 component-level annotations) and HyVE (Hypothesize, Validate, Explain). HyVE iterates observation, hypothesis generation, and causal validation to produce component-level explanations and circuit-level task descriptions. Evaluation across four LM backbones, plus a case study on Llama-3-8B.
AI.result: HyVE recovers useful component- and task-level explanations across backbones, though no single backbone dominates. Strong backbones tend to form observation-grounded hypotheses; validation often fails due to incomplete validation plans, code execution errors, or unresolved hypotheses.
AI.conclusion: LM agents are promising circuit explainers, but reliable validation remains the key obstacle. The approach generalizes to naturally trained models (e.g., arithmetic circuit in Llama-3-8B), indicating broader applicability.

title: Trustworthy Image Authentication using Forensic Knowledge Graphs
summary: Advances in generative AI have made image falsification highly realistic, demanding trustworthy authentication systems. Existing forensic detectors can target certain forgery types but lack interpretability, while vision-language models (VLMs) provide explanations but cannot exploit forensic traces for reliable detection. We propose Forensic Knowledge Graphs (FKGs), a unified framework that integrates forensic evidence extraction, structured reasoning, and human-interpretable explanation. Our FKG structure encodes forensic traces along with their causal dependencies and links to scene content. To generate accurate FKGs, we introduce a novel forensic authentication network and an Iterative Context Refinement strategy that guides VLMs to produce faithful, grounded explanations. We also present FKG-50K, a dataset of 50,000 realistic forgeries with ground-truth FKGs. Experiments demonstrate that FKG outperforms both forensic detectors and VLMs in detection, forgery identification and localization, and forensic justification.

AI.tldr: A unified forensic knowledge graph framework (FKG) for authenticating images by integrating forensic evidence extraction, causal reasoning, and interpretable explanations, paired with a large FKG-50K dataset; shows improvements over detectors and VLMs in detection, identification, localization, and justification.
AI.motivation: Recent advances in generative AI produce highly realistic forgeries. Existing detectors lack interpretability, and vision-language models provide explanations but don't leverage forensic traces for reliable detection. A unified, interpretable framework that grounds explanations in forensic evidence is needed.
AI.method: Introduce FKG structure that encodes forensic traces, their causal dependencies, and links to scene content. Develop a forensic authentication network and an Iterative Context Refinement strategy to guide VLMs toward faithful, grounded explanations. Create FKG-50K, a dataset of 50k realistic forgeries with ground-truth FKGs.
AI.result: FKG outperforms both forensic detectors and VLMs in detection, forgery identification, localization, and forensic justification on the FKG-50K dataset and related experiments.
AI.conclusion: FKG provides interpretable, grounded forensic reasoning by coupling forensic traces with scene content, aided by iterative refinement and a large-scale dataset, advancing trustworthy authentication of manipulated images.

title: NavWM: A Unified Navigation World Model for Foresight-Driven Planning
summary: Conventional visual navigation policies often struggle with myopic decision-making and mode collapse in complex environments. While world models offer a promising alternative, existing paradigms typically isolate perception, generation, and control, failing to capture their shared spatio-temporal dynamics. In this paper, we propose NavWM, a unified navigation world model that seamlessly integrates latent world reasoning, multimodal action prediction, and controllable visual generation. At its core, NavWM leverages latent world tokens to distill geometric and semantic priors, endowing the agent with robust structural understanding. To overcome the limitations of deterministic policies, we introduce an anchor-based multimodal trajectory forecasting framework that generates a diverse action space. This inherent diversity explicitly empowers the generative world model to act as a robust closed-loop planner, utilizing visual foresight to evaluate and select the optimal path. Extensive experiments across diverse robotics datasets demonstrate that NavWM significantly advances the state-of-the-art, delivering remarkable improvements in both high-fidelity future state generation and zero-shot navigation success.

AI.tldr: NavWM proposes a unified navigation world model that integrates latent world reasoning, multimodal action prediction, and controllable visual generation using latent world tokens; it introduces anchor-based multimodal trajectory forecasting to enable robust planning via visual foresight, achieving state-of-the-art results in high-fidelity future-state generation and zero-shot navigation.
AI.motivation: Conventional navigation policies are often myopic and prone to mode collapse; existing world-models tend to isolate perception, generation, and control, failing to capture shared spatio-temporal dynamics. A unified approach could better leverage joint dynamics for robust planning and generation.
AI.method: NavWM uses latent world tokens to encode geometric/semantic priors; introduces an anchor-based multimodal trajectory forecasting framework to produce diverse action hypotheses; treats the generative world model as a closed-loop planner guided by visual foresight; integrates controllable visual generation with planning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on diverse robotics datasets show significant improvements, including high-fidelity future state generation and enhanced zero-shot navigation success.
AI.conclusion: NavWM advances state-of-the-art by unifying perception, imagination, and control under a shared latent dynamics framework, with multimodal trajectory forecasting enabling robust, diverse planning and reliable navigation.

title: One Ruler: A Same-Hands Re-Evaluation of Bivariate Causal Direction on Tuebingen, with a Parameter-Free Compression Baseline
comment: 15 pages, 1 table. Code, pre-registrations and per-pair outputs: https://github.com/WStienstra/one-ruler-cep
summary: Headline accuracies on the Tuebingen cause-effect pairs are routinely compared across papers even though each is measured under its authors' own protocol -- different pair subsets, weightings, model-selection, and decision rates. We argue this is the wrong comparison and run the right one: a same-hands re-evaluation in which every method is run by us on the identical 102 pairs, with one strict rule -- no tuning and a decision forced on every pair. As a clean reference point we introduce a deliberately minimal baseline: sorted-conditional compression, which feeds quantized, sorted, first-differenced data to an off-the-shelf compressor (bz2) and has zero fitted parameters. Under the common ruler the ranking differs sharply from the literature. Our baseline reaches 74.7% weighted accuracy (p = 3.7e-7); on the same 100 pairs that SLOPE is evaluated on it scores 76.0%, a 1.2-point gap below the authors' own forced-decision SLOPE (77.2%) that is well inside noise (McNemar p = 0.39). A faithful re-run of RECI lands at 70.7% -- inside the original authors' reported error bar, not the 77.5% often quoted (which we trace to a mis-copied cell). SLOPE's published 82.4% is a decided-subset figure: scoring the authors' own stored output only on the pairs its significance test chose to answer reproduces 81.7%. Under the common ruler the methods cluster in the low-to-mid 70s and the zero-parameter compressor ties the strongest of them. We document the mechanisms that inflate published figures (test-set model selection, significance-gated abstention) and contribute two further results: compression score magnitude is a model-free confounding flag (p = 2.8e-68), and a pre-registered falsification test fails in an instructive way that bounds the method's theoretical interpretation. Code, pre-registrations, and per-pair outputs are released.

AI.tldr: A careful, common-evaluation re-run shows that published headline-accuracy figures on Tuebingen cause-effect pairs are inflated by evaluation artifacts; a zero-parameter baseline is competitive, and many methods cluster in the low-to-mid 70s under a uniform protocol.
AI.motivation: To enable fair, reproducible benchmarking of causal inference methods on cause-effect pair data by re-evaluating all methods under a single protocol and without tuning, exposing evaluation biases such as test-set model selection and abstention.
AI.method: Re-run a fixed set of 102 pairs for all methods with no parameter tuning and a forced decision on every pair. Introduce a minimal, zero-parameter baseline based on sorted-conditional compression fed into a standard compressor (bz2). Compare against published methods (SLOPE, RECI) under the same protocol, audit reported figures for inconsistencies, examine effects of data processing (e.g., mis-copied cells), and perform pre-registered falsification tests. Release code, pre-registrations, and per-pair outputs.
AI.result: Baseline: 74.7% weighted accuracy (p = 3.7e-7). SLOPE on the full 102 with common protocol: 76.0%; the authors’ forced-decision SLOPE: 77.2% (within noise, McNemar p = 0.39). RECI: 70.7% (within the original error bar; 77.5% often quoted due to a mis-copied cell). SLOPE’s published 82.4% reflects a decided-subset; using authors’ own outputs on the chosen pairs yields 81.7%. Under the common protocol, methods cluster in the low-to-mid 70s and the zero-parameter compressor ties the best performers. Compression-score magnitude emerges as a strong, model-free confounding flag (p = 2.8e-68). A pre-registered falsification test fails in a way that bounds the method’s theoretical interpretation.
AI.conclusion: Reported headline accuracies are susceptible to evaluation biases from test-set choices and abstention. A strict, no-tuning protocol with a simple baseline provides a more robust comparison, showing weaker performance for many claims and exposing the confounding role of compression magnitude. The authors’ data/code are released to support reproducibility.

title: Breaking the Filter Bubble: A Semantic Pareto-DQN Framework for Multi-Objective Recommendation
comment: IEEE International Conference on Responsible Artificial Intelligence (IRAI) - 2026
summary: Recommender systems often induce filter bubbles and semantic homogenization by monolithically optimizing for immediate user engagement. Standard single-objective models, including traditional Deep Q-Networks, are ill-equipped to navigate the trade-offs between platform retention and critical societal values like information diversity and provider fairness. To address these limitations, we introduce a multi-objective reinforcement learning framework that formalizes recommendation as a semantic multi-objective Markov decision process. By integrating high-fidelity semantic embeddings with a Pareto-DQN agent, our architecture treats engagement, diversity, and fairness as distinct, non-aggregable reward signals, avoiding the pitfalls of static reward scalarization. Empirical evaluations on the MovieLens small dataset shows that our hypervolume based action selection disrupts the feedback loops responsible for semantic collapse. By sustaining high state-trajectory variance, the Pareto-DQN effectively maps the Pareto frontier, achieving gains in auxiliary societal objectives with only marginal impacts on engagement. This work provides a path toward intrinsically aligned, responsible recommender systems.

AI.tldr: Multi-objective reinforcement learning using Pareto-DQN for recommender systems balances engagement, diversity, and fairness with semantic embeddings; demonstrates Pareto frontier discovery and reduced feedback loops on MovieLens with minimal engagement loss.
AI.motivation: Single-objective recommender systems optimize for engagement, causing filter bubbles, homogenization, and neglect of societal values like diversity and fairness; a multi-objective framework is needed to reconcile competing goals.
AI.method: Formalize recommendation as a semantic multi-objective MDP; integrate high-fidelity semantic embeddings and use a Pareto-DQN agent that treats engagement, diversity, and fairness as separate, non-aggregated rewards; employ hypervolume-based action selection to navigate the Pareto frontier without reward scalarization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on the MovieLens small dataset shows hypervolume-based action selection disrupts feedback loops causing semantic collapse; sustained state-trajectory variance enables Pareto frontier mapping, yielding gains on auxiliary societal objectives with only marginal impact on engagement.
AI.conclusion: The work advances intrinsically aligned, responsible recommender systems by providing a practical multi-objective RL framework that avoids scalarizing rewards and explicitly balances engagement with diversity and fairness.

title: DivRL: Disentangled Self-Similarity Rewards for Diverse Subject-Driven Generation
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Project page: https://qianwangx.github.io/DivRL/
summary: Subject-driven image generation faces an "Identity-Diversity Paradox", where strong identity preservation often leads to rigid and low-diversity outputs. We propose a post-training framework called DivRL that jointly optimizes identity consistency and structural diversity simultaneously by leveraging disentangled visual features from a robust similarity model. Specifically, we introduce a Negative Self-Similarity Measure (nSSM) to quantify structural diversity, and Visual Semantic Matching (VSM) to evaluate identity consistency. We propose an "Explore-and-Suppress" strategy that treats VSM as a gated constraint: the model freely explores structurally diverse configurations, and only samples that violate the identity threshold are penalized via a quadratic hinge loss. This converts identity preservation from a competing objective into a feasibility constraint, allowing nSSM and VSM to improve jointly. Experiments demonstrate that our method effectively pushes the model to generate both consistent and diverse images and improves structural diversity while maintaining comparable identity consistency through a gated optimization formulation.

AI.tldr: DivRL is a post-training framework that resolves the identity-diversity paradox in subject-driven image generation by jointly optimizing identity consistency and structural diversity through nSSM (negative self-similarity measure) and VSM (visual semantic matching) within an Explore-and-Suppress gated constraint.
AI.motivation: To address the tension where strong identity preservation enforces low output diversity, the work seeks a framework that maintains identity while enabling high structural diversity in generated images.
AI.method: A post-training approach DivRL uses disentangled visual features from a robust similarity model. It introduces nSSM to quantify structural diversity and VSM to assess identity consistency. The Explore-and-Suppress strategy treats VSM as a gated constraint: the model freely explores diverse structural configurations and only incurs penalization (quadratic hinge loss) when identity constraints are violated, thus converting identity preservation into a feasibility constraint and enabling joint improvement of nSSM and VSM.
AI.result: Empirical evidence shows DivRL pushes the model toward generating images that are both identity-consistent and structurally diverse, achieving improved structural diversity with comparable identity preservation under the gated optimization framework.
AI.conclusion: DivRL provides a practical mechanism to mitigate the identity-diversity paradox in subject-driven generation by coupling nSSM and VSM under a gated constraint, enabling post-training enhancement of diversity without sacrificing identity fidelity.

title: The Evaluation Cost of Task Specialization in Evolutionary Multi-Robot Systems
comment: Accepted for publication at GECCO '26 Companion: Proceedings of the Genetic and Evolutionary Computation Conference Companion. Supplementary video: https://youtu.be/U5LNICts7Ek
summary: Task specialization can improve the efficiency of multi-robot systems (MRSs). Previous works have investigated the emergence of task-specialist robot controllers through evolutionary optimization and have argued that task specialization is more likely to evolve when subtask behaviors are readily available as building blocks. However, the available evaluation budget must be distributed across all subtasks, whereas a single generalist behavior can exploit the entire budget for its own optimization. We present a cost-benefit analysis of evolving task-specialist versus generalist behaviors in a foraging scenario here. In a physics-based robotics simulator, we study the total evaluation budget required to evolve task-specialist behaviors that outperform generalist behaviors across MRS sizes. We show that with increasing MRS size, a lower total evaluation budget is sufficient to evolve specialists that outperform generalists.

AI.tldr: In an evolving multi-robot foraging scenario, specialists require less total evaluation budget than generalists as swarm size grows; a cost-benefit analysis shows specialization becomes more energy-efficient with larger MRSs.
AI.motivation: To evaluate when task specialization yields superior performance under budget constraints, addressing inconsistencies in prior claims that specialization depends on the availability of subtasks.
AI.method: Physics-based robotics simulator; vary MRS size; compare evolution of task-specialist controllers vs a single generalist controller under a fixed total evaluation budget; measure when specialists outperform generalists.
AI.result: With increasing MRS size, a lower total evaluation budget suffices to evolve specialists that outperform generalists.
AI.conclusion: Task specialization is more economical for larger MRSs; building-block subtasks can facilitate specialization; supports the view that evaluation budgets should be allocated considering swarm size and task decomposition.

title: Deciphering Fingerprints of 3D Molecular Surfaces for Accurate Epitope Prediction
summary: Molecular surfaces encode the geometric and physicochemical patterns that determine antibody-antigen recognition, central to epitope prediction. However, existing methods rely on sequences or backbone structures and struggle to capture discontinuous, surface-driven epitopes. This study presents SurfBind, a surface-centric learning framework for epitope prediction that operates directly on molecular surface representations. SurfBind integrates geometric and physicochemical cues through a Transformer-based architecture with patch-level surface modeling, binder-aware cross-attention, and a hierarchical coarse-to-fine prediction paradigm. Experiments on challenging epitope identification benchmarks, including SAbDab and DB5.5, demonstrate that SurfBind achieves state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization across unseen antibodies and conformational states, highlighting the value of interaction-aware surface modeling for understanding the crucial mechanisms of protein-protein interactions.

AI.tldr: SurfBind is a surface-centric Transformer-based framework for epitope prediction that models molecular surface representations with patch-level, binder-aware cross-attention and a hierarchical coarse-to-fine prediction strategy, achieving state-of-the-art results and strong generalization on core benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard epitope prediction relies on sequence or backbone structures and struggles with discontinuous, surface-driven epitopes. Direct surface modeling aims to capture geometric and physicochemical cues essential for antibody-antigen recognition.
AI.method: SurfBind operates on molecular surface representations using a Transformer-based architecture. It employs patch-level surface modeling, binder-aware cross-attention, and a hierarchical coarse-to-fine prediction paradigm to integrate geometric and physicochemical cues for epitope prediction.
AI.result: On challenging benchmarks SAbDab and DB5.5, SurfBind achieves state-of-the-art performance and demonstrates strong generalization across unseen antibodies and conformational states.
AI.conclusion: Surface-aware, interaction-informed surface modeling is valuable for understanding protein-protein interactions and can significantly improve epitope prediction and potential antibody design.

title: Ensemble Feature Selection and Harris Hawks Optimization for Explainable Mental Health Risk Prediction in Female Sex Workers
comment: Accepted and presented at the 2026 8th IEEE Symposium on Computers & Informatics (ISCI 2026). To appear in IEEE conference proceedings
summary: One of the significant mental health issues affecting female sex workers (FSWs) is mental disorders, especially depression. Exposure to violence, stigma, and economic hardship further increases their psychological risk. Current machine learning (ML) models are typically ineffective at capturing the high-dimensional and complex risk patterns that exist in this marginalized group. This paper suggests a hybrid predictive model that merges an ensemble feature selection strategy using ANOVA and mutual information and Harris Hawks optimization-tuned logistic regression and represents a new application of swarm intelligence to predict mental health in vulnerable groups. The explainable AI (XAI) methods can be used to understand the factors of trauma associated with model predictions. When applied to a group of 3,005 FSWs, it can be seen that the proposed model is more effective than traditional classifiers, with an accuracy of 95.78%, an F1 score of 95.77%, and an AUC of 0.96, and identifying post-traumatic stress, client-related violence, and occupational factors as major contributors to depression. This work bridges the gaps between conventional and ML approaches to develop an XAI tool that enables vulnerable groups to receive early assistance, evidence-based targeted psychosocial care, and health planning.

AI.tldr: Hybrid predictive model combining ensemble feature selection (ANOVA + mutual information) with Harris Hawks optimization-tuned logistic regression to predict depression among female sex workers (FSWs). Applied to 3,005 individuals; achieves high performance (accuracy 95.78%, F1 95.77%, AUC 0.96). Uses XAI to identify trauma-related factors (PTSD, client-related violence, occupational factors) as major contributors. Aims to support early intervention and targeted psychosocial care through an XAI tool.
AI.motivation: Address high-dimensional, complex risk patterns and limitations of traditional ML in predicting mental health outcomes in a marginalized population (FSWs) exposed to violence, stigma, and economic hardship; leverage swarm intelligence and XAI to enable actionable insights for care and planning.
AI.method: Hybrid model: ensemble feature selection using ANOVA and mutual information; optimization-tuned logistic regression via Harris Hawks optimization; evaluation on 3,005 FSWs; performance metrics include accuracy, F1, and AUC; explainable AI (XAI) techniques used to interpret feature contributions and identify drivers of depression.
AI.result: Proposed model outperforms traditional classifiers, achieving accuracy 95.78%, F1 95.77%, AUC 0.96. XAI identifies major contributors as post-traumatic stress, client-related violence, and occupational factors.
AI.conclusion: Integrates conventional ML with swarm intelligence and XAI to provide early intervention support and evidence-based psychosocial care; demonstrates the potential of swarm-optimized models for mental health prediction in vulnerable groups and informs health planning.

title: Token-to-Token Alignment of Text Embeddings for Semantic Blending
summary: In modern generative models, images are specified and controlled through text prompts. In practice, images are generated from sequences of tokens derived from these prompts. However, the space of token sequences lacks a consistent accessible structure: semantically similar images may correspond to sequences that differ in wording, ordering, and placement of concepts, while similar token sequences may encode very different semantics. This apparent lack of structure makes it difficult to perform smooth transitions in this space, hindering applications such as image blending and continuous control of edits. We argue that this limitation stems not from the absence of semantic structure, but from misalignment between representations. To address this misalignment, we introduce Token-to-Token alignment, a framework that establishes explicit semantic correspondence between tokens across prompts. Our approach transforms prompts into a structured representation in which semantically corresponding concepts are mapped to consistent positions across prompts, and then aligns their token embeddings based on semantic similarity. Concretely, the method consists of two stages: a structural alignment that rephrases prompts into a shared structured form, followed by an embedding-level alignment that matches token representations across prompts. With this alignment in place, simple linear interpolation becomes a meaningful operation, producing smooth and coherent semantic transitions and enabling applications such as blending and continuous editing. Our results show that text embedding spaces in text-to-image models implicitly encode a continuous semantic structure that becomes accessible once representations are properly aligned, suggesting that semantic control can be achieved by organizing existing representations rather than modifying the generative model.

AI.tldr: Token-to-Token alignment aligns tokens across prompts via structural and embedding-level alignment to enable smooth semantic transitions in text-to-image prompts.
AI.motivation: The token-space for prompting lacks a consistent semantic structure, causing misalignment between semantics and token sequences, hindering smooth transitions and editing.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: (1) structural alignment that rephrases prompts into a shared structured form, and (2) embedding-level alignment that aligns token embeddings across prompts based on semantic similarity, enabling meaningful linear interpolation.
AI.result: The method reveals that text embedding spaces encode a continuous semantic structure once representations are aligned, enabling coherent blending, blending, and continuous edits without modifying the generative model.
AI.conclusion: Semantic control can be achieved by organizing existing representations through alignment, rather than altering the underlying generative model.

title: Grounding Generative Policies in Physics: Optimization-Guided Diffusion for Robot Control
summary:
Diffusion models sample effectively from high-dimensional, multimodal distributions, but their outputs may violate deployment constraints. For task-space robot policies, generated grasps, waypoints, or trajectories can be distributionally valid yet infeasible, violating reachability, collision-avoidance, or closed-loop executability requirements. This embodiment gap limits zero-shot deployment across robots, even when the task-space behavior itself is transferable.
  We propose an inference-time optimization framework that couples the behavior generation to physical feasibility by formulating diffusion guidance as a constrained optimization problem. Our key insight is to replace the sampling perturbation in the backward process with an optimized correction, allowing hard constraints or soft penalties to be imposed during sampling without the need to retrain the diffusion model, while keeping samples close to the learned prior.
  We evaluate the method on dexterous grasp synthesis with reachability and collision-avoidance constraints, and dynamic manipulation with controller-level trackability constraints. Across settings and robot embodiments, optimization-guided denoising matches the feasibility of projection- and gradient-guidance baselines while better preserving grasp quality, and improving controller-level executability and task success, with task success improving by up to 20pp. on dexterous grasping and 23pp. on visuomotor manipulation over the best baseline.

AI.tldr: An inference-time optimization framework for diffusion models enforces task-space feasibility in robot control by replacing the backward sampling perturbation with a constrained optimization step, enabling hard soft constraints without retraining and preserving the learned prior.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models can generate distributionally valid task-space outputs (grasps, trajectories) that are physically infeasible (reachability, collisions, poor executability), hindering zero-shot deployment across robots.
AI.method: Treat diffusion guidance as a constrained optimization during denoising: replace perturbative sampling with an optimized correction that enforces hard constraints or soft penalties; no retraining needed; keeps samples close to the learned prior.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on dexterous grasping with reachability/collision constraints and visuomotor dynamic manipulation shows optimization-guided denoising matches baseline feasibility (projection/gradient-guidance) while better preserving grasp quality and improving controller-level executability and task success (up to +20 percentage points in dexterous grasping and +23 pp in visuomotor manipulation) over the best baseline.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time optimization for diffusion guidance can enforce physical feasibility in robot task-space outputs without retraining, maintaining alignment with the learned prior and improving task success and executability across embodiments.

title: Reconstructing GRACE Terrestrial Water Storage with Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Networks: An Application to South America
summary: Terrestrial water storage (TWS) integrates snow, soil moisture, surface water, and groundwater and is a key indicator of how climate variability and human activity reshape the global water cycle. The GRACE and GRACE-FO satellite missions provide the only direct, globally consistent observations of TWS change, but their record only begins in 2002 which is too short for many climate-scale analyses. We present a deep learning application that reconstructs monthly GRACE-like TWS anomalies (TWSA) back to 1940 by learning the relationship between daily ERA5 meteorological forcing (precipitation, evapotranspiration, runoff) and monthly GRACE observations. In contrast to prior reconstruction approaches based on grid-cell-wise regression, CNNs, or LSTMs, we adapt a multi-variate time series graph neural network (MTGNN) architecture, which was originally developed for mobility and traffic forecasting on urban sensor networks to this satellite-geodesy task. Spatial dependencies are encoded in a static, interpretable hybrid adjacency matrix that combines geodesic proximity with lagged correlations of climatic time series, capturing both local hydrological coupling and large-scale teleconnections. The reconstruction achieves a grid-cell Pearson correlation of 0.69, a basin-mean correlation of 0.94, and a near-zero bias, and it reproduces the spatial fingerprints of the 2015/16 El Niño and 2020/21 La Niña events. A systematic comparison with established reconstruction approaches (GTWS-MLrec, RM-REC, GRAiCE) shows that the graph-based model is statistically competitive at basin scale, reaching a correlation within 0.025 of the best baseline while using only roughly half to a tenth of the predictors the other models require and revealing characteristic weaknesses in arid regions in all models. The complete implementation is publicly available at github.com/hcu-cml/MTGNN-TWS-Reconstruction-GRACE

AI.tldr: A graph-based deep learning approach reconstructs GRACE-like terrestrial water storage anomalies (TWSA) back to 1940 using daily ERA5 forcing, achieving competitive accuracy with fewer predictors and interpretable spatial relations.
AI.motivation: GRACE/GRACE-FO provide unique TWS observations only from 2002 onward. A longer, climate-scale TWS record requires robust reconstruction methods that leverage meteorological drivers and spatial dependencies.
AI.method: A multi-variate time series graph neural network (MTGNN) is trained to map daily ERA5 forcing (precipitation, evapotranspiration, runoff) to monthly GRACE TWSA. Spatial dependencies are captured by a static, interpretable hybrid adjacency matrix combining geodesic proximity with lagged correlations of climatic time series. Compared against grid-cell regression, CNN/LSTM baselines, and existing reconstructions (GTWS-MLrec, RM-REC, GRAiCE).
AI.result: Grid-cell correlation 0.69; basin-mean correlation 0.94; near-zero bias. Reproduces El Niño 2015/16 and La Niña 2020/21 fingerprints. Statistically competitive at basin scale (within 0.025 of the best baseline) while using roughly 1/2 to 1/10 of the predictors. Reveals weaknesses in arid regions common to all models.
AI.conclusion: Graph-based MTGNN provides an effective, data-efficient framework for long-term TWS reconstruction from meteorological forcing, with interpretable spatial coupling and public code availability. Further work could address arid-region performance, uncertainty quantification, and broader climate applicability.

title: Beyond Trajectory Imitation: Strategy-Guided Policy Optimization for LLM Reasoning
summary: Distilling reasoning capabilities from strong to weak language models typically involves imitating specific solution trajectories, effectively transferring what to answer rather than how to reason. This trajectory-level imitation encourages memorization of instance-specific steps rather than acquisition of transferable problem-solving skills, limiting generalization to novel problems. We propose Strategy-Guided Policy Optimization (SGPO), which replaces instance-level trajectory imitation with reusable strategy distillation. SGPO extracts structured strategy descriptions from strong-model responses and, for each problem, constructs both autonomous and strategy-guided trajectories to enable direct comparison of the model's behavior with and without strategic guidance. The framework then addresses two key questions. For how to distill, a token-level forward-KL objective selectively transfers the distributional shift induced by strategy conditioning into the unguided policy, with proximal constraints ensuring stability. For when to distill, adaptive instance-level weighting strengthens guidance when autonomous exploration falls short and reduces it as the model's own competence grows. Experiments on four mathematical benchmarks across two model families show that SGPO consistently outperforms SFT, on-policy RL, and hybrid-policy baselines, improving the average score by 2.2 points over the strongest baseline on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct. Analysis reveals that the forward-KL objective provides an inherently selective distillation signal that outperforms direct trajectory imitation, and that strategy distillation exhibits complementary scaling with base model capability.

AI.tldr: Strategy-Guided Policy Optimization (SGPO) replaces trajectory imitation with reusable strategy distillation, improving generalization by transferring strategy conditioning via a forward-KL objective and adaptive weighting.
AI.motivation: Trajectory-level imitation leads to memorization of instance-specific steps and poor transfer to novel problems; there is a need to distill reusable problem-solving strategies instead of solution paths.
AI.method: Extract structured strategies from strong-model responses, generate autonomous and strategy-guided trajectories for each problem, and compare them. Distill via a token-level forward-KL objective to transfer the strategy-induced distributional shift with proximal constraints; apply adaptive instance-level weighting to scale guidance based on the model's current competence.
AI.result: SGPO consistently outperforms SFT, on-policy RL, and hybrid baselines across four mathematical benchmarks on two model families, increasing average score by 2.2 points over the strongest baseline (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct). Analysis shows forward-KL yields selective distillation and that strategy distillation complements base model capability.
AI.conclusion: Strategy-driven distillation provides transferable problem-solving behavior and scales with model capability, yielding better generalization than trajectory imitation.

title: DriveStack-VLA: Render-Teacher Alignment for BEV-Based DeepStack Vision-Language-Action Model
summary: Vision-Language-Action driving models convert a pretrained Vision-Language Model into a driving policy, allowing them to use world knowledge and follow language guidances. However, existing VLA driving models still lack driving-oriented spatial intelligence: their policies are mainly grounded on perspective image tokens and language priors, while precise motion planning requires metric geometry, top-down scene structure, and attention to safety-critical perceptual cues. This limitation makes current models vulnerable to weak visual geometry modeling and perceptual coverage in expert demonstrations. In this paper, we present DriveStack-VLA, a framework built upon a large VLM backbone. To strengthen the spatial grounding of VLA driving, we develop dual visual modeling components. We inject a Bird-Eye-View representation into the Large Language Model decoder through a DeepStack-style connection, and propose Render-Teacher Alignment to align the perceptual focus of real images with that of rasterized images. Furthermore, to bridge the gap in multimodal trajectory selection, we introduce a head-based self-critique module that ranks sampled trajectories and conditionally refines the best one. DriveStack-VLA achieves 91.6 PDMS on NAVSIMv1, 91.0 EPDMS on NAVSIMv2 (with the human penalty filter enabled), and a driving score of 79.49 with a success rate of 56.36\% on the closed-loop Bench2Drive. More visualizations are available on our project page: https://anonymous.4open.science/w/drivestack-vla/.

AI.tldr: A VLM-based driving model DriveStack-VLA enhances spatial grounding via BEV integration, Render-Teacher Alignment, and a self-critique module, achieving strong NVIS metrics on NAVSIM and Bench2Drive.
AI.motivation: Existing Vision-Language-Action driving models lack driving-oriented spatial intelligence (metric geometry, top-down structure, safety cues), relying on image tokens and language priors; this limits robust planning and perceptual coverage.
AI.method: Introduce dual visual modeling: 1) inject Bird-Eye-View (BEV) into the LLM decoder via a DeepStack-style connection. 2) Render-Teacher Alignment to align perceptual focus between real and rasterized images. 3) Head-based self-critique module ranks sampled trajectories and refines the best one.
AI.result: Quantitative: 91.6 PDMS (NAVSIMv1), 91.0 EPDMS (NAVSIMv2 with human penalty filter), 79.49 driving score and 56.36% success on Bench2Drive; project visuals on page.
AI.conclusion: DriveStack-VLA improves spatial grounding and safety-aware perceptual integration, achieving strong multi-benchmark performance; further validation and ablations warranted to assess generalization and compute costs.

title: RoBoSR: Structured Scene Representations for Embodied Robotic Reasoning
summary:
Despite rapid progress, embodied reasoning under real-world variability remains challenging. Existing approaches rely on demonstration-driven sequential biases, limiting flexibility in open-ended and long-horizon tasks that require structured reasoning over evolving states.
  We introduce RoBoSR, an intermediate structural representation that formulates manipulation as step-wise state transitions over semantically grounded, object-centric scene graphs. By modeling object states and their spatial relations at the perception-action interface, RoBoSR disentangles high-level task reasoning from raw inputs and enables structured reasoning over preconditions, effects, and goal states. This representation endows the agent with causal reasoning capability, enforcing subtask dependencies and supporting coherent long-horizon task planning.
  To learn such structure-aware reasoning, we construct Manip-Cognition-1.6M, an open-world dataset that jointly supervises scene understanding, instruction interpretation, and subtask planning across diverse tasks.
  Across several benchmarks and real-world demonstrations, our method consistently outperforms prompting-based methods and classical TAMP baselines in zero-shot generalization and long-horizon tasks. The results underscore structured intermediate representations as a critical inductive bias for scalable embodied reasoning.

AI.tldr: RoBoSR proposes an intermediate, object-centric scene-graph representation for manipulation as stepwise state transitions, enabling structured and causal reasoning over preconditions, effects, and goals. It is trained on Manip-Cognition-1.6M and outperforms prompting-based methods and classical TAMP baselines in zero-shot generalization and long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Embodied reasoning remains brittle under real-world variability; current demo-driven sequential biases restrict flexibility in open-ended, long-horizon tasks that require structured reasoning over evolving states. There is a need to disentangle perception-action from high-level planning and introduce causal, subtask-aware reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce RoBoSR: an intermediate structure-aware representation that uses semantically grounded, object-centric scene graphs to model object states and spatial relations at the perception-action interface. This enables reasoning over preconditions, effects, and goal states, enforcing subtask dependencies and supporting long-horizon planning. Train on Manip-Cognition-1.6M, a large open-world dataset that jointly supervises scene understanding, instruction interpretation, and subtask planning across diverse tasks.
AI.result: Empirically, RoBoSR-based methods outperform prompting-based approaches and classical TAMP baselines on zero-shot generalization and long-horizon tasks across multiple benchmarks and real-world demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: Structured intermediate representations are a critical inductive bias for scalable embodied reasoning, enabling robust long-horizon planning and improved generalization.

title: The Degeneracy Distillery
comment: 30 pages, 10 figures. Supporting code found at https://github.com/tlmakinen/degeneracy_distillery
summary: When two or more parameters or labels produce similar data, they are degenerate, or hard to distinguish. Degeneracies render both label prediction and inverse problems difficult, since both machine learning algorithms and probabilistic samplers rely on the distinguishability of data and its gradients with respect to parameters. However, identifying degeneracies in physical models or real-world datasets can be elucidating about the choice of model or the underlying process that produces the data. We present the degeneracy distillery, a method that (1) detects and (2) resolves degenerate parameter combinations (a) automatically and (b) symbolically, from parameter-data (or parameter-simulation) pairs alone, through estimation and flattening of the Fisher information matrix. By exploring the information geometry of the likelihood, we characterize degeneracies as an intrinsic property of the physical model, requiring no realised data observation. We demonstrate our approach on a range of synthetic and real-world problems, discovering symbolic coordinate transformations that identify the combinations of parameters of a model which yield independent effects on the data. The resulting coordinates flatten the Fisher information in expectation globally, in contrast to posterior-based methods that flatten only at a single point, and substantially reduce the simulation budget required for downstream neural posterior estimation. In test cases we require up to $10\times$ fewer simulations for posterior estimation at matched validation calibration whilst simultaneously gaining physical insight on the system.

AI.tldr: Introduces degeneracy distillery, a method to automatically and symbolically detect and resolve degenerate parameter combinations via Fisher information, yielding globally flat coordinates and reduced simulation needs for neural posterior estimation.
AI.motivation: Degeneracies hinder parameter identifiability and inference; identifying intrinsic degeneracies through information geometry can reveal fundamental model structure independent of observed data, improving model design and efficiency.
AI.method: Estimate Fisher information from parameter-data (or simulation) pairs, analyze the information geometry to identify degenerate directions, and derive symbolic coordinate transformations that diagonalize/flatten the Fisher information in expectation; implement automatic degeneracy resolution.
AI.result: Demonstrated on synthetic and real-world problems; discovers symbolic coordinate changes that decouple parameter effects; achieves global flattening of Fisher information and significantly reduces simulations for downstream neural posterior estimation (up to ~10x fewer for matched calibration).
AI.conclusion: Provides a data-agnostic, information-geometric approach to identifying and removing degeneracies, enabling more efficient and interpretable models; outperforms local, posterior-centric flatting and is broadly applicable to inference and model design.

title: Exploring Academic Influence of Algorithms by Co-occurrence Network Based on Full-text of Academic Papers
summary: Algorithms have become central to scientific research in the era of artificial intelligence (AI). Although algorithm mentions in papers are often used to indicate popularity and influence, existing studies usually evaluate individual algorithms in isolation and pay limited attention to the collective influence formed through their interconnections. This study constructs large-scale algorithm co-occurrence networks in natural language processing (NLP) based on the full text of academic papers and investigates algorithm influence from a network perspective. Using deep learning models, we extract algorithm entities and build overall, cumulative, and annual co-occurrence networks. We analyze their structural characteristics and apply multiple centrality measures to assess the group influence of algorithms across the whole field and over time. The results show that algorithm networks display typical features of complex networks, with increasingly dense connections developing over approximately two decades. Classic, high-performing algorithms and those located at the intersections of different research periods tend to have high popularity, control, centrality, and balanced influence. When the influence of an algorithm declines, it usually loses its core network position first, followed by weaker associations with other algorithms. This study is the first large-scale analysis of algorithm co-occurrence networks. Covering more than four decades of academic publications, it provides a temporal and structural view of algorithm influence and offers a foundation for future research on networks linking algorithms, scholars, and tasks.

AI.tldr: A large-scale study maps how NLP algorithms co-occur in papers, showing evolving network centrality and influence over time.
AI.motivation: Move beyond single-algorithm evaluations to understand collective influence and interconnections of algorithms in NLP via co-occurrence networks.
AI.method: Extract algorithm entities from full-text NLP papers using deep learning; build overall, cumulative, and annual co-occurrence networks; analyze structural properties and compute multiple centrality measures to assess group influence over time.
AI.result: Algorithm co-occurrence networks display typical complex-network features with increasing density over ~two decades; classic and cross-period algorithms show high popularity, control, centrality, and balanced influence; when an algorithm's influence declines, it first loses core network position and weaker ties, signaling a sequential decline of network centrality.
AI.conclusion: First large-scale analysis of algorithm co-occurrence networks in NLP; covers four decades, offering temporal and structural insights into algorithm influence and laying groundwork for future work linking algorithms with scholars and tasks.

title: EPEdit: Redefining Image Editing with Generative AI and User-Centric Design
summary: The demand for image manipulation has seen a significant increase recently. Traditional tools like Photoshop and Capture One, while powerful, require considerable expertise to use effectively. Generative AI has introduced alternative platforms, such as Luminar Neo, Pixlr X, and Canva. However, many of these solutions, including resource-heavy models like Stable Diffusion, often require substantial retraining and fine-tuning, leading to high costs for users. To address these challenges, we introduce Efficient Photo Editor (EPEdit), an application that integrates a robust backend framework with a user-friendly front-end interface. EPEdit supports a wide range of creative image editing tasks, including image generation, object replacement, object removal, background modification, changes in object pose or perspective, region-specific editing, and thematic collection design, all guided by masks and prompts. Users can interact with the system through simple text commands or by marking areas for precise adjustments, making it accessible even to those without technical expertise. At its core, EPEdit leverages zero-shot image editing algorithms based on Stable Diffusion model, removing the need for additional fine-tuning. This approach enables efficient image manipulation and thematic collection creation. User evaluations for tasks of image editing, thematic design, and overall system performance demonstrate that EPEdit outperforms existing solutions, offering a user-friendly, cost-effective solution for comprehensive image editing.

AI.tldr: A zero-shot, Stable Diffusion-based image editor enabling broad editing tasks via masks and prompts without fine-tuning, emphasizing usability and cost-efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address the cost and retraining burden of resource-heavy generative models and the inaccessibility of advanced image editing tools; provide an approachable, integrated editing platform for diverse editing tasks.
AI.method: Zero-shot image editing using Stable Diffusion integrated with mask-guided selections and text prompts. Supports generation, object replacement/removal, background modification, pose/perspective changes, region-specific editing, and thematic collection design. User-friendly front-end with simple commands or mask-based interactions; no additional fine-tuning required. Backend architecture emphasizes efficiency and broad task coverage. Evaluation via user studies across editing tasks, design tasks, and overall performance.
AI.result: User evaluations indicate EPEdit outperforms existing solutions in image editing, thematic design, and overall system performance, while remaining accessible and cost-effective.
AI.conclusion: EPEdit demonstrates that a zero-shot, mask-and-prompt-guided editing framework built on Stable Diffusion can deliver comprehensive, user-friendly image editing without fine-tuning, with strong potential for broad adoption and cost savings.

title: SlipSense: Multimodal Sensing for Online Slip Detection in Legged Robots
summary: Legged robots rely on accurate ground interaction awareness to traverse variable terrains, such as slippery surfaces. Existing slip detection methods often rely on kinematics and proprioception, which lack the sensitivity to detect early-stage slips that occur prior to catastrophic instability. Thus, this paper presents SlipSense, a novel framework for online force-based slip detection using a custom lightweight sensorized foot for quadrupeds to detect slip. The framework integrates a multimodal sensor design with a LSTM-based model to infer ground reaction forces and detect slip-indicative anomalies during locomotion. The proposed framework is deployed on a Unitree Go1 quadruped to demonstrate blind online slip detection over a slippery terrain. Our method detects early-stage slips down to an average displacement of 24.1 +/-6.4mm with an overall accuracy of 85.9%. This represents a 3.3-fold finer detection resolution and a 24% relative accuracy improvement over a standard kinematic baseline that uses foot velocity inferred through state estimation. The work in this paper serves as a foundation for force-aware gait adaptation in legged robotic locomotion, allowing future controllers to estimate terrain friction and adjust constraints, thus improving the overall stability of the system.

AI.tldr: Introduces SlipSense, a force-based slip detection framework using a multimodal sensorized foot and LSTM to infer ground reaction forces for early slip detection on a Unitree Go1; achieves 24.1 mm detection threshold, 85.9% accuracy, and 3.3x improvement over a kinematic baseline.
AI.motivation: Existing slip detection relies on kinematics and proprioception, which miss early-stage slips; need for early, force-aware slip sensing to enable gait adaptation on variable terrains.
AI.method: A custom lightweight sensorized foot with multimodal sensors is integrated with an LSTM model to infer ground reaction forces and detect slip-indicative anomalies during locomotion; deployed for online blind slip detection on a Unitree Go1 quadruped.
AI.result: The approach detects early-stage slips with an average displacement of 24.1 ± 6.4 mm and an overall accuracy of 85.9%; achieves 3.3-fold finer detection resolution and 24% relative accuracy improvement over a standard kinematic baseline based on foot velocity from state estimation.
AI.conclusion: Serves as a foundation for force-aware gait adaptation, enabling future controllers to estimate terrain friction and adjust locomotion constraints to improve stability.

title: Machine Learning Modeling for Real-Time Melt Pool Monitoring in Laser Powder Bed Fusion Additive Manufacturing: A Hybrid Approach
summary: This work investigates the implementation of artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI/ML) for real-time monitoring in laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) additive manufacturing. We developed a binary image classification framework for distinguishing normal and abnormal melt pool images using a balanced dataset of 1,200 images collected from Nickel superalloy 625 on the NIST AMMT platform. The study evaluates accuracy and inference time based on control requirements and hardware limitations of open-architecture LPBF machines. We benchmark three transfer learning architectures (ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, and MobileNetV2) against two Random Forest approaches: one trained on EfficientNetB0 feature embeddings (hybrid) and one trained on raw pixel features (baseline). Images are stratified into 80/20 train-test splits, with a further 90/10 validation split on the training set, and undergo standardized resizing, normalization, and label-preserving data augmentation to emulate realistic process variability. Each model is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), along with training time, inference latency, and CPU & GPU usage to capture deployability constraints relevant to factory-floor monitoring. The hybrid EfficientNetB0-plus-Random Forest approach achieves the best performance on the held-out test set, with an F1 score of 0.9451, accuracy of 0.9458, and AUC of 0.9904, while maintaining sub-millisecond per-image inference (1.15 ms). In contrast, purely deep learning models exhibit significantly higher inference times with lower accuracy. These results demonstrate that combining pre-trained convolutional features with classical ensemble methods provides a robust, computationally efficient route to real-time melt pool anomaly detection in data-limited additive manufacturing environments.

AI.tldr: Hybrid EfficientNetB0 features with Random Forest outperform other models for real-time melt pool anomaly detection in LPBF, achieving ~0.946 accuracy, 0.99 AUC, and ~1.15 ms per image; DL-only models lag in both speed and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Enable real-time, deployable anomaly detection in laser powder bed fusion under data-limited conditions, balancing accuracy, latency, and hardware constraints on open-architecture machines.
AI.method: Benchmark three transfer-learning CNNs (ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, MobileNetV2) and two Random Forest approaches (hybrid on CNN embeddings, baseline on raw pixels) using 1,200 Ni-based 625 images from NIST AMMT. Data splits: 80/20 train/test with 90/10 validation on train; standardized resizing/normalization; data augmentation. Evaluate accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC, plus training time, inference latency, CPU/GPU usage.
AI.result: Hybrid EfficientNetB0 + RF achieves best test metrics: F1 0.9451, accuracy 0.9458, AUC 0.9904; inference latency 1.15 ms per image. Pure DL models show higher latency and lower accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Combining pre-trained CNN features with classical ensemble methods provides a robust, computationally efficient solution for real-time melt pool anomaly detection in data-limited AM environments, supporting practical factory-floor deployment.

title: ReMMD: Realistic Multilingual Multi-Image Agentic Verification for Multimodal Misinformation Detection
comment: The project is available at https://dang-ai.github.io/ReMMD
summary: Multimodal misinformation detection is increasingly important because viral posts now combine long multilingual narratives, several images, mixed provenance, and subtle text--image framing errors. Existing benchmarks and methods remain poorly matched to this setting: they usually isolate short captions, single images, binary labels, or one manipulation source, while agentic verification remains costly under realistic evidence search. We present ReMMD, a realistic multilingual multi-image agentic verification framework for multimodal misinformation detection. ReMMD includes ReMMDBench, a real-world multimodal misinformation detection benchmark with 500 samples, 2,756 images, five monolingual languages, two cross-lingual settings, three text-length tiers, multi-image posts, five-way veracity labels, eight distortion labels, evidence provenance, and rationales. It also includes ReMMD-Agent, a persistent-memory verifier that decomposes posts into atomic points, builds a reusable evidence set, and predicts structured L1/L2/L3 outputs. Across proprietary systems, open LVLMs, MMD-Agent, and T2-Agent, ReMMD-Agent obtains the best five-way veracity performance, with 41.80% accuracy and 39.12% macro-F1 using GPT-5.2, while reducing cost by 17.5% relative to MMD-Agent and 79.9% relative to T2-Agent. The project is available at https://dang-ai.github.io/ReMMD.

AI.tldr: ReMMD offers a realistic multilingual, multi-image agentic verification framework for multimodal misinformation detection, with a real-world benchmark (ReMMDBench) and an agentic verifier (ReMMD-Agent). It achieves best five-way veracity performance among tested systems and reduces cost.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks/methods isolate channels (short captions, single image, binary labels) and neglect agentic, evidence-driven verification in realistic, multilingual, multi-image misinformation scenarios.
AI.method: Introduce ReMMD with ReMMDBench (500 samples, 2,756 images, 5 languages, multi-image posts, 5-way veracity, 8 distortion labels, evidence provenance, rationales) and ReMMD-Agent (persistent-memory verifier decomposing posts into atomic points, building reusable evidence, predicting L1/L2/L3 outputs). Evaluate across proprietary systems, open LVLMs, MMD-Agent, T2-Agent.
AI.result: ReMMD-Agent achieves best five-way veracity performance: 41.80% accuracy and 39.12% macro-F1 with GPT-5.2; cost reductions of 17.5% vs MMD-Agent and 79.9% vs T2-Agent.
AI.conclusion: ReMMD provides a realistic, scalable framework for multimodal misinformation detection with evidence-based verification; the benchmark and agent provide stronger performance and cost efficiency; project available at the provided link.

title: VisChronos: Revolutionizing Image Captioning Through Real-Life Events
summary: This paper aims to bridge the semantic gap between visual content and natural language understanding by leveraging historical events in the real world as a source of knowledge for caption generation. We propose VisChronos, a novel framework that utilizes large language models and dense captioning models to identify and describe real-life events from a single input image. Our framework can automatically generate detailed and context-aware event descriptions, enhancing the descriptive quality and contextual relevance of generated captions to address the limitations of traditional methods in capturing contextual narratives. Furthermore, we introduce a new dataset, EventCap (https://zenodo.org/records/14004909), specifically constructed using the proposed framework, designed to enhance the model's ability to identify and understand complex events. The user study demonstrates the efficacy of our solution in generating accurate, coherent, and event-focused descriptions, paving the way for future research in event-centric image understanding.

AI.tldr: A VisChronos framework uses LLMs and dense captioning to extract real-world events from a single image, generating detailed, context-aware captions and a new EventCap dataset; user studies show improved event-centric descriptions.
AI.motivation: Bridge the semantic gap between visual content and natural language by injecting real-world event knowledge to produce more contextual captions; current methods often miss narrative structure and event context.
AI.method: Propose VisChronos, a framework that combines large language models with dense captioning models to identify and describe real-life events from one image; automatically generate detailed, context-aware event descriptions; construct the EventCap dataset using this framework.
AI.result: User studies indicate the generated captions are accurate, coherent, and event-focused, validating the approach and dataset.
AI.conclusion: The framework advances event-centric image understanding by linking visual content to real-world events; EventCap enhances model capability, enabling future research in event-aware captioning.

title: PDS Joint: A Parametric Double-Spiral Joint Tailored for Dexterous Hands
summary: Compliant joints can embed safety and adaptability into dexterous hands, but achieving large-stroke anthropomorphic motion while maintaining joint-specific, directiondependent stiffness and reliable proprioception remains challenging. This paper presents the PDS joint, a parametric doublespiral (PDS) compliant joint that enables systematic shaping of directional stiffness across multiple deformation modes, including flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, and pronation/supination. We instantiate the joint using Archimedean and logarithmic spiral templates for different hand joints and introduce an asymmetry ratio to tailor stiffness distributions for both grasp stability and hyperextension resistance. To make the joint practically usable under large deformation, we co-design embedded inductive proprioception and propose a learningbased calibration pipeline that maps raw inductive signals to joint states using ArUco-marker tracking. Experiments characterize the stiffness landscapes across geometric parameters and demonstrate a non-monotonic dependence of lateral support on asymmetry, indicating the importance of principled parameter tuning. For joint-state estimation in the most challenging abduction/adduction motion, a learned multilayer-perceptron (MLP) mapping reduces the error compared with conventional curve fitting by 41.6%. Finally, we integrate the proposed joints into an open-source dexterous hand as a demonstration platform, on which the hand grasps a set of nine everyday objects and performs safe, contact-rich human-involved interactions.

AI.tldr: PDS joints enable large-stroke, directionally stiff, compliant actuators for dexterous hands by using parametric doublespiral templates with asymmetry-based stiffness shaping, embedded proprioception, and a learning-based calibration workflow, demonstrated on nine objects with an open-source hand.
AI.motivation: Achieve large-range, direction-dependent stiffness and reliable proprioception in anthropomorphic, compliant joints for dexterous hands.
AI.method: Introduce the parametric doublespiral (PDS) joint using Archimedean and logarithmic spiral templates, define an asymmetry ratio to shape stiffness distributions, co-design embedded inductive proprioception, and develop a learning-based calibration pipeline mapping raw inductive signals to joint states via ArUco-marker tracking. Perform stiffness landscape characterization across geometric parameters, analyze non-monotonic lateral support vs. asymmetry, train an MLP to improve abduction/adduction state estimation, and integrate joints into an open-source hand that grasps nine objects and supports safe human-involved interactions.
AI.result: Characterization of stiffness landscapes across joint parameters; discovery of non-monotonic dependence of lateral support on asymmetry; 41.6% reduction in joint-state estimation error with an MLP over conventional curve fitting for abduction/adduction; demonstration of safe grasps on nine everyday objects and human-involved interactions using the open-source hand.
AI.conclusion: PDS joints provide a systematic approach to shaping directional stiffness across deformation modes while maintaining large motion ranges and proprioceptive sensing, enabling robust, adaptive grasping and safe human–robot interaction; the work delivers an open-source hardware platform and calibration pipeline for broader adoption.

title: Sesame: Structure-Aware Molecular Generation via Spatial Density-Map Conditioning
summary: Generative molecular models for drug design are a promising direction with much active research. In the next phase of computational drug design, such models will need to understand small molecule structure and protein-ligand interactions, and they will need to possess the machinery to generate molecules \textit{de novo}. Incorporating each feature poses a critical challenge. Equally important, yet often treated as secondary, is the ability to grow a molecule from a partial starting point -- a scaffold or fragment supplied by a chemist -- which is the central operation of lead optimization. We present Sesame (Spatial Evoformer for a Structure-Aware Molecular Engine), a diffusion-based molecular generation model that leverages a novel spatial pairformer module to condition on partial molecular structure and the surrounding protein pocket, both expressed as continuous spatial density maps. This single conditioning mechanism supports both \textit{de novo} generation and fragment-conditioned lead optimization, letting a medicinal chemist prune a hit to a scaffold and have Sesame grow it in productive ways. In addition to this module, we also introduce a diffusion framework for joint denoising of atom types, bond types, and positions, along with a trajectory finetuning scheme that trains on the model's own sampling rollouts to improve generation quality. Sesame is trained on a large corpus of ligand-only and protein-ligand datasets.

AI.tldr: Sesame is a diffusion-based molecular generator with a spatial pairformer conditioning on partial structure and protein pocket, enabling both de novo design and fragment-based lead optimization; it uses joint denoising of atom types, bonds, and positions and trajectory finetuning, trained on ligand-only and protein–ligand data.
AI.motivation: To enable integrated de novo molecular design and fragment-guided lead optimization within a single framework that accounts for protein context and partial structures.
AI.method: Introduce a spatial pairformer to condition on partial structure and protein pocket expressed as continuous spatial density maps; a diffusion process denoises atom types, bond types, and positions jointly; trajectory finetuning using the model's own rollouts; training on large ligand-only and protein–ligand datasets.
AI.result: The model supports both de novo generation and fragment-conditioned growth; encodes spatial context to enable productive scaffold growth; trajectory finetuning improves generation quality; trained on large ligand-only and protein–ligand datasets.
AI.conclusion: Sesame provides a unified, structure-aware diffusion framework for molecular generation that integrates partial-structure conditioning and protein-pocket context to support de novo design and fragment-based optimization.

title: VeryTrace: Verifying Reasoning Traces through Compilable Formalism and Structured Verification
summary:
Multi-step reasoning with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting remains fragile: logical errors or hallucinations in early steps silently propagate, producing confident but incorrect conclusions.
