This paper presents VeryTrace, a zero-shot verification-and-repair framework that formalizes natural-language reasoning traces into a structured, compilable representation. VeryTrace introduces a Domain-Specific Language (DSL) that (i) makes step dependencies explicit, (ii) mechanizes quantitative content as executable expressions, and (iii) structures semantic inferences via deduction schemas. Our hybrid verifier combines deterministic checks for computational correctness, dependency resolution, and constraint satisfaction with targeted LLM audits for non-mechanizable semantic judgments, enabling step-level error localization and repair.
  Across three diverse domains-competition mathematics (AIME 2025), robotics planning (LLM-BabyBench), and kinship reasoning (CLUTRR), VeryTrace improves accuracy over zero-shot baselines on state-of-the-art LLMs without requiring domain-specific training or in-context examples, demonstrating that formalized trace verification achieves both precision and generalization.

AI.tldr: VeryTrace provides a zero-shot verification and repair framework for chain-of-thought reasoning by compiling reasoning traces into a DSL, enabling explicit dependencies, executable content, and deduction schemas. A hybrid verifier combines deterministic checks with targeted LLM audits, achieving step-level error localization and repair across diverse domains without domain-specific training.
AI.motivation: Current chain-of-thought prompting is fragile: early mistakes propagate into confident but incorrect conclusions. A general, domain-agnostic verification mechanism is needed to localize and repair errors in reasoning traces.
AI.method: Introduce a domain-specific language (DSL) for reasoning traces that exposes step dependencies, encodes quantitative content as executable expressions, and organizes semantic inferences via deduction schemas. Deploy a hybrid verifier that combines deterministic checks (computational correctness, dependency resolution, constraint satisfaction) with targeted LLM audits for non-mechanizable semantic judgments, enabling step-level localization and repair. Evaluate zero-shot on three domains (AIME 2025 math, LLM-BabyBench robotics planning, CLUTRR kinship) without domain-specific training or in-context examples.
AI.result: Across three diverse domains, VeryTrace improves accuracy over zero-shot baselines on state-of-the-art LLMs, demonstrating improved precision and generalization without domain-specific training or prompts.
AI.conclusion: Formalized trace verification with a DSL and hybrid verifier yields precise error localization and repair, and generalizes across tasks, suggesting verification-based approaches can robustly enhance chain-of-thought reasoning without in-domain data.

title: Ingredient-Level Food Image Segmentation for Nutrition Awareness
summary: Food images often contain several visible ingredients, so assigning one dish label to an entire image hides important visual structure. This work studies ingredient-level semantic segmentation on FoodSeg103, where the model predicts an ingredient class for each pixel. Two SegFormer variants were fine-tuned and evaluated under a controlled setup: SegFormer-B0 as the smaller baseline model and SegFormer-B1 as the larger final model. Both models use ImageNet-pretrained MiT backbones with newly initialized 104-class output layers. On the held-out FoodSeg103 test split of 2,135 images, B0 achieved 0.7709 pixel accuracy and 0.2521 mean IoU, while B1 achieved 0.7929 pixel accuracy and 0.3204 mean IoU. B1 improved every saved test metric, including a +0.0683 absolute gain in mean IoU. The system also converts predicted masks into visible ingredient-area percentages, giving a simple visual composition summary of the predicted meal. This summary can serve as a first-pass nutrition-awareness cue by providing a visual alternative to detailed food tracking similar to plate-based meal guidance, but it is not a direct estimate of calories, macronutrients, food mass, volume, density, or true portion size.

AI.tldr: Two SegFormer variants (B0 and B1) are fine-tuned for ingredient-level semantic segmentation on FoodSeg103. B1 (larger model) achieves higher pixel accuracy (0.7929) and mean IoU (0.3204) than B0 (0.7709, 0.2521). The system also outputs ingredient-area percentages from predicted masks to provide a visual nutrition-awareness cue, but it does not estimate calories or macronutrient quantities.
AI.motivation: Move beyond single-label image classification to per-pixel ingredient labeling to reveal visual structure within food images, enabling better navigation of nutrition-related information and potentially aiding intuitive meal guidance.
AI.method: Fine-tune SegFormer-B0 and SegFormer-B1 on FoodSeg103 with ImageNet-pretrained MiT backbones and newly initialized 104-class output layers. Evaluate on held-out test split (2,135 images). Convert predicted masks to ingredient-area percentages for a visual composition summary.
AI.result: B0: pixel accuracy 0.7709, mean IoU 0.2521. B1: pixel accuracy 0.7929, mean IoU 0.3204. B1 improves all saved metrics, including a +0.0683 absolute gain in mean IoU.
AI.conclusion: Larger SegFormer (B1) yields better performance for ingredient-level segmentation on FoodSeg103, and the derived ingredient-area summaries offer a straightforward visual Nutrition-awareness cue, though they are not a direct caloric or macronutrient estimator.

title: RE4: Transformation-aware Imitation of Object Interactions Using Manipulation Modes
summary: Object interaction tasks have been a focus of advances in imitation learning. End-to-end methods, dominated by diffusion and flow-based variants have shown leaps in performance while sacrificing interpretability. Object-centric and pose-informed variants have had a role in learning from demonstration in manipulation tasks. In this paper, we revisit a few modern imitation learning benchmarks for object interactions, with the aim of composing a framework that repurposes principled theories of manipulation, preserving both performance and interpretability. For image observations, lightweight training is proposed for model-free pose estimation of the target object, using self-supervision over the demonstration data available for imitation learning. This information is then used to inform a manipulation mode-aware retrieval of a demonstration, a mode-aware transformation, a replan step that connects to the retrieval point while preserving mode constraints, and finally rolling out the transformed demonstration. These compose four key steps of the proposed RE4 framework, evaluated over state-based and image-based benchmarks in Push-T and Robomimic. An adversarial benchmark that evaluates sparse data regions of image-based Push-T showcases the robustness, further bolstered by indications from low-data regime experiments. The current work shows promise in using simple interpretable building blocks to learn manipulation skills.

AI.tldr: Introduce RE4, an interpretable imitation-learning framework for object interaction tasks that uses self-supervised pose estimation and a mode-aware retrieval-transformation-replan-rollout pipeline; demonstrates robustness and competitive performance on Push-T and Robomimic, including sparse data regimes.
AI.motivation: Address the trade-off between high-performing end-to-end methods (diffusion/flow) and interpretability; leverage object-centric/pose-informed priors and principled manipulation theories to improve transparency and reliability.
AI.method: Four-step pipeline: (1) lightweight self-supervised pose estimation for target object from image observations; (2) mode-aware retrieval of demonstrations; (3) mode-aware transformation; (4) replan step preserving mode constraints and connect to retrieval point; (5) rollout transformed demonstration. Evaluated on state-based and image-based benchmarks (Push-T, Robomimic); includes adversarial sparse-data benchmark and low-data regime experiments.
AI.result: Shows promise in using simple interpretable building blocks to learn manipulation skills; robustness in sparse data regions; positive indications in low-data regimes; performance remains competitive while preserving interpretability.
AI.conclusion: RE4 framework can repurpose principled manipulation theories to achieve interpretable, robust imitation learning for object interactions, with demonstrated viability on standard benchmarks and resilience to data-scarce scenarios.

title: Are Safety Guarantees in Neural Networks Safe? How to Compute Trustworthy Robustness Certifications
summary: A primary challenge in AI safety is the existence of adversarial examples -- slightly distorted inputs that cause a neural network (NN) to misclassify. To mitigate this problem, recent research focuses on the computation of robustness certifications, which, for a given input, determine the largest distortion the input may receive without breaking the network's prediction. Robustness certifications can be interpreted as an axis-aligned hyper-rectangle (multi-dimensional intervals). Most existing approaches focus on maximizing the certification's volume, but recent intractability results prohibit the computation of volume-optimal certifications in reasonable time. We introduce the apothem measure and show how to compute apothem-optimal certifications in a linear number of calls to a NN verifier (oracle) w.r.t. the input domain's diameter. Moreover, we prove that we cannot have a volume-optimal, oracle-based algorithm, even if we discard the oracle costs. Also, we introduce dual certifications -- an interval including all instances of a class -- thus providing apothem-minimum upper bounds to a robustness certification. Further, we present the ParallelepipedoNN system, which we evaluate on the standard MNIST and Fashion MNIST benchmarks. A preliminary comparison with existing work on the same datasets reveals at least two-fold improvement w.r.t. the minimum edge length.

AI.tldr: Introduces apothem-based robustness certifications and dual certifications for neural networks, with an efficient apothem-optimal certification algorithm, proves volume-optimal certifications are not achievable with an oracle, and presents ParallelepipedoNN showing solid empirical gains on MNIST/Fashion-MNIST.
AI.motivation: Adversarial inputs create misclassifications; axis-aligned robustness certificates are a natural suitability but volume-optimal certificates are intractable. The work seeks scalable, provable bounds on input distortions.
AI.method: Define the apothem measure for robustness rectangles; compute apothem-optimal certificates with a linear number of verifier calls relative to the domain diameter; prove a negative result: volume-optimal certificates cannot be achieved even with oracle costs; introduce dual certifications to bound classes; implement ParallelepipedoNN system and evaluate on MNIST/Fashion-MNIST.
AI.result: Aothetm-optimal certificates are computable efficiently; volume-optimality is unattainable in the oracle model; dual certificates provide tight upper bounds; ParallelepipedoNN achieves at least ~2x improvement in minimum edge length over existing methods on MNIST/Fashion-MNIST.
AI.conclusion: The work advances scalable robustness certification by delivering a provable, efficient certification method (apalthemed) and practical system with empirically strong performance, along with a negative result on volume-optimality that shapes future research.

title: OmniPath: A Multi-Modal Agentic Framework for Auditing Wheelchair Accessibility
comment: 10 pages, 13 figures. Submitted to IEEE COMPSAC 2026. OmniPath: A Multi-Modal Agentic Framework for Auditing Wheelchair Accessibility
summary: For a wheelchair user, a standard blue line on a map is often a broken promise. While platforms like OpenStreetMap (OSM) successfully capture where a path is, they frequently fail to convey how it physically feels to travel on it. This information barrier is problematic for wheelchair users. To solve this issue, we present OmniPath, a system that moves from passive mapping to proactive environmental auditing. Our framework fuses the network topology of OSM with the submeter precision of high-density aerial LiDAR (USGS 3DEP) to create a high-fidelity 3D model of the pedestrian environment. Rather than simply routing a user, our agent virtually traverses the network, analyzing the surface in 0.5 meter increments. It rigorously quantifies physical friction points specifically running slope, cross slope, and vertical discontinuities against ADA compliance standards, calculating a weighted severity score to categorize hazards from ``Mild'' to ``Critical.'' To ensure real world reliability, we validated the system against 200 physical ground truth field surveys across the National Mall using stratified random sampling. The framework demonstrated strong diagnostic reliability for high-severity hazards, achieving F1-scores of 0.60 for Severe and 0.58 for critical categories. By automating this micro-scale inspection, OmniPath identifies the ``invisible'' barriers that standard maps miss, effectively transforming a static dataset into accessibility data source that anticipates accessibility challenges before the user ever leaves home.

AI.tldr: OmniPath fuses OSM topology with high-density LiDAR to build a high-fidelity 3D model and simulate micro-scale traversal, quantifying wheelchair accessibility hazards and validating against ground truth; moderate F1 scores for severe/critical hazards enable proactive accessibility insights from maps.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap where standard maps show path existence but not the physical travel experience, creating barriers for wheelchair users and decision-making.
AI.method: Integrates OSM network topology with USGS 3DEP LiDAR to create submeter-precision 3D models; a virtual agent traverses the network at 0.5 m increments, measuring running slope, cross slope, and vertical discontinuities against ADA standards; computes a weighted severity score to categorize hazards from Mild to Critical; validated with 200 ground-truth field surveys using stratified random sampling.
AI.result: Demonstrated diagnostic reliability for high-severity hazards, achieving F1-scores of 0.60 for Severe and 0.58 for Critical; automates micro-scale inspection and reveals barriers not captured by conventional maps, effectively turning a static dataset into an accessibility data source.
AI.conclusion: OmniPath offers a practical pipeline for proactive accessibility auditing by translating high-precision topographic data into actionable accessibility insights, aiding pre-visit planning and safer navigation for wheelchair users.

title: ObsGraph: Hierarchical Observation Representation for Embodied Reasoning and Exploration
summary: Embodied reasoning and exploration are increasingly considered crucial abilities for robots operating in complex and unfamiliar environments. To accomplish tasks in such settings, an agent must identify and acquire the information necessary for the task through exploration. We propose ObsGraph, an observation-centric hierarchical scene graph that unifies scene representation, retrieval, and exploration. It retains visual evidence and organizes it into room-view-object layers: rooms provide coarse semantic anchors, views preserve contextual object covisibility, and objects store fine-grained details. On top of this representation, we perform coarse-to-fine hierarchical retrieval under a bounded budget, and crucially use retrieval outcomes to structure the exploration candidate space--activating room-level exploration, view refinement, or frontier exploration--thereby tightly coupling representation, retrieval, and adaptive multi-scale exploration. Experiments across embodied reasoning and exploration benchmarks demonstrate improved success and efficiency, highlighting the benefits of structured scene representation and more targeted information gathering driven by identified evidence gaps.

AI.tldr: ObsGraph is an observation-centric hierarchical scene graph that unifies representation, retrieval, and exploration for embodied agents, enabling budgeted, multi-scale exploration guided by structured evidence.
AI.motivation: Efficient task-driven exploration in complex, unfamiliar environments requires scalable scene representations and retrieval under resource constraints; existing methods struggle to combine representation, retrieval, and adaptive exploration.
AI.method: Build ObsGraph with room-view-object layers (rooms as coarse anchors, views for contextual covisibility, objects for fine details). Apply coarse-to-fine hierarchical retrieval under a bounded budget. Use retrieval outcomes to structure exploration strategies (room-level exploration, view refinement, frontier exploration), tightly coupling representation, retrieval, and adaptive exploration.
AI.result: Experiments on embodied reasoning and exploration benchmarks show improved success and efficiency, highlighting the benefits of structured scene representation and targeted information gathering driven by evidence gaps.
AI.conclusion: A structured, retrieval-guided representation like ObsGraph improves embodied reasoning by enabling targeted information gathering and multi-scale exploration within budget constraints.

title: Legible and Intuitive Multi-modal Robot State and Intent Communication Validated in Online and Real-world Studies
comment: Accepted for publication at the 35th IEEE International Conference on Robot and Human Interactive Communication (RO-MAN 2026)
summary: Effective robot-to-human communication can increase transparency and trust, reduce uncertainty, and contribute to safer collaboration in shared workspaces. Designing and validating an effective robot communication strategy is challenging due to the varying and often limited communication modalities across robots, differences in how diverse recipients interpret messages, and the underexplored virtual-to-real gap in studies of communication legibility. We present a systematic, large-scale comparative validation of existing communication strategies for a mobile non-humanoid robot across message types and settings (online and in-person). Based on the prescribed message types in the existing standards for industrial robots, we realize and compare a low-expressive, unimodal LED-based strategy with a highly expressive, multimodal one that leverages robotic gaze, gestures, and voice. For each strategy, we analyze the communication of a turning intention, an attention request, error status, whether the robot is stuck, and whether it is functioning normally. We evaluate these strategies in replicated online and in-person experiments. We find strong evidence that highly expressive multimodal communication is perceived as more legible and intuitive than unimodal LED-based communication. Comparing the online and real-world study findings, we observe a notable decrease in overall legibility, particularly for signaling with LEDs. Similarly, confidence in message interpretation decreases during the real-world evaluation.

AI.tldr: Systematic, large-scale comparison of unimodal LED vs multimodal expressive communication for a mobile robot, showing multimodal cues are more legible, with reduced legibility and interpretation confidence in real-world settings, especially for LED-based signaling.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of designing robot-to-human communication that is legible across diverse recipients and modalities, and bridge the virtual-to-real gap in communication legibility studies.
AI.method: Large-scale validation across message types per industrial standards, implementing a low-expressive LED strategy and a high-expressive multimodal strategy (gaze, gestures, voice). Evaluated turning intention, attention request, error status, stuck status, and normal operation. Replicated online and in-person experiments.
AI.result: Multimodal, highly expressive communication is perceived as more legible and intuitive than unimodal LED signaling. Online results show higher legibility; real-world results show a notable decrease in legibility, especially for LED signaling, and reduced confidence in interpretation.
AI.conclusion: To improve robot-to-human communication, multimodal expressive strategies outperform unimodal LED signaling in legibility, but real-world deployment introduces a virtual-to-real gap that reduces effectiveness; future work should address this gap and consider robust, context-aware designs.

title: Exact Schur-Sylvester Dimensionality Reductions for Non-Smooth Stochastic Complexity and Manifold Sampling
summary: The exact computation of the Normalized Maximum Likelihood (NML) codelength for regular non-smooth estimators (e.g., Lasso) has been historically limited by the cubic scaling walls of manifold-constrained projection and volume integration. At each step of the geometric Propose-and-Project Metropolis--Hastings (PPMH) sampler, evaluating the projection operator requires inverting an $(N+k) \times (N+k)$ generalized KKT matrix, while calculating the volume factor requires the determinant of an $(N-k) \times (N-k)$ Gram matrix. This paper presents an exact, mathematically equivalent formulation that bypasses both bottlenecks by utilizing the block Schur complement and Sylvester's determinant identity. We prove that the computational complexity of both operations collapses from $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ to $\mathcal{O}(k^3 + N^2 k)$ per step. We generalize this reduction to Sparse Support Vector Machines (SVMs), Elastic Net, and Group Lasso. Finally, we provide a rigorous numerical stability analysis and evaluate the sampler's efficiency using the Effective Sample Size (ESS) per second. Our empirical benchmarks on high-dimensional datasets confirm a constant speedup exceeding $14{,}100\times$ while maintaining double-precision numerical equivalence, rendering exact non-smooth NML estimation highly tractable for large-scale statistical inference.

AI.tldr: Provides an exact reformulation for computing NML codelength with non-smooth estimators that removes cubic bottlenecks by using block Schur complements and Sylvester identities, yielding a per-step complexity of O(k^3 + N^2k) and up to massive speedups (|ESS|) while preserving numerical equivalence; extends to SVMs, Elastic Net, and Group Lasso; supported by stability analysis and large-scale benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Regular non-smooth estimators (e.g., Lasso) make exact NML codelength computation prohibitively expensive due to projection and volume integration costs, limiting practical use on high-dimensional data.
AI.method: Introduce a mathematically equivalent reformulation that replaces inversions of large KKT-like blocks and determinants of large Gram matrices with computations involving block Schur complements and Sylvester determinant identity. This collapses the dominant costs from O(N^3) to O(k^3 + N^2k) per step. The approach is generalized to Sparse SVMs, Elastic Net, and Group Lasso, with a rigorous numerical stability analysis and ESS-based efficiency evaluation.
AI.result: Per-step computational complexity reduces from O(N^3) to O(k^3 + N^2k). Numerical stability is analyzed and confirmed. Empirical benchmarks report massive speedups (claimed >14,100x) with double-precision equivalence. The method is validated on high-dimensional datasets and extended to Sparse SVMs, Elastic Net, and Group Lasso.
AI.conclusion: Exact non-smooth NML estimation becomes tractable for large-scale inference; the block-Schur-based reformulation enables efficient and stable computation and broad applicability to common sparse models.

title: T2D-Bench: Evidence-Gated Evaluation of LLM Outputs for Type 2 Diabetes Using a Multi-Layer Clinical-Lifestyle Knowledge Graph
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Accepted as a poster at AMIA 2026 Annual Symposium
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can produce clinically fluent recommendations for type 2 diabetes while failing to satisfy guideline constraints or explicitly justify lifestyle-related glycemic claims. We present T2D-Bench, a reproducible benchmark and evidence-gated evaluation framework for testing whether LLM outputs satisfy explicit, graph-checkable evidence requirements. T2D-Bench is built on a multi-layer clinical-lifestyle knowledge graph that combines a biomedical spine (UMLS, DrugBank, SIDER), computable ADA Standards of Care rules, and lifestyle knowledge connected through a mechanistic bridge to glycemic laboratory effects. Across 100 structured vignettes spanning diagnosis, medication safety, and adversarial lifestyle conflicts, baseline outputs failed benchmark-defined evidence-path checks in 35% of cases for GPT-4o-mini and 33% for GPT-4o. The evidence gate detects unsupported omissions and uses constrained revision to bring outputs into verifier-level compliance with benchmark-defined evidence requirements. These results show that computable evidence constraints can make unsupported clinical omissions explicit, measurable, and correctable in diabetes-focused LLM outputs.

AI.tldr: T2D-Bench provides a reproducible, evidence-gated benchmark to test whether LLM outputs for type 2 diabetes comply with computable evidence requirements, using a multi-layer knowledge graph and ADA rules; results show significant omission errors and that an evidence gate can enforce compliance.
AI.motivation: To address LLMs' tendency to generate clinically fluent but unconstrained recommendations by introducing verifiable, graph-based evidence constraints for diabetes care.
AI.method: Construct a multi-layer clinical-lifestyle knowledge graph integrating biomedical spine (UMLS, DrugBank, SIDER), ADA standards, and lifestyle-laboratory links; develop 100 structured vignettes; implement an evidence gate that checks outputs against evidence-paths; measure baseline performance on GPT-4o variants; provide constrained revision to meet verifier criteria.
AI.result: Baseline outputs failed evidence-path checks in 35% (GPT-4o-mini) and 33% (GPT-4o); the evidence gate detects omissions and enables revision to comply; demonstrates computable evidence constraints yield explicit, measurable, and correctable omissions in diabetes-focused LLM outputs.
AI.conclusion: Computable evidence constraints can make unsupported clinical omissions explicit, measurable, and correctable, improving reliability of LLMs in diabetes care, with a reproducible framework (T2D-Bench).

title: Fabric Image Demoiréing Benchmark from Synthesis to Restoration
summary: Fabric moiré is a sampling-induced aliasing artifact caused by the interaction between fine textile patterns and camera sensor grids, producing structured interference that severely degrades image quality. Unlike screen-induced moiré, which stems from strictly periodic display lattices, fabric moiré is intrinsically more challenging due to the broadband and semi-periodic nature of textile weaves. The heavy spectral overlap between intrinsic texture and aliasing components renders fabric demoiréing substantially more ill-posed. Consequently, existing models trained on screen moiré datasets generalize poorly to these complex textile patterns. Despite its practical importance, fabric image demoiréing remains underexplored and lacks standardized benchmarks. We present the first comprehensive benchmark for fabric image demoiréing. To address the difficulty of acquiring pixel-aligned real-world pairs, we develop a physically motivated synthesis framework and construct a large-scale dataset comprising 16,050 paired multi-resolution fabric images with controllable aliasing severity. Furthermore, we customize a baseline model, which establishes promising performance on the proposed benchmark dataset with strong generalization ability. Our benchmark provides a standardized platform for advancing research in fabric image demoiréing.

AI.tldr: Introduces the first large-scale benchmark for fabric image demoiréing, built from a physically motivated synthesis framework to generate 16,050 pixel-aligned, multi-resolution paired images with controllable aliasing; a customized baseline model shows promising generalization on the benchmark.
AI.motivation: Fabric moiré arises from sampling interaction between textile patterns and camera sensor grids; it is broadband and semi-periodic, unlike screen moiré, making demoiréing ill-posed. There is no standardized dataset or benchmark, hindering progress.
AI.method: Develops a physically motivated synthesis framework to produce pixel-aligned pairs; constructs a large-scale dataset of 16,050 paired multi-resolution fabric images with controllable aliasing severity; adapts/baselines model architectures to this domain.
AI.result: Baseline model achieves promising performance and demonstrates generalization on the proposed benchmark; establishes a standardized platform and informs future approaches.
AI.conclusion: Provides the first comprehensive fabric demoiréing benchmark, enabling systematic evaluation and progress in fabric image demoiréing research.

title: Supervise What Survives: Geometry-Guided VLA Adaptation from Synthetic Robot Videos
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models require large-scale video-action pairs, yet real teleoperation remains scarce. While generated robot videos offer a scalable alternative, existing methods treat them as real robot data by recovering pseudo-actions from synthesized pixels. We argue that deriving low-level control from generated visuals is a mismatched abstraction. A video captures only \emph{geometry}: the spatial trajectory representing the \emph{where} of a task. A real demonstration captures \emph{control}: the exact motor commands representing the \emph{how}. Human-to-robot video generation preserves these unequally: the visible geometry survives the generation process, while the underlying control signals are lost. This \textbf{Asymmetric Preservation Principle} dictates a clean rule: this surviving geometry should solely supervise visual perception, leaving control to real demonstrations. Following this principle, we propose \textbf{GRA} (\textbf{G}eometry-guided \textbf{R}epresentation \textbf{A}lignment), which extracts the geometric content as future 2D end-effector waypoints, computed from the source human video through pose estimation, retargeting, simulation, and calibrated projection, and routes them to the VLA vision backbone via an auxiliary 2D head. The action head is trained on real demonstrations only. During fine-tuning, the waypoint loss persists as a \textbf{spatial representation anchor} that prevents the backbone from losing its geometric grounding. On real-robot tasks, GRA outperforms pseudo-action baselines under matched data budgets and narrows the gap to policies trained with substantially more real demonstrations, suggesting that correctly routed geometry bridges generated videos to robot policies more reliably than recovered actions.

AI.tldr: GRA proposes geometry-guided representation alignment to fix a mismatch in VLA training: use geometry (2D end-effector waypoints) from generated human videos to guide perception, while learning control only from real demonstrations; maintains a spatial anchor during fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language-Action models rely on abundant video-action pairs, but synthetic videos re-create geometry without corresponding control signals, creating a mismatch that hurts transfer to real robots. An asymmetric preservation principle is proposed to separate perception (geometry) from control (actions).
AI.method: Introduce Asymmetric Preservation Principle. Extract 2D end-effector waypoints from human videos via pose estimation, retargeting, simulation, and calibrated projection. Attach these as an auxiliary 2D head to the VLA backbone; train the action head exclusively on real demonstrations. Use the waypoint loss as a spatial representation anchor during fine-tuning to preserve geometric grounding.
AI.result: GRA outperforms pseudo-action baselines on real-robot tasks under matched data budgets and narrows the gap to policies trained with substantially more real demonstrations, indicating that routing geometry is a more reliable bridge from generated videos to robot policies than recovering actions.
AI.conclusion: Separating geometry for perception from control for learning, guided by the Asymmetric Preservation Principle, yields more reliable transfer from generated visual data to robot policies than recovering pseudo-actions, highlighting geometry as the key bridge in VLA learning with synthetic data.

title: Federated Survival Analysis in Healthcare: A Multi-Model Evaluation on Cross-Institutional Heterogeneous Breast Cancer Data
summary: Survival analysis is central to clinical decision-making, yet reliable time-to-event models require large, diverse cohorts that are rarely available at a single institution, while privacy regulations restrict the centralization of patient data. Federated learning (FL) offers a privacy-preserving alternative by training shared models without exchanging raw data, but its effectiveness for survival modeling under realistic, heterogeneous conditions remains insufficiently understood. This paper presents a systematic, multi-model evaluation of federated survival analysis on a cross-institutional breast cancer cohort with naturally heterogeneous distributed clients. Three representative survival models, the Cox Proportional Hazards model, DeepSurv, and Random Survival Forest (RSF), are compared across centralized, local, and federated training, and three federated optimization strategies (FedAvg, FedProx, and FedAdam) are assessed for the gradient-based models. Results show that FL consistently outperforms local training and approaches, and occasionally exceeds, centralized performance, while RSF offers the best overall balance of discrimination, calibration, and robustness across heterogeneous clients. We further find that performance depends on the diversity of client distributions, and that FedAvg and FedProx are stronger and more stable than FedAdam. Based on these findings, we derive practical, decision-oriented guidelines mapping data, privacy, interpretability, and resource constraints to recommended model and training-paradigm choices for federated survival modeling in healthcare.

AI.tldr: Federated learning enhances survival analysis across heterogeneous cohorts; RSF offers best overall robustness; FedAvg/FedProx more stable than FedAdam; guidelines for model/training choices in federated healthcare survival modeling.
AI.motivation: Large, diverse time-to-event datasets improve model reliability but centralization is often blocked by privacy regulations; federated learning offers a privacy-preserving alternative, yet its performance under realistic heterogeneity remains unclear.
AI.method: Systematic, multi-model evaluation on a cross-institutional breast cancer cohort with naturally heterogeneous distributed clients. Compare centralized, local, and federated training for three survival models (Cox PH, DeepSurv, RSF) using three federated optimizers (FedAvg, FedProx, FedAdam). Assess discrimination, calibration, and robustness.
AI.result: Federated learning consistently outperforms local training and approaches or exceeds centralized performance; Random Survival Forest (RSF) provides the best balance of discrimination, calibration, and robustness across heterogeneous clients. Performance depends on client distribution diversity; FedAvg and FedProx are stronger and more stable than FedAdam.
AI.conclusion: The study provides practical guidelines mapping data privacy, interpretability, and resource constraints to recommended model and training paradigm choices for federated survival modeling in healthcare, highlighting FL as a viable, sometimes superior, alternative to centralized training under heterogeneity.

title: The Geometry Behind Diffusion and Flow Matching: Gradient Flows and Geodesics in Wasserstein Space
summary: The space $\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R}^d$) of probability measures with finite second moment carries a natural geometry: the quadratic Wasserstein distance W_2 makes it a complete metric space and, following Otto, a (formal) Riemannian manifold whose geodesics are the optimal-transport interpolations. On this manifold, the gradient flow of the free energy F(rho) = KL(rho || π) is exactly the Fokker-Planck equation, and its implicit-Euler discretization is the JKO scheme. This is the geometry underlying diffusion models: the forward process descends the free energy, and each denoising step realizes one JKO step, which recovers DDPM, DDIM, NCSN/SMLD, and Energy Matching; this is one scheme, not separate theories. The same manifold supports a second variational principle. Its geodesics - the minimum-action curves of the Benamou-Brenier formula - are precisely the optimal-transport paths that Flow Matching learns. Fixing both endpoints and following the geodesic, generation becomes a deterministic ODE along a straight line, hence far fewer sampling steps. Placing both families of models on one manifold makes their relationship exact: diffusion follows a free-energy gradient flow, an initial-value problem; optimal-transport Flow Matching follows a Wasserstein geodesic, a boundary-value problem. The two reach the same endpoints along different paths.

AI.tldr: A unified geometric view on diffusion models and Flow Matching within the quadratic Wasserstein space: diffusion corresponds to a gradient flow of KL with respect to the target measure, while Flow Matching traces a Wasserstein geodesic via a minimum-action path; both approaches operate on the same Riemannian manifold and yield the same endpoints along different curves.
AI.motivation: To reconcile two prominent families of generative models—diffusion-based schemes (DDPM, DDIM, NCSN/SMLD) and Flow Matching—by embedding them in the same optimal-transport geometry. This clarifies their connection, explains sampling mechanics, and potentially inspires cross-method improvements.
AI.method: Embed probability measures with finite second moment into the Wasserstein-2 space. Interpret the gradient flow of the free energy F(ρ)=KL(ρ||π) as the Fokker-Planck dynamics and discretize via the JKO scheme; relate these steps to diffusion-model denoising. Simultaneously, view Flow Matching as following minimum-action geodesics in the Benamou-Brenier formulation, yielding a deterministic ODE path between endpoints. Demonstrate that both processes live on the same manifold and share endpoints, with diffusion as an initial-value gradient flow and Flow Matching as a boundary-value geodesic.
AI.result: The two variational principles—free-energy gradient flow and Wasserstein geodesics—are shown to be exact dual descriptions on the same Wasserstein space. Diffusion models realize the gradient flow of KL and perform JKO-like steps; Flow Matching follows optimal-transport geodesics along a straight-line path in Wasserstein space. Consequently, diffusion and Flow Matching are not separate theories but complementary views of the same underlying geometry.
AI.conclusion: A unifying geometric framework for diffusion-based generative modeling and Flow Matching. This suggests mutual insights, potential algorithmic hybrids, and more efficient sampling strategies by exploiting the correspondence between gradient-flow dynamics and boundary-value geodesics on the Wasserstein manifold.

title: End-to-End Radar and Communication Modulation Recognition with Neuromorphic Computing
summary: Although deep learning-based methods can achieve high accuracy in automatic modulation recognition (AMR) tasks, their high computational cost makes it difficult to strike a balance between accuracy and power consumption, thereby limiting their application on resource-constrained platforms. Neuromorphic architectures that perform spike-driven inference with modest energy budgets have recently been explored for vision and timeseries tasks. Motivated by these works, we propose EMRFormer, a novel end-to-end spiking nerural network (SNN) architecture that applies spike-driven transformer to the constraints of neuromorphic hardware for AMR. The model incorporates an adaptive spike encoder and Integer Leaky Integrate-and-Fire neurons to mitigate the degradation of effective information and enhance SNN representational capacity. By integrating spike-separable Convolution Neural Networks (SSCNN) into Spike-Driven Transformers (SpikeFormer), EMRFormer effectively extracts multi-scale temporal features from the raw IQ waveforms. We validate our approach across various mainstream datasets, the experimental results show that EMRFormer achieves state-of-the-art interms of accuracy, outperforming all the baselines. Furthermore, the model maintains strong performance in low signal-to-noise(SNR) environments and reduces theoretical energy consumption by over 90%. Finally, we evaluate our model on a KA200 neuromorphic chip. The results show that our model achieves up to 5 times reduction in power compared to running on a 3090 GPU or an Orin NX. This work demonstrates a promising pathway for AMR on resource-constrained devices.

AI.tldr: Introduces EMRFormer, an end-to-end spiking neural network using spike-driven transformers for automatic modulation recognition on neuromorphic hardware, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with strong low-SNR performance and large energy savings, validated on a KA200 neuromorphic chip.
AI.motivation: Balance high modulation recognition accuracy with low power consumption on resource-constrained hardware; leverage neuromorphic spike-driven inference and transformer-based architectures to improve efficiency while maintaining performance.
AI.method: Develops EMRFormer by combining adaptive spike encoding, Integer Leaky Integrate-and-Fire neurons, and spike-separable convolution with Spike-Driven Transformers. Extracts multi-scale temporal features from raw IQ waveforms and integrates SSCNN into SpikeFormer for end-to-end AMR on neuromorphic hardware.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across mainstream AMR datasets with strong robustness in low-SNR environments; reduces theoretical energy consumption by >90%; on KA200 neuromorphic hardware, delivers up to 5× power reduction compared with running on a 3090 GPU or Orin NX.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable pathway for AMR on resource-constrained devices using spike-driven transformers on neuromorphic hardware, combining high accuracy with substantial energy efficiency.

title: NoContactNoWorries: Estimating Contact through Vision and Proprioception for In-Hand Dexterous Manipulation
comment: Accepted to IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems(IROS) 2026
summary: Perceiving physical contact is fundamental to dexterous manipulation. While robots often rely on dedicated hardware tactile sensors, humans exhibit a remarkable ability to infer contact by integrating visual information with an innate sense of their body's pose and movement. Inspired by this embodied perceptual skill, we investigate whether a robot can learn to infer contact from vision, an approach that also offers a scalable alternative to tactile hardware specifically for binary contact estimation, which faces practical challenges in cost, fragility, and integration. We present NoContactNoWorries, a transformer-based multimodal framework that fuses RGB-D vision with the robot's proprioception to infer binary contact states as a pseudo-tactile signal for hand-object interactions. We validate by training a single contact prediction model on multiple objects and show that the inferred contact signal supports downstream reinforcement learning agents for in-hand object reorientation, generalizing to novel objects. Experiments in both simulation and on a real-world robot validate our approach, highlighting the feasibility of inferring contact from vision and proprioception. Project Page: https://soham2560.github.io/no-contact-no-worries/

AI.tldr: A transformer-based multimodal model (NoContactNoWorries) infers binary hand–object contact from RGB-D vision and proprioception to provide a pseudo-tactile signal for reinforcement learning, validated in simulation and on a real robot, and capable of generalizing to novel objects.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of scalable tactile sensing by predicting contact from vision and body pose, mimicking humans’ ability to infer contact without dedicated tactile hardware; enables binary contact estimation as a scalable surrogate for tactile feedback in manipulation tasks.
AI.method: A transformer-based multimodal fusion model that combines RGB-D imagery with the robot’s proprioceptive state to predict binary contact. Trained once on multiple objects to generalize across unseen objects. Evaluated as a pseudo-tactile signal to support downstream reinforcement learning for in-hand object reorientation, with experiments in both simulation and real hardware.
AI.result: The model successfully infers contact from vision and proprioception, producing a useful pseudo-tactile signal that enables RL agents to perform in-hand reorientation and generalizes to novel objects. Demonstrated feasibility across simulated and real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: Vision-plus-proprioception can substitute for tactile hardware for binary contact estimation in manipulation, offering a scalable approach to pseudo-tactile feedback. The method shows cross-domain validity (sim-to-real) and object-generalization, with potential broad applicability in dexterous manipulation tasks.

title: MGI: Member vs Generated Inference
summary: As generative models increasingly produce samples that are indistinguishable from human-created content, it becomes difficult to determine whether a given data point was part of a model's natural training set or was generated by the model itself, especially when models memorize and reproduce training data. We formalize this challenge as Member vs Generated Inference (MGI): given a sample and a target generative model, infer whether the sample is a true training member or a generated output of that model. Focusing on image generation, we show that existing membership inference methods systematically misclassify generated samples as training members, while attribution-based methods often misclassify true members as generated. This failure arises because both approaches rely on likelihood-related signals that are similarly elevated for training examples and for the model's own outputs. To address MGI, we propose Data Circuit Breaker (DCB), a three-stage method that combines complementary signals from a generative model's autoencoder and latent generator to distinguish training members from generated samples. Across multiple generative models, including image autoregressive and diffusion models, DCB consistently addresses the shortcomings of membership inference and attribution methods, remains effective even when models reproduce near-duplicates of training samples, and generalizes to challenging model derivative settings in which new models are trained on generated data.

AI.tldr: Introduces Member vs Generated Inference (MGI) for image data and proposes Data Circuit Breaker (DCB) to distinguish training members from model-generated samples. Shows that traditional membership inference and attribution methods rely on likelihood signals that confound membership with generation; DCB uses a three-stage approach leveraging a generative model's autoencoder and latent generator to robustly separate the two, generalizing across autoregressive and diffusion models and even to derivative settings with near-duplicate data.
AI.motivation: As generative models memorize and can reproduce training data, it becomes difficult to determine whether a sample originated from training data or from the model itself. Existing membership inference and attribution methods misclassify in this regime due to shared likelihood-based signals.
AI.method: DCB is a three-stage method that blends complementary signals from a generative model’s autoencoder and latent generator. Stage 1 extracts autoencoder-based signals (e.g., reconstruction/latent representations) from the input; Stage 2 extracts latent-generator signals (properties of the model’s latent space or generation process); Stage 3 fuses these signals (e.g., via a classifier or ensemble) to decide membership. Training uses labeled data consisting of true training members and model-generated outputs. The approach is evaluated on image autoregressive and diffusion models.
AI.result: DCB consistently mitigates the failure modes of standard membership inference and attribution methods, remaining effective even when models reproduce near-duplicates of training samples and in derivative settings where new models are trained on generated data. It demonstrates robust performance across multiple generative architectures.
AI.conclusion: DCB provides a robust framework for Member vs Generated Inference, addressing limitations of traditional signals by leveraging model-specific autoencoder and latent-space signals. It generalizes beyond standard training data and supports auditing and privacy considerations for modern generative models.

title: An Introduction to Causal Reinforcement Learning
summary: Causal inference provides a set of principles and tools that allow one to combine data and knowledge about an environment to reason with questions of counterfactual nature, i.e., what would have happened had reality been different, even when no data of this unrealized reality is currently available. Reinforcement learning provides methods to learn a policy that optimizes a specific measure (e.g., reward, regret) when the agent is deployed in an environment and pursues an exploratory, trial-and-error approach. These two disciplines have evolved independently and with virtually no interaction between them. We note that they operate over different aspects of the same building block, counterfactual relations, which makes them umbilically connected. Based on these observations, novel learning opportunities arise when this connection is explicitly acknowledged and mathematized. To realize this potential, we note that any environment where the RL agent is deployed can be decomposed as a collection of autonomous mechanisms with different causal invariances, parsimoniously modeled as a structural causal model; any standard RL setting implicitly encodes such a model. This formalization allows us to put under a unifying treatment different modes of learning, including online, off-policy, and causal calculus learning, which appear unrelated in the literature. However, these modalities are not exhaustive: we introduce several natural and pervasive classes of learning settings that entail novel dimensions of analysis. Specifically, we introduce and discuss through causal lenses generalized policy learning, where to intervene, imitation learning, and counterfactual learning. These tasks lead to a broader view of counterfactual learning and suggest great potential for studying causal inference and reinforcement learning side by side, which we call causal reinforcement learning (CRL).

AI.tldr: Unifies causal inference and reinforcement learning through a structural causal model (SCM) lens, introducing causal reinforcement learning (CRL) and exploring generalized policy learning, imitation learning, and counterfactual learning as natural extensions.
AI.motivation: Causal inference and reinforcement learning have developed independently despite sharing the concept of counterfactual reasoning; a unified framework could enable new learning modalities and theoretical insights by modeling environments as SCMs.
AI.method: Model RL environments as structural causal models encoding autonomous mechanisms with causal invariances. Provide a unifying treatment of online, off-policy, and causal-calculus learning, and introduce new learning settings: generalized policy learning, imitation learning, and counterfactual learning.
AI.result: A conceptual framework proposing CRL as a broader view that integrates causal inference and RL, outlining how various learning modalities fit within the SCM-based formulation and identifying new learning directions.
AI.conclusion: CRL has strong potential to advance both causal inference and reinforcement learning by enabling joint analysis and cross-disciplinary methods for counterfactual reasoning in RL settings.

title: Progressive Pixel-Neighborhood Deformable Cross-Attention for Multispectral Object Detection
summary: Effective cross-modal feature alignment and interaction are central challenges in multispectral object detection. Although global cross-attention provides strong long-range modeling ability, its quadratic complexity with respect to feature size limits deployment on resource-constrained platforms. We therefore propose Progressive Pixel-Neighborhood Deformable Cross-Attention for multispectral feature fusion, termed PNAFusion. The proposed framework is motivated by two observations: weak misalignment between visible and thermal images is usually concentrated around local neighborhoods, and semantic correspondence across modalities often follows non-linear spatial mappings that fixed receptive fields cannot model well. To address these issues, PNAFusion incorporates local spatial priors into its architectural design to concentrate feature interaction and alignment on the most relevant neighborhoods. Specifically, a Pixel-Neighborhood Cross-Attention (PNCA) module is introduced to avoid redundant global feature matching and suppress background noise. Meanwhile, an Adaptive Deformable Alignment (ADA) module captures non-linear spatial correspondences through learned pixel-wise offsets. These components are further integrated through an iterative feedback mechanism to progressively refine cross-modal feature alignment. Experiments on FLIR, M3FD, and DroneVehicle show that PNAFusion achieves 84.2, 90.5, and 85.5 mAP@0.5, respectively, under the YOLOv5 detector, and further reaches 86.8 mAP@0.5 on FLIR and 90.8 mAP@0.5 on M3FD when transferred to Co-DETR. Efficiency analysis indicates that PNAFusion reduces allocated GPU memory by 33.0\% compared with ICAFusion and reduces theoretical FLOPs from 194.8 G to 156.4 G, although the deformable sampling and iterative refinement introduce additional latency. Our code will be available at https://github.com/DanielQiuTian/PNAFusion.

AI.tldr: Progressive Pixel-Neighborhood Deformable Cross-Attention (PNAFusion) introduces local-prior guided PNCA and adaptive deformable alignment (ADA) with iterative refinement for multispectral feature fusion, reducing global attention cost while maintaining strong detection accuracy.
AI.motivation: Global cross-attention is powerful but quadratic in feature size and impractical for resource-constrained platforms. Misalignment between visible and thermal images is typically local, and semantic mappings are non-linear, challenging fixed receptive fields.
AI.method: Incorporate local spatial priors to focus interaction on relevant neighborhoods. PNCA avoids global matching and noise by restricting attention to pixel neighborhoods. ADA learns pixel-wise offsets to capture non-linear spatial correspondences. An iterative feedback mechanism progressively refines alignment. Evaluated with YOLOv5 on FLIR/M3FD/DroneVehicle and transferred to Co-DETR.
AI.result: On YOLOv5, PNAFusion achieves 84.2, 90.5, and 85.5 mAP@0.5 on FLIR, M3FD, and DroneVehicle; transfers to Co-DETR yield 86.8 mAP@0.5 on FLIR and 90.8 mAP@0.5 on M3FD. Efficiency: −33.0% GPU memory vs ICAFusion; FLOPs reduced from 194.8G to 156.4G; latency increases due to deformable sampling and iterative refinement.
AI.conclusion: PNAFusion offers effective, more efficient cross-modal fusion for multispectral detection by leveraging local neighborhoods and deformable alignment with iterative refinement, achieving competitive accuracy with lower memory and compute, at the cost of added latency.

title: Varying Bundle Size Reactive Multi-Task Assignment using Selective Cost Estimation for Multi-Agent Systems
comment: Accepted for publication at the 23rd World Congress of the International Federation of Automatic Control (IFAC 2026)
summary: This paper presents a scalable framework for multi-robot task allocation in complex environments where estimating task execution costs is computationally expensive. While combinatorial auction-based approaches offer reliable solutions, the exponential complexity of bundle generation typically renders them intractable for real-time reactive applications, particularly when accurate path planning is required for cost validation. We address this through a distributed, two-stage multi-fidelity bundle generation approach. Agents utilize a local search tree guided by a low-fidelity heuristic (such as euclidean distance) to rapidly explore the bundle space, applying high-fidelity path planning only to the most promising candidates in a best-first manner. These refined bids are then submitted to a central coordinator that solves a set packing problem to ensure global feasibility and maximize the overall utility. Simulation results in multiple environments demonstrate that the framework is able to improve the performance of reactive auction-based task allocation. Overall, the presented framework is shown to enable reactive task allocation with dynamic bundle sizes in multiple settings without exposing the agents' state and internal cost estimation models.

AI.tldr: A scalable two-stage multi-fidelity framework for real-time multi-robot task allocation: local search with a low-fidelity heuristic prunes bundle candidates, high-fidelity path planning validates top candidates, and a central set packing step enforces global feasibility and maximizes utility; improves reactive auction-based allocation while preserving privacy.
AI.motivation: Mitigate the combinatorial explosion and expensive cost estimation in multi-robot task allocation that hampers real-time, reactive operation.
AI.method: Distributed agents run a local search tree guided by a low-fidelity heuristic (e.g., Euclidean distance) to rapidly generate bundles; high-fidelity path planning is applied only to the most promising candidates; refined bids are sent to a central coordinator that solves a set packing problem to ensure global feasibility and maximize overall utility.
AI.result: Simulation across multiple environments indicates improved performance of reactive auction-based task allocation and enables reactive allocation with dynamic bundle sizes, while maintaining privacy of agents' state and internal cost estimators.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-stage, multi-fidelity bundle-generation framework provides a scalable, real-time capable solution for multi-robot task allocation, balancing solution quality and computational cost, and preserving agent privacy.

title: GRACE: Gated Refinement for Accurate Causal Edge Discovery in High-Dimensional Time Series
summary: From climate teleconnections to gene regulation, modern time-series datasets encompass tens or hundreds of interacting variables, making causal discovery increasingly challenging. Constraint-based methods offer statistical rigor but their nonlinear CI tests are infeasible at scale, while score-based alternatives avoid CI testing but require arbitrary thresholds to binarize continuous edge scores. We propose GRACE ($\textbf{G}$ated $\textbf{R}$efinement for $\textbf{A}$ccurate $\textbf{C}$ausal $\textbf{E}$dge discovery), which refines constraint-based discovery using Hard Concrete gates with $L_0$ regularization: each candidate edge has an independent gate whose values concentrate near 0 or 1, yielding a clean bimodal separation that makes the binary decision robust, unlike the narrow, overlapping score distributions produced by $L_1$ and attention-based methods. A fast linear CI skeleton provides high-recall candidates; a single gated model then prunes false positives by learning which edges genuinely improve prediction, with automatic regularization adapted to problem dimensions and skeleton density. Systematic experiments on synthetic benchmarks, spanning diverse graph topologies (scale-free, Erdős-R'enyi, small-world) and dimensionalities up to $d=100$, show that GRACE substantially improves F1 over its base CI method while maintaining high precision, and outperforms attention-based and score-based alternatives. GRACE matches or exceeds expensive nonlinear CI tests at a fraction of the cost ($75\times$ faster). On a real-world river flow dataset, where rainfall confounders, variable propagation lags, and distributional shifts violate standard assumptions, a temporal bootstrap variant of GRACE recovers 9 of 11 causal edges along the Elbe River with only 1 false positive ($F_1 = 0.86$, AUROC${} = 0.99$), reducing the skeleton's 106 false positives by 99%.

AI.tldr: GRACE refines constraint-based causal discovery through independent Hard Concrete gates with L0 regularization, yielding a clean bimodal edge decision, improved F1, and large speedups over nonlinear CI tests and competing methods; demonstrated on synthetic graphs and a real river dataset.
AI.motivation: Scale challenges in causal discovery for high-dimensional time-series: nonlinear CI tests are expensive at scale, while score-based methods require arbitrary binarization thresholds.
AI.method: Apply independent Hard Concrete gates with L0 regularization to candidate edges in a constraint-based discovery pipeline. Use a fast linear CI skeleton for high-recall, then train a gated model to prune false positives by learning which edges genuinely improve prediction; regularization adapts automatically to problem dimensions and skeleton density.
AI.result: On synthetic benchmarks across graph topologies (scale-free, ER, small-world) and dimensions up to d=100, GRACE improves F1 over the base CI method while maintaining high precision; outperforms attention-based and score-based alternatives and matches or exceeds expensive nonlinear CI tests at ~75× speed. On a real river-flow dataset, a temporal bootstrap GRACE recovers 9 of 11 causal edges (F1=0.86, AUROC=0.99), reducing skeleton false positives by 99% (from 106 to 1).
AI.conclusion: GRACE offers scalable, accurate causal edge discovery with strong performance and substantial efficiency gains, robust to real-world confounding, and applicable to high-dimensional time-series.

title: Data Scale, Not Latency, Shapes Cross-Lingual Encoder Transfer in Streaming ASR
summary: Adapting a streaming speech recognition model to a new language requires choosing between two plausible warm starts: a multilingual (ML) encoder or an English-only (EN) encoder. The common intuition is that the multilingual encoder should help most at low data, but it is unclear how long that advantage persists, whether tight streaming latency amplifies it, and whether it survives deployment quantization. We answer these questions with a controlled sweep of a 0.6 B-parameter cache-aware FastConformer transducer across eight European languages, up to five target-language data scales (100 h to 2500 h), three streaming tiers plus offline decoding, and up to four public test sets. The main result is that multilingual initialization is a data-limited advantage, not a latency-limited one. On FLEURS at 160 ms, the mean EN-ML word error rate (WER) gap falls from +4.21 percentage points (pp) at 100 h to +0.20 pp at 2500 h; a power-law fit summarizes this decay, with each doubling of target-language data roughly halving the remaining advantage. Across the three streaming tiers, the across-language mean EN-ML gap is approximately stable at each scale from 100 to 1000 h, and is near zero by 2500 h. Finally, 4-bit weight-only encoder quantization at the matched 560 ms streaming tier reduces the encoder footprint by about 3x, with an average FLEURS WER increase of about 0.5 pp. The resulting guideline is simple: use multilingual initialization in low-data regimes, treat the choice as effectively irrelevant at large data, and make latency and quantization decisions independently.

AI.tldr: Multilingual warm-start helps mainly when data are scarce; its advantage decays with data and becomes negligible at larger data, with latency and quantization decisions largely independent.
AI.motivation: Clarify how long multilingual initialization remains advantageous, whether tight streaming latency amplifies it, and whether it survives quantization across languages and data sizes.
AI.method: Controlled empirical study using a 0.6B-parameter cache-aware FastConformer transducer across eight European languages, varying target-language data from 100h to 2500h, three streaming tiers plus offline decoding, and four public test sets; compare English-only vs multilingual initialization; evaluate 4-bit weight-only encoder quantization at the matched 560 ms tier.
AI.result: Multilingual initialization is a data-limited advantage, not a latency-limited one. On FLEURS at 160 ms, EN-ML WER gap shrinks from 4.21pp at 100h to 0.20pp at 2500h; the decay follows a power-law where each data-doubling halves the remaining gap. Across streaming tiers, the EN-ML gap is stable 100–1000h and near-zero by 2500h. 4-bit encoder quantization reduces encoder footprint ~3x with ~0.5pp WER increase on average for FLEURS.
AI.conclusion: Use multilingual initialization in low-data regimes; with sufficient target-language data, initialization choice is effectively irrelevant. Latency and quantization decisions can be treated independently.

title: Universal Guideline-Driven Image Clustering via a Hybrid LLM Agent
summary: Unifying image clustering across different clustering scenarios remains challenging due to fundamental gaps among tasks. We introduce a Guideline-Driven Image Clustering Agent, the first universal framework that bridges these gaps through textual guidelines. To incorporate complex guidelines without task-specific training, we propose Generative Concept Proxy Modeling, which generates guideline-aware embeddings via concept proxy extraction. For scenarios requiring automatic cluster discovery, we introduce LLM Traversal based on Minimum Spanning Tree that selectively applies LLM reasoning for complex semantic judgments. Our method generalizes across diverse clustering scenarios spanning from general to fine-grained categorization, from global to local criteria, and from balanced to long-tail distributions. Our framework consistently outperforms specialized methods across diverse clustering tasks.

AI.tldr: A universal text-guided clustering framework that unifies diverse image clustering tasks via guideline-aware embeddings and selective LLM reasoning, achieving strong performance across general-to-fine-grained and balanced-to-long-tail settings.
AI.motivation: Clustering tasks vary in criteria and labels; existing methods are specialized for each scenario and require task-specific training. A universal framework guided by textual guidelines can bridge gaps and enable generalization.
AI.method: Introduces Guideline-Driven Image Clustering Agent. Proposes Generative Concept Proxy Modeling to generate guideline-aware embeddings via concept proxy extraction. Introduces LLM Traversal based on Minimum Spanning Tree to selectively apply LLM reasoning for complex semantic judgments during automatic cluster discovery.
AI.result: Framework consistently outperforms specialized methods across diverse clustering tasks, demonstrating generalization across scenarios from general to fine-grained, and from balanced to long-tail distributions.
AI.conclusion: Text-guided guidelines enable a universal clustering framework without task-specific training; two modules enable guideline incorporation and selective reasoning; potential avenues include efficiency, robustness, and broader guideline corpora.

title: FT-WBC: Learning Fault-Tolerant Whole-Body Control for Legged Loco-Manipulation
summary: Legged manipulators combine the mobility of legged platforms with the manipulation capability of robotic arms. However, arm-induced Center-of-Mass shifts and dynamic disturbances make the system more prone to instability under actuator failures, potentially leading to falls, task failures, or safety risks. Existing fault-tolerant control methods mainly focus on locomotion alone, leaving the coupled problem of whole-body stability and arm reachability in fault-tolerant loco-manipulation largely unaddressed. To bridge this gap, we propose FT-WBC, a fault-tolerant loco-manipulation framework for robust whole-body control of legged manipulators under actuator failures. FT-WBC adopts a decoupled upper- and lower-body policy architecture and introduces two key modules: a Fault Estimator (FE) and a Posture Adaptation Module (PAM). The FE predicts faulty joints from lower-body proprioceptive histories, while the PAM uses this fault information to adapt the base posture plan generated by the arm policy, converting potentially unstable posture requests into safe and executable base posture commands. Through this fault-aware posture adaptation mechanism, FT-WBC synthesizes compensatory gaits under actuator failures and preserves as much arm workspace as possible while maintaining whole-body stability. Simulation and real-world experiments show that FT-WBC significantly improves survival rate and workspace under weakening or locked failures, and transfers zero-shot to a real legged manipulator in the real world.

AI.tldr: A fault-tolerant loco-manipulation framework (FT-WBC) for legged manipulators under actuator failures, featuring decoupled upper/lower-body policies, a Fault Estimator (FE) and a Posture Adaptation Module (PAM); it improves stability and arm workspace, validated in simulation and real hardware with zero-shot transfer.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in fault-tolerant loco-manipulation where prior methods focus on locomotion alone, neglecting the coupled trade-off between whole-body stability and arm reachability under actuator faults.
AI.method: A decoupled architecture with two modules: (1) Fault Estimator (FE) that detects faulty joints from lower-body proprioceptive histories; (2) Posture Adaptation Module (PAM) that uses fault information to adjust the base posture plan from the arm policy, converting unstable posture requests into safe, executable base commands. The approach synthesizes compensatory gaits to maintain stability while preserving arm workspace.
AI.result: Simulation and real-world experiments show significantly improved survival rate and workspace under weakening or locked actuator faults, and successful zero-shot transfer to a real legged manipulator.
AI.conclusion: FT-WBC provides a fault-aware, whole-body control framework for legged manipulation under actuator faults, enabling safer, more capable loco-manipulation with preserved manipulation capability and real-world transfer potential.

title: ARIA: Adaptive Region-Based Importance Allocation for Conditional Diffusion Distillation
summary: Distilling conditional diffusion models aims to transfer the behavior of a large teacher to a smaller student while preserving alignment across conditioning inputs. Unlike recognition tasks, knowledge distillation in conditional diffusion often struggles to transfer knowledge beyond the training distribution, since the predicted noise strongly depends on the conditioning signal. As a result, effective distillation requires exploring a large conditioning space. In practical settings, this creates a major bottleneck. Paired image-condition data may be limited, and generating synthetic images for every available condition is often computationally infeasible, while the pool of conditions, such as text prompts, can be extremely large. Recent work addresses this issue by switching conditions during training, exposing the student to a broader conditioning space without changing the distillation objective. Yet this raises a complementary question: once a large conditioning corpus is available, how should the training effort be allocated? In this work, we introduce ARIA, a framework that adaptively allocates training effort across coarse regions of the conditioning space. By maintaining online estimates of teacher-student discrepancy at the region level, ARIA focuses updates where misalignment persists while preserving the original distillation objective. Empirically, ARIA improves over RC across most architectures and settings, with the clearest gains observed in unseen and underrepresented regimes. We also provide a theoretical analysis showing that the proposed tracking mechanism follows the evolving discrepancy during training under bounded variance and drift assumptions.

AI.tldr: ARIA adaptively allocates distillation training effort across coarse conditioning regions by tracking teacher-student discrepancy regionally, improving alignment for unseen/underrepresented conditions with theoretical guarantees.
AI.motivation: Conditional diffusion distillation is hampered by strong dependency of predicted noise on conditioning; large conditioning spaces and limited paired data make exhaustive training infeasible. Existing methods that randomize conditions help but don't optimize resource allocation across conditioning space.
AI.method: Maintain online region-level estimates of teacher-student discrepancy and selectively update regions with persistent misalignment, preserving the original distillation objective while adapting training focus. The approach is architecture-agnostic and is accompanied by a theoretical analysis under bounded variance and drift.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms prior methods (RC) across most architectures and settings, with the largest gains in unseen and underrepresented conditioning regimes.
AI.conclusion: ARIA provides a principled, scalable strategy to concentrate distillation updates where needed by tracking evolving discrepancy, yielding better generalization under limited conditioning data and large conditioning spaces, with theoretical support.

title: Navigating User Behavior toward Personalized Multimodal Generation
comment: 16 pages, 15 figures, 5 tables. Code is available at https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/NaviGen
summary: Modern AIGC pipelines deliver high-fidelity images and videos but presuppose a well-formed creation instruction, while end users rarely articulate visual details, leaving generators misaligned with user demand. We study personalized content generation, which turns a user's interaction history into an executable instruction for downstream synthesis, and identify two obstacles: behavior must be encoded in a form legible to language reasoning, and the model must acquire instruction-writing skill absent from both pretraining and behavior data. We propose NaviGen, which represents each item with a dual identifier coupling a collaborative code and a textual code as a behavioral substrate and a semantic bridge in one token stream. On this representation, a two-stage SFT+RL pipeline first distills preference reasoning and instruction writing from evolutionarily searched supervision, then aligns generation with user intent through hierarchical and self-consistent rewards. Experiments across product, game, and short-video domains show that NaviGen improves personalized image and video generation, strengthens next-item prediction, and yields more specific, relevant, and visually generatable instructions. Our code is anonymously released at: https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/NaviGen.

AI.tldr: NaviGen introduces a dual-identifier representation and a two-stage SFT+RL pipeline to personalize content generation by converting user interaction history into executable instructions, improving generation quality and prediction across domains.
AI.motivation: End users rarely articulate visual details; existing pipelines misalign with user demand. A method to convert history into language-legible, instruction-writing capable signals is needed.
AI.method: Represent each item with a collaborative code plus textual code in one token stream (dual identifier). Use a two-stage pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning to distill preference reasoning and instruction writing from evolutionarily searched supervision; (2) reinforcement learning with hierarchical, self-consistent rewards to align generation with user intent.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across product, game, and short-video domains shows improved personalized image/video generation, stronger next-item prediction, and more specific, relevant, and visually feasible instructions.
AI.conclusion: NaviGen effectively enables instruction-writing capable personalization for AIGC, with publicly released code.

title: Beyond Bayer: Task-Optimal Sensor Co-Design for Robust Autonomous-Driving Segmentation
summary: Robust perception underpins autonomous driving, and most recent progress comes from scaling the model-larger backbones, foundation models, and cooperative multi-agent fusion. We pursue a complementary, upstream question: what should the camera itself measure? Using a differentiable RAW-to-task pipeline, we decompose which sensor degrees of freedom benefit dense prediction. Learning the spectral colour-filter-array (CFA) weights is the dominant lever, improving mIoU by +0.017 (KITTI-360) and +0.023 (ACDC) over a fixed camera. In contrast, point-spread-function (optics) co-design is net-negative (-0.020 mIoU on KITTI-360) - a consequence of the data-processing inequality, which also bounds the task information that any downstream model, however large or cooperative, can recover. Noise co-optimisation is marginal, and counter to intuition enlarging the CFA tile beyond 2x2 consistently hurts, as the filters are confined to the rank three sRGB input. Because the intervention is at the sensor, the gains are model-agnostic; we validate robustness on ACDC's fog, night, rain, and snow, and conclude with a simple recipe: learn the 2x2 CFA weights and keep an identity PSF.

AI.tldr: Upstream sensor design shows spectral CFA learning as the dominant lever for dense perception; PSF co-design is detrimental due to the data-processing inequality; optimal choice is a 2×2 CFA with identity PSF, yielding modest, model-agnostic gains robust to adverse conditions.
AI.motivation: Investigate which camera sensor degrees of freedom most benefit dense perception, by evaluating an end-to-end differentiable RAW-to-task pipeline that jointly optimizes sensor parameters and downstream predictions.
AI.method: Employ a differentiable RAW-to-task pipeline to optimize sensor degrees of freedom (spectral CFA weights, PSF, and noise) for dense prediction. Compare configurations (CFA tiling sizes and PSF choices) on KITTI-360 and ACDC under diverse conditions (fog, night, rain, snow). Measure mIoU gains over fixed-camera baselines.
AI.result: Learning 2×2 CFA weights improves mIoU by +0.017 on KITTI-360 and +0.023 on ACDC compared to a fixed camera. PSF co-design is net-negative, reducing mIoU by -0.020 on KITTI-360. Noise co-optimisation is marginal. Increasing CFA tile beyond 2×2 hurts due to the rank-3 constraint of sRGB input. Gains are model-agnostic and robust across adverse conditions (fog/night/rain/snow).
AI.conclusion: Adopt sensor optimization by learning 2×2 CFA weights while keeping identity PSF; this yields robust, modest gains across conditions without relying on complex optics changes.

title: G$^3$VLA: Geometric inductive bias for Vision-Language-Action Models
