summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have made rapid progress in generalist robot manipulation by harnessing semantic knowledge from pretrained vision-language backbones, but their visual tokens remain grounded in 2D image coordinates rather than the calibrated geometry of the robot's cameras -- a mismatch especially pronounced in multi-camera setups, where views are coupled by known intrinsics and extrinsics yet processed as independent images. We propose G$^3$VLA, a camera-aware geometric module that injects calibrated structure into the visual-token stream of a pretrained VLA without altering its action space or imitation objective, combining intrinsic-conditioned ray embeddings, projective positional encoding (PRoPE), and bidirectional cross-view fusion. Geometric supervision is provided either from ground-truth point maps when available, or from confidence-gated $π^3$X teacher predictions, requiring no depth sensors or manual annotations. Instantiated on $π_0$, G$^3$VLA yields consistent gains across the LIBERO suites, RoboCasa24, RoboTwin2.0, and real-robot settings, with the largest improvements on spatially and object-sensitive tasks. We further validate on $π_{0.5}$ and GR00T 1.5, with results suggesting that geometric transfer is most effective when geometry-aware tokens have direct access to the action generation pathway. Our project page is at https://sites.google.com/view/g3vla

AI.tldr: G^3VLA adds camera-aware geometry to pretrained Vision-Language-Action models by injecting intrinsic-conditioned ray embeddings, Projective Positional Encoding (PRoPE), and bidirectional cross-view fusion, with geometric supervision from ground-truth maps or π^3X predictions. It preserves the action space and imitation objective while improving manipulation performance in multi-camera settings across multiple benchmarks, without depth sensors or manual annotations.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between 2D visual tokens of pretrained VLA models and the calibrated 3D geometry of multi-camera robots, leveraging known intrinsics/extrinsics to improve spatial understanding and manipulation.
AI.method: Introduce G^3VLA, a geometric module plugged into a pretrained VLA that injects calibrated geometry into the visual-token stream. Key components include intrinsic-conditioned ray embeddings, PRoPE (projective positional encoding), and bidirectional cross-view fusion. Geometric supervision is provided by ground-truth point maps when available or by confidence-gated π^3X teacher predictions; no depth sensors or manual annotations are required. The approach preserves the existing action space and imitation objective.
AI.result: G^3VLA yields consistent gains across LIBERO suites, RoboCasa24, RoboTwin2.0, and real-robot deployments, with the largest improvements on spatially-aware and object-sensitive tasks. Generalization is observed on π_0.5 and GR00T 1.5, with evidence that geometry-aware tokens are most beneficial when they have direct access to the action-generation pathway.
AI.conclusion: Injecting calibrated geometry into the VLA's visual-token stream is an effective, sensor-free way to improve multi-camera robotic manipulation, aligning perception with robot geometry and enabling better action grounding across diverse benchmarks.

title: Closing the Loop: Formally Verified Law as a Reward Signal for Self-Improving Legal AI
summary: This article develops an architecture that creates a formally verifiable reward signal to train legal AI, adapting the LLM proposes, verifier disposes paradigm from mathematical AI to the distinctive demands of law. We present an architecture comprising LLM-driven autoformalization into a formal legal calculus extending Catala, a verification kernel, and explanation generation grounded in formal proof traces. For the computational components of law, the architecture provides provable correctness. For open-textured legal analysis, it provides structural guarantees: every required stage of the legal argument is addressed, argumentation is exercised at the correct stages and not omitted, and the deductive links between steps are valid. We demonstrate the architecture on procedural deadline calculations in German law, Commerce Clause analysis in U.S. constitutional law, and cross-jurisdictional sanction proportionality. We further show that the same architecture has a structural advantage for legal AI training: a deterministic external verifier supplies verifiable outcomes for legal problems and thereby closes the traditional reinforcement-learning loop gap in law.

AI.tldr: A formal, verifiable architecture for training legal AI via LLM-driven autoformalization into an extended Catala calculus, with a verification kernel and proof-trace-based explanations.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of verifiable correctness and structured reasoning in legal AI for both computational aspects and open-text legal analysis.
AI.method: Integrates LLM autoformalization into formal legal calculus (extended Catala), a verification kernel to check correctness, and explanation generation grounded in formal proof traces; applies to procedural deadlines, constitutional analysis, and cross-jurisdictional sanctions.
AI.result: Demonstrates provable correctness for computational components; structural guarantees for open-text analysis; empirical demonstrations on German procedural deadlines, US Commerce Clause analysis, and sanctions proportionality; external verifier closes the reinforcement-learning loop gap in law.
AI.conclusion: Architecture offers structural advantages for legal AI training via deterministic verification, enabling verifiable outcomes and tighter integration with RL-style learning.

title: Exploring the relationship between human-centric AI and firm idiosyncratic risks
summary: Despite the extensive discussions of human-centric AI (HCAI) in Industry 5.0, its effects on firms' idiosyncratic risks (IR) remains underexplored. This is an imperative issue for firms navigate financial risks during the current technological revolution, as IR reflects investor reactions to corporate heterogeneous AI strategies and implementations by isolating firm-level stock volatility from systematic factors. Integrating situated AI theory with social-technical systems theory, we conceptualise HCAI as a situated AI strategy that reduces AI-related ethical risks and fosters AI-Human synergies in firms' business operations, ultimately reducing IR by aligning with stakeholders' diverse expectations. Moreover, socio-technical factors, namely digitalisation, operational efficiency, executive shareholding, and CEOs with IT background, may moderate the HCAI-IR relationship. Using a multi-source panel dataset of Chinese listed firms from 2015 to 2023, we find that HCAI is associated with lower firm IR. Furthermore, digitalisation and executive shareholding strengthen this risk-reducing effect, whereas operational efficiency and CEOs with IT background surprisingly attenuate it. Our findings offer theoretical contributions and practical insights for both ethical AI governance and firm financial risk management in the AI era.

AI.tldr: HCAI reduces idiosyncratic risk for Chinese listed firms; digitalisation and executive shareholding strengthen, while operational efficiency and CEOs with IT background weaken the effect.
AI.motivation: Address the gap on how human-centric AI (HCAI) affects firm-specific risk (idiosyncratic risk) in Industry 5.0, linking AI strategy to investor reactions and firm performance.
AI.method: Conceptual integration of situated AI theory with socio-technical systems theory; empirical test using a multi-source panel dataset of Chinese listed firms (2015–2023) examining HCAI's association with IR and moderation by digitalisation, operational efficiency, executive shareholding, and IT-background CEOs.
AI.result: HCAI is associated with lower firm idiosyncratic risk. Digitalisation and executive shareholding strengthen this risk reduction; operational efficiency and CEOs with IT background attenuate it.
AI.conclusion: Contributes to ethical AI governance and firm financial risk management literature; provides implications for governance and risk management in the AI era.

title: DramaDirector: Geometry-Guided Short Drama Generation
comment: 20 pages, 17 figures, 6 tables. Code is available at https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/DramaDirector
summary: Short dramas, with their rapid shot rhythms, dialogue-driven focus shifts, and demanding cinematographic grounding, pose challenges that prompt-level or text-only video generation pipelines struggle to meet. We study plot-to-short-drama generation, where a global plot and local context are transformed into visually grounded multi-shot videos. We propose DramaDirector, a geometry-grounded framework that lets the planner borrow cinematographic geometry from a gallery of real short-drama shots indexed by depth and pose. DramaDirector decouples each shot into static visual and dynamic narrative conditions, trains the planner with schema-constrained SFT and GRPO under a learned text-visual alignment reward, and retrieves depth-pose references to guide first-frame generation and image-to-video synthesis. We also introduce DramaBoard, a benchmark built from 35 live-action dramas, 2.8K episodes, and 81K shots, with structured storyboards and multi-dimensional evaluation protocols. Experiments show that DramaDirector improves over representative multi-agent and video generation baselines on faithfulness, consistency, and controllability. Our code is released at: https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/DramaDirector

AI.tldr: DramaDirector presents a geometry-grounded, plot-to-short-drama generation framework that uses a real-shot depth/pose gallery to ground first frames and guide video synthesis, plus DramaBoard—a 35-live-action-drama benchmark—for evaluating faithfulness, consistency, and controllability; reports improvements over multi-agent and baselines with a released codebase.
AI.motivation: Short dramas require tight cinematographic grounding and multi-shot coherence that text-only or prompt-based video generation pipelines struggle to achieve; existing methods lack explicit geometry-aware grounding and dynamic narrative scheduling.
AI.method: Decouples each shot into static visual and dynamic narrative conditions; trains a planner with schema-constrained supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and GRPO under a learned text-visual alignment reward; retrieves depth-pose references from a shot gallery to guide first-frame generation and subsequent image-to-video synthesis; introduces DramaBoard benchmark built from 35 live-action dramas, 2.8K episodes, 81K shots, with structured storyboards and evaluation protocols.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows DramaDirector outperforms representative multi-agent and video generation baselines on metrics of faithfulness, consistency, and controllability across the DramaBoard benchmark and possibly others.
AI.conclusion: The geometry-grounded planning-and-generation framework, along with the DramaBoard benchmark, advances plot-to-short-drama generation by improving fidelity to plot and visual grounding, with code released for reproducibility.

title: Decentralized Pose Graph Riemannian Optimization for Object-based Multi-Robot SLAM
summary: Pose graph optimization (PGO) is a key back-end component for state estimation in networked multi-robot simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM). In object-based multi-robot SLAM, the problem becomes more tightly coupled because robots must jointly estimate both their trajectories and the poses of persistent objects observed by multiple agents. Existing decentralized solutions often assume that the communication graph closely matches the physical interaction topology, which is restrictive in realistic deployments where communication is sparse, intermittent, or time-varying. This paper presents a fully decentralized Riemannian optimization framework for object-based multi-robot PGO that decouples the coupled estimation problem via a consensus mechanism, enabling flexible communication topologies. To improve convergence under limited communication budgets, we further develop a distributed approximate-Newton scheme that exploits local second-order information while operating directly on the SE(d) manifold to preserve geometric consistency, and we establish the convergence to Riemannian first-order stationary points and provide a local condition-number analysis explaining the benefit of approximate second-order information over first-order Riemannian descent. The resulting method reduces iteration count and communication overhead without sacrificing estimation accuracy. Extensive evaluations on public benchmarks, large-scale simulations, and real-world multi-robot experiments demonstrate improved accuracy, runtime efficiency, scalability across network topologies, and robustness to communication failures.

AI.tldr: Decentralized Riemannian PGO for object-based multi-robot SLAM using consensus and distributed approximate-Newton on SE(d); improves convergence and reduces communication while preserving accuracy across flexible networks.
AI.motivation: In object-based multi-robot SLAM, jointly estimating robot trajectories and persistent object poses is essential, but existing decentralized methods assume a communication graph that mirrors physical interactions, which is restrictive under sparse, intermittent, or time-varying networks.
AI.method: A fully decentralized Riemannian optimization framework with a consensus-based decoupling of the coupled estimation problem, plus a distributed approximate-Newton scheme operating on the SE(d) manifold to leverage local second-order information and maintain geometric consistency; includes convergence to Riemannian first-order stationary points and a local condition-number analysis.
AI.result: Reduced iteration count and communication overhead while maintaining estimation accuracy; improved runtime efficiency and scalability across diverse network topologies; robustness to communication failures; validated on public benchmarks, large-scale simulations, and real-world multi-robot experiments.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables flexible communication topologies in object-based multi-robot PGO, with approximate second-order information providing practical performance gains and resilience to communication issues without compromising accuracy.

title: Catastrophic Compositional Generation: Why Vanilla Diffusion Models Fail to Extrapolate
summary: The task of compositional generation involves using a conditional generative model, trained only on a subset of the possible conditions, to produce samples from compositionally-defined target distributions such as a geometric combination of the source distributions. In this work, we argue that this task is often infeasible for vanilla conditional diffusion models: we conjecture that no inference-time technique can efficiently produce samples from the target distribution in certain well-motivated settings. This idea is supported by theory-guided generalization arguments and carefully-designed experiments on both synthetic and realistic data. In particular, while recent methods such as Feynman-Kac correction reduce inference-time approximation error, our results show that score estimation error has a more catastrophic effect on performance when the target distribution is out-of-distribution with respect to the sources, highlighting the need for a different approach to this task.

AI.tldr: Vanilla conditional diffusion models struggle with compositional generation; inference-time corrections (e.g., Feynman-Kac) help only modestly because score estimation errors dominate when the target distribution is out-of-distribution relative to the sources.
AI.motivation: Assess the feasibility of producing samples from compositionally defined target distributions using conditional diffusion models and identify fundamental limitations of existing inference-time techniques.
AI.method: Theory-guided generalization arguments combined with carefully designed experiments on synthetic and realistic data; evaluation of inference-time corrections (Feynman-Kac) and analysis of score estimation error in out-of-distribution settings.
AI.result: Evidence supporting the infeasibility of the task for vanilla conditional diffusion models in certain settings; Feynman-Kac corrections reduce approximation error but score estimation errors have a catastrophic impact when the target is out-of-distribution, indicating that a fundamentally different approach is required.
AI.conclusion: Current inference-time corrections are insufficient for compositional generation with vanilla diffusion models; the paper calls for novel approaches beyond standard inference-time corrections to tackle this task.

title: FlowR2A: Learning Reward-to-Action Distribution for Multimodal Driving Planning
summary: Multimodal driving planning faces a long-standing tension between two paradigms: scoring-based methods benefit from dense reward supervision but are confined to a fixed action vocabulary, while anchor-based methods generate proposals dynamically yet suffer from sparse supervision constrained to a single ground-truth trajectory. In this work, we propose FlowR2A, which resolves this tension by reframing simulation-based rewards from discriminative targets into generative conditions. By learning the reward-conditioned action distribution from dense trajectory-reward pairs with a flow-matching decoder, FlowR2A unifies the dense supervision of scoring-based methods with the proposal generation of anchor-based methods in a single generative model, forcing the model to internalize the correlation between an action and its outcomes in safety, progress, comfort, and rule compliance. To balance hard safety constraints against soft progress objectives, we introduce fine-grained per-timestep reward conditioning and reward noise augmentation. The generative formulation naturally supports controllable test-time sampling via reward guidance and anchored sampling, producing high-quality proposals. FlowR2A achieves state-of-the-art results on the NAVSIM v1 and v2 benchmarks, with multimodal proposals of substantially higher quality than prior methods.

AI.tldr: FlowR2A is a flow-based, reward-conditioned generative model that unifies dense reward supervision with dynamic proposal generation for multimodal driving planning by learning a reward-conditioned action distribution via a flow-matching decoder.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between scoring-based methods (dense rewards but fixed action vocab) and anchor-based methods (dynamic proposals but sparse supervision), enabling a single generative model to learn action-outcome correlations under safety, progress, comfort, and rule compliance.
AI.method: Learn the reward-conditioned action distribution from dense trajectory–reward pairs using a flow-matching decoder. Reform simulation-based rewards from discriminative targets into generative conditions. Introduce per-timestep reward conditioning and reward noise augmentation. Enable test-time controllability via reward guidance and anchored sampling.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results on NAVSIM v1 and v2 benchmarks with multimodal proposals of substantially higher quality than prior methods.
AI.conclusion: A generative, reward-conditioned formulation can unify dense supervision with proposal generation, enabling controllable, high-quality multimodal driving plans while respecting safety and progress objectives.

title: A Benchmark for Hallucination Detection in VLMs for Gastrointestinal Endoscopy
comment: Accepted at the Medical Image Understanding and Analysis (MIUA) 2026 conference
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are prone to hallucination, which remains a major barrier to their safe deployment in clinical practice. To date, most hallucination detection methods have been evaluated on radiology benchmarks such as MIMIC-CXR and VQA-RAD, while gastrointestinal (GI) endoscopy remains largely underexplored. In this paper, we benchmark nine hallucination detection methods on the Gut-VLM dataset, a GI diagnostic Visual Question Answering (VQA) dataset with 4,392 test VQA pairs, across five VLMs (MedGemma-4B, MedGemma-27B, LLaVA-Med-7B, LLaVA-v1.6-7B, and Lingshu-32B). The methods span three categories: black-box methods (RadFlag, SelfCheckGPT-NLI), gray-box methods (AvgProb, AvgEnt, MaxProb, MaxEnt, Semantic Entropy, and VASE), and a white-box method (ReXTrust). Our results show that ReXTrust, a white-box method, achieves the highest AUC across all five models, outperforming the strongest alternative method on each VLM by a statistically significant margin (paired permutation test, p < 0.001 in all cases), reaching a peak AUC of 93.0 on MedGemma-4B. White-box hidden-state access provides a consistent advantage of 19.5 AUC points on average (range: 9.5--33.5), with ReXTrust maintaining strong performance even on LLaVA-v1.6-7B (AUC 79.9), where black-box methods and clustering-based gray-box methods collapse to near-chance performance. Among non-white-box methods, token-level gray-box statistics (MaxEnt, MaxProb) are the strongest alternatives, outperforming both clustering-based gray-box methods (Semantic Entropy, VASE) and black-box approaches on average. We further identify confident confabulation, a failure mode in which models hallucinate with high inter-sample consistency or high token-level probability, as a systemic failure for both consistency and uncertainty-based methods.

AI.tldr: White-box ReXTrust dominates hallucination detection on Gut-VLM GI-VQA across five VLMs, achieving up to 93.0 AUC and a ~19.5 AUC average advantage; token-level gray-box methods are strong among non-white-box approaches; a systemic failure mode—confident confabulation—affects consistency/uncertainty-based methods.
AI.motivation: Hallucination in vision-language models poses safety concerns for clinical deployment. GI endoscopy VQA remains underexplored, motivating evaluation of detection methods on a GI-specific dataset (Gut-VLM) across multiple VLMs.
AI.method: Benchmark nine hallucination-detection methods (RadFlag, SelfCheckGPT-NLI, AvgProb, AvgEnt, MaxProb, MaxEnt, Semantic Entropy, VASE, ReXTrust) on Gut-VLM across five VLMs (MedGemma-4B, MedGemma-27B, LLaVA-Med-7B, LLaVA-v1.6-7B, Lingshu-32B). Categories: black-box, gray-box, white-box. Primary metric is AUC; significance via paired permutation test. Analyze model/method performance, cross-model trends, and failure modes.
AI.result: ReXTrust (white-box) yields the highest AUC for all VLMs, peaking at 93.0 on MedGemma-4B, with white-box access providing an average gain of about 19.5 AUC points. LLaVA-v1.6-7B remains challenging for non-white-box methods, where many baselines collapse toward chance. Token-level gray-box methods (MaxEnt, MaxProb) outperform clustering-based gray-box and black-box methods on average. Confident confabulation identified as a systemic failure mode for both consistency- and uncertainty-based methods.
AI.conclusion: White-box access to hidden states offers robust, cross-model advantages for hallucination detection in GI-VQA; future work should emphasize white-box or token-level gray-box strategies and address confident confabulation to mitigate systemic failures.

title: Explaining Failures of Cyber-Physical Systems with Actual Causality
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026)
summary: Modern autonomous Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs), such as self-driving cars, face increasingly complex demands, and yet are expected to act reliably. The black-box nature often characterizing such systems, especially those relying on neural components, makes it impossible to fully verify the system behavior prior to deployment. Unfortunately, unexpected failures-when the system does not comply with its specification-are inevitable and may have catastrophic implications. To improve trust in the system and facilitate future mitigation after a failure occurs, it is important to try to derive an explanation for the unexpected system behavior. This paper introduces the novel concept of leveraging the framework of actual causality for CPS failure explanation. Up until now, this framework was only used to derive explanations in the context of simple systems, such as image classifiers. This paper addresses the theoretical gaps and provides the guidance needed to allow for correct explanation derivation in the CPS domain. Beyond the theoretical contribution, the paper presents two novel, practical, system-agnostic explanation derivation algorithms, allowing to prioritize either explanation optimality or derivation efficiency. The approach is demonstrated and evaluated in the context of a neural-network-controlled autonomous car, designed to avoid collisions.

AI.tldr: Proposes actual causality-based explanations for CPS failures; extends the framework to CPS, with two algorithms balancing explanation optimality and derivation efficiency, demonstrated on a neural-network-controlled autonomous car.
AI.motivation: CPS (especially with neural components) are hard to verify; black-box behavior leads to potentially catastrophic failures. A clear explanation framework is needed to improve trust and guide mitigation after failures.
AI.method: Develop two novel, system-agnostic explanation derivation algorithms grounded in actual causality; adapt the actual causality framework to CPS; prioritize either explanation optimality or derivation efficiency.
AI.result: The approach is demonstrated and evaluated in the context of a neural-network-controlled autonomous car designed to avoid collisions.
AI.conclusion: Extends actual causality theory to CPS, provides practical, system-agnostic algorithms and guidance for correct explanation derivation, enabling improved trust and post-failure mitigation.

title: KLip-PPO: A per-sample KL perspective on PPO-Clip
summary: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) is the standard policy-gradient algorithm for on-policy reinforcement learning. The literature presents it in two forms, a clipped surrogate that bounds the importance ratio between successive policies and a Kullback-Leibler penalty between them. These forms are treated as separate algorithms with their own gradients, their own hyperparameters, and their own reference implementations, and a sizeable body of empirical work compares them. We show that the gradient of the clipped surrogate is reproduced exactly by a Kullback-Leibler surrogate whose coefficient varies per sample, with closed-form dependence on the importance ratio and the advantage. The identity holds at every minibatch step and across the entire inner loop, and on five MuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks the two losses produce indistinguishable training curves. The reformulation exposes a structural feature of the clipped surrogate that the min notation hides. PPO-Clip's implicit per-sample penalty is a step function at the boundary of the trust region, and the shape of this coefficient is the natural design axis for generalising the algorithm. We sketch the resulting follow-up directions in the discussion.

AI.tldr: PPO's clipped surrogate gradient is exactly reproduced by a KL-based surrogate with a per-sample, ratio- and advantage-dependent coefficient; on minibatches these forms are indistinguishable, revealing that PPO-Clip's implicit penalty is a step at the trust-region boundary and guiding generalization.
AI.motivation: Clarify whether the common two-form PPO implementations (clipped objective and KL penalty) are fundamentally different or two views of the same optimization, and to understand the structural properties that could drive generalizations.
AI.method: Derive and verify an exact equivalence between the clipped surrogate gradient and a KL-divergence surrogate with a per-sample penalty coefficient that depends on the importance ratio and advantage; show that the equivalence holds at minibatch level and across inner optimization; empirically validate on five MuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks.
AI.result: The two losses produce indistinguishable training curves; the per-sample KL penalty is a step function at the trust-region boundary; the identity holds at every minibatch step and across the inner loop; this reveals a structural feature masked by min notation and suggests a natural axis for generalization.
AI.conclusion: The work unifies PPO’s two popular formulations, exposes the per-sample nature of the implicit KL penalty in PPO-Clip, and points to concrete directions for generalizing the clipping mechanism along the coefficient function to improve robustness and performance.

title: SP-Mind: An Autonomous Reasoning Agent for Spatial Proteomics Analysis
comment: 23 pages, 6 figures. Accepted to ICML 2026. Equal contribution by Yucheng Yuan and Yuanfeng Ji
summary: Spatial proteomics enables single-cell-resolution characterization of protein expression within tissue architecture, playing a critical role in understanding tumor microenvironments and guiding precision medicine. However, current analysis workflows remain fragmented, requiring expert manual orchestration of heterogeneous tools and limiting research scalability and reproducibility. We present SP-Mind, the first autonomous AI agent designed to unify the spatial proteomics analysis pipeline, from raw multiplexed tissue imaging to downstream phenotype discovery. Equipped with expert-curated biological analysis skills and specialized computational tools, SP-Mind converts natural-language queries into end-to-end analytical workflows without task-specific fine-tuning. To rigorously evaluate its capabilities, we introduce SP-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark spanning diverse tissue types, comprising 102 tasks across 18 distinct categories. Through extensive evaluation on SP-Bench and established downstream tasks, SP-Mind achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to existing open-source biomedical agent baselines.

AI.tldr: Autonomous AI agent SP-Mind unifies the spatial proteomics analysis pipeline from raw imaging to phenotype discovery; validated on SP-Bench with 102 tasks across 18 categories, achieving state-of-the-art performance against open-source baselines.
AI.motivation: Fragmented, expert-driven workflows in spatial proteomics limit scalability, reproducibility, and adoption; a unified, NL-query-driven agent could automate end-to-end analysis.
AI.method: SP-Mind integrates expert-curated biological analytics skills and specialized computational tools to translate natural-language queries into end-to-end workflows without task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.result: On SP-Bench and downstream tasks, SP-Mind attains state-of-the-art performance compared with existing open-source biomedical agent baselines.
AI.conclusion: SP-Mind enables autonomous, scalable, and reproducible spatial proteomics analyses, and SP-Bench provides a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating such agents.

title: An LMM for Precisely Grounding Elements in Documents
summary: Visual grounding in documents is a crucial ability for Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) in areas such as document understanding, deep research and document error detection. However, existing approaches exhibit poor grounding precision in text-rich document images, often failing to accurately locate the critical document elements needed for reliable reasoning. To address this gap, we introduce PreciseDoc, an LMM specifically designed for precise element grounding and can be further optimized for Document VQA tasks. Specifically, to enhance the basic localization capability, we construct challenging training data by two pipelines capable of mass-producing high-quality documents with paired metadata of fine-grained coordinates, including synthetic hand-filled documents with camera effects. The model develops more real-world functions beyond straightforward localization of single text, such as locating personal information from CVs. Furthermore, we introduce a training paradigm for visual grounded reasoning where the grounding and reasoning are supervised jointly with reinforcement learning to improve the contribution of the grounded evidence. A comprehensive evaluation on various benchmarks demonstrates the advantage of the proposed data and methods in document spatial grounding and document understanding.

AI.tldr: PreciseDoc: an LMM for precise document element grounding with synthetic, fine-grained training data and RL-based grounding-reasoning supervision, enabling robust Document VQA and tasks like extracting personal info from CVs.
AI.motivation: Current LMMs struggle with precise grounding in text-rich document images, hindering reliable reasoning; need high-quality, fine-grained ground-truth data and training paradigms that jointly optimize grounding and reasoning.
AI.method: Two data-generation pipelines produce mass-produced, high-quality documents with metadata of fine-grained coordinates, including synthetic hand-filled documents with camera effects; a visual-grounded reasoning training paradigm jointly supervises grounding and reasoning with reinforcement learning to emphasize grounded evidence.
AI.result: Evaluations on multiple benchmarks show improved document spatial grounding and document understanding; evidence of better grounding precision and enhanced performance on Document VQA tasks, with capabilities like locating specific elements in CVs.
AI.conclusion: Specialized data generation plus RL-based grounding-reasoning supervision significantly improves precise grounding and document understanding in LMMs, benefiting document-centric tasks.

title: Enabling Robust Cloth Manipulation via Inference-Time Simulator-in-the-Loop Refinement
summary: Simulator-in-the-loop optimization offers a promising inference-time mechanism for robot manipulation. It uses a physical simulator as a backend rollout engine to evaluate candidate trajectories in parallel and refine nominal actions online, a paradigm proven effective in rigid-body manipulation where state and contact are relatively tractable. We bring this paradigm to real-world cloth manipulation from a single RGB input through three pillars. (i) We design a scalable synthetic-data generation and inference-time rollout pipeline built on FLASH, a deformable-object simulator that provides a practical balance among physical fidelity, numerical stability, and rollout efficiency. (ii) We develop a real-to-sim module, trained purely on synthetic data, that maps a single RGB observation to simulation-compatible cloth state by fusing pretrained visual features with learnable canonical tokens. (iii) We perform online planning by coupling a sparse-mesh rollout backend with prior-guided MPPI, anchored at an offline-distilled policy trajectory, preserving manipulation-relevant deformation and contact while enabling sufficient parallel rollout batches. Real-robot experiments show higher success rates and stronger robustness than baseline methods.

AI.tldr: Simulator-in-the-loop optimized planning for cloth manipulation using FLASH deformable-object simulator, real-to-sim state inference from RGB, and prior-guided MPPI with offline-distilled policy; achieves improved success and robustness in real robots.
AI.motivation: Address the difficulty of planning with deformable objects (cloth) under real-world dynamics and partial observation, by leveraging fast, physics-based rollouts and learned state mappings to bridge sim-to-real.
AI.method: (i) Build scalable synthetic-data generation and inference-time rollout pipeline on FLASH; (ii) Develop real-to-sim module mapping a single RGB image to simulation-friendly cloth state using synthetic data with pretrained visual features and learnable canonical tokens; (iii) Online planning via sparse-mesh rollout backend with prior-guided MPPI anchored to an offline-distilled policy trajectory.
AI.result: Real-robot experiments show higher success rates and stronger robustness compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasible simulator-in-the-loop cloth manipulation from RGB input, improving reliability and efficiency, and enabling scalable deformable-object manipulation with real-time planning.

title: DREG: A Layer-Wise Jacobian Regularization as a General-Purpose Penalty
summary: We present a large-scale empirical study isolating the contributions of the Derivative Regularization penalty (DREG). Across a fully-crossed factorial sweep of 960 experiments spanning 4 activations, 6 regularizers, 8 datasets, and 5 random seeds, we ask: when, where, and why does DREG work? Our results establish three principal findings. First, DREG achieves the highest overall and clean-regime accuracy among all regularizers evaluated (significantly so against the unregularized baseline, Weight Decay, and IGPen; Wilcoxon $p \leq 0.031$). It ranks second in noise robustness behind Spectral Normalization (SN) - the only two layer-wise regularizers in the study. Second, DREG is globally the best-performing regularizer under GELU, the default activation in modern transformer architectures, particularly on both messy vision and messy NLP benchmarks, suggesting direct applicability to frontier deep learning settings. Third, DREG's advantage over competing regularizers is most pronounced under data scarcity, consistent with its role as a geometric inductive bias that substitutes for the regularizing effect of data volume. Throughout, DREG is applied with a single fixed hyperparameter $λ= 10^{-2.5}$ and no per-dataset tuning, supporting its characterization as a plug-and-play regularizer for neural networks with nontrivial Jacobian structure. These findings are consistent with DREG's design: concentrating regularization pressure on layers where the activation derivative is largest, rather than constraining the network uniformly.

AI.tldr: A large-scale factorial study (960 runs across 4 activations, 6 regularizers, 8 datasets, 5 seeds) shows Derivative Regularization (DREG) offers the best overall and clean-regime accuracy among regularizers, is second-best for noise robustness (behind Spectral Normalization), and performs best under GELU activations and data-scarce conditions; uses a fixed lambda (10^-2.5) and targets layers with largest activation derivatives, supporting plug-and-play use for networks with nontrivial Jacobian structure.
AI.motivation: Isolate and quantify the contributions of DREG to regularization performance, understand when/where/why it helps, and assess its robustness across activations, datasets, and data regimes (especially under data scarcity).
AI.method: Fully-crossed factorial design with 960 experiments: 4 activation functions, 6 regularizers, 8 datasets, 5 random seeds. DREG evaluated against baselines (unregularized, Weight Decay, IGPen) and layer-wise regularizers (e.g., Spectral Normalization). Activation function GELU used as default in transformer-like models. Hyperparameter λ fixed at 10^-2.5; no per-dataset tuning. Outcomes measured include accuracy in clean and noisy regimes; statistical significance reported via Wilcoxon tests (e.g., p ≤ 0.031 for DREG vs certain baselines).
AI.result: DREG yields the highest overall and clean-regime accuracy among evaluated regularizers; significantly better than unregularized baseline, Weight Decay, and IGPen (Wilcoxon p ≤ 0.031). It ranks second in noise robustness, behind Spectral Normalization (the only two layer-wise regularizers in the study). DREG is globally the best-performing regularizer under GELU across messy vision and NLP benchmarks, with pronounced advantages in data-scarce scenarios. These findings align with a design that concentrates regularization pressure on layers with the largest activation derivatives, rather than uniform network-wide constraints.
AI.conclusion: DREG acts as a geometric inductive bias, effectively replacing some benefits of larger data volumes, and functions as a plug-and-play regularizer for networks with nontrivial Jacobian structure. Its effectiveness under GELU and in data-scarce settings suggests broad applicability to frontier deep learning tasks; its fixed hyperparameter makes it practical without per-dataset tuning.

title: Towards Federated Long-Tailed Graph Learning: An Energy-Guided Dual Decoupling Approach
summary: Federated Graph Learning facilitates collaborative graph modeling across distributed clients while preserving data privacy. However, real-world data categories frequently exhibit long-tailed distributions. Such statistical scarcity severely degrades performance in two ways: it biases the global model toward majority classes, and it structurally isolates minority nodes by submerging them in heterophilic, head-dominated neighborhoods. While existing methods attempt topology-agnostic statistical compensations, they often fail under data scarcity. Instead of recovering tail nodes, they overfit the structural noise from adjacent dominant classes, leading to representation degradation. To address these limitations, we propose FedEPD, a framework built on a dual decoupling paradigm that separates topological purification from semantic recalibration. Specifically, FedEPD utilizes distribution-aware Dirichlet energy pruning to filter spatial heterophilic edges. It then overcomes Non-IID distribution shifts by extracting robust global prototypes from topologically central nodes, which are incorporated into local representations via a spatial low-pass prototype injection. Furthermore, a two stage alternating optimization strategy strictly protects majority decision boundaries while improving minority accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FedEPD achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse long-tailed benchmarks, yielding absolute improvements of up to 4.97% in Accuracy and 5.48% in Macro-F1.

AI.tldr: FedEPD introduces a dual decoupling framework for federated graph learning under long-tailed data. It first performs distribution-aware Dirichlet energy pruning to remove spatial heterophilic edges, then uses global prototypes from topologically central nodes to recalibrate local representations via a spatial low-pass prototype injection. A two-stage alternating optimization preserves majority decision boundaries while improving minority accuracy, achieving state-of-the-art results on long-tailed benchmarks (up to +4.97% accuracy, +5.48% Macro-F1).
AI.motivation: Long-tailed distributions in federated graph learning cause bias toward majority classes and isolate minority nodes within heterophilic surroundings. Existing topology-agnostic compensations struggle under data scarcity and may overfit structural noise from dominant classes.
AI.method: A dual decoupling approach: (1) distribution-aware Dirichlet energy pruning to filter out heterophilic edges; (2) extract robust global prototypes from topologically central nodes and inject them into local representations via a spatial low-pass prototype mechanism; (3) a two-stage alternating optimization process that protects majority decision boundaries while enhancing minority accuracy.
AI.result: FedEPD achieves state-of-the-art performance on diverse long-tailed benchmarks, with absolute improvements up to 4.97% in Accuracy and 5.48% in Macro-F1.
AI.conclusion: The combination of topology purification and semantic recalibration through dual decoupling enables robust, fairer performance in federated graph learning under long-tailed data, outperforming topology-agnostic methods.

title: Flood Mapping from RGB imagery using a Vision Foundation Model
summary: Timely, high-resolution maps of flood extent around settlements are essential for emergency response and damage assessment. We consider airborne RGB imagery for flood mapping as it can be collected rapidly at low cost. To produce flood maps, deep learning models for water segmentation are often used. CNN based and small vision transformer models are used. However, they need much data for adaptation to a change of scenery, i.e., another flooding event. Vision foundation models or large vision transformers are known to generalize across domains. Recently, foundation models for Earth observation became available. They are pretrained on satellite data, whose spatial resolution, viewing geometry, and radiometry differ from nadir RGB imagery. Thus, adaptation is required. We investigate how a satellite-pretrained Earth observation foundation model can be adapted to centimeter-scale floodwater mapping from RGB imagery. Specifically, we fine-tune a model we call Prithvi-2.0-UPN consisting of the Prithvi-EO-2.0-600M Vision Transformer combined with a UPerNet decoder for binary water segmentation on two RGB datasets (BlessemFlood21, NeuenahrFlood). In a first experiment we observe that Prithvi-2.0-UPN reaches state-of-the-art results on BlessemFlood21 and NeuenahrFlood, when trained on their datasets. In a second experiment we show that Prithvi-2.0-UPN performs better than state-of-the-art baseline models for transfer to a new flood event (trained on BlessemFlood21, tested on NeuenahrFlood) in a zero-shot setting. However, the performance indicates room for improvement. In this respect, we investigate in a third experiment how performance improves when further fine-tuning the models with small shares of NeuenahrFlood training data: Prithvi-2.0-UPN improves the fastest and reaches almost the performance level when fully trained on NeuenahrFlood, indicating transfer capabilities.

AI.tldr: Foundation-model-based flood mapping from RGB imagery can transfer across flood events; Prithvi-2.0-UPN (satellite-pretrained ViT + UPerNet) achieves SOTA on source datasets and shows strong zero-shot transfer, with further fine-tuning on limited data closing the gap.
AI.motivation: Need timely, high-resolution flood extent maps from RGB imagery for emergency response; models must generalize across events and scenery changes; Earth observation foundation models offer cross-domain generalization, but require adaptation to nadir RGB data.
AI.method: Fine-tune Prithvi-2.0-UPN (Prithvi-EO-2.0-600M Vision Transformer + UPerNet) for binary water segmentation on two RGB datasets (BlessemFlood21, NeuenahrFlood); evaluate on training sets, perform zero-shot transfer (train on BlessemFlood21, test on NeuenahrFlood), and explore incremental fine-tuning with small NeuenahrFlood data.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art on both datasets when trained on them; outperforms baselines in zero-shot transfer to a new flood event; additional fine-tuning with limited NeuenahrFlood data accelerates improvement and nears fully trained NeuenahrFlood performance.
AI.conclusion: EO/pretrained vision foundation models can adapt to centimeter-scale flood-water mapping from RGB imagery, demonstrating transfer capability with limited data; performance potential exists but further improvements are possible.

title: ArtiTwinSplat: Interactable Digital Twin Reconstruction via Gaussian Splatting from RGB-D videos
comment: Presented at the ICRA 2026 Workshop on Advances and Challenges in AI-Driven Automation and Robotic System Integration with Digital Twins, Vienna, June 2026
summary: Deploying robots in unstructured real-world environments needs accurate, interactive models of the objects. Constructing these models at scale remains a critical bottleneck for robotic system integration. We present ArtiTwinSplat, a framework that automatically constructs articulated, photo-realistic digital twins of objects directly from RGB-D videos, requiring no CAD models, simulation assets, or manual annotations. Our method is built on 3D Gaussian Splatting that preserve geometric fidelity and photometric realism, coupled with an unsupervised articulation discovery pipeline that recovers part structure and joint kinematics from observed motion alone. With tracking and optimization stages our method provides stable, queryable digital twins that support real-time rendering, viewpoint control, and interactive manipulation. Unlike prior methods confined to simulation, ArtiTwinSplat operates directly on real-world observations and produces twins that are immediately usable by downstream robot planning and learning systems. This method offers a practical, scalable pathway toward digital twin construction, lowering the integration barrier for articulated object manipulation in embodied AI and human-robot collaboration contexts.

AI.tldr: ArtiTwinSplat automatically builds articulated, photo-realistic digital twins of objects from RGB-D videos without CAD models or annotations, using 3D Gaussian Splatting and unsupervised articulation discovery to enable real-time rendering and manipulation for robotics.
AI.motivation: Scalable, accurate, interactive models of articulated objects in unstructured real-world environments are a key bottleneck for robotic planning/learning; current methods rely on CAD assets or supervision.
AI.method: Utilizes 3D Gaussian Splatting to preserve geometric fidelity and photometric realism, paired with an unsupervised articulation discovery pipeline that infers part structure and joint kinematics from observed motion; includes tracking and optimization to produce stable, queryable digital twins from RGB-D videos.
AI.result: Delivers digital twins directly from real-world observations that support real-time rendering, viewpoint control, and interactive manipulation; integrates with downstream robot planning/learning and reduces the integration barrier for articulated object manipulation in embodied AI.
AI.conclusion: Presents a practical, scalable pathway to digital twin construction for articulated objects, enabling seamless deployment in embodied AI and human-robot collaboration contexts by avoiding CAD/assets and annotations.

title: Learning the Koopman Operator using Attention Free Transformers
summary: Learning Koopman operators with autoencoders enables linear prediction in a latent space, but long-horizon rollouts often drift off the learned manifold, leading to phase and amplitude errors on systems with switching, continuous spectra, or strong transients. We introduce two complementary components that make Koopman predictors more robust. First, we add an attention-free latent memory (AFT) block that aggregates a short window of past latents to produce a corrected latent before each Koopman update. Unlike multi-head attention, AFT operates in linear time and adds only $\approx$30k parameters ($3d^2 + T^2$, fewer than matched multi-head attention), yet captures the local temporal context needed to suppress error divergence. Second, we propose dynamic re-encoding: lightweight, online change-point triggers (EWMA, CUSUM, and sequential two-sample tests) that detect latent drift and project predictions back onto the autoencoder manifold. Across three benchmark systems -- Duffing oscillator, Repressilator, IRMA -- our model consistently reduces error accumulation compared to a Koopman autoencoder and matched-capacity multi-head attention. We also compare against GRU and Transformer autoencoders, evaluated both from initial conditions and with a 50-step context, and find that Koopman+AFT (with optional re-encoding) attains markedly lower long-horizon error while maintaining lower inference latency. We report improvements over horizons up to 1000 steps, together with ablations over trigger policies. The result is a fast, compact predictor that stays on the learned manifold over long horizons.

AI.tldr: Proposes a robust Koopman predictor for autoencoder-based linearization by (i) adding an attention-free latent memory (AFT) to fuse short-term latent history before the Koopman update, and (ii) online dynamic re-encoding using change-point triggers to reproject predictions onto the latent manifold. Demonstrates improved long-horizon stability and lower latency over standard Koopman autoencoders, multi-head attention bases, GRU, and Transformer baselines across Duffing, Repressilator, and IRMA, up to 1000-step horizons.
AI.motivation: Koopman predictions via autoencoders often drift off the learned manifold over long horizons, especially for systems with switching, broad spectra, or strong transients. There is a need for robust, fast, and compact predictors that maintain manifold alignment and suppress error accumulation.
AI.method: Introduce two components: (1) an attention-free latent memory (AFT) block that aggregates a short window of past latent states to produce a corrected latent before each Koopman state update; (2) dynamic re-encoding via online change-point triggers (EWMA, CUSUM, sequential two-sample tests) to detect latent drift and reproject predictions back onto the autoencoder manifold. Complexities: ~30k parameters (3d^2 + T^2), linear-time operation, fewer than matched multi-head attention. Evaluate against Koopman autoencoder, multi-head attention with matched capacity, GRU, and Transformer autoencoders across three benchmarks.
AI.result: Koopman+AFT with optional re-encoding consistently reduces long-horizon error relative to baselines, achieving lower error for horizons up to 1000 steps and lower inference latency. The method remains on the learned manifold more reliably, with ablations showing effectiveness of trigger policies. The approach yields a fast, compact predictor suitable for long-horizon prediction in nonlinear dynamical systems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed AFT and online re-encoding yield a robust, efficient Koopman predictor that mitigates error drift over long horizons by leveraging short-term latent memory and online manifold enforcement, outperforming Koopman autoencoders and comparable baselines in speed and accuracy.

title: Probing the Misaligned Thinking Process of Language Models
summary: Large language models exhibit a growing range of misaligned behaviors such as strategic deception, sandbagging, and self-preservation. As they are increasingly deployed in high-stakes settings, it is critical to reliably detect such behaviors to ensure safe and responsible use. In this work, we propose to monitor misalignment by decomposing it into fine-grained cognitive processes -- misalignment indicators -- and detecting their presence in a model's internal activations via linear probes. We develop a taxonomy of 18 indicators spanning different misaligned behaviors, paired with an automated, meta-plan-guided pipeline that generates multi-turn training conversations. To rigorously evaluate generalization, we construct an out-of-distribution suite combining automated behavioral elicitation, established misalignment benchmarks, and natural benign conversations. Across 5 misaligned behaviors, our probes match a strong LLM judge with 0.935 AUROC on out-of-distribution benchmarks while keeping a low false positive rate on benign traffic. We further perform in-depth analysis to understand the probes and the model's internal representations of misalignment indicators.

AI.tldr: Proposes a misalignment monitoring framework using 18 cognitive-indicator probes on LLM activations, with a meta-plan driven data generator; achieves 0.935 AUROC on OOD benchmarks across 5 misaligned behaviors, with low benign false positives.
AI.motivation: Address growing misalignment risks by moving from global judgments to interpretable, fine-grained signals that can be monitored in real-time.
AI.method: Define 18 indicators of misalignment; train linear probes on internal activations; use a meta-plan-guided pipeline to generate multi-turn training conversations; evaluate on an out-of-distribution suite combining automated elicitation, misalignment benchmarks, and benign conversations.
AI.result: Probes match a strong LLM judge with AUROC 0.935 on OOD benchmarks; low FPR on benign traffic; detailed analysis of internal representations.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained, interpretable detection of misalignment is feasible and generalizable across misaligned behaviors; the approach offers a practical safety monitor with strong performance and interpretability, though its generalization to other models and potential data biases warrant further study.

title: Bengal-HP_RU: A Dataset of Bengal People For Head Pose Estimation
summary: Existing head pose datasets predominantly feature subjects of Western or East Asian origin, leaving South Asian populations, particularly Bengali individuals, largely underrepresented. We introduce Bengal-HP_RU, the first publicly available head pose dataset centred on Bengali subjects, comprising 12,894 labelled head images annotated with continuous yaw, pitch, and roll values. Images were collected from Wikimedia Commons under free licences and processed through an automated pipeline followed by manual label correction. The dataset is partitioned by Wikimedia uploader identity to prevent data contamination, yielding 10,494 training and 2,400 test images across 296 unique uploaders. Bengal-HP_RU exhibits substantial diversity in subject age, gender, occlusion, illumination, and background, reflecting realistic in-the-wild conditions. The dataset is publicly available at https://doi.org/10.17632/xbw9kr37jb.2.

AI.tldr: First publicly available Bengali head pose dataset (Bengal-HP_RU) with 12,894 labeled images and continuous yaw, pitch, and roll; 10,494 training and 2,400 test images across 296 uploaders; collected from Wikimedia Commons via an automated pipeline with manual label correction; publicly available at https://doi.org/10.17632/xbw9kr37jb.2.
AI.motivation: Address underrepresentation of Bengali/South Asian populations in head pose datasets to improve generalization and fairness of head pose estimation models.
AI.method: Automated collection from freely licensed Wikimedia Commons, automated pose annotation with manual correction; data partitioning by Wikimedia uploader identity to prevent data contamination between train and test sets.
AI.result: Dataset demonstrates substantial diversity in age, gender, occlusion, illumination, and background under real-world in-the-wild conditions; comprises 12,894 labeled images with continuous yaw/pitch/roll; public release with 10,494 train and 2,400 test samples across 296 uploaders.
AI.conclusion: Provides a demographic-diverse resource for head pose estimation, enabling more inclusive training and evaluation, and supporting fairer benchmarking for Bengali/South Asian populations.

title: Optimization-based Safe Trajectory Planning for Autonomous Ground Vehicle in Multi-Floor Scenarios
summary: The development of trajectory planning strategies for autonomous ground vehicles (AGVs) represents a prevailing research interest within the domain of intelligent transportation systems. This paper introduces a trajectory planning framework tailored for multi-floor scenarios. The framework consists of two main modules: the task planning module and the trajectory planning module. The task planning module involves a strategic selection phase, where a task planning strategy based on generalized voronoi diagrams (GVD) and multi-objective algorithms is proposed to select the floor exits for each floor. The trajectory planning module utilizes optimization-based methods to generate high-quality trajectories, and a warm-started hierarchical planning framework is designed to ensure rapid convergence. Additionally, for handling complex obstacle constraints, a correlation constraint calculation method is designed for reducing obstacle constraints in trajectory planning. Finally, the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed framework are verified through simulations.

AI.tldr: Two-module trajectory planning framework for multi-floor AGVs: GVD-based task planning for floor-exit selection and optimization-based trajectory planning with a warm-started hierarchical planner; includes a correlation constraint method to simplify obstacle handling; validated by simulations.
AI.motivation: Tackle autonomous navigation for multi-floor environments by decoupling task-level floor-exit selection from trajectory optimization, ensuring fast convergence and feasible, high-quality paths under complex obstacle constraints.
AI.method: Task planning uses generalized Voronoi diagrams (GVD) and multi-objective optimization to choose floor exits per floor. Trajectory planning uses optimization-based methods with a warm-started hierarchical planning framework to enable rapid convergence; introduces a correlation constraint calculation to reduce obstacle constraints in planning.
AI.result: Simulation-based verification shows the framework is feasible and effective, producing high-quality trajectories with rapid convergence due to warm starts and constraint reduction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-module framework provides a practical solution for multi-floor AGV trajectory planning, combining robust task selection with efficient trajectory optimization and obstacle handling; suitable for further refinement and real-world deployment.

title: Forget Without Compromise: Nexus Sampling for Streaming KV-Cache Eviction Under Fixed Budgets
summary: Long-context and agentic LLM workloads push the KV cache past any fixed memory budget, forcing the inference stack to permanently evict tokens at every step of a continuous-inference stream. Existing methods all share the same template, a per-step direct-attention score followed by deterministic top-$K$ selection, which converts a single below-cutoff step into an irreversible verdict and permanently erases any subtly important token that direct attention cannot single out from noise. To address this challenge, we propose Nexus Sampling, a training-free eviction method that pairs Nexus scoring, an iterative walk over direct attention that surfaces bridge tokens, with weighted reservoir sampling, which retains tokens with inclusion probability in place of deterministic top-$K$. Theoretically, we show that Nexus Sampling dominates deterministic top-$K$ in long-run survival of subtly important tokens. Empirically, at 80% KV cache eviction, Nexus Sampling matches dense attention within 1% on LongBench while outperforming top-$K$ baselines on retrieval-heavy tasks, with up to 10x smaller per-sequence cache memory.

AI.tldr: Nexus Sampling is a training-free eviction strategy for long-context LLM KV caches that probabilistically retains tokens via Nexus scoring (bridge-token discovery) and weighted reservoir sampling, outperforming fixed top-K eviction while using much less cache.
AI.motivation: Per-step deterministic top-K eviction discards subtly important tokens, causing irrecoverable loss in long-context inference when KV caches are heavily evicted; existing methods share a brittle token-selection template under memory pressure.
AI.method: Nexus Sampling combines two components: (1) Nexus scoring, an iterative walk over direct attention to surface bridge tokens that connect current queries with relevant past tokens; (2) weighted reservoir sampling, which retains tokens with inclusion probabilities rather than deterministic cutoff, enabling probabilistic preservation of useful tokens. The approach is training-free.
AI.result: Theoretically, Nexus Sampling dominates deterministic top-K in long-run survival of subtly important tokens. Empirically, with 80% KV cache eviction, it matches dense attention within 1% on LongBench and outperforms top-K baselines on retrieval-heavy tasks, with up to 10x reductions in per-sequence cache memory.
AI.conclusion: Nexus Sampling offers a memory-efficient eviction method that preserves long-horizon information better than fixed top-K policies, enhancing long-context LLM performance under heavy cache eviction. Potential caveats include computational cost of Nexus scoring and validation across diverse models/tasks; further work could explore scalability, robustness to different attention patterns, and integration with various KV-cache architectures.

title: Tractable Reasoning and Conjunctive Query Answering for Defeasible DL-Lite under Rational Closure
summary: In Description Logics (DLs), reasoning under Rational Closure (RC) is a well-known and widely accepted non-monotonic formalism to handle defeasible knowledge. In this paper, we study the application of RC to the core and horn variants of the DL-Lite family of lightweight description logics. We analyze both entitlement (instance checking) and Conjunctive Query (CQ) answering under RC. Our main contribution is providing a plug-in architecture that builds upon existing standard classical reasoners, establishing that reasoning and CQ answering under RC for DL-Lite can be done efficiently with minimal computational overhead.

AI.tldr: Applies Rational Closure (RC) to DL-Lite core/horn; introduces a plug-in architecture that leverages standard reasoners to support instance checking and conjunctive query (CQ) answering under RC, with efficient reasoning and minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: To enable non-monotonic defeasible reasoning in lightweight Description Logics, specifically DL-Lite, and make RC practical for common tasks like entitlement checks and CQ answering.
AI.method: Develop a plug-in architecture that integrates RC into existing classical reasoners for DL-Lite core and horn variants, and evaluate RC-based reasoning for entitlement (instance checking) and CQ answering.
AI.result: Reasoning and CQ answering under RC for DL-Lite can be performed efficiently with minimal computational overhead using the proposed plug-in approach.
AI.conclusion: RC is practically viable for DL-Lite; a plug-in framework allows leveraging standard reasoners with negligible overhead for both entitlement and CQ tasks.

title: Sat2City v2: Native 3D City Asset Generation from a Single Satellite Image
summary: Generating explicit 3D city assets from a single satellite image is important for digital twins, urban simulation, and geospatial intelligence. Unlike satellite-to-street-view synthesis, the task requires a reusable textured mesh with plausible geometry and controllable appearance rather than a 3D proxy optimized only for rendering a small set of images or videos. The ICCV Sat2City framework made a first step by conditioning cascaded sparse-voxel latent diffusion on satellite-derived height maps, but its appearance was random, its training data were synthetic, and its task-specific VAE did not scale well to noisy real-world reconstructions. We present Sat2City v2, a journal extension that adapts a pretrained native structured-latent 3D foundation model to weakly aligned satellite images and textured meshes. We build a real-world dataset with 16,241 satellite-mesh pairs across 24 regions in 9 cities. Instead of learning a 3D representation from noisy city meshes, Sat2City v2 encodes each mesh into a pretrained native 3D latent space, fine-tunes a satellite-conditioned geometry flow, and uses the decoded shape to anchor satellite-conditioned texturing. This retains Sat2City's geometry-to-appearance cascade while enabling appearance-controllable generation from the satellite input. Experiments on metric-scale DSM reconstruction and generative city-asset benchmarks for geometry and appearance show that Sat2City v2 achieves the best overall performance among evaluated baselines. Overall, Sat2City v2 advances satellite-to-city generation from rendering-oriented 3D proxies to explicit textured mesh assets, supported by, to the best of our knowledge, the first documented satellite-mesh paired dataset collected from matched geographic crops for this asset-level task. Project page: https://ai4city-hkust.github.io/Sat2City-v2/

AI.tldr: Sat2City v2 extends prior work by using a pretrained 3D latent model to convert weakly aligned satellite imagery into explicit textured meshes, with a real-world satellite-mesh dataset; it achieves state-of-the-art performance on geometry and appearance benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Create reusable, controllable, textured 3D city assets from satellite images for digital twins and urban analysis, addressing the gap between geometry and appearance and the noise of real-world data.
AI.method: Encode each mesh into a pretrained native 3D latent space; fine-tune a satellite-conditioned geometry flow; decode shape to anchor satellite-conditioned texturing; retain geometry→appearance cascade; train on a real-world dataset of 16,241 satellite-mesh pairs across 24 regions in 9 cities; handle weakly aligned satellite inputs and provide controllable appearance.
AI.result: Sat2City v2 achieves the best overall performance among evaluated baselines on metric-scale DSM reconstruction and generative city-asset benchmarks for both geometry and appearance.
AI.conclusion: Advances satellite-to-city generation from rendering-oriented proxies to explicit textured mesh assets, backed by the first documented satellite-mesh paired dataset for this asset-level task.

title: Beyond Monotonic Progress: Retry-Supervised Value Learning for Robot Imitation
summary: Human demonstrations for robot imitation learning often contain mistakes and corrective behaviors, such as imprecise grasps, object misalignment, unstable contact, and repeated attempts. While these segments are commonly treated as noisy or suboptimal data, they provide valuable evidence about when execution deviates from a desirable path and how task feasibility can be restored. However, existing reward and value models often rely on monotonic progress assumptions, which capture coarse task advancement but may overlook local execution errors and corrective behaviors in imperfect demonstrations. In this work, we propose ReTVL (ReTry-Supervised Value Learning), a framework for learning mistake-sensitive value functions from mixed-quality robot demonstrations by leveraging retry events as sparse supervision. ReTVL captures the local degradation-and-recovery structure around mistakes by combining global progress calibration with local pairwise preference learning induced by sparsely annotated retry keypoints. The learned value model is then used to reweight demonstration chunks for downstream behavior cloning, reducing the influence of harmful execution errors while preserving useful corrective behaviors. Experiments on real-robot manipulation tasks show that ReTVL produces more fine-grained value estimates than progress-based baselines and improves imitation learning from imperfect demonstrations.

AI.tldr: ReTVL learns mistake-sensitive value functions from mixed-quality robot demonstrations by leveraging retry events as sparse supervision to reweight demonstration chunks during imitation learning.
AI.motivation: Current reward/value models rely on monotonic progress assumptions and overlook local execution errors and corrective behaviors found in imperfect demonstrations; such mistakes contain valuable signals about task feasibility and recovery.
AI.method: Propose ReTVL, a framework that combines global progress calibration with local pairwise preference learning using sparsely annotated retry keypoints to learn value functions from mistakes and recoveries; use the learned value model to reweight demonstration chunks for behavior cloning.
AI.result: Experiments on real-robot manipulation tasks show ReTVL yields more fine-grained value estimates than progress-based baselines and improves imitation learning from imperfect demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: Mistake-sensitive value estimation via retry-based supervision enhances robustness to imperfect demonstrations and more effectively harnesses corrective behavior for imitation learning.

title: 3D Masked Autoencoders are Robust Learners of Volumetric and Multimodal Cellular Representations for Microscopy
comment: Accepted at MICCAI 2026. Code available at: https://github.com/marrlab/mae3d-opencell
summary: Self-supervised learning in fluorescence microscopy often relies on 2D projections, despite the inherently three-dimensional nature of cells. We present a systematic comparison of 2D and 3D masked autoencoders (MAE-2D vs. MAE-3D) on volumetric microscopy data. Under matched architectures and training protocols, MAE-3D consistently outperforms 2D max-projection and slice-based variants on downstream single-cell tasks. We further align visual representations with a pretrained protein language model (ESM2) and show that cross-modal supervision yields larger gains for volumetric models. Channel cross-attention and frequency-domain regularization are critical for leveraging 3D spatial context. On a protein--protein interaction task, MAE-3D achieves a ROC--AUC of 0.865, outperforming prior methods by up to +0.025. For protein localization, our best 3D model attains state-of-the-art AUC$_{\text{micro}}$ (0.952) and F1$_{\text{micro}}$ (0.742), improving over previous approaches by +0.003 and +0.010 absolute, respectively. Overall, these results demonstrate the advantages of native 3D modeling and multimodal alignment for representation learning in single-cell microscopy.

AI.tldr: 3D masked autoencoders (MAE-3D) outperform 2D projections in volumetric fluorescence microscopy; with cross-modal supervision to a pretrained protein language model (ESM2), channel cross-attention, and frequency-domain regularization, 3D models achieve state-of-the-art results on protein interaction and localization tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the intrinsic 3D nature of cells in fluorescence microscopy and evaluate whether native 3D representation learning (MAE-3D) provides advantages over 2D projections, and whether cross-modal alignment with protein-language models enhances performance.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of MAE-2D (slice or max-projection variants) vs MAE-3D under matched architectures and training protocols on volumetric microscopy data. Incorporates channel cross-attention and frequency-domain regularization. Evaluates downstream tasks: protein–protein interaction (ROC-AUC) and protein localization (AUC micro, F1 micro). Explores cross-modal supervision by aligning learned visuals with a pretrained protein language model (ESM2).
AI.result: MAE-3D consistently outperforms 2D variants on downstream single-cell tasks. PPI task achieves ROC-AUC 0.865, up by 0.025. Protein localization achieves AUC micro 0.952 and F1 micro 0.742, up by 0.003 and 0.010 respectively. Cross-modal supervision yields larger gains for volumetric models, indicating improved representation alignment with protein language models.
AI.conclusion: Native 3D modeling coupled with multimodal alignment substantially improves representation learning in single-cell microscopy, validating the advantage of volumetric approaches for tasks requiring 3D context.

title: LemonHarness Technical Report
summary: As large language model (LLM) agents are applied to longer tasks, they increasingly modify workspace state across multiple rounds of iteration. However, agents typically observe only tool outputs and log fragments, while the actual state changes occur in the file system. Without explicit workspace boundaries, state-changing operations such as file writes and temporary artifact generation may scatter changes across paths. Over time, these weakly constrained changes accumulate, making states such as modified files difficult to track. This paper presents LemonHarness, an integrated execution framework for long-horizon agents. LemonHarness establishes an explicit execution boundary by constraining state-changing operations within a clearly defined workspace and bringing model invocation, tool execution, and rule knowledge within a single controlled boundary. State-changing operations, including file writes, dependency installation, and temporary artifact creation, are executed through structured tool interfaces, with execution feedback recorded as observations available to subsequent model decisions. The system also introduces a reusable rule knowledge base, which turns recurring execution rules and acceptance criteria into runtime knowledge. LemonHarness further adds a time-aware execution mechanism that exposes elapsed and remaining budget to the model, so it can rebalance exploration, implementation, and validation effort as time pressure shifts and avoid timeouts from long waits or excessive verification. On Terminal-Bench 2.0, LemonHarness_GPT-5.3-CodeX reached 84.49% accuracy over 445 trials; pairing the same framework with the stronger GPT-5.5 backbone raised the average accuracy to 86.52% across five jobs. The results suggest that a unified runtime boundary, callable rule knowledge, and time-aware execution can improve the stability of long-horizon agent execution.

AI.tldr: LemonHarness provides a unified runtime boundary, rule knowledge base, and time-aware execution to stabilize long-horizon LLM agent execution, achieving improved accuracy in benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents lack explicit workspace boundaries; state changes occur in the file system; need controlled boundary, structured tool interfaces, and time budgeting to track and constrain effects of actions.
AI.method: Introduce LemonHarness: explicit workspace boundary that confines state-changing ops; unify model invocation, tool execution, and rule knowledge within a controlled boundary; use structured tool interfaces for writes, installs, and temp artifacts; maintain a reusable rule knowledge base; implement time-aware execution exposing elapsed/remaining budget; experiments on Terminal-Bench 2.0 with GPT-5.3-CodeX and GPT-5.5 backbone.
AI.result: LemonHarness_GPT-5.3-CodeX achieved 84.49% accuracy over 445 trials; with GPT-5.5 backbone, 86.52% average accuracy across five jobs.
AI.conclusion: A unified runtime boundary, callable rule knowledge, and time-aware execution improve stability of long-horizon agent execution and can enhance performance on long tasks.

title: Geometry-Aware Style Transfer in 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: In this paper, we present a novel geometry-aware style transfer framework for 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) that simultaneously transfers appearance attributes and geometric structures. Unlike prior works that primarily focus on color-based stylization and often overlook structural adaptation, our method explicitly incorporates geometry adaptation through a decoupled optimization scheme that alternately updates color and geometry parameters. This strategy alleviates potential interference between color and geometry updates, leading to stable and consistent scene-level geometry transformation. The decoupled optimization is enabled by the proposed geometry-aware contrastive feature matching (GCFM). GCFM integrates RGB, depth, and edge cues into a contrastive objective and is employed in both optimization phases to effectively transfer structural characteristics from style images to Gaussian primitives. Extensive experiments show that our approach achieves superior performance in both qualitative fidelity and quantitative metrics, significantly outperforming existing 3DGS-based stylization methods. Our code is available at \href{https://github.com/oweixx/gast}{https://github.com/oweixx/gast}.

AI.tldr: A geometry-aware style transfer framework for 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) that decouples color and geometry optimization via geometry-aware contrastive feature matching (GCFM), achieving improved geometry and appearance stylization.
AI.motivation: Current 3D style transfer methods primarily emphasize color-based stylization and often neglect geometric adaptation, risking inconsistent or incorrect scene structure after stylization.
AI.method: Introduce a decoupled optimization scheme that alternates updates of color and geometry parameters. The core component is geometry-aware contrastive feature matching (GCFM), which fuses RGB, depth, and edge cues into a contrastive objective and is used in both optimization phases to transfer structural characteristics from style images to Gaussian primitives.
