AI.tldr: Reframe test-time canonicalization as out-of-distribution (OOD) detection to robustify vision models against affine transformations, evaluating many OOD scores and search algorithms, and introducing a gated mechanism to avoid harming in-distribution accuracy; best results come from distance-based scores with random search and local refinement, with code provided.
AI.motivation: Vision models misclassify inputs under affine transforms (rotation, scale, shear) despite unchanged class; canonicalization methods exist but are restricted by their scoring functions and optimizers, necessitating a broader exploration and a practical, non-disruptive approach.
AI.method: Treat canonicalization as energy minimization over transformations using any OOD score as the energy. Systematically evaluate ~20 OOD scores and 9 search algorithms across diverse datasets (handwritten characters, sketches, natural images, 3D point clouds). Implement a gated mechanism to apply canonicalization only when the input appears OOD, preserving in-distribution accuracy.
AI.result: Across benchmarks, distance-based OOD scores combined with random search and local refinement yielded the best overall performance. The gated mechanism preserved most in-distribution accuracy while delivering robustness gains on transformed inputs.
AI.conclusion: Framing canonicalization as OOD detection with flexible scoring and optimization strategies is effective and broadly applicable; it enables robust, test-time correction without retraining and without sacrificing in-distribution accuracy, with publicly available code.

title: Compact Object-Level Representations with Open-Vocabulary Understanding for Indoor Visual Relocalization
summary: Indoor visual relocalization plays a critical role in emerging spatial and embodied AI applications. However, prior research was predominantly devoted to low-level vision schemes, struggling to perceive scene semantics and compositions, which limits both interpretability and applicability. In this paper, we explore the issue of how to organize rich object information in a scene, including semantics, layout, and geometry, into a structured map representation, thereby utilizing object units exclusively to drive the camera relocalization task. To this end, we propose OpenReLoc, a camera relocalization system designed to provide scene understanding and accurate pose estimation capabilities. Leveraging recent foundation models, we first introduce a multi-modal mechanism to integrate open-vocabulary semantic knowledge for effective 2D-3D object matching. Additionally, we design object-oriented reference frames as position priors, paired with a reference frame selection strategy based on the Distance-IoU (DIOU), enabling extension to scalable scenes. Moreover, to ensure stable and accurate pose optimization, we also propose a dual-path 2D Iterative Closest Pixel loss guided by object shape. Experimental results demonstrate that OpenReLoc achieves superior relocalization recall and accuracy across various datasets. Our source code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: An object-centric indoor relocalization system, OpenReLoc, that uses open-vocabulary semantics and object-level frames to achieve accurate pose estimation; introduces a 2D-3D object matching pipeline, DIOU-based frame selection, and a dual-path ICP loss guided by object shape; shows improved recall/accuracy and will release code.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of low-level vision methods that lack semantics and interpretability; desire to leverage semantic- and geometry-rich object-level representations for scalable indoor localization.
AI.method: OpenReLoc integrates foundation-model-based open-vocabulary semantic knowledge for robust 2D-3D object matching; defines object-oriented reference frames as priors; uses a Distance-IoU (DIOU) based reference frame selection strategy for scalability; employs a dual-path 2D Iterative Closest Pixel (ICP) loss guided by object shapes to stabilize pose optimization; operates on object units to drive relocalization.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple indoor datasets show that OpenReLoc achieves superior relocalization recall and accuracy, demonstrating effective integration of open-vocabulary semantics and object-centric pose optimization.
AI.conclusion: The study validates an object-centric, semantically informed approach to indoor relocalization, offering improved interpretability, scalability, and performance; code release is planned upon acceptance.

title: Information-Theoretic Classifier-Free Guidance with Adaptive Schedule Optimization
summary: Diffusion models have achieved strong performance in image, text-to-image, and video generation, where conditional generation is often controlled by classifier-free guidance (CFG). CFG improves condition consistency by increasing a guidance weight, but stronger guidance typically reduces diversity and distributional coverage. It remains unclear how this consistency-coverage trade-off should be controlled across the reverse trajectory, since the distribution induced by CFG is not simply the fixed-time tilted distribution given by the guided score field. To address this issue, we propose an information-theoretic framework for CFG schedule optimization. Our approach uses a clean endpoint reference to specify the desired consistency-coverage trade-off, while optimizing the actual distribution induced by the guided sampler toward this reference. We derive trajectory-level formulas to estimate the objective from samples and score evaluations, avoiding explicit density estimation. On ImageNet-512 with EDM-XXL and COCO with SD-XL, the learned schedules achieve competitive or improved trade-offs over constant guidance and allocate guidance selectively across noise levels.

AI.tldr: Information-theoretic CFG schedule optimization for diffusion models; end-point reference guides trajectory-level consistency-coverage trade-off; achieves better or competitive trade-offs on ImageNet-512 (EDM-XXL) and COCO (SD-XL) by distributing guidance across noise levels.
AI.motivation: CFG improves condition consistency by increasing guidance weight but harms diversity/coverage; the trade-off must be controlled across the reverse trajectory since the guided distribution is not simply the fixed-time tilted score.
AI.method: Introduce an information-theoretic objective with a clean end-point reference to specify the desired trade-off; optimize the actual distribution induced by the guided sampler toward this reference. Derive trajectory-level estimators from samples and score evaluations, avoiding explicit density estimation; results include learned CFG schedules that allocate guidance selectively across noise levels.
AI.result: On ImageNet-512 with EDM-XXL and COCO with SD-XL, the learned CFG schedules achieve competitive or improved consistency-coverage trade-offs compared to constant guidance and allocate guidance across noise levels adaptively.
AI.conclusion: A principled, scalable CFG scheduling framework that aligns the generated distribution with a user-defined trade-off, improving coverage where needed while maintaining consistency; demonstrated on large-scale image and vision-language tasks; potential for broader diffusion-model applications.

title: Cycle-Consistent Neural Explanation of Formal Verification Certificates
summary: Formal verification produces machine-checkable certificates that attest to the satisfaction or violation of temporal properties, yet these certificates remain opaque to non-specialist stakeholders. We propose a cycle-consistent neural architecture that generates faithful natural language explanations of verification certificates. A forward network NN1 maps certificates to explanations, and an inverse network NN2 reconstructs certificates from explanations; a symbolic verifier closes the loop, providing a differentiable faithfulness proxy. A pointer-generator mechanism ensures lexical grounding by copying state names directly from the certificate. We evaluate on 420 test certificates spanning six verification methods (bounded proof, k-induction, inductive invariant, lasso, reachability, witness pair) in both YES and NO verdict variants, drawn from a financial compliance domain with 207 named states. Our trained architecture, combined with a hybrid inference-time routing strategy, achieves 90.0% cycle-verified soundness, surpassing a multi- LLM few-shot baseline (76.1% for the best of 16 LLM combinations across four frontier models) by 13.9 percentage points. The neural model wins on 10 of 12 verdict/kind categories, with three categories reaching 100% soundness. The architecture offers 860x faster inference (185 ms vs. 160 s per certificate for the full multi-LLM baseline), offline operation, deterministic outputs, and zero per-inference cost. These results demonstrate that trained specialization outperforms general-purpose LLM prompting for structured certificate explanation, while eliminating the deployment constraints of cloud-based inference.

AI.tldr: Cycle-consistent neural architecture translates formal verification certificates into faithful natural-language explanations and regenerates certificates from explanations, using a differentiable faithfulness proxy and lexical grounding, enabling fast offline explanations that outperform multi-LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: Make machine-checkable verification certificates understandable to non-specialists while ensuring explanations faithfully reflect the certificates; reduce deployment constraints and latency.
AI.method: Forward NN1 maps certificates to explanations; inverse NN2 reconstructs certificates from explanations; a symbolic verifier provides a differentiable faithfulness proxy; a pointer-generator copies state names from the certificate; hybrid inference-time routing; evaluated on 420 certificates across six verification methods and two verdict variants in a financial domain with 207 named states.
AI.result: Achieves 90.0% cycle-verified soundness, outperforming the best multi-LLM baseline by 13.9 percentage points (76.1%), wins on 10 of 12 verdict/kind categories, with 3 categories at 100% soundness; 860x faster inference (185 ms vs. 160 s for the LLM baseline); offline operation, deterministic outputs, zero per-inference cost.
AI.conclusion: Structured, specialized neural explainer can surpass generic LLM prompting for explaining formal verification certificates, while avoiding cloud-based deployment constraints; cycle-consistency with a symbolic verifier and lexical grounding are key to high-fidelity explanations.

title: Deep Learning Approaches for 3D Medical Scene Completion: From Geometric Modeling to Generative Paradigms
summary: Three-dimensional scene completion has evolved as a major problem in computer vision and robotics, and its applications are diverse, including autonomous navigation and augmented reality. In this study, a systematic review has been conducted to compile the research contributions made in the last ten years, i.e., 2016 to 2026, which has revolutionized the field from the voxel semantic completion paradigm represented by SSCNet to the latest paradigm that combines generative diffusion priors with real-time rendering using a Gaussian splatting technique. The evolution in representation paradigms, such as voxel grids, point learning, implicit neural fields, transformer networks, diffusion networks, and the latest paradigm based on rendering-aware 3D Gaussian primitives, has been discussed in this study. A comprehensive analysis has been carried out on the contributions made in the last ten years, and a taxonomy has been developed to provide a clear idea about the contributions made in the field. The study has also discussed the research contributions made in the field, along with the challenges that still need to be addressed. Finally, the study has presented a research agenda that will provide a clear idea about the directions that can be followed in the development of the next-generation system

AI.tldr: Systematic review shows evolution from voxel-based SSCNet to diffusion-prior, rendering-aware 3D Gaussian primitives; taxonomy and roadmap for next-gen 3D scene completion.
AI.motivation: To synthesize a decade of research (2016–2026) on 3D scene completion, capturing representation shifts and guiding future work.
AI.method: Comprehensive literature review, taxonomy construction, cross-paradigm analysis of representations (voxels, points, implicit fields, transformers, diffusion networks, Gaussian splatting); synthesis of challenges and agenda.
AI.result: Provides a unified chronology and taxonomy of 3D scene completion methods; highlights performance and complexity trends; identifies open challenges (data, generalization, real-time rendering, integration of priors); proposes a research agenda.
AI.conclusion: Next-generation systems should blend generative priors with efficient rendering using rendering-aware 3D Gaussian primitives, and prioritize real-time performance and robustness across scenes.

title: RoPE-Aware Bit Allocation for KV-Cache Quantization
summary: Existing low-bit KV-cache quantizers often treat each cached key as a flat vector. Under RoPE, however, a key's contribution to a future attention logit decomposes into a position-dependent sum over two-dimensional frequency blocks. This makes key-cache quantization a block-wise bit-allocation problem: high-energy RoPE blocks are more sensitive to quantization error and should receive more bits. We introduce Block-GTQ, a RoPE-aware bit allocator for key-cache quantization built on TurboQuant-MSE(TQ-MSE). For each layer and KV head, Block-GTQ computes a label-free energy score for each RoPE block and greedily allocates integer bit widths by marginal gain. Under matched K/V bit budgets, Block-GTQ better preserves RoPE query-key logits on a ten-model diagnostic panel, cutting per-layer MAE by 32-80% at 2 and 3 b/dim K-only quantization and winning all 367/367 layer comparisons against uniform TQ-MSE. These fidelity gains translate to stronger downstream long-context retrieval, understanding, and reasoning. At K2V2 on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Block-GTQ raises the six-task NIAH average from 70.6 to 97.4, and the LongBench-EN average from 36.87 to 53.31. On AIME 2024/2025 with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, without an fp16 recent-key buffer, Block-GTQ at K3V2 scores 51.7/37.5, close to fp16's 54.2/37.9, whereas uniform TQ-MSE collapses to 0.0/0.0. We further implement a packed-cache serving path. On a single H800 GPU with Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, packed K3V3 achieves 3.24x KV-cache compression with fp16-comparable quality, runs 1.34x faster than fp16 FlashAttention2 at 128K context, reduces peak memory from 56.31 GB to 19.85 GB, and remains feasible at 256K and 512K where fp16 OOMs. Code is available at https://github.com/JIA-Lab-research/blockgtq.

AI.tldr: Block-GTQ offers RoPE-aware, block-wise bit allocation for key-cache quantization, improving fidelity and downstream performance, with significant compression and speedups plus an open-source implementation.
AI.motivation: Under RoPE, a key's contribution to attention logits decomposes into a position-dependent sum over 2D frequency blocks. Treating keys as flat vectors ignores this structure, making uniform low-bit quantization suboptimal.
AI.method: Block-GTQ computes a label-free energy score per RoPE block for each layer and KV head, then greedily assigns integer bit widths by marginal gain. It builds on TurboQuant-MSE (TQ-MSE), and includes a packed-cache serving path. Evaluation spans a ten-model diagnostic panel, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, and AIME 2024/2025 setups with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B; hardware experiments on H800 GPU with Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct; code release.
AI.result: Better preserve RoPE query-key logits (lower MAE by 32–80% under 2–3 b/dim K-only quantization; outperforms uniform TQ-MSE in all 367/367 layer comparisons). Downstream gains: improved long-context retrieval, understanding, and reasoning (NIAH average 70.6→97.4; LongBench-EN 36.87→53.31 on K2V2/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct). In DeepSeek-Qwen-7B setups (AIME 2024/25) with no fp16 recent-key buffer, K3V2 scores near fp16 (51.7/37.5 vs 54.2/37.9). Packed K3V3 achieves 3.24x KV-cache compression with fp16-like quality, 1.34x speedup over FlashAttention2 at 128K context, and memory reduced from 56.31 GB to 19.85 GB, scalable to 256K/512K contexts where fp16 OOMs occur.
AI.conclusion: RoPE-aware, block-wise bit allocation (Block-GTQ) yields substantial fidelity and practical benefits for KV-cache quantization, enabling high compression with maintained or improved downstream performance; code provided for reproducibility.

title: Agentic AI for Bilevel Long-Term Optimization of Policy-Driven Physical Layer Systems
summary: Network operators' changing policies, service requirements, and stringent real-time constraints render existing methods designed with fixed objectives and constraints ineffective. This paper presents Agentic long-term performance optimization (Agentic-LTPO), a nested bilevel optimization framework that can be applied to adaptive physical layer problem configuration. The key idea is to employ agentic AI to generate upper-level configurations in a bilevel optimization structure, where evolving operator policies, environment summaries, and historical experiences are translated into structured lower-level optimization problem configurations. The lower level solves the problems with updated configurations for real-time physical-layer decisions. Considering cell-free MIMO beamforming as a use case, we embody Agentic-LTPO by designing a new multi-agent decision process with retrieval-augmented experience-based verification in the upper level, together with a closed-form beamformer in the lower level. Experiments demonstrate that Agentic-LTPO exhibits strong adaptability to dynamic operator policies and effectively enhances the system's long-term performance by 57.2% compared to traditional methods.

AI.tldr: Agentic-LTPO is a nested bilevel optimization framework that uses agentic AI to generate upper-level configurations for adaptive physical layer problems, combining retrieval-augmented, experience-based upper-level verification with a closed-form lower-level beamformer; demonstrated on cell-free MIMO beamforming with 57.2% long-term performance gain over traditional methods.
AI.motivation: To address dynamic operator policies, changing service requirements, and stringent real-time constraints that degrade fixed-objective optimization methods.
AI.method: Nested bilevel optimization; upper level uses agentic AI to propose configurations based on evolving operator policies, environment summaries, and historical experiences; lower level resolves updated configurations in real-time with a closed-form beamformer in cell-free MIMO; upper level includes a multi-agent decision process with retrieval-augmented verification.
AI.result: Experimentally shows strong adaptability to policy dynamics and a 57.2% improvement in long-term performance versus traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: Agentic-LTPO provides a practical framework for adaptive long-term optimization in dynamic communications environments, with potential applicability to other physical-layer problems beyond cell-free MIMO.

title: Towards Fast and Effective Long Video Understanding of Multimodal Large Language Models via Adaptive Quasi-Gaussian Sampling
summary: Long video understanding remains a daunting challenge for \emph{Multimodal Large Language Models} (MLLMs) due to the excessive computation and memory footprint. Thus, \emph{keyframe selection} is often adopted to mitigate this shortcoming, which however still suffers from low flexibility and high noise due to its hard sampling principle. In this paper, we define video frame selection as a problem of \emph{Quasi-Gaussian Sampling}, and propose an adaptive and training-free approach termed \textbf{\emph{AdaQ}}. Inspired by the $3$-$σ$ rule of Gaussian distribution, the objective of AdaQ is to achieve the optimal $3$-$σ$ interval for different examples, \emph{i.e.}, a smaller $3$-$σ$ interval for the local query and a larger one for the global query, thereby facilitating robust and adaptive frame sampling. To validate AdaQ, we apply it to four MLLMs with three embedding models. The extensive experimental results not only show its obvious performance gains over the default MLLMs and the SOTA keyframe selection methods, \emph{e.g.}, helping Qwen3-VL-8B outperform GPT4o by 15.8\% on average by using only 64 frames, but also confirm its superior robustness and high efficiency for long-video understanding, \emph{e.g.}, \textbf{only 1 hyper-parameter} needs to be set. \textbf{Our code project} is given at \href{https://github.com/Zkayovo-xmu/AdaQ}{https://github.com/Zkayovo-xmu/AdaQ}.

AI.tldr: AdaQ introduces adaptive quasi-Gaussian sampling for long-video frame selection, using a 3-sigma interval that adapts between local and global queries. It is training-free with only one hyper-parameter, and yields significant performance gains across multiple MLLMs and embeddings.
AI.motivation: Long videos challenge MLLMs due to heavy computation and memory costs; existing keyframe selection is rigid (hard sampling) and noisy. A flexible, robust, low-cost sampling strategy is needed.
AI.method: Define frame selection as quasi-Gaussian sampling. Use a 3-σ interval that adapts per example: smaller for local queries, larger for global queries. Training-free approach; validated across four MLLMs and three embedding models.
AI.result: AdaQ achieves clear performance gains over default MLLMs and state-of-the-art keyframe methods. Demonstrates robustness and efficiency (e.g., Qwen3-VL-8B beats GPT4o by 15.8% on average using only 64 frames; only one hyper-parameter to tune).
AI.conclusion: AdaQ provides an effective, lightweight solution for long-video understanding by adaptive, training-free frame sampling, reducing computation without sacrificing accuracy; code released.

title: Rapid FinFET Modelling Using an Autoencoder
summary: This work presents a machine learning framework that leverages an autoencoder (AE) for the efficient modeling of FinFET. We first calibrated a BSIM-CMG model to generate a dataset of current-voltage (ID-VG) characteristics. This data was used to train an autoencoder that compresses full I-V curves into a low-dimensional latent space, which intrinsically encodes key device physics. A key innovation is the explicit incorporation of parameter such as drain to source voltage (VDS) as an input feature, enhancing the model ability to capture bias dependent variation. The trained model successfully reconstructs full I-V curves and directly extracts critical device metrics including threshold voltage (VTH), subthreshold slope (SS), and peak transconductance (gm). This approach demonstrates that data driven compact models, built from actual characterization data, can achieve high accuracy with minimal training data, providing a powerful tool for rapid device characterization, modelling and circuit level simulation.

AI.tldr: An autoencoder-based, VDS-aware compact model for FinFETs trained on BSIM-CMG-generated ID-VG data to reconstruct curves and extract VTH, SS, gm with minimal data.
AI.motivation: Need fast, physics-informed data-driven compact models that capture bias-dependent variation and support rapid device characterization and circuit-level simulation.
AI.method: Calibrate BSIM-CMG to produce ID-VG datasets across VDS; train an autoencoder to compress full I-V curves into a low-dimensional latent space, explicitly including VDS as an input feature; reconstruct full I-V curves from latent; directly extract VTH, SS, and gm from the latent representation or reconstruction.
AI.result: Successful reconstruction of full I-V curves and direct extraction of VTH, SS, and gm; high accuracy achieved with limited training data; demonstrates a data-driven route for rapid device characterization, modeling, and circuit-level simulation.
AI.conclusion: Shows that data-driven compact models built from real characterization data can achieve high accuracy with minimal data, enabling efficient device characterization and faster integration into circuit design workflows.

title: Can Aggregate Invariants Accelerate Continuous Subgraph Matching? Limits, Laws, and a Dynamic Spectral Index
summary: Spectral filtering recently delivered substantial pruning for \emph{static} subgraph matching: Laplacian interlacing rejects candidates whose neighborhoods cannot host the query. We study whether such aggregate structural tests can accelerate \emph{continuous} subgraph matching (CSM) over dynamic graphs, and answer in three parts. First, lazily maintained spectral bounds are infeasible exactly where spectral pruning has value: we characterize the tightest safe rule over a formalized perturbation relaxation and show that even it loses essentially all pruning power within four touching updates. Second, exact maintenance is affordable when selective: pruning utility and recomputation cost are anti-correlated across vertices -- hubs provably never prune -- so recomputing small-neighborhood spectra on touch sustains exact local spectra at microseconds per update, complete by construction. Third, integrated into a decoupled CSM benchmark against an identical-minus-spectra control, the tests remove up to $51\%$ of candidates or safely skip up to $47\%$ of update enumerations, yet enumeration intermediates remain unchanged -- beyond the gates' skipped first-level bindings, typically zero -- across two engines, four real graphs, two stream types, and $77$ solved queries; a constructed radius-stratified workload confirms the instrument detects the exception when one exists ($-99.9\%$ intermediates, $748\times$ faster). Aggregate tests accelerate what scales with candidate sets -- construction, list scans -- never adjacency-guided exploration. We distill an intermediate-invariance methodology for evaluating CSM filters and release a reusable dynamic local-spectra index.

AI.tldr: Extends spectral pruning from static subgraph matching to continuous subgraph matching (CSM) on dynamic graphs. Lazily maintained bounds lose pruning power quickly; selective exact maintenance remains feasible and effective. Empirical evaluation shows notable pruning and update-skipping gains; however, many intermediates stay unchanged. Proposes an intermediate-invariance evaluation methodology for CSM filters and releases a dynamic local-spectra index.
AI.motivation: Determine whether aggregate spectral tests can accelerate CSM on dynamic graphs, and identify when and how spectral pruning remains valuable under graph updates.
AI.method: Formalize a perturbation-relaxation safe-pruning rule; compare lazily maintained versus exact spectral maintenance, with hubs analyzed as non-pruning; compute spectra on small neighborhoods with microsecond costs; integrate into a decoupled CSM benchmark against a spectra-removed control; use radius-stratified workloads and multiple engines/graphs/streams; measure pruning, update enumeration, and intermediates.
AI.result: Lazily maintained spectral bounds lose pruning power soon after updates (within four touching updates). Exact, selective maintenance is affordable and preserves pruning ability; hubs never prune; recomputing small-neighborhood spectra yields microsecond per-update costs. In benchmarks, aggregate tests prune up to 51% of candidates and safely skip up to 47% of update enumerations, with enumeration intermediates largely unchanged. A constructed workload shows exceptional gains (up to 748× speedup and −99.9% fewer intermediates). Overall, aggregation tests accelerate candidate-dominated steps but not adjacency-guided exploration; an intermediate-invariance methodology and a reusable dynamic local-spectra index are proposed/released.
AI.conclusion: Aggregate spectral tests can accelerate components of CSM that scale with candidate sets. A principled evaluation framework is proposed (intermediate-invariance) and a dynamic local-spectra index is released to support future work.

title: Co-occurring associated retained concepts in Diffusion Unlearning
comment: Accepted as a poster at ICLR 2026. Code available at https://github.com/damilab/CARE
summary: Unlearning has emerged as a key technique to mitigate harmful content generation in diffusion models. However, existing methods often remove not only the target concept, but also benign co-occurring concepts. As illustrated in Fig.1, unlearning nudity can unintentionally suppress the concept of person, preventing a model from generating images with person. We define these undesirably suppressed co-occurring concepts that must be preserved CARE (Co-occurring Associated REtained concepts). Then, we introduce the CARE score, a general metric that directly quantifies their preservation across unlearning tasks. With this foundation, we propose ReCARE (Robust erasure for CARE), a framework that explicitly safeguards CARE while erasing only the target concept. ReCARE automatically constructs the CARE-set, a curated vocabulary of benign co-occurring tokens extracted from target images, and leverages this vocabulary during training for stable unlearning. Extensive experiments across various target concepts (Nudity, Van Gogh style, and Tench object) demonstrate that ReCARE achieves overall state-of-the-art performance in balancing robust concept erasure, overall utility, and CARE preservation.

AI.tldr: A framework (ReCARE) for robust erasure in diffusion models that preserves co-occurring benign concepts (CARE) while removing a target concept, using a CARE score and an automatically built CARE-set to stabilize unlearning; achieves state-of-the-art balance across erasure, utility, and CARE preservation.
AI.motivation: Unlearning methods often erase not only the target concept but also benign co-occurring concepts, degrading generation quality and controllability. There is a need to quantify and preserve co-occurring associated concepts during unlearning.
AI.method: Introduce CARE score to quantify preservation of co-occurring concepts. Propose ReCARE, which automatically constructs a CARE-set from target images and uses it during training to safeguard CARE while erasing the target concept. Evaluation across targets (Nudity, Van Gogh style, Tench object) to assess erasure, utility, and CARE preservation.
AI.result: ReCARE achieves state-of-the-art performance in balancing robust erasure, overall utility, and CARE preservation across multiple target concepts, outperforming baselines on preserving benign co-occurring concepts while removing the target.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly safeguarding CARE during unlearning is feasible and beneficial. A data-driven CARE-set and the CARE score enable stable, targeted erasure without unnecessarily degrading unrelated concepts, enabling safer diffusion-model unlearning.

title: RAVEN: A Regime-Aware Variable-context Expert Network for Financial Time Series Forecasting
summary: Financial time series forecasting presents structural challenges absent from standard benchmarks. Log-returns are non-stationary, exhibit exceptionally low signal-to-noise (SNR) ratios, and are governed by regime-dependent temporal dependencies. We identify a key limitation of state-of-the-art (SOTA) time series models in financial settings. A fixed context window is mismatched to the time-varying optimal look-back of non-stationary price processes. We propose the Regime-Aware Variable-context Expert Network (RAVEN), a Mixture-of-Experts framework designed to adaptively determine the temporal context for each input sample. Instead of relying on a fixed look-back horizon, RAVEN constructs a hierarchy of nested contiguous windows whose lengths are determined by the data itself. Specifically, RAVEN scores patches by learned importance in reverse chronological order and applies the Cumulative Importance Thresholding (CIT) mechanism to derive nested prefix windows, each routed to a scale-specialized expert. A Global Compressed Representation (GCR) branch runs in parallel over the full context, preserving global temporal coherence that local experts cannot guarantee. Because the nested routing induces structured overlap among expert inputs, we introduce a Correlation-Aware Weighting (CAW) to align variable-length expert outputs and penalize pairwise cosine similarity prior to aggregation. Experiments on cumulative log-return prediction (HS300, S&P500) and fund sales forecasting demonstrate that RAVEN achieves SOTA performances, improves Pearson correlation by 9.2% on HS300 and 20.2% on S&P500, and reduces MSE by 18.2% on fund sales forecasting, while achieving the best results in 14 of 16 metrics on four PEMS traffic benchmarks.

AI.tldr: RAVEN is a regime-aware, variable-context Mixture-of-Experts for financial time series that adaptively selects nested look-back windows via CIT, while a Global Compressed Representation preserves global context; CAW aligns outputs and discourages redundancy. It achieves SOTA results across multiple financial tasks.
AI.motivation: Financial time series are non-stationary with regime shifts, low SNR, and regime-dependent temporal dependencies. Fixed context windows fail to capture optimal look-back across samples, hindering predictive performance.
AI.method: Introduce Regime-Aware Variable-context Expert Network (RAVEN), a Mixture-of-Experts framework with a hierarchy of nested contiguous windows determined by data via Cumulative Importance Thresholding (CIT). Patches are scored in reverse chronological order and routed to scale-specialized experts. A Global Compressed Representation (GCR) branch processes full context. Correlation-Aware Weighting (CAW) penalizes pairwise cosine similarity to align outputs before aggregation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on cumulative log-return prediction (HS300, S&P 500), fund sales forecasting, and four PEMS traffic benchmarks shows SOTA performance: +9.2% Pearson on HS300, +20.2% on S&P 500, -18.2% MSE on fund sales forecasting, and best results in 14 of 16 metrics on PEMS benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, regime-aware context selection via RAVEN yields substantial gains over fixed-context models, demonstrating the value of dynamic context and structured overlap in financial time-series forecasting.

title: ReM-MoA: Reasoning Memory Sustains Mixture-of-Agents Scaling
summary: Mixture-of-Agents (MoA) architectures improve inference-time scaling by organizing multiple LLM agents into layered reasoning pipelines. However, existing MoA variants fail to sustain gains as depth increases, exhibiting degradation, early plateauing, or saturation. We propose ReM-MoA, a memory-augmented MoA framework that sustains scaling through two mechanisms: (1) a Ranked Reasoning Memory that persistently stores and ranks reasoning traces from all layers using a comparative Reviewer Agent, and (2) a Curated Diversified Memory Routing scheme that exposes different agents to distinct combinations of successful and failed traces, preserving exploration diversity while propagating high-quality reasoning. We further introduce an optional multi-domain Reviewer distillation pipeline that improves ranking quality through frontier-model supervision. Across five reasoning benchmarks spanning math, formal logic, code, knowledge, and commonsense, ReM-MoA consistently outperforms prior MoA variants across both depth and width scaling, and its advantage widens with depth, establishing structured cross-layer reasoning memory as a key missing mechanism for scalable multi-agent inference.

AI.tldr: ReM-MoA introduces memory-augmented MoA to sustain scaling by storing/ranking reasoning traces and diversifying routing; optional distillation improves rankings; achieves consistent, growing advantages with depth across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: MoA-based multi-agent inference suffers degradation as depth grows; need persistent cross-layer reasoning memory to preserve useful traces and exploration while propagating high-quality reasoning.
AI.method: Two mechanisms: (1) Ranked Reasoning Memory with a comparative Reviewer Agent that stores and ranks traces from all layers; (2) Curated Diversified Memory Routing exposing agents to distinct combinations of successful/failed traces to maintain exploration while promoting high-quality traces. Optional multi-domain Reviewer distillation with frontier-model supervision.
AI.result: Across five reasoning domains (math, formal logic, code, knowledge, commonsense), ReM-MoA outperforms prior MoA variants in both depth and width scaling, with the benefit increasing with depth.
AI.conclusion: Structured cross-layer reasoning memory is a key missing mechanism for scalable multi-agent inference; memory augmentation enables sustained gains as MoA depth grows.

title: Inclusive Interactive Collisions for Multi-View Consistent Compositional 3D Generation
summary: Recent breakthroughs in 3D generation have advanced notably with the development of text-to-image diffusion model. However, existing methods remain two practical challenges: (1) They primarily generate single 3D object, but struggle to generate multi-object compositional 3D assets due to the lack of the modeling for Gaussian primitives in reasonable interactions. (2) They often suffer from cross-view inconsistency during 3D optimization, as Score Distillation Sampling inherently performs on each single view, inevitably resulting in cross-view hallucinations. To solve above issues, we propose I2C-3D, a novel optimization-based method to generate multi-view consistent compositional 3D assets with reasonable interactions. Specifically, we propose an Inclusive Interactive Collisions strategy to guide Gaussian primitives appearing in reasonable interaction regions naturally, thereby ensuring objects in the compositional scene interact in a physically plausible and visually coherent way. Additionally, to enhance multi-view consistency, Multi-View Adaptive Score Distillation Sampling is devised to distill multi-view consistency prior and layout prior from pre-trained diffusion model by modulating attention map of instance token and spatial token across viewpoints. Benefiting from above elaborate designs, I2C-3D not only generates high-fidelity multi-view consistent compositional 3D assets but also supports 3D editing flexibly, facilitating complex scene generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate our I2C-3D outperforms existing methods in generation quality and multi-view consistency.

AI.tldr: I2C-3D introduces an optimization-based framework for multi-view, multi-object 3D generation that ensures plausible object interactions and cross-view consistency by (a) Inclusive Interactive Collisions guiding Gaussian primitives to interact in reasonable regions, and (b) Multi-View Adaptive Score Distillation Sampling modulating diffusion-based priors across viewpoints. It achieves higher quality, better consistency, and supports 3D editing compared to prior methods.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based 3D generation struggles with (i) composing multiple objects with plausible interactions, and (ii) cross-view inconsistencies arising from single-view score distillation. A method that enforces physical-like interactions and cross-view coherence is needed for reliable scene-level 3D assets.
AI.method: Propose Inclusive Interactive Collisions to steer Gaussian primitives into interaction regions that yield physically plausible and visually coherent scenes. Introduce Multi-View Adaptive Score Distillation Sampling to distill multi-view priors (layout and consistency) from pre-trained diffusion models by modulating attention maps (instance and spatial tokens) across viewpoints, enhancing cross-view coherence during optimization. The approach is optimization-based and supports flexible 3D editing of compositional scenes.
AI.result: Empirical results show I2C-3D outperforms existing methods in both generation quality and multi-view consistency for compositional 3D assets, and offers improved 3D editing capabilities for complex scenes.
AI.conclusion: I2C-3D provides a principled solution to multi-object, multi-view 3D generation by combining interaction-aware primitive placement with cross-view guidance from diffusion priors, enabling higher fidelity and coherence in compositional scenes and facilitating flexible 3D editing.

title: Blockwise Policy-Drift Gating for On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains a student policy using teacher signals computed on trajectories sampled by the student itself. Recent work shows that sampled-token OPD can be fragile on long-horizon reasoning tasks and that local teacher-support matching is a simple and effective repair. This paper introduces blockwise policy-drift gating, a lightweight student-only old-current drift controller for OPD under rollout reuse. The method computes log-probability shifts between the behavior student and the current student on the sampled token path, aggregates these shifts over fixed blocks or spans, and uses the resulting detached, mean-normalized gates to reweight OPD position losses. It does not change teacher targets, teacher top-K supports, or the rollout policy. In a six-variant Qwen3 math reasoning benchmark with a uniform 200-step training budget for all trained variants, we use pass@8 as the primary problem-level solve-rate metric. Fixed 64-token block gating improves sampled-token OPD mean pass@8 from 0.4978 to 0.5160 across AIME24, AIME25, MATH500, and AMC23. On Teacher-TopK/LSM, Block64 gives the best four-benchmark mean pass@8 among trained students. The results identify local old-current policy drift as a practical control signal for reused OPD rollouts and motivate block-level gating as a simple default for improving solve-rate robustness.

AI.tldr: Blockwise policy-drift gating for on-policy distillation (OPD) improves robustness by reweighting OPD losses with block-aggregated drift signals, achieving higher solve rates on long-horizon math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: OPD with rollout reuse is prone to long-horizon policy drift; a lightweight, teacher-agnostic mechanism is needed to stabilize learning without changing targets.
AI.method: Compute log-probability shifts between the behavior student and the current student along sampled token paths, aggregate these shifts over fixed blocks (e.g., 64-token blocks), detach and mean-normalize to form gates, and reweight OPD position losses accordingly. No changes to teacher targets, Top-K supports, or rollout policy.
AI.result: In a six-variant Qwen3 math reasoning benchmark with a uniform 200-step budget, fixed 64-token block gating raised mean pass@8 from 0.4978 to 0.5160 across AIME24, AIME25, MATH500, and AMC23; on Teacher-TopK/LSM, Block64 yielded the best four-benchmark mean pass@8 among trained students.
AI.conclusion: Local old-current policy drift is a practical control signal for reused OPD rollouts; block-level gating is a simple default that enhances solve-rate robustness without altering teacher targets or rollout policy.

title: Bayesian control for coding agents
summary: Modern coding agents pair LLM generators with various tools, including cheap diagnostics and expensive verifiers. The tool-use decisions are typically governed by orchestrators that often use fixed rules and ignore uncertainty. We formulate orchestration as cost-sensitive sequential hypothesis testing: a Bayesian controller maintains a belief over candidate correctness and dynamically decides whether to gather more evidence, refine the candidate, verify it, or stop. Across six generators and nine coding benchmarks, Bayesian control proves to be most valuable when verification is costly and critics are informative but imperfect. Beyond control, the belief state yields an interpretable correctness score that outperforms token-probability and raw tool-success baselines for uncertainty quantification.

AI.tldr: Bayesian-controlled tool orchestration for coding agents improves decision-making under costly verification; belief state provides a robust correctness score for uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: Current orchestrators use fixed rules and ignore uncertainty; enable cost-sensitive sequential hypothesis testing to optimize evidence gathering and verification.
AI.method: Model the problem as Bayesian sequential hypothesis testing; maintain a belief over candidate correctness; decisions include gather more evidence, refine, verify, or stop; evaluate across six generators and nine benchmarks.
AI.result: Bayesian control outperforms baselines when verification is costly and critics are informative but imperfect; belief-derived correctness score outperforms token probability and raw tool-success baselines for uncertainty quantification.
AI.conclusion: Belief state serves both for control and interpretable uncertainty estimation; suggests broader utility of Bayesian orchestration for tool-use in LLM systems.

title: MorVess: Morphology-Aware Pulmonary Vessel Segmentation Network
summary: Accurate pulmonary vessel segmentation remains challenging due to the sparse, tortuous, and multi-scale nature of vascular structures, where small branches are easily lost and topology integrity is difficult to preserve under voxel-wise supervision. Existing deep segmentation models primarily optimize binary masks, lacking explicit geometric constraints, thus struggling to recover continuous tubular morphology and fine vascular connectivity. In this study, we introduce MorVess, a morphology-aware segmentation framework that integrates differentiable geometric priors with large-scale foundation model adaptation to achieve fine-grained vascular parsing. MorVess jointly predicts vessel masks, distance maps, and thickness maps, providing explicit supervision for vascular boundaries, centerline consistency, and smooth diameter transitions. A lightweight 2.5D adapter bridges 3D spatial context and 2D SAM representations, while a global-local fusion block aggregates multi-level semantics and geometric cues for high-fidelity topology reconstruction. Across two challenging pulmonary CT benchmarks, MorVess delivers superior Dice, clDice, and HD95 scores, substantially improving small-vessel recovery and global connectivity. These results demonstrate that embedding geometric intelligence into pretrained vision models offers a principled and scalable pathway toward precise vessel analysis and clinically reliable structural quantification. Our source code is available at https://github.com/MaoFuyou/MorVess.

AI.tldr: MorVess introduces a morphology-aware segmentation framework that adds differentiable geometric priors and multi-task outputs (masks, distance, thickness) with a 2.5D adapter and global-local fusion to improve pulmonary vessel segmentation, achieving better Dice, clDice, and HD95 on two CT benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Pulmonary vessel segmentation is difficult due to sparse, tortuous, multi-scale vascular structures; voxel-wise supervision loses topology and small branches; existing models lack explicit geometric constraints to enforce continuous tubular morphology and connectivity.
AI.method: MorVess jointly predicts vessel masks, distance maps, and thickness maps; incorporates differentiable geometric priors; uses a lightweight 2.5D adapter to bridge 3D context with 2D SAM representations; employs a global-local fusion block to integrate multi-level semantics and geometric cues for topology reconstruction; adapts large foundation models.
AI.result: On two challenging pulmonary CT benchmarks, MorVess achieves superior Dice, clDice, and HD95 scores, with notable improvements in small-vessel recovery and global connectivity.
AI.conclusion: Embedding geometric intelligence into pretrained vision models provides a principled, scalable pathway for precise vessel analysis and clinically reliable quantification; source code is available at the provided GitHub URL.

title: NeuroSonic: Conditional Flow Matching for EEG-to-Speech Reconstruction
summary: Reconstructing continuous speech from scalp electroencephalography (EEG) remains fundamentally challenging. EEG provides a weak, spatially diffuse, and highly variable measurement of distributed cortical activity, whereas speech is organized as a coherent acoustic trajectory with strong harmonic and temporal structure. The resulting mismatch makes waveform regression unstable and causes stochastic multi-step generation to be sensitive to artifact-dependent conditioning and subject variability. We introduce NeuroSonic, a conditional flow-matching framework for EEG-to-speech reconstruction. Instead of predicting waveforms directly or refining them through stochastic denoising, NeuroSonic learns a deterministic probability-flow velocity field that transports a noise-corrupted acoustic state toward clean speech under EEG conditioning. EEG and audio are embedded into a shared token space and processed by a time-conditioned gated Transformer that parameterizes the transport ordinary differential equation. This formulation models trajectory evolution explicitly while avoiding iterative stochastic sampling. We evaluate NeuroSonic on the CineBrain and EAV benchmarks under cross-subject evaluation. Across both datasets, the proposed method improves distributional realism, spectral fidelity, and perceptual quality over representative GAN-, diffusion-, and mean-flow baselines, with up to a 26.3\% gain in overall perceptual quality. The performance gap is most evident in artifact-heavy segments, where conditioning variability is strongest. These findings indicate that deterministic conditional transport provides a stable and effective formulation for EEG-driven speech reconstruction. Code is available at https://github.com/Y-Research-SBU/NeuroSonic/ .

AI.tldr: NeuroSonic uses deterministic conditional transport (flow-matching) to map EEG-conditioned noise to clean speech, avoiding stochastic denoising and iterative sampling, achieving better realism and perceptual quality than GAN/diffusion baselines on CineBrain and EAV.
AI.motivation: EEG-to-speech reconstruction is challenged by weak, diffuse, variable EEG signals and the structured, harmonic nature of speech; existing waveform regression and denoising approaches are unstable under EEG artifact and subject variability.
AI.method: Proposes a conditional flow-matching framework where a transport velocity field moves a noise-corrupted acoustic state to clean speech under EEG conditioning. EEG and audio are embedded into a shared token space and processed by a time-conditioned gated Transformer that parameterizes the transport ODE.
AI.result: Outperforms representative GAN, diffusion, and mean-flow baselines on CineBrain and EAV with improved distributional realism, spectral fidelity, and perceptual quality; up to 26.3% gain in overall perceptual quality; largest gains in artifact-heavy segments; cross-subject evaluation; code released.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic conditional transport offers a stable and effective formulation for EEG-driven speech reconstruction, addressing instability in waveform regression and stochastic sampling.

title: CompressKV: Semantic-Retrieval-Guided KV-Cache Compression for Resource-Efficient Long-Context LLM Inference
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2508.02401
summary: Long-context large language model (LLM) inference is increasingly constrained by the memory footprint and decoding cost of key-value (KV) caches, limiting sustainable deployment on resource-constrained hardware. Existing KV cache eviction methods typically apply heuristic token scoring over all heads in GQA-based LLMs. These methods ignore the different functionalities of attention heads, leading to the eviction of critical tokens and thus degrading the performance of LLMs. To address this issue, we propose CompressKV, a resource-efficient KV-cache compression framework for GQA-based LLMs. Instead of aggregating attention scores from all heads, CompressKV identifies Semantic Retrieval Heads (SRHs) that capture both the initial and final tokens of a prompt and semantically important mid-context evidence, and uses them to select tokens whose KV pairs should be retained. Furthermore, CompressKV allocates cache budgets across layers according to offline estimates of layer-wise eviction error. Experiments on LongBench and Needle-in-a-Haystack show that CompressKV consistently outperforms existing KV-cache eviction methods across memory budgets. Notably, it preserves over 97\% of full-cache performance using only 3\% of the KV cache on LongBench question-answering tasks and achieves 90\% accuracy with just 0.7\% KV storage on Needle-in-a-Haystack. These results demonstrate an improved resource--performance trade-off for long-context LLM inference. Our code is publicly available at: https://github.com/TUDa-HWAI/CompressKV

AI.tldr: CompressKV reduces KV-cache memory/compute for GQA-based LLMs by selecting Semantic Retrieval Heads to preserve important KV pairs and by layer-wise budget allocation, yielding strong memory-efficiency with minimal performance loss on LongBench and Needle-in-a-Haystack.
AI.motivation: KV-cache eviction in long-context LLMs wastes memory and can degrade performance; existing methods treat all attention heads equally, ignoring head functionality.
AI.method: Identify Semantic Retrieval Heads (SRHs) that capture initial/final tokens and semantically important mid-context evidence; retain KV pairs for tokens selected by SRHs; allocate per-layer cache budgets based on offline estimates of eviction error.
AI.result: Outperforms existing KV-cache eviction methods across memory budgets; preserves >97% of full-cache performance using only 3% of KV cache on LongBench; achieves 90% accuracy with 0.7% KV storage on Needle-in-a-Haystack.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates an improved resource-performance trade-off for long-context LLM inference; code is publicly available.

title: Geometry-Instructed Video Editing
summary: Object-level geometric edits, including translating, rotating, scaling, duplicating, or removing an object, are routine operations in digital content creation (DCC) workflows, yet they remain unreliable in generative video editing. The key challenge lies in specifying the target object's 3D state change unambiguously across viewpoint and time, while consistently updating geometry-dependent secondary effects such as shadows and reflections. We introduce GIVE, a geometry-instructed video editing framework that represents edits through a unified object-state formulation. Two video-aligned geometry streams describe the target object before and after editing: a depth-box encoding coarse 3D placement and extent, and an orientation-box providing an appearance-agnostic orientation cue. Together, these streams provide a compact pre/post geometric specification for object-state transitions. To provide paired supervision for learning these edits, we build a scalable graphics-engine pipeline that executes object-level edit programs and renders controlled before/after pairs, isolating the intended geometric edit while keeping secondary effects consistent with the transformation. Experimental results demonstrate that GIVE produces faithful geometric edits with temporal coherence and consistent secondary effects across operators in a unified framework, and shows promising transfer to in-the-wild videos. Project page: https://geometry-instructed-video-editing.github.io/give/

AI.tldr: GIVE introduces geometry-instructed video editing using two video-aligned geometry streams (depth-box and orientation-box) to specify pre/post object states, with a scalable graphics-engine pipeline that renders controlled before/after pairs for supervision, achieving temporally coherent edits and consistent secondary effects, with transfer potential to in-the-wild videos.
AI.motivation: Object-level geometric edits in generative video editing are unreliable due to ambiguity in 3D state changes across viewpoints and time, and the need to maintain geometry-dependent secondary effects (shadows, reflections).
AI.method: Propose GIVE, a unified object-state framework: two geometry streams—depth-box for coarse 3D placement/extent and orientation-box for appearance-agnostic orientation. These provide a compact pre/post geometric spec. A scalable graphics-engine pipeline renders controlled before/after pairs by executing object-level edit programs, isolating the geometric edit while keeping secondary effects consistent, providing paired supervision for learning.
AI.result: Experiments show GIVE achieves faithful geometric edits with temporal coherence and consistent secondary effects across operators, with promising transfer to in-the-wild videos.
AI.conclusion: GIVE offers a unified, geometry-guided approach to object-level video edits, enabling reliable state transitions and coherent secondary effects, with demonstrated transferability and learnability through paired supervision.

title: FedUP: One-Shot Federated Unlearning via Centroid-Guided Plug-in Filters
summary: Federated unlearning (FU) is critical for complying with legal mandates like the right to be forgotten in decentralized systems, yet current methods face a persistent dilemma between non-target knowledge loss and high request latency. To resolve these issues, we propose FedUP, a one-shot federated unlearning framework utilizing lightweight pluggable filters that act as a "knowledge funnel" to screen out target data while preserving original model performance. By freezing original model parameters and training filters at the server side using differentially private (DP)-protected class centroid samples, FedUP bypasses the need for multi-round client-server communication and complex retraining, reducing unlearning latency from minutes to mere seconds. Additionally, the framework's pluggable architecture ensures inherent reversibility, enabling the seamless restoration of forgotten knowledge by simply removing the filters. Extensive experiments on diverse image and text tasks demonstrate that FedUP effectively reduces non-target knowledge loss and achieves superior unlearning precision and efficiency across various scenarios. Code is available at: https://github.com/suows/FedUP-code.

AI.tldr: FedUP offers a one-shot federated unlearning framework using pluggable filters trained with DP-protected centroids, freezing the base model to achieve fast, reversible unlearning with minimal non-target knowledge loss.
AI.motivation: Address legal requirements (e.g., right to be forgotten) in federated/decentralized systems, where existing unlearning methods incur non-target knowledge loss or high latency due to retraining and multiple communication rounds.
AI.method: Freeze the original model; train lightweight, pluggable filters at the server using DP-protected class centroids; filters act as a knowledge funnel to remove target data while preserving overall performance; no multi-round client-server communication; reversible via removing filters.
AI.result: Empirical results on image and text tasks show reduced non-target knowledge loss and improved unlearning precision and efficiency; unlearning latency drops from minutes to seconds; code released.
AI.conclusion: FedUP enables efficient, one-shot federated unlearning with strong privacy protection and reversibility, balancing unlearning fidelity and latency.

title: The Latent Bridge: A Continuous Slow-Fast Channel for Real-Time Game Agents
summary: A real-time agent for general computer use - with games as the most demanding case - must act within tens of milliseconds while still planning over seconds. These two regimes sit at opposite ends of the latency-quality tradeoff. A reasoning VLM (Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking) deliberates effectively but requires ~1.5 s per response - far too slow for a 15 Hz control loop. In contrast, a reactive VLM (MiniCPM-o 4.5) acts in milliseconds but underperforms on planning-heavy tasks. We couple two frozen models of matched scale (9B reactive, 8B reasoning), leaving the communication channel as the sole trainable component. The standard coupling is a Text Bridge (T): the slow model writes a suffix the fast model reads. We introduce a learned continuous Latent Bridge (L) that projects the slow model's residuals into the fast model's input-embedding space in a LLaVA-style manner, avoiding any text round-trip; both are compared against Fast-Only (F). On 7 Atari games and a driving domain (MetaDrive), tuning the action decoder per channel on held-out seeds, the Latent Bridge matches or beats the Text Bridge in every domain: it significantly improves two games (MsPacman +57%, RoadRunner +28%) and is a safe drop-in elsewhere. Combining both channels interferes destructively (RoadRunner -96%), so only one should be used. The benefit is highly predictable: the bridge helps if and only if slow reasoning already beats fast reaction (T > F) - the Latent and Text gains over Fast-Only move together at r=0.93. MetaDrive is the controlled negative, where the Latent Bridge is demonstrably inert because the Text Bridge adds no value. We release replay recordings and reproducible pipelines.

AI.tldr: Introduces a Latent Bridge to couple slow-reasoning (8B) and fast-reacting (9B) VLMs without text round-trips. Across Atari and MetaDrive, Latent Bridge matches/beats a Text Bridge, but channel interference occurs when combining both. Benefits arise when slow reasoning beats fast reaction; MetaDrive shows no gain. Release reproducible pipelines.
AI.motivation: Real-time agents must balance low latency (ms) with long-horizon planning (s). Text-based interchanges (Text Bridge) incur latency; a latent, routing-based bridge could preserve latency while leveraging slow reasoning.
AI.method: Two frozen models (9B reactive, 8B reasoning). Text Bridge: slow model writes a suffix; fast model reads. Latent Bridge: learn a continuous projection of the slow model's residuals into the fast model's input embedding (LLaVA-style), avoiding text round-trips. Compare to Fast-Only (F). Evaluate on 7 Atari games and MetaDrive; tune the action decoder per channel on held-out seeds; assess performance and interference when combining channels.
AI.result: Latent Bridge matches or surpasses Text Bridge in all domains. Notable gains: MsPacman +57%, RoadRunner +28%. Safe drop-in elsewhere. Combining both channels yields destructive interference (RoadRunner -96%). Benefit is predictive: the bridge helps only when slow reasoning beats fast reaction (T > F); gains from Latent and Text align with r=0.93. MetaDrive shows inert behavior where Text Bridge adds no value. Public replay recordings and reproducible pipelines released.
AI.conclusion: Latent Bridge provides a robust, text-free mechanism to fuse slow planning with fast reaction, outperforming a text-based bridge across tasks and enabling reproducible results. Use a single bridge; avoid simultaneous use of both channels; leverage the bridge when slow-vs-fast advantage exists.

title: FiCA: Feed-forward instant Gaussian Codec Avatars from a Single Portrait Image
summary: We introduce FiCA, a Feed-forward, instant Gaussian Codec Avatar generation pipeline that creates lifelike avatars from a single portrait image. Generating a photorealistic and drivable avatar from just a single image is significantly challenging due to the limited visual information available to accurately infer the 3D appearance and geometry of human heads. To address this, we develop a novel system that combines human-centric vision foundation models with a diffusion model. This system is designed to fully exploit partial visual observations to generate lifelike human avatars. Our proposed diffusion model learns a generative mapping from these partial observations to complete and authentic 3D mesh reconstruction. Additionally, we introduce a feed-forward mesh refinement network that enhances the fidelity and identity preservation of the generated avatars, eliminating the need for person-specific test-time optimization. By leveraging a universal prior model that decodes a generated mesh into a set of 3D Gaussians, we generate a photorealistic 3D Gaussian avatar, capable of being driven with novel expressions in real-time. Our experiments demonstrate that the avatars generated by our feed-forward approach faithfully represent diverse identities and surpass the visual quality of avatars produced by recent competing methods.

AI.tldr: FiCA offers a fast, single-image 3D avatar pipeline using diffusion and Gaussian representations, with no test-time optimization, achieving photorealism and real-time expression control, surpassing recent methods.
AI.motivation: Generating photorealistic, drivable 3D avatars from a single portrait is severely underconstrained due to limited 3D cues. The work aims to leverage human-centric foundation models and diffusion to exploit partial observations and infer plausible 3D geometry.
AI.method: Integrate human-centric vision foundation models with a diffusion model to map partial observations to complete 3D mesh. Use a feed-forward mesh refinement network to boost fidelity and identity preservation, removing test-time optimization. Employ a universal prior model that decodes the mesh into 3D Gaussians to render a photorealistic Gaussian avatar that supports real-time expression driving.
AI.result: Experiments show avatars faithfully represent diverse identities and surpass visual quality of competing methods, with real-time expression control and no per-instance optimization required.
AI.conclusion: FiCA demonstrates a practical, single-image 3D avatar pipeline that achieves high fidelity and real-time drive by combining partial-observation diffusion, fast mesh refinement, and mesh-to-Gaussian decoding, without test-time optimization.

title: When Top-1 Fails: Calibrating LoRA Monitors for Masked Diffusion LMs
comment: 14 pages, 3 figures. Code and result artifacts: https://github.com/lucky-verma/top1-fails-dlm-lora-monitors
summary: Discrete diffusion language model (DLM) fine-tuning inherits inexpensive diagnostics from denoising-time confidence monitors, but their PEFT-training meaning is untested. We test top-1 argmax concentration as a collapse warning. Across 816 LoRA/PEFT configurations from three DLM families, the warning fires for every configuration while logs record 0/816 actual collapses at the 200 step horizon, giving zero precision. The cause is pre-equilibrium saturation: top-1 concentration is already high before optimization and quickly becomes insensitive to final training stability. We then evaluate max LoRA gradient norm, a parameter-side signal that samples gradient routing rather than token concentration. On a pooled held-out LLaDA-family split, a train-optimized threshold identifies top-decile final-loss configurations with precision 0.68 and F1=0.79, above the all-positive top-1 baseline even at the lower split-bootstrap confidence bound. Autoregressive controls and cross-family threshold failures bound the result to short-horizon DLM-LoRA inspection rather than a universal collapse detector. Workflow: drop top-1 as a PEFT alarm, log max-gradient early in training, and calibrate thresholds per DLM family before routing runs for inspection.

AI.tldr: Top-1 argmax concentration is an unreliable DLM-PEFT collapse signal; gradient-norm signal with per-family thresholds yields better short-horizon detection, but is not universal.
AI.motivation: PEFT diagnostics from denoising-time confidence monitors may produce false alarms; reliable early warnings for training collapse in discrete diffusion language models are needed.
AI.method: Empirically evaluate 816 LoRA/PEFT configurations across three DLM families. Assess top-1 argmax warning; analyze pre-equilibrium saturation causing false alarms. Then evaluate max LoRA gradient norm on a pooled held-out LLaDA-family split, derive train-optimized thresholds for top-decile final-loss configurations, reporting precision and F1. Test autoregressive controls and cross-family threshold failures to bound universality. Propose workflow: drop top-1 alarm, log max-gradient early, calibrate thresholds per DLM family before inspection.
AI.result: Top-1 concentration triggers for every configuration but yields zero precision at the 200-step horizon due to pre-equilibrium saturation. Max grad-norm threshold identifies high-risk configurations with precision 0.68 and F1 0.79 on a pooled LLaDA-family split. Autoregressive controls and cross-family thresholds expose limited universality, restricting conclusions to short-horizon DLM-LoRA inspection. Thresholds must be calibrated per DLM family; cross-family generalization is weak.
AI.conclusion: Discard top-1 as a PEFT alarm; instead log max gradient norm early and calibrate per-DLM-family thresholds before inspection for short-horizon monitoring. The approach is not universal and requires domain-specific calibration.

title: On the Smallness of the Large Language Models Scaling Exponents
summary: We discuss reasons why the scaling exponents of current Large Language Models (LLMs) applications are indicating an unsustainable regime in terms of energy resources. We further show that attributing the smallness of such exponents to a numerical bias due to the neglect of a non-zero value of the loss function in the limit of infinite data (``pedestal effect") does not remove the unsustainability issue. Finally, the effects of the smoothness (roughness) of the data on the scaling exponents is commented upon based on an analogy with phenomenological models of fluid turbulence.

AI.tldr: Pedestal-bias correction does not rescue the unsustainable energy implications of LLM scaling; data smoothness/roughness can influence scaling exponents, analogous to turbulence models.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether scaling exponents for LLM resource use misrepresent energy costs and whether a pedestal bias could mask unsustainability; to assess how data smoothness affects exponent behavior using turbulence-inspired analogies.
AI.method: Theoretical, conceptual analysis combining critique of pedestal bias and qualitative analogy to phenomenological turbulence models; no new empirical data reported in the abstract.
AI.result: Even after accounting for pedestal bias (non-zero loss at infinite data), the unsustainability issue persists. The smoothness/roughness of data can affect the observed scaling exponents, as suggested by an analogy with turbulence models.
AI.conclusion: Scaling exponents alone do not guarantee sustainable scaling for LLMs. Pedestal corrections are insufficient to remove energy concerns; data regularity plays a significant role in exponent values, warranting more nuanced modeling and energy-focused considerations.

title: Latent Visual States for Efficient Multimodal Reasoning
summary: The integration of visual evidence has significantly enhanced the capabilities of large multimodal models. However, this integration predominantly relies on generating discrete outputs (etc., code or box coordinates) to invoke external tools, a process that introduces rigid dependencies and substantial latency. To overcome these limitations, we propose {EVA} (LatEnt Visual StAtes), a novel framework that natively generates continuous latent visual representations. These internal representations manifest as an adaptive sequence of Latent\_slot tokens, serving as intermediate visual thoughts during the reasoning process. These Latent\_slot tokens are then trained end-to-end with the discrete text tokens. This co-optimization, notably, causes extreme policy deviation in the 'transition window' following the Latent\_slot tokens. We develop D-GSPO (Decouple-GSPO) to target this root cause by decoupling the optimization of latent and discrete components. To support SFT, we construct EVA-230K, a high-quality text-image interleaved CoT dataset encompassing a diverse range of real-world scenes, documents, charts and OCR tasks. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks confirm that EVA achieves significant performance gains while enhancing inference efficiency.

AI.tldr: EVA introduces continuous latent visual states (Latent_slot tokens) for multimodal models, enabling internal visual reasoning without discrete tool calls, using D-GSPO to decouple latent and discrete optimization, plus EVA-230K dataset; achieves better accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Relying on discrete outputs to invoke external tools in multimodal systems causes latency, rigidity, and suboptimal reasoning. Continuous latent representations aim to streamline reasoning and improve efficiency.
AI.method: EVA constructs adaptive Latent_slot tokens as intermediate visual thoughts and trains them end-to-end with discrete text tokens. A policy-deviation issue in the transition window is addressed with Decouple-GSPO (D-GSPO) that separates optimization of latent and discrete components. EVA-230K is built as a high-quality interleaved text-image CoT dataset spanning real-world scenes, documents, charts, and OCR tasks. Extensive benchmarks validate performance gains and faster inference.
AI.result: Significant performance gains across multiple benchmarks, coupled with enhanced inference efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Latent visual states enable smoother, more efficient multimodal reasoning; decoupled optimization mitigates policy deviation, making end-to-end training feasible for latent and discrete components. EVA and EVA-230K offer a scalable framework for advanced LMMs.

title: Holistic Data Scheduler for LLM Pre-training via Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning
summary: The composition of training data, governed by the diversity of sources and their mixing strategy, is a cornerstone of Large Language Model (LLM) pre-training. Online Data Mixing (ODM), the technique of adaptively adjusting data mixtures during training, has emerged as a promising direction to improve efficiency. However, existing methods are constrained by their reliance on a singular optimization perspective, which fundamentally overlooks the need for complex LLM pre-training to consider the dynamic data composition from multiple dimensions. To overcome this limitation, we introduce the Holistic Data Scheduler (HDS), a novel online data mixing framework. HDS formulates the data scheduling challenge as a reinforcement learning problem in a continuous control space and leverages the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm for its stability and sample efficiency in exploring the high-dimensional policy space. At the core of HDS lies a novel multi-objective, holistic reward function that integrates three critical perspectives: a data-driven reward for quality, a loss-driven reward capturing inter-domain influence, and a model-driven reward based on weight norms. To validate our design and determine its optimal configuration, we conducted systematic experiments on LLMs of various sizes. On The Pile benchmark, HDS reaches the final validation perplexity of the next best method with 44% fewer training iterations. Furthermore, it achieves a 7.2% improvement on the MMLU 0-shot task along with consistent gains on other benchmarks, showcasing its ability to enhance both training efficiency and final model capability.

AI.tldr: Holistic Data Scheduler (HDS) proposes an online data mixing framework for LLM pre-training, using SAC in continuous action space with a multi-objective holistic reward to optimize data composition across quality, cross-domain loss influence, and weight norms; achieves faster convergence and improved downstream performance on The Pile and MMLU.
AI.motivation: Current online data mixing (ODM) methods rely on a single optimization perspective, failing to account for dynamic, multi-dimensional data composition required in large-scale LLM pre-training.
AI.method: Formulate data scheduling as a continuous-control reinforcement learning problem and optimize with Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). Introduce a multi-objective holistic reward combining (i) data-driven quality, (ii) loss-driven inter-domain influence, and (iii) model-driven weight-norm guidance.
AI.result: Empirical validation across LLMs of various sizes shows HDS reaches the final validation perplexity of the next best method with 44% fewer training iterations on The Pile; yields a 7.2% improvement on MMLU 0-shot and gains on other benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A holistic, RL-based data-scheduling framework can significantly improve training efficiency and downstream performance by integrating multi-dimensional signals into data composition during pre-training.

title: A specialized reasoning large language model for accelerating rare disease diagnosis: a randomized AI physician assistance trial
summary: Rare diseases affect millions of individuals worldwide, yet timely diagnosis remains a major public health challenge due to scarcity of specialized clinical expertise. While large language models (LLMs) show promise to support rare disease diagnosis, current models are constrained by insufficient clinical deployability, limited clinically grounded evidence, and scarcity of training data. Here we present RaDaR (Rare Disease navigatoR), an open-source, compact reasoning LLM (32B parameters) for rare disease diagnosis. RaDaR was trained with 49,170 publicly available free-text cases and 104,666 synthetic cases with reasoning-enhanced training. RaDaR showed the strongest performance among evaluated open-source models, including the 671B DeepSeek-R1, across public benchmarks and four external validation centers. In a retrospective cohort, RaDaR prioritized the final diagnosis before documented clinical suspicion in 61.06 percent of cases, corresponding to a potential lead time of 1.87 months and 50.18 percent of the within-center interval. In a randomized physician-assistance trial, RaDaR assistance improved physicians' rare-disease diagnostic accuracy by 21.44 percentage points compared with internet search alone. Synthetic-data ablations suggested that phenotype-anchored narratives provide useful training signal for long-tail rare diseases, with a monotonic scaling trend within the tested data range. Together, RaDaR and its development and validation framework provide a deployable rare-disease reasoning model and a reproducible development framework for diagnostic AI under data scarcity.

AI.tldr: Open-source 32B RaDaR LLM for rare-disease diagnosis trained on real and synthetic cases; achieves strongest performance among open-source models; improves clinician accuracy and lead time; provides reproducible development framework under data scarcity.
AI.motivation: Public health challenge of timely rare-disease diagnosis due to scarcity of clinical expertise; limitations of current LLMs in deployability, evidence grounding, and data scarcity.
AI.method: Train a compact 32B model on 49,170 public text cases and 104,666 synthetic cases with reasoning-enhanced training; use phenotype-anchored narratives; evaluate on public benchmarks and four external centers; retrospective cohort and randomized physician-assistance trial; ablation analyses.
AI.result: RaDaR outperforms evaluated open-source models (including 671B DeepSeek-R1). In retrospective cohort, prioritized final diagnosis before suspicion in 61.06% of cases; lead time 1.87 months and 50.18% of within-center interval. In physician-assistance trial, improved diagnostic accuracy by 21.44 percentage points vs internet search. Synthetic-data ablations show monotonic scaling with data, phen anchored narratives beneficial.
