AI.conclusion: RaDaR provides a deployable rare-disease reasoning AI and a reproducible framework for diagnostic AI under data scarcity.

title: A Time-Reparameterized Cumulative Intensity Extrapolation Sampler for Discrete Flow Matching
summary: Discrete flow matching (DFM) provides a principled framework for generative modeling on discrete state spaces via continuous-time Markov chain dynamics. In practice, sampling for DFM commonly employs discretizations such as $τ$-leaping, yet efficient sampling methods under a limited number of function evaluations (NFE) remain less studied. To address this gap, we propose the Time-Reparameterized Cumulative Intensity Extrapolation (TR-CIE) sampler, which aims to improve sampling quality when function evaluations are restricted. TR-CIE consists of two components. First, a schedule-based time reparameterization rescales the time grid according to the noise schedule. Under standard factorized DFM rate parameterizations, this transformation of variables absorbs the schedule-dependent growth term and mitigates stiffness near the terminal sampling stage. Second, we introduce a cumulative-intensity extrapolation updating rule. By reusing cached model outputs from the previous step as a history term, this improves the approximation of stepwise cumulative intensities on the resulting non-uniform time grid. We provide a theoretical analysis that bounds the local approximation error of cumulative intensities and establishes convergence results. The resulting sampler requires one NFE per step and introduces no additional model evaluations compared to the standard $τ$-leaping sampler. Extensive experiments on synthetic tasks, text generation, and text-to-image benchmarks demonstrate that our method improves sampling quality under limited NFE.

AI.tldr: Proposes TR-CIE sampler for discrete flow matching to enable efficient sampling with few function evaluations; combines schedule-based time reparameterization and cumulative-intensity extrapolation with cached history; provides theoretical error bounds and convergence; achieves one NFE per step and shows improved sampling quality on synthetic, text, and image tasks.
AI.motivation: Efficient sampling in discrete-flow models (DFM) under limited NFE; τ-leaping struggles with stiffness near terminal sampling; needs methods that reuse information and adapt time grids.
AI.method: Two key components: 1) time reparameterization aligned with noise schedule to absorb growth terms and reduce stiffness; 2) cumulative-intensity extrapolation that reuses previous step outputs as history to better approximate stepwise cumulative intensities on a nonuniform grid; analysis of local error and convergence.
AI.result: The sampler achieves one NFE per step without extra model evaluations and improves sampling quality under restricted NFE; validated on synthetic tasks, text generation, and text-to-image benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: TR-CIE provides an efficient, provably-accurate sampling strategy for DFM with limited evaluations, combining schedule-aware time scaling and history-based intensity extrapolation; showing broad empirical gains.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Computer-Use Agents with Autonomous Evaluation
comment: Accepted to the 4th International Workshop on Generalizing from Limited Resources in the Open World (GLOW @ IJCAI 2026)
summary:
Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) execute high-level user goals by perceiving and acting directly within graphical user interfaces. However, reinforcement learning for CUAs remains difficult because open-ended desktop environments rarely provide scalable, machine-readable reward signals: task success is often visually grounded and hard to specify with handcrafted reward functions or dense manual labels.
  We propose an RL fine-tuning framework that uses autonomous vision-language evaluation as a scalable supervision signal for GUI agents. Given a final screenshot and the original instruction, a Vision-Language Model judges task completion and provides terminal feedback without task-specific heuristics or manual labels during policy optimization.
  Because autonomous evaluators are imperfect, we model their feedback as a noisy binary reward channel and derive a noise-corrected reward estimator for Proximal Policy Optimization. Experiments across macOSWorld, Windows Agent Arena, and OSWorld show that corrected evaluator rewards outperform both zero-shot baselines and raw evaluator rewards, improving success rates by an average of 12.6 percentage points over zero-shot performance and 5.1 points over raw evaluator fine-tuning. These results suggest that autonomous evaluation can serve as a practical reward signal for RL in GUI environments when evaluator noise is explicitly modeled and corrected.

AI.tldr: Presents a framework to fine-tune GUI-reliant agents with an autonomous vision-language evaluator as a scalable reward, correcting for evaluator noise in PPO, yielding improved success rates on multiple GUI benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning for computer-use agents is hindered by scarce scalable, machine-readable rewards in open-ended desktops; visual task success is hard to specify with handcrafted rewards.
AI.method: Use a Vision-Language Model to judge task completion from the final screenshot and instruction; model feedback as a noisy binary reward channel; derive a noise-corrected reward estimator for Proximal Policy Optimization; evaluate on macOSWorld, Windows Agent Arena, and OSWorld.
AI.result: Corrected evaluator rewards outperform zero-shot baselines and raw evaluator rewards; averaging 12.6 percentage-point improvement over zero-shot and 5.1 points over raw evaluator fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Autonomous evaluation can serve as a practical reward signal for RL in GUI environments when evaluator noise is modeled and corrected.

title: M^2C-EvDet: Multi-Domain Multi-Order Cross-Modal Knowledge Distillation for Event-based Object Detection
summary: Event-based object Detection (EvDet), as a biologically inspired visual perception paradigm, demonstrates superior performance in scenarios demanding high temporal resolution and a wide dynamic range. Nevertheless, the inherent sparse representations and inadequate visual semantics of event data result in a considerable performance disparity between EvDet and frame-based object detection. Previous works attempt to alleviate this cross-modal discrepancy through knowledge distillation, yet they only focus on spatial visual semantics or pair-wise relational information, thus limiting performance in more complex scenarios. To address this challenge, this paper proposes M^2C-EvDet, a Multi-domain and Multi-order Cross-modal knowledge distillation framework for EvDet. Built upon frequency learning and hypergraph computation, M^2C-EvDet integrates two specialized modules: Adaptive Frequency-Decoupled Feature Distillation (AF^2D^2) and Multi-Order Relational Distillation (MORD).

AI.tldr: A cross-modal distillation framework for event-based object detection, M^2C-EvDet, integrating Adaptive Frequency-Decoupled Feature Distillation (AF^2D^2) and Multi-Order Relational Distillation (MORD) via frequency learning and hypergraph computation.
AI.motivation: Event-based detection (EvDet) faces sparse representations and semantic gaps with frame-based detectors. Prior distillation methods target only spatial semantics or pairwise relations, limiting performance in complex scenarios.
AI.method: Proposes M^2C-EvDet with two modules: Adaptive Frequency-Decoupled Feature Distillation (AF^2D^2) and Multi-Order Relational Distillation (MORD), built upon frequency learning and hypergraph computation to enable multi-domain and multi-order cross-modal knowledge distillation.
AI.result: Not reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Not reported in the abstract.

title: AsyncOPD: How Stale Can On-Policy Distillation Be?
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains a student on its own rollouts guided by teacher feedback and is becoming increasingly important for large language model (LLM) post-training. Like reinforcement learning (RL), however, OPD faces an on-policy systems bottleneck, as rollouts can dominate training time for reasoning workloads. Asynchronous training pipelines can alleviate this bottleneck by decoupling rollout generation from learner updates, but doing so introduces stale-policy data. While prior work has studied stale data in asynchronous RL, its effects in OPD remain underexplored. We present the first systematic study of staleness in asynchronous OPD, focusing on a practical setting where teacher feedback is implemented through local KL losses and full-vocabulary teacher logits are too expensive to store or transfer, necessitating finite teacher-score caches. We first show that KL direction changes the stale-data problem: teacher-weighted forward KL is more robust to stale rollouts, whereas student-weighted reverse KL is vulnerable. Second, for this vulnerable reverse-KL case, we study whether methods designed to stabilize asynchronous RL can mitigate OPD staleness. In our experiments, they do not improve over a simpler OPD-specific surrogate: recomputing the reverse-KL signal under the current student at learner time. Third, we analyze how finite teacher-score caches create a bias-variance tradeoff for sparse and sampled reverse-KL OPD estimators. This motivates multi-sample Monte Carlo (MC), which preserves MC correctability while reducing one-sample variance. Finally, we present and open-source AsyncOPD, a fully asynchronous OPD training pipeline built from these estimator choices. Experiments show that AsyncOPD improves training throughput by $1.6\times$ to $3.8\times$ over strict synchronous training while reaching comparable accuracy.

AI.tldr: Asynchronous on-policy distillation (OPD) can suffer from data staleness; forward KL with teacher weighting is robust to staleness, reverse KL with student weighting is fragile. A simple OPD-specific surrogate (recomputing reverse-KL under the current student) often outperforms RL-style stabilization, finite teacher-score caches introduce bias-variance, and multi-sample MC mitigates variance. An open-source AsyncOPD pipeline achieves 1.6–3.8x throughput gains with comparable accuracy.
AI.motivation: OPD accelerates LLM post-training by training from the student’s own rollouts guided by teacher feedback. However, asynchronous pipelines decouple rollout generation from updates, introducing data staleness. Prior work on stale data in asynchronous RL motivates studying its impact in OPD, especially under practical constraints where full-teacher logits are costly and only finite caches are available.
AI.method: Systematic study of staleness in asynchronous OPD focusing on practical local KL-based teacher feedback and finite teacher-score caches. Compare forward KL (teacher-weighted) vs. reverse KL (student-weighted) in the presence of stale rollouts. Evaluate stabilization techniques from asynchronous RL and a simple OPD surrogate that recomputes the reverse-KL signal at learner time. Analyze bias-variance tradeoffs from finite caches for sparse/sampled reverse-KL estimators. Propose multi-sample Monte Carlo to preserve unbiasedness while reducing one-sample variance. Implement AsyncOPD pipeline and benchmark throughput and accuracy against synchronous training.
AI.result: KL direction alters staleness sensitivity: forward KL (teacher-weighted) is more robust to stale data; reverse KL (student-weighted) is vulnerable. RL-stability methods do not improve OPD staleness; a simple surrogate—recomputing reverse-KL under the current student at learner time—performs better. Finite teacher-score caches induce a bias-variance tradeoff for sparse/sampled reverse-KL estimators; multi-sample MC mitigates variance while preserving MC corrective property. AsyncOPD pipeline delivers 1.6×–3.8× training throughput gains over strict synchronous training with comparable accuracy; codebase is open-sourced.
AI.conclusion: Asynchronous OPD with carefully chosen estimators (notably multi-sample MC for reverse-KL and on-the-fly recomputation) can substantially improve throughput without sacrificing accuracy. The work provides a practical, open-source AsyncOPD pipeline and clarifies the impact of KL direction and finite caches on staleness in OPD.

title: Governed Shared Memory for Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: Multi-agent LLM environments require robust mechanisms for shared knowledge management. This paper formalizes the fleet-memory problem and identifies four foundational failure modes: unauthorized leakage, stale propagation, contradiction persistence, and provenance collapse. To address these, we define explicit systems-level primitives: scoped retrieval, temporal supersession, provenance tracking, and policy-governed memory propagation. These primitives are implemented in MemClaw, a production multi-tenant memory service, and evaluated via ArgusFleet, a reproducible harness testing four governance dimensions. Rather than a baseline comparison, this study measures a live production service, emphasizing real-world architectural insights and negative results. Key Evaluation Results Provenance: Successfully reconstructed 100% of depth-four derivation chains with correct writer identity at sub-second per-hop latency. Propagation: Demonstrated high intra-fleet visibility with zero cross-fleet leakage. Under strong write mode, write-to-visible latency was optimized to a single search round-trip. Production Architectural Issues Discovered Asymmetric Scope Enforcement: Tenant isolation held, but sub-tenant scope was initially bypassed on direct GET-by-id requests for agent-scoped credentials (disclosed and remediated during the study). Pipeline Ordering Conflict: While contradiction supersession works for admitted writes, a synchronous near-duplicate gate can prematurely reject contradictory writes before the asynchronous contradiction detector can evaluate them. Conclusion: Long-context retrieval alone is insufficient for production multi-agent memory. Governed shared memory demands explicit systems-level abstractions, and live evaluation is vital to expose enforcement and pipeline-ordering failures missed by design-only treatments.

AI.tldr: Formalizes the fleet-memory problem in multi-agent LLM settings, identifies four failure modes, defines four governance primitives, and implements MemClaw with ArgusFleet for live, production-scale evaluation. Finds that long-context retrieval is insufficient; governance primitives and live testing reveal enforcement and pipeline-ordering issues, plus production architectural challenges.
AI.motivation: To ensure robust, provenance-preserving, and policy-governed memory sharing across multi-tenant LLM agents in production, addressing failure modes not captured by baseline or theoretical analyses.
AI.method: Define systems-level primitives (scoped retrieval, temporal supersession, provenance tracking, policy-governed memory propagation); implement MemClaw as a production multi-tenant memory service; build ArgusFleet as a reproducible harness testing governance dimensions; perform live production evaluation focusing on architecture, enforcement, and pipeline ordering rather than baseline comparisons.
AI.result: Provenance: reconstructed 100% of depth-4 derivation chains with writer identity; propagation: high intra-fleet visibility with zero cross-fleet leakage; latency: under strong write mode, write-to-visible latency reduced to a single search round-trip. Architectural issues: asymmetric scope enforcement (sub-tenant scope bypass on direct GET-by-id for agent credentials, remediated); pipeline ordering: near-duplicate gate can prematurely reject contradictory writes before asynchronous detector evaluates them.
AI.conclusion: Long-context retrieval alone is insufficient for production multi-agent memory. Effective governance requires explicit primitives and live evaluation to reveal enforcement gaps and pipeline-ordering failures.

title: TuringViT: Making SOTA Vision Transformers Accessible to All
summary: Modern VLMs and VLA systems commonly adopt off-the-shelf ViTs such as SigLIP2 as visual encoders, but diverse downstream requirements in latency, temporal modeling, and VLM integration often call for customized SOTA-level ViTs. Training such encoders remains beyond the reach of much of the community, as it requires massive image-text data, while standard softmax attention makes high-resolution or dynamic-resolution pretraining prohibitively costly and often forces low-resolution pretraining followed by post-hoc adaptation. TuringViT addresses these challenges with three key designs: Turing Linear Attention (TLA) for efficient sequence modeling, VISTA-Curation to construct supervision-rich image-video training data, and native dynamic-resolution pretraining that supports flexible inputs from the start and transfers seamlessly to downstream VLMs. As a result, TuringViT outperforms leading open-source ViT baselines with only 10% of the data, achieves stronger downstream VLM performance, and delivers substantially better latency scaling on high-resolution inputs. Our scaling-law analysis further shows that TuringViT continues to improve predictably with curated data scale, far from saturation. Its fast adaptation, hardware-friendly design, and efficient deployment have made it a unified visual foundation across XPeng's AI systems. More broadly, TuringViT provides a reproducible pipeline that dramatically lowers the cost for the community to train, customize, and deploy SOTA-level ViTs, moving toward making such Vision Transformers accessible to all.

AI.tldr: TuringViT proposes a compact, scalable ViT for visual-language models by combining (1) Turing Linear Attention for efficient sequence modeling, (2) VISTA-Curation to create rich image-video supervision data, and (3) native dynamic-resolution pretraining to handle flexible inputs from the start and ease transfer to downstream VLMs.
AI.motivation: Address the high data and compute demands of training SOTA ViTs for VLM integration, the inefficiency of softmax attention on high/high-resolution or dynamic-resolution pretraining, and the need for a reproducible, accessible pipeline to enable broad community adoption of powerful ViTs.
AI.method: Three designs: (1) Turing Linear Attention (TLA) for efficient attention with high-resolution inputs, (2) VISTA-Curation to assemble supervision-rich image-video datasets for robust multimodal learning, (3) native dynamic-resolution pretraining that supports flexible input resolutions from the outset and transfers with minimal adaptation to downstream VLMs.
AI.result: TuringViT outperforms leading open-source ViT baselines using only about 10% of the data, achieves stronger downstream VLM performance, and exhibits substantially better latency scaling on high-resolution inputs. A scaling-law analysis indicates continued gains with increased curated data. The approach provides fast adaptation and hardware-friendly deployment, serving as a unified visual foundation across XPeng’s AI systems.
AI.conclusion: TuringViT offers a reproducible, low-cost pipeline to train, customize, and deploy SOTA-level ViTs, lowering barriers for the community to build high-performance visual transformers and enabling broader access to advanced VLM capabilities.

title: Lightweight Transformer Models for On-Device Fault Detection: A Benchmark Study on Resource-Constrained Deployment
summary: On-device fault detection enables real-time diagnostics without cloud dependency, but deploying machine learning models on resource-constrained hardware demands careful tradeoffs between accuracy, latency, and model size. We present a benchmark comparing traditional ML methods (Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM, Logistic Regression) against lightweight transformer architectures (DistilBERT, TinyBERT-6L, TinyBERT-4L, MobileBERT) for binary fault detection across three public datasets: NASA C-MAPSS turbofan degradation, SECOM semiconductor manufacturing, and UCI AI4I 2020 predictive maintenance. We evaluate classification performance (F1-score, AUC), model size, and CPU inference latency, and further assess INT8 dynamic quantization and a two-stage adaptive inference pipeline. Our results reveal that on well-separated sensor data (C-MAPSS), lightweight transformers match traditional ML at 87.8% F1 but at 100x the model size and 9000x the latency. TinyBERT-4L emerges as the most deployment-friendly transformer at 55 MB and 18 ms CPU latency. INT8 quantization reduces size by 25% while preserving 86.9% F1. Our adaptive pipeline, routing 97.9% of predictions through a quantized triage model and only 2.1% to a larger expert, achieves 87.6% F1 at 19.5 ms average latency. On severely imbalanced datasets (SECOM, UCI-PM), both traditional and transformer methods struggle significantly, highlighting fundamental limitations of current approaches for extreme class imbalance in fault detection. All code is publicly available.

AI.tldr: Lightweight transformers can match traditional ML performance on well-separated fault data, but incur substantially larger model size and latency; INT8 quantization and a two-stage adaptive inference pipeline mitigate some costs; severe class imbalance remains challenging.
AI.motivation: Assess the feasibility of deploying ML-based fault detection on resource-constrained devices by comparing traditional ML and lightweight transformer models across multiple datasets, and by evaluating model size, latency, and quantization/adaptive inference techniques to balance accuracy and efficiency.
AI.method: Benchmark traditional ML methods (Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM, Logistic Regression) against lightweight transformers (DistilBERT, TinyBERT-6L, TinyBERT-4L, MobileBERT) on binary fault detection using three public datasets (NASA C-MAPSS turbofan degradation, SECOM semiconductor manufacturing, UCI AI4I 2020 predictive maintenance). Evaluate F1-score, AUC, model size, and CPU inference latency; investigate INT8 dynamic quantization and a two-stage adaptive inference pipeline routing most predictions through a quantized triage model and a minority to a larger expert.
AI.result: On well-separated sensor data (C-MAPSS), lightweight transformers match traditional ML at 87.8% F1 but with 100× larger model size and 9,000× higher latency. TinyBERT-4L is notably deployment-friendly at 55 MB and 18 ms CPU latency. INT8 quantization reduces size by about 25% while preserving 86.9% F1. The adaptive two-stage pipeline achieves 87.6% F1 with 19.5 ms average latency by routing 97.9% of predictions through a quantized triage model and only 2.1% to the larger expert. In severely imbalanced datasets (SECOM, UCI-PM), both traditional and transformer approaches perform poorly, highlighting fundamental limitations under extreme class imbalance. All code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: For well-separated fault data, lightweight transformers can match traditional ML accuracy but incur substantially higher resource usage; quantization and adaptive inference can help mitigate latency and size; however, extreme class imbalance remains a challenge for fault detection, underscoring a need for methods tailored to imbalanced data and deployment-aware architectures.

title: GUI vs. CLI: Execution Bottlenecks in Screen-Only and Skill-Mediated Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-use agents can execute software tasks through either graphical interfaces or programmatic command interfaces, but existing evaluations confound interaction modality with differences in tasks, initial states, verifiers, and permitted actions. We introduce a matched execution-layer benchmark of 440 desktop tasks across 18 applications and 12 workflow categories, where screen-only GUI agents and skill-mediated CLI agents receive identical goals, states, and final-state verifiers while being restricted to modality-native actions. In this controlled setting, the strongest GUI agent reaches a 59.1% full pass rate, outperforming the strongest original-skill CLI agent at 48.2%; however, verifier-guided skill augmentation raises CLI success to 69.3%, showing that much of the CLI deficit comes from incomplete skill coverage rather than model capability alone. These results suggest that GUI and CLI expose different execution bottlenecks: GUI agents are limited by reliable grounded interaction over long-horizon workflows, whereas CLI agents are limited by the coverage and scalability of their skill interfaces.

AI.tldr: Benchmark shows GUI vs CLI performance under matched tasks; CLI improves with verifier-guided skill augmentation; both modalities have distinct bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: Disentangle interaction modality from task differences by creating a matched execution-layer benchmark with identical goals, states, and verifiers for GUI and CLI agents.
AI.method: 440 desktop tasks across 18 apps and 12 workflow categories; compare screen-only GUI agents vs skill-mediated CLI agents with modality-native actions; assess pass rates; apply verifier-guided skill augmentation to CLI.
AI.result: GUI strongest full pass 59.1%; original-skill CLI strongest 48.2%; CLI with verifier-guided skill augmentation 69.3%; reveals bottlenecks: GUI limited by long-horizon grounded interaction; CLI limited by skill coverage/scalability.
AI.conclusion: GUI and CLI present complementary bottlenecks; improving CLI skill coverage can close or surpass GUI gap; matched benchmarks are essential to fairly compare modalities.

title: Social Structure Matters in 3D Human-Human Interaction Generation
summary: Although text-to-motion generation has achieved strong progress in synthesizing realistic single-person motions from language, extending it to text-driven 3D human-human interaction (HHI) remains non-trivial, as HHI requires modeling the underlying \textbf{social structure} that governs phase progression, actor roles, and inter-actor coordination. In this paper, we formulate HHI generation as a social structure modeling and grounding problem: the model must first infer how an interaction unfolds and how the two actors coordinate their roles, and then realize this structure as continuous, physically plausible, and partner-aware 3D motion. To study how such structure should be modeled, we first examine the capability boundary of large language models (LLMs) for HHI generation. Our analysis shows that LLMs can \textit{think} by recovering phase decompositions and partner-aware roles, but cannot directly \textit{move}, as they fail to generate dynamic, physically plausible, and interaction-aware motion. This motivates our planner-executor paradigm, \textbf{Think with LLM, Move with Motion Skill}. The LLM planner converts implicit interaction semantics into motion-aligned social supervision by decomposing interactions into phases, assigning partner-aware actor roles, and aligning them with motion sequence. The motion executor then grounds the planned social structure into coordinated two-person motion by adapting a pretrained solo motion model with LoRA, previous-phase self-conditioning, and ego-relative partner conditioning. Together, our Solo-to-Social framework bridges social organization and motion realization, producing 3D HHI with improved phase consistency, role alignment, and partner-aware coordination.

AI.tldr: Proposes a planner–executor framework for 3D two-person HHI by using an LLM to parse social structure (phases, roles, coordination) and a motion grounded executor to realize it. Demonstrates improved phase consistency, role alignment, and partner-aware coordination over solo motion baselines by adapting with LoRA and conditioning.
AI.motivation: Extend text-to-motion generation to 3D human–human interaction (HHI), addressing the need to model social structure (phase progression, actor roles, coordination) which solo motion models or plain text-to-motion methods struggle with.
AI.method: An LLM planner decomposes interactions into phases, assigns partner-aware actor roles, and aligns them with a motion sequence. A motion executor grounds the planned social structure into coordinated two-person motion by adapting a pretrained solo motion model using LoRA, previous-phase self-conditioning, and ego-relative partner conditioning.
AI.result: Presents 3D HHI with improved phase consistency, role alignment, and partner-aware coordination via the Solo-to-Social framework that bridges social organization and motion realization.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively bridges social organization (planning) and motion realization (execution), enabling more coherent two-person interactions in 3D HHI tasks.

title: Project Ariadne: Prompt-Conditioned Route Generation for Synthesis Planning
summary: Retrosynthetic planning seeks to connect a target molecule to commercially available starting materials through a multistep route. Classical planners construct such routes by iteratively applying single-step reaction models within a search procedure; constrained variants often require specialized algorithms or architectural changes. Direct route generation reframes retrosynthesis as sequence generation, but existing direct-generation methods still train separate models for different planning specifications. We introduce Ariadne, a decoder-only route generator that represents the target, optional constraints, and route in one prompt-completion sequence. On the RetroCast/PaRoutes mkt-cnv-160 benchmark family, one 24-layer checkpoint follows route-depth and required-starting-material prompts: adding the corresponding prompt fields raises Solv-0 by 13.7 points for depth constraints and 31.2 points for required-leaf constraints. Ariadne also improves over DESP, a bidirectional search planner, on required-leaf Top-10 and Solv-0 in 24 GPU-minutes versus 6.8 GPU-hours. On standard reconstruction, Ariadne is comparable to DMS Explorer XL at about half the reported inference time. Across additional target-only benchmarks, Ariadne's clearest gains are on route-holdout reconstruction, whereas AiZynthFinder MCTS remains stronger on several Solv-0 comparisons. These results extend sequence generation from specialist retrosynthesis models to prompt-conditioned structural route generation. We release the codebase and training scripts to support further work, but do not introduce Tier-1--3 route checkers; those remain the main bottleneck before models of this kind can become useful to experimental chemists.

AI.tldr: Ariadne is a decoder-only retrosynthesis route generator that encodes target and constraints in a single prompt, yielding notable benchmark gains and faster inference, but relies on external route checkers and shows mixed performance across tasks.
AI.motivation: To unify constraint handling in retrosynthesis by treating route planning as prompt-conditioned sequence generation, removing the need for separate planning modules or multiple models for different constraints.
AI.method: Ariadne uses a 24-layer decoder-only model that performs route generation via a single prompt-completion sequence, conditioning on target, route-depth constraints, and required starting-material constraints; evaluated on RetroCast/PaRoutes mkt-cnv-160 benchmarks and compared against DESP and other baselines.
AI.result: Significant improvements on required-leaf and depth constraints (Solv-0 up by 13.7 and 31.2 points respectively); faster vs DESP (24 GPU-minutes vs 6.8 GPU-hours) and competitive with DMS Explorer XL on standard reconstruction (about half inference time); gains mainly on route-holdout reconstruction, with AiZynthFinder MCTS stronger on several Solv-0 cases; codebase and training scripts released but no Tier-1–3 route checkers included.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that sequence-generation approaches can extend to prompt-conditioned, end-to-end retrosynthesis planning, but practical deployment awaits incorporation of robust route checkers and broader performance consistency across benchmarks.

title: Quant Convergence: Bridging Classical Value Investing and Modern Factor Models for Systematic Equity Selection
summary: Modern finance relies heavily on complex machine learning models to find patterns in the stock market. However, as these AI models get more complicated, they often memorize short-term market noise instead of finding companies with real, lasting value. We designed this research to test if Benjamin Graham's classic value investing rules could act as a mathematical "low-pass filter" to keep these modern models in check. We built three different sets of features - pure Graham rules, modern market factors, and a mix of both - and tested them against highly complex models (XGBoost and AutoGluon) using 20 years of S&P 500 data. By applying a strict buy-and-hold strategy over a four-year test period (March 2022 to March 2026), the results showed that more complex algorithms do not always win. While the AutoGluon model captured high returns (222.68%), it suffered a substantial 39.78% drop because it bought volatile tech stocks right before the market crashed. On the other hand, the pure Graham Random Forest achieved the highest overall return (232.13%) with much less risk (1.38 Calmar Ratio). Furthermore, the Combined Random Forest successfully mixed momentum with Graham's rules, making a 202.91% return while keeping the lowest maximum drop (34.53%) of any model tested. Ultimately, this research proves that Graham's "margin of safety" isn't outdated; it is actually a highly effective way to prevent modern AI from taking on too much risk.

AI.tldr: Graham's value-investing margin of safety acts as a low-pass filter that constrains modern ML stock-picking models.
AI.motivation: To test whether Benjamin Graham's classic value rules can mitigate overfitting and short-term noise memorization in complex ML models used for equity selection.
AI.method: Three feature sets (Graham rules only, modern market factors, and a mixed set) were evaluated with high-capacity models (XGBoost and AutoGluon) on 20 years of S&P 500 data. A strict buy-and-hold strategy over March 2022–March 2026 was used to compare performance and risk (Calmar ratio).
AI.result: AutoGluon achieved a high return (222.68%) but suffered a 39.78% drawdown during a tech stock crash. The pure Graham-based Random Forest yielded the highest overall return (232.13%) with relatively low risk (1.38 Calmar). The Combined Random Forest (momentum + Graham rules) delivered 202.91% return and the lowest maximum drawdown (34.53%), outperforming others on risk control.
AI.conclusion: Graham's margin of safety remains an effective risk-control mechanism for AI-driven investing, acting as a robust low-pass filter against volatile, overfit signals in modern ML models.

title: Trimming the Long-Tail of Visual World Modeling Evaluation
summary: Physical interactions follow a long-tailed distribution: a set of common and regular interactions dominates human experience and visual data, while a broad spectrum of rare and irregular interactions remains underrepresented. Although recent visual world models, including image and video generation models, achieve impressive realism on existing benchmarks, they primarily focus on simulating common physical interactions. This raises a central question: Do current visual world models internalize and generalize physical principles? In this work, we introduce Tailor-Bench, a benchmark that challenges world models to simulate irregular physical interactions. To enable systematic evaluation, we design three scenario modes that progressively challenge model reasoning: Regular scenarios reflect common tool-task pairs, Unconventional scenarios replace conventional tools with attribute-compatible substitutes to test affordance generalization, and Impossible scenarios introduce attribute-violating tools to probe constraint awareness. Additionally, we design two complementary settings under a unified evaluation protocol: predictive generation requires inferring outcomes without guidance, while descriptive generation specifies the target outcome for faithful realization. Our experimental results reveal a clear long-tail gap in physical world modeling: performance degrades from Regular to Unconventional and Impossible scenarios, indicating limited generalization beyond common interactions. Failure analysis further shows that models rely on superficial visual patterns: image models fail to realize correct state changes, while video models further suffer from temporal inconsistencies.

AI.tldr: Tailor-Bench reveals a long-tail gap in physical world modeling by evaluating models on Regular, Unconventional, and Impossible scenarios under predictive and descriptive generation, showing degraded performance and reliance on superficial cues in image and video models.
AI.motivation: To assess whether current visual world models internalize and generalize physical principles beyond common interactions and tools, addressing the underrepresented irregular long-tail of physical interactions.
AI.method: Introduce Tailor-Bench with three scenario modes (Regular: common tool-task pairs; Unconventional: substitutes affordance-compatible tools; Impossible: attribute-violating tools). Two evaluation settings (predictive generation and descriptive generation). Evaluate image and video models on physical interactions; conduct failure analysis focusing on state changes and temporal consistency.
AI.result: Models show a clear long-tail gap: performance declines from Regular to Unconventional to Impossible. Image models fail to realize state changes; video models exhibit temporal inconsistencies. Indicates limited generalization beyond common interactions and reliance on superficial visual cues.
AI.conclusion: Current visual world models struggle to generalize physical principles to irregular interactions; Tailor-Bench provides a targeted benchmark to reveal these deficiencies and guide future work toward robustness in affordance understanding and constraint awareness.

title: Managing Task Execution for Unknown Workloads in Batteryless IoT: A Hardware-Agnostic Evaluation
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Sustainable Computing
summary: In recent years, the Internet of Things (IoT) paradigm has been shifting toward batteryless, energy-harvesting architectures. Sustaining reliable operation in these systems requires intelligent management of highly volatile stored energy. As edge applications grow in complexity, traditional energy-aware schedulers struggle with unpredictable workloads due to their reliance on static execution thresholds or pre-measured, hardware-specific task profiles. To overcome this, we propose two novel, hardware-agnostic dynamic scheduling strategies treating applications as a "black box," requiring no prior energy information: a model-free Reinforcement Learning (RL) agent and an on-the-fly Approximated Prediction (AP) method. We evaluate these methods against an adaptive task rate approach (AsTAR) and optimized static thresholds using a custom-built, physically accurate simulation framework driven by real-world solar data and dynamic LoRa transmission profiles. Rather than claiming universal superiority, our analysis exposes the distinct operational trade-offs of each method: the AP approach delivers lightweight, near-oracle task throughput; the RL agent provides tunable survival-execution balancing; and AsTAR excels at execution pacing across long energy gaps. Finally, we demonstrate that while these advanced strategies provide critical resilience for severely constrained systems with small capacitors, devices with larger energy buffers can efficiently rely on simpler, less computationally expensive static policies.

AI.tldr: Two hardware-agnostic dynamic energy schedulers for batteryless IoT are proposed: a model-free reinforcement-learning (RL) agent and an on-the-fly Approximated Prediction (AP) method. They are evaluated against an adaptive task rate (AsTAR) and optimized static thresholds in a solar-data and LoRa-transmission-driven simulation. AP achieves near-oracle throughput, RL offers tunable survival-execution trade-offs, and AsTAR excels at pacing across long energy gaps; large energy buffers favor simpler static policies over complex strategies.
AI.motivation: Need to manage highly volatile, harvested energy in batteryless IoT devices with unpredictable workloads, where static thresholds or pre-measured task profiles fail to adapt. The goal is to develop hardware-agnostic, dynamic schedulers that do not rely on prior energy information.
AI.method: Introduce two dynamic, hardware-agnostic strategies: (1) a model-free RL agent and (2) an on-the-fly Approximated Prediction (AP) method. Evaluate against adaptive task rate (AsTAR) and optimized static thresholds using a custom, physically accurate simulation framework driven by real-world solar data and dynamic LoRa transmission profiles.
AI.result: AP yields lightweight, near-oracle task throughput; RL provides tunable balance between survival and execution; AsTAR performs best for pacing across long energy gaps. The strategies enhance resilience for severely energy-constrained systems with small capacitors, while devices with larger energy buffers can rely on simpler static policies.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic energy management strategies offer resilience for severely constrained batteryless IoT; the choice between AP, RL, and static policies depends on energy buffer size and the required balance between throughput, reliability, and energy conservation.

title: LLMs Prompted for Legal Context Object More: Overrefusal from Small On-Premises LLMs in Criminal Legal Context
summary:
While the validity of LLMs' use in the legal context remains subject to ethical and legal debate, legal professionals are already experimenting with personal LLMs, if only for translation and reformulation. However, even such a seemingly innocuous use can introduce biases through case processing speed if LLM assistants selectively refuse assistance on certain topics. To better anticipate such biases, we investigate several modern small LLMs that are most likely to be used as on-device assistants, to assess the impact of overrefusal on legal prompts.
  Surprisingly, we find that authority-style prefixes (``you are acting as an assistant of the national supreme court'', ``[...] defense lawyer'') systematically increase refusal rates by 2--20x over the no-prefix baseline, while a known role-play jailbreak prefix shows mixed effects, sharply increasing refusals in some models and barely shifting them in others. The finding suggests that small on-prem deployable LLMs are unstable under contextual framings that a real institutional user might naturally introduce, and further investigation is essential to minimize opportunities for bias.

AI.tldr: Small on-device LLMs show prompt-induced bias via language which increases refusals when authority-style framing is used, with mixed effects from jailbreak-prefix prompts.
AI.motivation: To assess bias risk in on-device legal assistants by testing how contextual prompts influence refusal behavior in small LLMs used for legal tasks.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation of several modern small LLMs on legal prompts, testing authority prefixes (e.g., 'you are acting as an assistant of the national supreme court', 'defense lawyer') and a jailbreak role-play prefix, and measuring refusal rates.
AI.result: Authority prefixes increase refusals by 2–20x across models; jailbreak prefixes yield mixed effects, significantly increasing refusals in some models and little to none in others; overall instability under contextual framing.
AI.conclusion: On-device LLMs are prone to bias through prompt context; further work is needed to minimize bias opportunities, improve prompt robustness, and ensure consistent, unbiased legal assistance.

title: 3DCarGen: Scalable 3D Car Generation via 3D-consistent Multi-view Synthesis
summary: High-quality 3D vehicle assets are essential for autonomous driving simulation. Although multi-view diffusion-based paradigms enable controllable single-image reconstruction, they typically produce limited viewpoints and exhibit cross-view geometric inconsistencies, thereby reducing reconstruction fidelity in real-world scenarios. In this work, we introduce 3DCarGen, a scalable single-view 3D car generation framework designed for real-world images by synthesizing an arbitrary number of 3D-consistent multi-view images. Specifically, given a single image as input, we first synthesize a set of images from fixed viewpoints. These images are then fed into a feed-forward reconstruction model, resulting in a coarse 3D representation based on 3D Gaussian Splatting. Conditioned on this explicit 3D prior, our multi-view diffusion model generates 3D-consistent images from arbitrary camera viewpoints. We further extend a fast mesh reconstruction algorithm by incorporating color-normal joint optimization to recover detailed and coherent 3D vehicle models from the synthesized dense views. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves robust geometric consistency and reconstruction fidelity compared to existing methods. Code and models will be released.

AI.tldr: 3DCarGen: a scalable single-view 3D car generation framework that synthesizes arbitrary numbers of 3D-consistent multi-view images from a single image using a two-stage process: initial fixed-view synthesis and coarse 3D reconstruction via 3D Gaussian Splatting, followed by 3D-consistent diffusion conditioning on the 3D prior, then fast color-normal optimized mesh reconstruction; demonstrates improved geometric consistency and fidelity on synthetic and real data; code/models to be released.
AI.motivation: High-quality 3D vehicle assets are essential for autonomous driving simulation. Existing multi-view diffusion methods produce limited viewpoints and cross-view geometric inconsistencies, reducing reconstruction fidelity in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: 1) Input a single image and synthesize a set of images from fixed viewpoints. 2) Use a feed-forward reconstruction model to obtain a coarse 3D representation via 3D Gaussian Splatting. 3) Condition a fast diffusion model on this 3D prior to generate 3D-consistent multi-view images from arbitrary camera viewpoints. 4) Apply a fast mesh reconstruction algorithm with color-normal joint optimization to recover detailed, coherent 3D vehicle models from the synthesized dense views.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show robust geometric consistency and higher reconstruction fidelity compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework achieves robust geometric consistency and reconstruction fidelity for 3D car generation from a single image, with code and models to be released.

title: Parallel Manifold Steering: Efficient Adaptation of Large Associative Memories via Residual Energy Shaping
summary: Large Transformer models function as Dense Associative Memories (DAMs), retrieving knowledge via high-dimensional attractor dynamics driven by the self-attention mechanism \citep{ramsauer2020hopfield, wu2024attention}. However, adapting these frozen memory systems to new tasks presents a fundamental ``Plasticity-Stability'' dilemma. Current methods either risk catastrophic interference by modifying synaptic weights directly (e.g., LoRA) \citep{hu2021lora} or degrade associative capacity by clogging the retrieval buffer with static prompt tokens (e.g., VPT) \citep{jia2022vpt}. In this work, we propose \textbf{H-Res} (Hierarchical Residual Steering), a mechanism that modulates the effective energy landscape of the Transformer without altering its global equilibrium or expanding its sequence length. By formulating adaptation as a control problem on the activation manifold \citep{chen2018neuralode}, H-Res learns a state-dependent vector field that steers token trajectories into task-specific basins of attraction. We formally prove that H-Res preserves the attention entropy of the foundation model and facilitates Neural Collapse \citep{papyan2020prevalence}. Empirically, Manifold Steering outperforms global weight modification by 26\% on associative retrieval tasks and eliminates the computational overhead of prompt-based methods, scaling effectively to structured domains \citep{zha2023vtab}.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Residual Steering (H-Res) uses a state-dependent vector field to steer Transformer activations, enabling task adaptation for Dense Associative Memory models without changing their global equilibrium or sequence length, surpassing weight-tuning and prompt-based methods.
AI.motivation: Addresses the Plasticity-Stability trade-off in adapting frozen DAMs: avoiding catastrophic interference from weight updates (e.g., LoRA) and avoiding retrieval-capacity loss from static prompts (e.g., VPT).
AI.method: Formulates adaptation as a control problem on the activation manifold and learns a hierarchical residual vector field that modulates the energy landscape, guiding token trajectories into task-specific basins of attraction; preserves attention entropy and supports Neural Collapse.
AI.result: Empirically, Manifold Steering (H-Res) outperforms global weight modification by 26% on associative retrieval tasks and eliminates prompt-based overhead, scaling effectively to structured domains.
AI.conclusion: H-Res provides a lightweight, scalable adaptation mechanism for frozen DAMs that maintains global equilibrium and sequence length while achieving robust, task-specific retrieval without catastrophic interference.

title: AdversaBench: Automated LLM Red-Teaming with Multi-Judge Confirmation and Cross-Model Transferability
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, 5 tables. Code and data at https://github.com/khanak0509/AdversaBench
summary: Scaling adversarial evaluation of large language models requires both a method for generating hard inputs and a reliable way to confirm that resulting failures are real. We present AdversaBench, an end-to-end red-teaming pipeline that mutates seed prompts with five structured operators, queries a target model, and confirms failures through a three-judge panel with a meta-judge tiebreaker. We report experiments on 45 seeds across three categories: reasoning, instruction-following, and tool use. Every seed produced a confirmed failure. Four findings stand out. First, operator effectiveness varies sharply by category: inject_distractor scores 0.00 mean reward on instruction-following seeds but 0.80-0.83 on reasoning and tool-use. Second, binary failure rate hides difficulty: instruction-following seeds required 2.4 attacker iterations on average versus 1.1 for other categories, a gap visible in survival curves. Third, pairwise judge agreement of 80-87% coexists with near-zero Cohen's kappa due to label skew; category-level disagreement rates are more informative. Fourth, adversarial prompts generated against Llama 3.1 8B transfer zero-shot to Llama 3.3 70B, suggesting the mutations exploit general behavioral patterns rather than model-specific weaknesses. Code, dataset, and analysis scripts are available at https://github.com/khanak0509/AdversaBench .

AI.tldr: AdversaBench is an end-to-end red-teaming pipeline that mutates seed prompts with five operators, queries a target LLM, and confirms failures via a three-judge panel (with a meta-judge). Across 45 seeds in reasoning, instruction-following, and tool-use, every seed produced a confirmed failure. Key findings: operator effectiveness is category-dependent; binary failure rates hide difficulty (instruction-following needs more attacker iterations); judge agreement is high but Cohen's kappa is near zero due to label skew; mutations transfer zero-shot between Llama models, indicating general, non-model-specific weaknesses. Code and data are publicly available.
AI.motivation: There is a need for scalable, reliable adversarial evaluation of large language models that not only generates hard inputs but also robustly confirms that observed failures are real, enabling reproducible comparisons and guiding robustness research.
AI.method: An end-to-end red-teaming workflow (AdversaBench) that mutates seed prompts using five structured operators, queries a target model, and uses a three-judge system with a meta-judge to confirm failures. Experiments run on 45 seeds across three categories: reasoning, instruction-following, and tool use.
AI.result: All seeds yielded confirmed failures. The four standout findings are: (1) operator effectiveness varies sharply by category (inject_distractor: 0.00 reward on instruction-following vs. 0.80–0.83 on reasoning/tool-use); (2) binary failure rate masks difficulty (instruction-following requires 2.4 attacker iterations on average vs. 1.1 for other categories); (3) pairwise judge agreement is 80–87% but Cohen's kappa is near zero due to skew; category-level disagreements are more informative; (4) adversarial prompts transfer zero-shot from Llama 3.1 8B to Llama 3.3 70B, suggesting general behavioral patterns rather than model-specific weaknesses.
AI.conclusion: AdversaBench offers a rigorous, scalable adversarial evaluation pipeline that reveals category-specific dynamics, highlights appropriate interpretation of agreement metrics, and points to generalizable vulnerability patterns across models. The authors provide code, data, and analyses at the linked repository.

title: MotifGen: Spatiotemporal interpolation of misaligned satellite images via multi-source generative modeling, in an application to tropical cyclones
summary: Microwave satellite imagery plays a crucial role in monitoring tropical cyclone precipitation and intensity worldwide, but suffers from long revisit times, potentially missing rapid storm evolution phases. While this raises the need for an interpolation method, it is made challenging by the high level of heterogeneity of microwave data coming from different instruments. In this work, we introduce the first generative model that can be applied to multiple geospatial sources that change across samples, occur at irregular time intervals, are misaligned geographically, and come from instruments with varying characteristics. We apply this model to the case of spatio-temporal interpolation of tropical cyclone microwave images from other microwave and infrared instruments. We train using a self-supervised task in which a random source is masked and reconstructed, and show that it leads to a significant decrease in Continuous Ranked Probability Score over supervised training. We show a further improvement by combining infrared and microwave data compared to microwave only. Using these improvements, the generative model produces an ensemble mean on par with that of a deterministic model, while generating a power spectrum significantly closer to that of true observations. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first generative model that interpolates microwave images of cyclones by combining multiple microwave instruments and infrared observations at irregular time intervals.

AI.tldr: A multi-source, irregular-time spatio-temporal interpolation model for cyclone microwave imagery, integrating microwave and infrared data via self-supervised masked reconstruction; achieves lower CRPS and better spectral fidelity than baselines, with ensemble mean comparable to deterministic models.
AI.motivation: Address long revisit times and heterogeneity of microwave satellite data across instruments, enabling accurate cyclone precipitation/intensity monitoring via cross-instrument data fusion and irregular sampling.
AI.method: A generative model that handles samples from different sources with varying time intervals and misalignment; trained with self-supervised masked reconstruction; extends to combining infrared and microwave observations.
AI.result: CRPS improves over supervised training; IR+ microwave improves beyond microwave-only; ensemble mean comparable to deterministic model; power spectrum closer to true observations.
AI.conclusion: First generative model interpolating microwave cyclone images across multiple instruments and infrared observations at irregular intervals; demonstrates benefits in probabilistic interpolation and spectral fidelity.

title: Natural Identifiers for Privacy and Data Audits in Large Language Models
summary: Assessing the privacy of large language models (LLMs) presents significant challenges. In particular, most existing methods for auditing differential privacy require the insertion of specially crafted canary data during training, making them impractical for auditing already-trained models without costly retraining. Additionally, dataset inference, which audits whether a suspect dataset was used to train a model, is infeasible without access to a private non-member held-out dataset. Yet, such held-out datasets are often unavailable or difficult to construct for real-world cases since they have to be from the same distribution (IID) as the suspect data. These limitations severely hinder the ability to conduct scalable, post-hoc audits. To enable such audits, this work introduces natural identifiers (NIDs) as a novel solution to the above-mentioned challenges. NIDs are structured random strings, such as cryptographic hashes and shortened URLs, naturally occurring in common LLM training datasets. Their format enables the generation of unlimited additional random strings from the same distribution, which can act as alternative canaries for audits and as same-distribution held-out data for dataset inference. Our evaluation highlights that indeed, using NIDs, we can facilitate post-hoc differential privacy auditing without any retraining and enable dataset inference for any suspect dataset containing NIDs without the need for a private non-member held-out dataset.

AI.tldr: Natural identifiers (NIDs) embedded in LLM training data enable scalable post-hoc differential privacy auditing and dataset inference without retraining or private held-out data.
AI.motivation: Auditing DP for large language models is hindered by the need for specially crafted canaries and access to private held-out datasets, making post-hoc audits impractical. A method that works with existing data and distributions is needed.
AI.method: Introduce structured random strings (NIDs) such as cryptographic hashes and shortened URLs that occur naturally in training data. Their format allows generating unlimited additional strings from the same distribution, serving as alternative canaries and as same-distribution held-out data for dataset inference. Use NIDs to perform post-hoc DP auditing and dataset inference without retraining or private hold-out data.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows NIDs enable post-hoc differential privacy auditing without retraining and enable dataset inference for suspect datasets containing NIDs without the need for a private non-member held-out dataset.
AI.conclusion: NIDs provide a practical, scalable framework for post-hoc privacy auditing of LLMs, reducing reliance on retraining and private held-out data. Their effectiveness relies on the presence of NIDs in the data; future work should address robustness to obfuscation, broader applicability, and potential privacy risks associated with NIDs.

title: ASALT: Adaptive State Alignment for Lateral Transfer in Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) addresses the problem of training multiple agents that pursue collaborative, competitive, or mixed objectives. Prior work has investigated transfer learning between source and target domains in MARL; however, the majority of existing approaches impose the constraint that the dimensionalities of the observation space and the global state space must be identical across domains. In this paper, we introduce a method that explicitly accommodates mismatched state-space dimensionalities between source and target domains. The proposed approach, ASALT, incorporates both observation-level and state-level adapters that map the target-domain observations and global states into a shared embedding space, thereby enabling more effective transfer of knowledge across both actors and critics. These adapters can generate embeddings that support efficient strategy transfer across heterogeneous domains. Experimental results on multiple configurations in standard benchmark environments demonstrate that ASALT surpasses existing baselines in terms of sample efficiency and global return in cooperative settings, but its effectiveness depends on the degree of mismatch between source and target domains. Furthermore, our findings indicate that ASALT mitigates negative transfer, which frequently constitutes a major obstacle when transferring policies between domains with differing observation and action spaces.

AI.tldr: ASALT enables MARL transfer across domains with mismatched state spaces by using observation- and state-level adapters to map target-domain data into a shared embedding, improving sample efficiency and cooperative performance, while mitigating negative transfer; effectiveness depends on the degree of domain mismatch.
AI.motivation: Current MARL transfer methods typically require identical observation and state dimensionalities, limiting cross-domain knowledge transfer. There is a need to handle heterogeneous state spaces to enable shared learning of actors and critics.
AI.method: ASALT introduces both observation-level and state-level adapters that transform target-domain observations and global states into a common embedding space. This shared embedding supports transfer of policies for actors and value functions for critics across heterogeneous domains.
AI.result: ASALT outperforms baselines in sample efficiency and global return in cooperative settings. Its effectiveness varies with the mismatch degree between source and target domains, and it helps mitigate negative transfer.
AI.conclusion: ASALT provides a principled framework for cross-domain transfer in MARL with mismatched state spaces, enabling knowledge transfer through learned embeddings. Its benefits depend on the mismatch level, prompting further study of adapter design and domain characterization.

title: UniRED: Unified RGB-D Video Frame Interpolation with Event Guidance
summary: High frame-rate RGB-D videos are crucial for a variety of downstream tasks, including motion analysis, dynamic scene understanding, and 3D reconstruction. However, due to hardware and sensing constraints, practical RGB-D cameras are typically limited to low frame rates, making it difficult to capture rapid scene dynamics. Existing video interpolation methods have achieved strong performance on RGB data, but they are not readily applicable to RGB-D scenarios, where they often yield blurry boundaries, visible artifacts, and degraded geometric consistency. Furthermore, motion estimation from only two boundary frames is inherently under-constrained in complex dynamic scenes. Event cameras, by contrast, provide asynchronous measurements with ultra-high temporal resolution, offering dense motion cues. In this paper, we propose a unified multimodal framework for RGB-D video interpolation that jointly exploits RGB appearance, depth geometry, and event-based temporal cues. Specifically, it first extracts and fuses RGB, depth and event cues, then estimates bidirectional flow with motion basis refinement for RGB and Z-axial refinement for depth, and finally synthesizes the target RGB-D frame via bidirectional warping and soft blending. In addition, we construct a new RGB-D-Event dataset to alleviate the scarcity of tri-modal training data. Extensive experiments on a public benchmark and the proposed dataset demonstrate that our method achieves superior photometric fidelity for RGB interpolation and stronger geometric accuracy for depth interpolation than existing approaches.

AI.tldr: Unified multimodal RGB-D video interpolation using RGB, depth, and event cues with bidirectional flow and depth refinement; introduces RGB-D-Event dataset; achieves better photometric and geometric fidelity.
AI.motivation: RGB-D cameras suffer from low frame rates; existing RGB-based interpolation struggles to preserve depth geometry; event cameras provide high-temporal-resolution motion cues enabling better interpolation in dynamic scenes.
AI.method: Extract and fuse RGB, depth, and event cues; estimate bidirectional flow with motion basis refinement for RGB and Z-axial refinement for depth; synthesize target RGB-D frame via bidirectional warping and soft blending; train/evaluate on public benchmark and a newly introduced RGB-D-Event dataset.
AI.result: Outperforms existing approaches in photometric fidelity for RGB interpolation and geometric accuracy for depth interpolation; robust to rapid dynamics across both benchmark and proposed dataset.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that combining RGB, depth, and event information yields improved RGB-D interpolation; the RGB-D-Event dataset mitigates tri-modal data scarcity and supports future research on multimodal dynamic scene reconstruction.

title: Data Augmentation: A Fourier Analysis Perspective
summary: Data augmentation is a simple and model-agnostic approach for exploiting known invariances in learning problems. Given a group acting on the input space, one augments the training set with transformed copies of each sample. Because it exploits symmetries without modifying the underlying learning algorithm, data augmentation can be applied broadly across learning methods. However, this universality comes at a computational cost: when the group is large, full group-sized augmentation quickly becomes computationally infeasible. This raises a fundamental question: Can partial data augmentation achieve the same statistical benefits as full augmentation in terms of generalization and sample complexity? We develop a general framework for investigating this question using Fourier analysis and the representation theory of finite groups. We show that, for a broad class of classical learning problems, partial data augmentation based on a randomly sampled subset of group elements achieves the same minimax rates as full augmentation, up to an approximation error that vanishes as the subset size increases. Our results provide a theoretical explanation for why partial augmentation can retain the statistical benefits of full augmentation despite enforcing symmetry only approximately, and shed light on a recently raised question in learning with symmetries: whether statistically optimal learning under general group invariances can be achieved using computationally scalable methods. Moreover, we prove a complementary impossibility result: enforcing exact invariance via data augmentation requires averaging over the entire group, and cannot be achieved by any strict subset when the hypothesis space is sufficiently expressive. Together, these results provide a unified perspective on full and partial data augmentation, as well as exact and approximate symmetry enforcement.

AI.tldr: Partial data augmentation with a randomly sampled subset of group elements can match full augmentation’s minimax rates (up to a vanishing approximation error); exact invariance via augmentation requires averaging over the whole group.
AI.motivation: To determine whether computationally scalable, approximate symmetry enforcement via partial augmentation can retain the statistical benefits of full augmentation, and to connect this to the theory of learning with group invariances.
AI.method: Apply Fourier analysis and finite-group representation theory to derive minimax rates for learning with partial augmentation; compare with full augmentation; prove an impossibility result that exact invariance cannot be achieved by any strict subset.
AI.result: For a broad class of classical learning problems, partial augmentation with a randomly sampled subgroup achieves the same minimax rates as full augmentation up to a vanishing approximation error that shrinks as the subset size grows; there is a complementary impossibility showing that exact invariance requires averaging over the entire group.
AI.conclusion: Partial augmentation offers nearly optimal generalization with scalable computation; exact symmetry enforcement via augmentation remains feasible only with full-group averaging. The work unifies full/partial and exact/approximate symmetry enforcement and explains the conditions under which partial augmentation is statistically equivalent to full augmentation.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Longitudinal Forecasting of Alzheimer's Disease Progression Using Deep Learning
summary: Longitudinal modelling of Alzheimer's disease progression is clinically useful only if it can describe not just the most likely next diagnosis, but how a patient may evolve over time and how reliable that forecast is. Most deep learning approaches reduce this problem to single-step classification, treating cognitively normal, mild cognitive impairment, and dementia as flat categories while providing limited insight into how uncertainty accumulates across future visits. We propose a probabilistic framework that combines ordinal diagnosis prediction, multi-horizon trajectory generation, and decomposed uncertainty estimation. A Temporal Fusion Transformer encoder is adapted with a CORAL ordinal output layer, asymmetric loss weighting, and converter oversampling to respect disease-stage ordering and improve sensitivity to MCI-to-dementia transitions. Conditioned on the learned patient-context representation, an autoregressive Mixture Density Network generates five-year probabilistic trajectories for diagnosis state, CDR Sum of Boxes, MMSE orientation, and hippocampal volume. On ADNI, the model outperforms linear, recurrent, and transformer baselines for next-visit diagnosis prediction, with the strongest gains on MCI-versus-dementia discrimination. Generated trajectories achieve near-nominal 90% credible interval coverage, widening uncertainty across the forecast horizon, and biomarker dynamics consistent with expected Alzheimer's disease progression. We further separate aleatoric from epistemic uncertainty using analytic mixture variance and a five-member bootstrap ensemble, which provides the strongest encoder diversity and output-level epistemic signal. Epistemic uncertainty is higher for rare progression archetypes, MCI and dementia patients, and under external evaluation on OASIS-3, where it increases alongside prediction error.

AI.tldr: Probabilistic, multi-horizon AD progression forecasting using a Temporal Fusion Transformer with CORAL ordinal outputs and autoregressive Mixture Density Networks, achieving calibrated trajectories and decomposed uncertainty on ADNI and OASIS-3.
AI.motivation: Move beyond single-step classification of AD stages to long-range, uncertainty-aware trajectories; address ordinal disease progression, how uncertainty accumulates over time, and improve sensitivity to MCI-to-dementia transitions.
AI.method: Adapted Temporal Fusion Transformer with CORAL ordinal output layer, asymmetric loss weighting, and converter oversampling to enforce disease-stage ordering; conditioned on learned patient-context embeddings; autoregressive Mixture Density Network generates five-year probabilistic trajectories for diagnosis state, CDR-Sum of Boxes, MMSE orientation, and hippocampal volume; decomposed uncertainty via analytic mixture variance and five-member bootstrap ensemble.
AI.result: On ADNI, outperforms linear, RNN, and vanilla transformer baselines for next-visit diagnosis prediction, with notable gains in MCI-to-dementia discrimination; trajectories show near-nominal 90% credible interval coverage and biomarker patterns aligned with AD progression; epistemic uncertainty higher for rare progression archetypes and in dementia/MCI groups, increasing with prediction error on external OASIS-3 evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates accurate, calibrated multi-horizon forecasts and meaningful uncertainty decomposition, enabling better clinical interpretation and risk assessment; encourages broader use of probabilistic, ordinal, and trajectory-based modeling for neurodegenerative disease progression.

title: ActiveScope: Actively Seeking and Correcting Perception for MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated impressive vision-language understanding, yet still struggle with fine-grained perception in high-resolution images. While existing training-free methods typically rely on attention-based localization or coarse-to-fine search, they are often misled by distractors and fail to locate multiple targets. Our investigation attributes these failures to Contextual Dominance, where salient distractors overwhelm target attention and cause inaccurate localization, and Semantic Bias, where global semantics cause the model to fixate on the most salient concept, resulting in incomplete localization in multi-object scenarios. Built on these insights, we propose ActiveScope, a training-free framework that enhances MLLMs by actively seeking and correcting perception. ActiveScope features two modules. The Semantic Anchor Localization (SAL) utilizes fine-grained semantic anchors to independently localize key targets, thereby mitigating semantic bias. The Interference-Suppressed Refinement (ISR) refines localization by suppressing attention on salient distractions to overcome contextual dominance. Extensive experiments on high-resolution image understanding benchmarks demonstrate that ActiveScope outperforms existing training-free methods (e.g., 96.34 percent accuracy on $V^{*}$ Bench), validating the superiority of the active search and self-correction paradigm. Our code is available at https://github.com/jasmine-ww/ActiveScope.

AI.tldr: ActiveScope is a training-free framework that improves multimodal LLM perception on high-resolution images by addressing Contextual Dominance and Semantic Bias via two modules: Semantic Anchor Localization (SAL) and Interference-Suppressed Refinement (ISR); it achieves state-of-the-art results on V*Bench (96.34% accuracy).
AI.motivation: Fine-grained localization in high-resolution imagery is hampered in current MLLMs by distractor-driven contextual dominance and global semantic bias, leading to incomplete or incorrect multi-target localization. Training-free remedies are desirable for robust, plug-and-play enhancement.
AI.method: ActiveScope introduces two modules: (1) Semantic Anchor Localization (SAL) that uses fine-grained semantic anchors to independently localize key targets and reduce semantic bias; (2) Interference-Suppressed Refinement (ISR) that refines localization by suppressing attention to salient distractors, mitigating contextual dominance. The framework operates in a training-free, active-search, self-correction manner.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on high-resolution image understanding benchmarks shows that ActiveScope outperforms existing training-free methods, achieving example-level gains such as 96.34% accuracy on the V*Bench benchmark. The authors also provide a public codebase.
AI.conclusion: ActiveScope demonstrates that an active search and self-correction paradigm can substantially improve fine-grained localization in MLLMs without additional training, particularly in multi-object and high-resolution settings; this approach provides a path toward more robust, generalizable vision-language models.

title: An LLM-based Two-Stage Transformer Framework for Cross-Domain Bearing Fault Diagnosis with Limited Data
comment: Accepted as a conference article of AIM 2026
summary: Bearing fault diagnosis faces critical challenges when dataset heterogeneity, operating condition variations, and limited labeled data occur simultaneously in industrial environments. Existing approaches address these issues in isolation and rely on implicit feature alignment, limiting effectiveness under concurrent challenges. This paper proposes a knowledge-guided two-stage transfer learning framework that employs a lightweight GPT-2-style Transformer with causal self-attention for hierarchical feature extraction from vibration signals, establishing explicit pathways where pre-trained encoder weights and fault prototype embeddings serve as knowledge carriers from multi-source pre-training to target adaptation. The framework addresses the dual-shift challenge through multi-source learning for generalizable representations, prototype-based knowledge modulation for target adaptation, and taxonomy-adaptive classification for seamless transfer across heterogeneous fault categories. Experimental validation on four real-world datasets demonstrates 92.61% average accuracy with only 10% labeled target data, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by 17.24 percentage points, establishing a practical pathway toward cost-effective predictive maintenance in Industry 4.0 applications.

AI.tldr: A knowledge-guided two-stage transfer learning framework using a lightweight GPT-2-style Transformer for bearing fault diagnosis, leveraging multi-source pre-training and prototype embeddings to handle dataset heterogeneity, operating condition variations, and limited labels; achieves 92.61% avg accuracy with 10% labeled data, beating SOTA by 17.24 pp.
AI.motivation: Address simultaneous challenges of data heterogeneity, operating condition shifts, and label scarcity; existing methods tackle issues in isolation and rely on implicit feature alignment, limiting performance under combined shifts; a knowledge-driven transfer mechanism with explicit pathways is needed.
AI.method: A two-stage transfer learning framework: multi-source pre-training with encoder weights and fault prototype embeddings acting as knowledge carriers; a lightweight GPT-2-style Transformer with causal self-attention for hierarchical feature extraction from vibration signals; taxonomy-adaptive classification; prototype-based knowledge modulation for target adaptation; explicit knowledge pathways to transfer across heterogeneous fault categories.
AI.result: Validated on four real-world datasets with 92.61% average accuracy using only 10% labeled target data; uses targeted multi-source learning to obtain generalizable representations and prototype guidance to adapt to the target taxonomy, outperforming state-of-the-art by 17.24 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates a practical, cost-effective pathway for predictive maintenance in Industry 4.0, enabling robust transfer under concurrent data shifts and limited labels; it provides a framework for generalizable representations and adaptable fault taxonomy across heterogeneous industrial settings.

title: ScaleToT: Generalizing Structured LLM Reasoning for Billion-Scale Low-Activity User Modeling
summary: Accurate user modeling often depends on rich interaction histories, which are unavailable for billions of low-activity users. Large Language Models (LLMs) can infer latent user states from static profiles, but this reasoning becomes unreliable when profiles are sparse, and applying an LLM to billions of users is prohibitively expensive. We present ScaleToT, which learns structured reasoning from a small LLM-processed subset and extends it to the broader low-activity user population. To improve reasoning reliability, ScaleToT constructs typed user-state chains with a bounded entropy-guided Tree-of-Thought (ToT) refinement procedure. To make this structured reasoning usable from sparse profiles, the teacher-curated chains are used to train a student model on static profiles through supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and Outcome-Driven Segment-Aware Implicit Reward Policy Optimization (OSIPO).
