ScaleToT then transfers the student's reasoning representations to a lightweight profile encoder, providing shared reasoning signals for the remaining users without LLM inference. We evaluate ScaleToT on lifetime value (LTV) prediction in a billion-scale advertising deployment. A randomized online A/B test increased LT30 by 6.738\%, while offline reasoning covered only 7.32\% of the potential population, greatly reducing compute cost compared with full-population reasoning.

AI.tldr: ScaleToT uses a small LLM to learn structured reasoning on a subset, trains a student on static profiles, and transfers reasoning to a lightweight encoder to serve billions of low-activity users, achieving scalable, cost-efficient LTV improvements.
AI.motivation: Low-activity users have sparse histories; LLM inference on billions is too costly; need reliable reasoning from static data.
AI.method: Teacher-student pipeline: curated user-state chains with entropy-guided Tree-of-Thought (ToT); supervised fine-tuning on static profiles; OSIPO reward optimization; transfer of reasoning to a lightweight profile encoder; apply reasoning to remaining users without LLM inference.
AI.result: Online A/B increased LT30 by 6.738%; offline reasoning covers only 7.32% of population; substantial compute savings vs full-population inference.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates scalable, structured reasoning for lifecycle value under resource constraints; effective way to extend LLM-derived reasoning to broad, low-activity user bases.

title: Hierarchical Spatial and Channel Aggregation for Cross-domain Few-shot Segmentation
summary: Cross-domain Few-shot Segmentation (CD-FSS) aims to learn generalizable segmentation capability from abundant annotated samples in the source domain, enabling accurate segmentation of novel classes in the target domain with only a few annotated samples. Existing CD-FSS methods mainly focus on mitigating feature distribution shifts caused by style gaps while ignoring significant differences in class semantic granularity and discriminative attributes across domains, leading to two key degradations in support-query matching: semantic over-alignment and attribute over-alignment. To this end, we propose the Dual Hierarchical Aggregation Network (DHANet), which comprises three key modules. First, the Hierarchical Spatial Aggregation (HSA) module performs multi-scale region aggregation of pixel features along the spatial dimension, generating hierarchical semantic-enhanced features to alleviate semantic over-alignment. Additionally, the HCA module conducts multi-scale attribute aggregation along the channel dimension, generating hierarchical attribute-enhanced features to mitigate attribute over-alignment. Finally, we propose the Online Probabilistic Semantic Bank (OPSB), which progressively constructs and updates class probability distributions from query predictions during inference, and samples multiple pseudo-prototypes as additional support information to mitigate insufficient support. Extensive experiments on four target-domain datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: DHANet enhances cross-domain few-shot segmentation with hierarchical spatial/attribute aggregation and an online probabilistic semantic bank, achieving state-of-the-art results while mitigating semantic and attribute over-alignment.
AI.motivation: CD-FSS suffers from cross-domain gaps not only in feature distributions but also in semantic granularity and discriminative attributes between domains, causing semantic over-alignment and attribute over-alignment which degrade support-query matching.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Hierarchical Spatial Aggregation (HSA) for multi-scale region-based spatial feature aggregation to boost semantic discrimination; (2) Hierarchical Attribute Aggregation (HCA) for multi-scale channel-wise attribute aggregation to reduce attribute over-alignment; (3) Online Probabilistic Semantic Bank (OPSB) that progressively builds class-probability distributions from query predictions during inference and samples multiple pseudo-prototypes as supplementary support.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four target-domain datasets shows state-of-the-art performance, validating the effectiveness of DHANet in mitigating both semantic and attribute misalignments in CD-FSS.
AI.conclusion: DHANet offers a principled solution to semantic and attribute over-alignment in CD-FSS by combining hierarchical regional/attribute aggregation with an online probabilistic memory, yielding robust cross-domain segmentation with limited support.

title: A Fair Evaluation of Graph Foundation Models for Node Property Prediction
comment: Accepted at The Workshop on Graph Foundation Models at ICML 2026
summary: Due to the wide use of graph-structured data in different fields of industry and science, the development of Graph Foundation Models (GFMs) has recently attracted a lot of attention. While many different types of models are called GFMs, particular interest has been paid to GFMs designed for node property prediction tasks, which is one of the most popular settings in Graph ML with lots of real-world applications from fraud detection in financial and social networks to recommendation systems for e-commerce and user-generated content platforms. While a number of GFMs for this task have been recently proposed, the field has not converged to a unified evaluation setting, and different works evaluate their models in widely different ways, preventing reliable comparison of GFMs with each other and with other types of models. In this work, we conduct a fair and rigorous reevaluation of 9 recent GFMs for node property prediction, comparing them to strong Graph Neural Network (GNN) baselines. We find that, among these GFMs, only the most recent ones based on the Prior-data Fitted Networks paradigm outperform well-tuned GNNs in predictive performance, although at a higher inference cost.

AI.tldr: Only the latest PDFN-based GFMs outperform strong GNN baselines on node property prediction, but at higher inference cost; the evaluation landscape is inconsistent across works.
AI.motivation: Graph data are central in many domains; GFMs for node property prediction have proliferated, but lack a unified, fair evaluation to enable reliable comparisons with GNNs.
AI.method: Reevaluate 9 recent GFMs for node property prediction under a fair and rigorous evaluation setting, comparing them to strong GNN baselines across standard benchmarks.
AI.result: Among the evaluated GFMs, only the most recent ones based on the Prior-data Fitted Networks (PDFN) paradigm surpass well-tuned GNNs in predictive performance, and this comes with higher inference cost; other GFMs do not consistently beat GNNs.
AI.conclusion: GFMs can surpass GNNs in node property prediction but with notable trade-offs in inference efficiency; the field lacks standardized evaluation, underscoring the need for unified benchmarks for reliable comparison.

title: AI Tokenomics: The Economics of Tokens, Computation, and Pricing in Foundation Models
summary: Tokens have become the practical accounting unit for modern foundation model services, linking information processing, computation, memory use, energy expenditure, pricing, and economic value. This paper develops a framework for AI tokenomics: the study of how tokens are generated, consumed, priced, allocated, and optimized across AI systems. We connect token-level technical costs to workflow-level production functions, enterprise resource allocation, measurement and instrumentation methods, and emerging market-design questions. The framework shows that token expenditure and economic value are distinct: value depends on marginal productivity, workflow position, hidden reasoning activity, risk, and downstream propagation effects. The paper concludes by identifying open research directions in hidden-token measurement, empirical calibration, token productivity, dynamic allocation, and token-based markets.

AI.tldr: A framework redefining tokens as the accounting unit of AI systems, showing token spend ≠ value and outlining open research on token measurement, calibration, productivity, and markets.
AI.motivation: Understand how tokens underpin cost, pricing, and allocation in foundation-model services and how they relate to workflow productivity and market design.
AI.method: Conceptual framework linking token-level technical costs to workflow production functions, resource allocation, measurement, and market-design questions; analyzes determinants of value beyond expenditure.
AI.result: Distinguishes token expenditure from value, maps costs to production and market mechanisms, and outlines open directions (hidden-token measurement, empirical calibration, token productivity, dynamic allocation, token-based markets).
AI.conclusion: Provides a scaffolding for empirical study and system design, calling for measurement tools and market mechanisms to align token costs with realized productivity and value.

title: Training-free Cross-domain Few-shot Segmentation via Robust Semantic Representation and Matching
summary: Cross-domain Few-shot Segmentation (CD-FSS) aims to transfer knowledge learned from source domain to distinct target domains, segmenting unseen target classes with only a few annotated samples. Although existing methods have made significant progress, they still rely on training or fine-tuning processes, which incur high computational costs and risk overfitting. We observe that when powerful and general-purpose vision foundation models are incorporated into these methods, their performance shows only marginal improvement or even degrades due to overfitting. To address this, we eliminate trainable parameters and propose a training-free framework to avoid both training overhead and overfitting. Built upon the self-supervised vision encoder DINOv3, our framework addresses cross-domain challenges through three core modules. First, the Semantic-aware Feature Re-fusion (SAFR) module identifies and re-fuses features that emphasize semantic patterns, generating representations with enhanced semantic discriminability. Additionally, the Adaptive Support Enhancement (ASE) module narrows semantic gaps between support and query through robust query information aggregation. Finally, the Hybrid Prototype Matching (HPM) module integrates matching results from diverse prototypes to adapt to varying semantic complexity across domains. Extensive experiments on four target domain datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in CD-FSS without any training.

AI.tldr: A training-free CD-FSS method that leverages a self-supervised DINOv3 encoder with SAFR, ASE, and HPM to achieve SOTA performance without training.
AI.motivation: To reduce training cost and overfitting in cross-domain few-shot segmentation, addressing limitations of large vision foundation models that may degrade performance; propose a training-free, inference-only framework.
AI.method: Utilize the self-supervised encoder DINOv3 as backbone and introduce three modules with no trainable parameters: (1) SAFR to re-fuse features emphasizing semantic patterns; (2) ASE to narrow semantic gaps between support and query via robust query information aggregation; (3) HPM to fuse results from multiple prototypes for varying semantic complexity across domains.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four target-domain datasets show state-of-the-art performance in CD-FSS without any training.
AI.conclusion: The training-free framework demonstrates that semantic-aware feature fusion, adaptive support enhancement, and hybrid prototype matching, when built on a self-supervised encoder, can surpass training-based methods in CD-FSS while eliminating training costs and overfitting risk.

title: Reasoning as Attractor Dynamics: Latent Memory Retrieval via Gibbs-Weighted Energy Minimization
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are traditionally viewed as autoregressive generators. However, from the perspective of collective computation, they function as high-dimensional Dense Associative Memories that store complex reasoning patterns as latent attractors. In this work, we investigate the energy landscape of mathematical reasoning. We posit that correct reasoning chains correspond to deep, wide attractor basins ("flat minima") in the model's output distribution, whereas hallucinations manifest as sharp, unstable local minima. To exploit this geometry, we introduce a retrieval mechanism based on a Gibbs measure of the trajectory's spectral entropy. By sampling multiple reasoning paths and weighting them by their inverse energy ($P \propto e^{-βE}$), we approximate the equilibrium distribution of the associative memory, effectively ``relaxing'' the system into a robust solution. Empirically, this physics-inspired mechanism improves Microsoft Phi-3.5 performance on GSM8K by 5.38\% (84.7\% $\to$ 90.1\%), demonstrating that inference is better modeled as a dynamic settling process into an attractor basin rather than greedy next-token prediction.

AI.tldr: Treats LLM reasoning as navigating an energy landscape of attractors; proposes Gibbs-based path sampling to relax to robust solutions, yielding accuracy gains on GSM8K.
AI.motivation: Address reasoning reliability and hallucinations by interpreting model outputs as attractor basins in a high-dimensional energy landscape and leveraging dynamical relaxation to retrieve robust solutions.
AI.method: Characterize the output distribution as an energy landscape; identify flat (robust) vs sharp (fragile) minima via spectral entropy of trajectories. Introduce a retrieval mechanism that samples multiple reasoning paths and weights them by inverse energy (P ∝ e^{-βE}) to approximate the equilibrium distribution of an associative memory. Apply this dynamic relaxation to Microsoft Phi-3.5 on GSM8K.
AI.result: Empirical improvement on GSM8K from 84.7% to 90.1% accuracy (5.38 percentage points) demonstrating that inference benefits from settling into attractor basins rather than greedy next-token prediction.
AI.conclusion: A physics-inspired view of LLM reasoning yields practical gains by treating inference as a dynamical relaxation process to robust attractor basins; suggests broader applicability of energy-based retrieval and raises questions about scalability, computation, and applicability to other tasks.

title: Abstractions of Queries in Ontology-Based Data Access
comment: Extended version of a paper published in the proceedings of KR 2025
summary:
In ontology-based data access (OBDA), multiple data sources are integrated via mappings to an ontology. We consider an OBDA setting based on existential rules and the certain answer semantics. We address the recent issue of query abstraction, which consists of abstracting data queries by translating them to the ontology layer. Since a perfect abstraction may not exist, the notions of minimally complete and maximally sound abstractions have been introduced.
  We study abstractions within an extension of UCQs with a limited form of inequality and a special predicate marking database constants. While this extension does not lead to an increased complexity of the problems of interest, it is able to express minimally complete abstractions, hence perfect abstractions when they exist. We also characterize maximally sound abstractions by making a new connection with the notion of maximum recovery stemming from data exchange.

AI.tldr: Framework for query abstraction in OBDA using existential rules under certain answer semantics. Introduces minimally complete and maximally sound abstractions via an extended UCQ language with limited inequality and a predicate for database constants; preserves complexity; can express minimally complete (hence perfect) abstractions; links maximally sound abstractions to maximum recovery from data exchange.
AI.motivation: Address the problem of query abstraction in OBDA by providing expressive abstractions and precise semantics that can capture minimal completeness and maximal soundness, while analyzing complexity and linking to data exchange concepts.
AI.method: Extend UCQs with a constrained form of inequality and a special predicate to mark database constants. Study abstractions within this extended language under existential-rule OBDA with certain-answer semantics. Prove that this extension can express minimally complete abstractions and that maximally sound abstractions can be characterized via maximum recovery from data exchange.
AI.result: The extension does not increase the complexity of the problems of interest. It can express minimally complete abstractions (thus enabling perfect abstractions when they exist). Maximally sound abstractions are characterized by a connection to maximum recovery from data exchange.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a formal framework for OBDA query abstraction, showing that a carefully constrained extension of UCQs suffices to capture minimally complete abstractions and to relate maximally sound abstractions to maximum recovery, without increasing computational complexity.

title: MM-TRELLIS: Point-Cloud Guided Multi-Modal 3D Vehicle Generation in Autonomous Driving
summary: Recovering realistic 3D vehicle models from autonomous driving scenes is crucial for synthesizing training data and building simulation environment. However, most existing vehicle generation methods fail to fully exploit multimodal sensors i.e. multi-view images and LiDAR point clouds) and rely on neural rendering based reconstruction, leading to low-quality mesh. Recently, native 3D generative models have made significant progress, yet they are not built for arbitrary multi-view inputs and often struggle with in-the-wild driving images. In this work, we present MM-TRELLIS, a multi-modal version of TRELLIS for in-the-wild 3D vehicle generation that integrates LiDAR and image sensors from autonomous driving datasets into native 3D generative models. Specifically, multi-view images are cycled as conditioning inputs, while LiDAR point clouds provide test-time guidance to ensure geometric accuracy and cross-view consistency. During denoising, we first align the guidance point cloud with the model priors, then enforce consistency between the generated geometry and the guidance point cloud. Finally, we introduce a voxel filtering strategy based on the opacity of 3D Gaussian Splatting to suppress floaters and produce clean meshes. Comprehensive experiments on Waymo dataset demonstrate our method outperforms existing methods in high-fidelity 3D vehicle generation. Code is available at https://github.com/HongliXiao/MM-TRELLIS.

AI.tldr: MM-TRELLIS is a multi-modal 3D vehicle generator that fuses multi-view images and LiDAR guidance into native 3D generative models for in-the-wild driving scenes, delivering high-fidelity meshes and cross-view consistency.
AI.motivation: Realistic 3D vehicle models are essential for synthetic data and simulation; existing methods underutilize multimodal sensors and rely on neural rendering, yielding lower-quality meshes; native 3D generative models struggle with arbitrary multi-view inputs and in-the-wild images; there is a need for cross-view consistency and geometric accuracy.
AI.method: Use multi-view images as conditioning inputs; LiDAR point clouds provide test-time guidance; during denoising, align the guidance point cloud with model priors; enforce consistency between generated geometry and guidance; apply voxel filtering leveraging opacity of 3D Gaussian Splatting to suppress floaters and produce clean meshes; evaluate on Waymo dataset; code released at the provided link.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on Waymo demonstrate MM-TRELLIS outperforms existing methods in high-fidelity 3D vehicle generation.
AI.conclusion: MM-TRELLIS effectively integrates LiDAR and imagery into native 3D generative models for robust multi-view vehicle generation in the wild, achieving improved geometry accuracy and clean mesh output; code is publicly available.

title: QC-SMOTE: Quality-Controlled SMOTE for Imbalanced Classification
summary: Class imbalance poses a significant challenge in classification, where existing methods such as SMOTE often generate low-quality synthetic samples in regions with noise or class overlap. We propose QC-SMOTE, a quality-controlled oversampling framework that estimates minority sample reliability using a composite neighbourhood trustworthiness score combining local density, safe-level, and isolation from the majority class. Synthetic candidates are generated using an IPQ-guided best-of-K strategy that evaluates midpoint purity and, when required, majority clearance, with allocation guided by sample reliability and boundary informativeness. Generation behaviour adapts across overlap--imbalance regimes, adjusting interpolation range and selection criteria to match local data geometry. Low-quality synthetic samples are replaced with original minority duplicates when neighbourhood purity falls below an adaptive threshold, providing graceful degradation by reverting to duplication in severely noisy regions. Experiments on 30 imbalanced datasets using repeated stratified cross-validation show that QC-SMOTE achieves the strongest average AUC-ROC and Macro F1 among the compared oversampling methods, with particularly clear gains under moderate and severe imbalance. These results demonstrate the importance of quality-aware, geometry-adaptive synthetic sampling for robust imbalanced classification.

AI.tldr: QC-SMOTE: a quality-controlled oversampling framework for imbalanced classification that uses a composite neighbourhood trustworthiness score to guide synthetic sample generation with an IPQ-guided best-of-K strategy, adaptive behavior across overlap-imbalance regimes, and graceful degradation via duplicates in noisy regions; achieves leading AUC-ROC and Macro F1 on 30 datasets.
AI.motivation: SMOTE and its variants often produce low-quality samples in noisy or overlapping regions, harming classifier performance. A need exists for quality-aware, geometry-adaptive oversampling that robustly handles varying degrees of class overlap and imbalance.
AI.method: Compute a composite neighbourhood trustworthiness score (local density, safe-level, isolation from majority). Generate synthetic candidates via an IPQ-guided best-of-K strategy evaluating midpoint purity and, if needed, majority clearance. Allocate synthetic samples based on sample reliability and boundary informativeness. Adapt interpolation range and selection criteria to local data geometry across overlap-imbalance regimes. If neighbourhood purity is below an adaptive threshold, replace low-quality samples with original minority duplicates for graceful degradation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 30 imbalanced datasets with repeated stratified cross-validation shows QC-SMOTE achieves the strongest average AUC-ROC and Macro F1 among compared oversampling methods, with clear gains under moderate and severe imbalance.
AI.conclusion: Quality-aware, geometry-adaptive synthetic sampling is beneficial for robust imbalanced classification, with graceful degradation in noisy regions via duplication.

title: When CQs Go Wrong: Challenges in CQ Verification with OE-Assist
comment: Acceted poster at https://2026.eswc-conferences.org/program/accepted-papers/poster-demo/ 23rd European Semantic Web Conference (Satellite Event)
summary: Competency Questions (CQs) are the central component of CQ-verification, an established process in which an ontology is evaluated against a set of natural language questions to determine whether the intended purpose of the ontology has been properly modelled. However, CQ-verification is often time-consuming and error-prone, as it requires careful interpretation of linguistic nuances and precise alignment with formal ontology constructs. Ambiguities and complexity in CQs can further complicate this process, leading to inconsistent modelling decisions and verification outcomes. In this paper, we investigate what makes a CQ challenging and possible solutions to enhance the users' performance in the CQ-verification process. We experimented with the data of 19 participants who performed CQ-verification on 20 tasks using an LLM assistant to support ontology evaluation. The results show the necessity of a tool to refine CQs before publishing them to avoid ambiguity or excessive complexity in later phases of the ontology engineering process.

AI.tldr: CQ-verification study identifies ambiguity and complexity in Competency Questions; 19 participants, 20 tasks, with LLM-assisted evaluation; findings support a CQ-refinement tool to improve ontology engineering outcomes.
AI.motivation: CQ-verification is central to ensuring ontologies capture intended semantics, but linguistic ambiguity and misalignment with formal constructs make CQs hard to verify. A tool for refining CQs before publication could reduce errors and rework.
AI.method: Empirical study with 19 participants performing 20 CQ-verification tasks; an LLM assistant supported ontology evaluation. Data collected on performance and perceptions of difficulty; analysis aimed at identifying factors contributing to CQ challenges and potential mitigations.
AI.result: Results suggest a need for a CQ-refinement tool to reduce ambiguity and excessive complexity in CQs, thereby improving downstream ontology engineering processes.
AI.conclusion: A CQ-refinement tool is a practical remedy to enhance CQ-verification; future work should design, implement, and rigorously evaluate such tools across broader tasks and datasets.

title: TrOCR for Medieval HTR: A Systematic Ablation Study with Cross-Dataset Validation
comment: Accepted at Document Analysis Systems Workshop 2026 (ICDAR Satellite event)
summary: Fine-tuning transformer-based handwritten text recognition (HTR) models on medieval manuscripts is challenging because these models are pre-trained on modern text and must adapt to a very different visual domain. This paper studies how three controllable fine-tuning choices (contrast normalization, data augmentation, and layer freezing) affect recognition accuracy when adapting TrOCR to small historical datasets. We run controlled experiments on a 13th-century Italian manuscript (I-CT 91 "Cortonese") and replicate the same experimental grid on the public READ-16 benchmark as robustness evidence. On Cortonese, our best configuration achieves 8.03% character error rate (CER). Statistical comparisons across 13 configurations show that freezing up to three encoder layers or six decoder layers does not significantly harm accuracy, while deeper freezing becomes progressively detrimental. Removing contrast normalization (CLAHE) yields 7.84% CER, comparable to a domain-specialized baseline, suggesting strong optimization can reduce reliance on image preprocessing. Cross-dataset validation on READ-16 shows that decoder freezing thresholds transfer more robustly than encoder thresholds, and combined freezing strategies require dataset-specific re-validation. Finally, we use Grad-CAM gradient attributions and decoder cross-attention maps to diagnose error patterns and failure modes revealed by the ablations. Source code is available at https://github.com/LaudareProject/TrOCR-analysis

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning strategies for TrOCR on medieval manuscripts reveal that moderate encoder/decoder freezing can preserve accuracy, CLAHE removal can match domain-specific baselines, and cross-dataset transfer favors decoder freezing; best CER ~7.8–8.0% on Cortonese with controllable fine-tuning, with Grad-CAM diagnostics clarifying error modes.
AI.motivation: Tackle the domain shift when adapting transformer-based HTR models from modern text to historical manuscripts by evaluating three controllable fine-tuning levers (contrast normalization, data augmentation, and layer freezing) and assessing generalization across small and public datasets.
AI.method: Conduct controlled grid experiments varying CLAHE (contrast normalization), data augmentation, and freezing of encoder/decoder layers on a 13th-century Italian manuscript (Cortonese) and replicate the grid on the READ-16 benchmark. Measure character error rate (CER), perform statistical comparisons across 13 configurations, and use Grad-CAM gradient attributions and decoder cross-attention maps for error diagnosis. Provide source code.
AI.result: Best configuration achieves 8.03% CER on Cortonese. Removing CLAHE reduces CER to 7.84%, competitive with a domain-specialized baseline. Freezing up to three encoder layers or six decoder layers does not significantly harm accuracy; deeper freezing becomes progressively detrimental. Cross-dataset validation shows decoder freezing thresholds transfer more robustly than encoder thresholds; combined freezing strategies require dataset-specific re-validation.
AI.conclusion: Optimization can lessen reliance on image preprocessing; freezing strategies exhibit dataset-dependent effects with decoder-side freezing transferring more robustly across datasets. Diagnostics via Grad-CAM and attention maps illuminate error patterns and failure modes from the ablations.

title: FlowPipe: LLM-Enhanced Conditional Generative Flow Networks for Data Preparation Pipeline Construction
summary: Data preparation pipelines improve data quality in machine learning by transforming raw tables into learning-ready data through sequential cleaning and feature transformation operators. However, automatically constructing such pipelines is computationally difficult because operator sequences are combinatorial and end-to-end evaluation is expensive. Existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) Multi-DQN methods still face three key limitations: decoupled value estimators weaken long-horizon credit assignment, dataset context is only weakly injected into the policy, and exploration is inefficient in a sparse search space with many invalid states. To address these issues, we propose FlowPipe, a unified framework that formulates pipeline synthesis as conditional probabilistic flow generation over a directed acyclic graph. FlowPipe uses Conditional Generative Flow Networks (C-GFlowNets) with a Trajectory Balance objective to connect terminal validation rewards with early pipeline decisions. It further introduces Deep Semantic Modulation through Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM), allowing LLM-derived logical priors to condition the policy's internal activations according to dataset semantics. In addition, FlowPipe incorporates failure awareness into the flow objective to avoid invalid states and concentrate search on high-potential regions. Experiments on two benchmark suites with 74 real-world datasets show that FlowPipe outperforms SOTA baselines, improving accuracy by 11.96% on average and achieving 12.5x faster training convergence. Source code is available at https://github.com/KunyuNi/FlowPipe.

AI.tldr: FlowPipe formulates data-pipeline synthesis as conditional probabilistic flow generation over a DAG, using C-GFlowNets with Trajectory Balance, FiLM-based semantic conditioning from LLM priors, and failure-aware objectives to guide search. It yields substantial accuracy gains and faster convergence on 74 real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Automated construction of data preparation pipelines is combinatorially hard and end-to-end evaluation is expensive. SOTA Multi-DQN approaches suffer from weak long-horizon credit assignment, limited dataset-context injection, and inefficient exploration in sparse invalid-state spaces.
AI.method: FlowPipe uses Conditional Generative Flow Networks (C-GFlowNets) with a Trajectory Balance objective to connect terminal rewards to early decisions, represents pipelines as DAGs, implements Deep Semantic Modulation via FiLM for dataset-aware conditioning using LLM-derived priors, and incorporates a failure-aware flow objective to focus search on high-potential regions.
AI.result: On two benchmark suites with 74 real-world datasets, FlowPipe outperforms SOTA baselines, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 11.96% and 12.5x faster training convergence. Source code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: FlowPipe provides a unified, more sample-efficient and context-aware framework for automatic data-pipeline synthesis, addressing long-horizon credit assignment, contextual conditioning, and exploration challenges, with strong empirical gains and accessible implementation.

title: Themis: An explainable AI-enabled framework for Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback
comment: The extended version of a paper published at the 2026 IEEE Conference on Artificial Intelligence (CAI). Includes an additional appendix with extended derivations and supplementary results. The main paper has 8 pages, 6 figures, 1 table
summary: Training safe Reinforcement Learning (RL) systems is inherently challenging, with no guarantee of avoiding unwanted behaviors. The most effective defenses against this are (i) transparency through explainability and (ii) alignment via human feedback. While both show promising results, no publicly available framework currently combines them. To address this, we introduce Themis, an XAI-enabled testing and evaluation framework for Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. Themis supports over 200 widely used environments and is easily configurable for experiments in RL, transparency, and alignment. Our results show that Themis can train reward models that match or outperform the environment's true reward signal using human preferences. We also provide a cloud-based platform for collecting human feedback and managing experiments. It is user-friendly, auto-scalable, and supports large participant groups across multiple experiments without extra development overhead. Tests show Themis can support one thousand users in back-to-back experiments on a modest commercial machine.

AI.tldr: Themis is an XAI-enabled RLHF testing/evaluation framework enabling training of reward models from human preferences across 200+ environments, plus a scalable cloud platform for large-scale experiments.
AI.motivation: Safety in RL requires transparency and alignment; there is no publicly available framework that combines explainability with human-feedback-driven evaluation.
AI.method: Introduce Themis, an XAI-enabled framework for RL from human feedback; supports 200+ environments; configurable for RL, transparency, and alignment experiments; provides a cloud platform for collecting human feedback; scalable to 1000 users on modest hardware.
AI.result: Themis can train reward models that match or exceed the environment's true reward using human preferences; the cloud platform supports up to about 1000 users in back-to-back experiments.
AI.conclusion: Themis bridges explainability and alignment in RLHF research, enabling scalable, transparent evaluation and reward-model training via human feedback.

title: REDI-Match: Rotation-Equivariant Distillation for Efficient and Robust Dense Matching
summary: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) have significantly advanced dense feature matching, yet severe in-plane rotation remains a critical challenge. Existing solutions face a fundamental dilemma: data-driven methods require inefficient parameter scaling to implicitly learn rotations, whereas strictly equivariant networks lack the semantic capacity of modern VFMs. Consequently, current frameworks typically freeze VFMs and shift the entire burden of rotation generalization to the downstream decoder. To break this architectural bottleneck, we propose REDI-Match, an efficient framework driven by a novel Rotation-Equivariant Distillation (REDI) paradigm. Instead of relying on rotation data augmentation to establish rotational correspondences, REDI distills the non-equivariant semantic representations of a VFM into a lightweight, strictly rotation-equivariant encoder, leveraging an equivariant geometric architecture to constrain robust high-dimensional semantics. To fully exploit these features, we equip the decoder with an entropy-driven spatial alignment module. By evaluating discrete rotation hypotheses, this mechanism explicitly locks onto the canonical coordinate system, eliminating global ambiguity before continuous refinement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that REDI-Match establishes a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) across multiple benchmarks. Notably, it achieves a 13.89% absolute pose accuracy improvement on the highly challenging SatAst dataset while operating 1.9x faster than the current SOTA (RoMa v2), enabling real-time inference (~41 FPS) on a single RTX 4090 GPU. Code: https://github.com/YinjiGe/REDI-Match.

AI.tldr: REDI-Match introduces a Rotation-Equivariant Distillation framework that transfers non-equivariant semantic representations from a Vision Foundation Model into a lightweight, strictly rotation-equivariant encoder, augmented by an entropy-driven spatial alignment decoder. It achieves state-of-the-art dense feature matching under in-plane rotation while running in real time (≈41 FPS on a single RTX 4090).
AI.motivation: Dense feature matching is highly sensitive to in-plane rotations. Data-driven rotation handling requires heavy parameter scaling, while strictly equivariant networks often lack the semantic capacity of modern Vision Foundation Models (VFMs). Existing work typically freezes VFMs and leaves rotation generalization to the downstream decoder, creating an architectural bottleneck.
AI.method: Proposes Rotation-Equivariant Distillation (REDI) to distill non-equivariant VFM semantics into a lightweight, strictly rotation-equivariant encoder using an equivariant geometric architecture. The decoder employs an entropy-driven spatial alignment module that evaluates discrete rotation hypotheses to lock onto a canonical coordinate system before continuous refinement.
AI.result: Achieves new state-of-the-art (SOTA) across multiple benchmarks. Reports a 13.89 percentage-point absolute improvement in pose accuracy on the SatAst dataset. Demonstrates 1.9× speed advantage over RoMa v2 and real-time performance (~41 FPS) on a single RTX 4090. Code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The REDI-Match framework effectively breaks the bottleneck of rotation generalization by distilling VFM semantics into a rotation-equivariant encoder and coupling it with an entropy-driven spatial alignment decoder, delivering robust, real-time dense matching under in-plane rotation.

title: Grad Detect: Gradient-Based Hallucination Detection in LLMs
comment: Accepted to the 2nd Workshop on Compositional Learning at ICML 2026, Seoul, South Korea. Copyright 2026 by the author(s)
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across diverse tasks, yet they remain prone to generating hallucinations. Detecting these hallucinations is critical for deploying LLMs reliably in high-stakes applications. We present Grad Detect, a gradient-based approach for predicting hallucinations by analyzing layer-wise gradient patterns from a single forward-backward pass during inference. Our method shows that the internal gradient structure of a model carries rich information about the correctness of its output. This information is not accessible through output-level signals alone. We evaluate Grad Detect on several Q&A benchmarks across both hallucination detection and model abstention prediction, where it consistently outperforms confidence-based and sampling-based baselines. Through comprehensive layer ablation studies across all eleven models from four architectural families, we find that the final five layers concentrate over 97% of the discriminative gradient signal, enabling efficient deployment with minimal performance loss. Grad Detect provides a unified framework for predicting multiple dimensions of LLM reliability, offering strong predictive performance alongside interpretable insights into where and how model failures originate.

AI.tldr: Grad Detect uses layer-wise gradients from a single forward-backward pass to predict LLM hallucinations, with the last five layers containing >97% of the discriminative signal, outperforming confidence- and sampling-based baselines on multiple Q&A benchmarks and model families, enabling efficient abstention decisions.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations pose a risk in high-stakes deployments; output-level signals are often insufficient. Internal gradient structure carries richer information about output correctness, enabling earlier and more reliable detection.
AI.method: Compute and analyze layer-wise gradient patterns from a single forward-backward pass during inference. Perform layer ablations to identify where discriminative signal resides; evaluate on Q&A benchmarks for hallucination detection and abstention prediction across eleven models from four architectural families.
AI.result: Grad Detect consistently surpasses confidence-based and sampling-based baselines on multiple Q&A benchmarks for both hallucination detection and abstention prediction. Layer ablation shows the final five layers capture over 97% of the discriminative gradient signal, allowing efficient deployment with minimal performance loss across eleven models and four architectures.
AI.conclusion: A unified, gradient-based framework for predicting multiple dimensions of LLM reliability. It provides strong predictive performance and interpretable insights into where and how model failures originate, facilitating reliable deployment with efficient computation.

title: SAFARI: Scaling Long Horizon Agentic Fault Attribution via Active Investigation
comment: Published at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD) at ICML 2026
summary: As autonomous agents tackle increasingly complex multi-step, multi-agent tasks, their execution trajectories have scaled beyond the constraints of even the largest context windows. Current methods for effectively diagnosing agent failures load the full trajectory into an LLM's context window, which suffers from attention dilution and fails when agentic traces inevitably exceed context limits. To address this, we introduce SAFARI (Scaling long-horizon Agentic Fault AttRibution via active Investigation), a framework that replaces linear context loading with a tool-augmented diagnostic loop. By equipping LLMs with a specialized toolbox to read and search trajectory segments alongside a persistent Short-Term Memory (STM) for cross-turn reasoning, SAFARI effectively decouples diagnostic accuracy from architectural context limits. Our experiments demonstrate that SAFARI outperforms state-of-the-art results by 20% on the Who&When dataset within a 1M token budget, and by 19% on TRAIL GAIA subset on a 25K token budget. Most significantly, SAFARI maintains a 0.58 precision even when the target fault resides 5x beyond the model's native context window, a scenario where traditional evaluators fail entirely.

AI.tldr: A tool-augmented diagnostic framework (SAFARI) enables long-horizon fault attribution without loading full trajectories into context windows, using a toolbox and persistent memory to diagnose faults beyond the model's context.
AI.motivation: Diagnosing failures in autonomous, multi-step, multi-agent tasks whose trajectories exceed LLM context limits, causing attention dilution and missed faults.
AI.method: SAFARI replaces linear context loading with an active diagnostic loop: an LLM equipped with a specialized toolbox to read/search trajectory segments, plus a persistent Short-Term Memory (STM) for cross-turn reasoning; decouples diagnostic accuracy from architectural context limits.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art by 20% on the Who&When dataset within 1M token budget; 19% on TRAIL GAIA subset within 25K tokens; preserves 0.58 precision when the fault is 5x beyond the model's context window, where baselines fail.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that tool-augmented LLMs with persistent memory can reliably attribute faults in long-horizon tasks beyond standard context windows, offering a scalable approach to diagnostic reasoning.

title: UniTranslator: A Unified Multi-modal Framework for End-to-end In-Image Machine Translation
summary: In-Image Machine Translation (IIMT) aims to translate scene text in an image and render the translated text back into the original regions while preserving the overall visual appearance. Recent unified multimodal models provide a promising solution by combining visual-text understanding and image generation within a single framework. However, directly adapting such models to IIMT remains challenging. In particular, they often suffer from understanding-generation conflicts, where the translation inferred during understanding is inconsistent with the text supervision used in generation, and spatial position misalignment, where the rendered text does not accurately match the target text regions. To address these issues, we present UniTranslator, a unified multimodal framework for IIMT that tightly couples translation understanding and text editing. Specifically, we introduce an Understand-Generation Alignment Module (UGAM) to bridge the representation gap between understanding and generation, encouraging semantic consistency between translated content prediction and text rendering. We further propose a Spatial Mask Decoder (SMD) with pixel-level supervision over text regions to improve spatial grounding, geometric alignment, and layout controllability during generation. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that UniTranslator achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse language directions and complex real-world layouts. Moreover, our results reveal a strong mutual reinforcement effect between translation understanding and image generation, highlighting the advantage of unified translation multimodal learning. Code is available at https://github.com/SeerRay-Lab/Unitranslator.

AI.tldr: UniTranslator is a unified multimodal framework for In-Image Machine Translation (IIMT) that couples translation understanding and text editing via UGAM and improves spatial grounding with SMD, achieving state-of-the-art results across diverse layouts and languages.
AI.motivation: IIMT requires accurate translation within images while preserving layout, but existing unified multimodal models suffer from understanding–generation conflicts and spatial misalignment when adapted to IIMT, limiting translation fidelity and rendering accuracy.
AI.method: Propose UniTranslator, a unified multimodal architecture that tightly couples translation understanding and text editing. It introduces the Understand-Generation Alignment Module (UGAM) to align semantic predictions with rendering, and a Spatial Mask Decoder (SMD) with pixel-level supervision on text regions to improve spatial grounding, geometry, and layout controllability during generation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks and language directions, with a notable mutual reinforcement between understanding and image generation under the unified framework; code is released.
AI.conclusion: The work validates the benefits of unified translation multimodal learning for IIMT, achieving better semantic–visual consistency and precise region rendering, and suggesting strong potential for future multi-task, multimodal translation systems.

title: Real vs. Complex Spectral Bases for Neural Operators: The Role of Green's Function Alignment
comment: Submitted to/in consideration for the 62nd Allerton Conference on Communication, Control, and Computing
summary: Fourier Neural Operators (FNO) learn solution operators of partial differential equations by parameterizing global convolutions in the complex Fourier domain. For real-valued PDE solutions, the complex FFT carries representational redundancy through conjugate symmetry. We introduce the Hartley Neural Operator (HNO), the exact real-valued mirror of FNO: it replaces the FFT with the purely real Discrete Hartley Transform and learns a single real multiplier per retained spectral mode, with no complex arithmetic. Because the real Hartley spectrum is not halved by conjugate symmetry, HNO retains twice as many frequency corners as FNO but one real weight where FNO carries a complex pair, so the two operators are iso-parametric at equal width and differ only in spectral basis. Our central thesis is that the best basis is a property of the operator. Self-adjoint elliptic operators (Poisson, biharmonic) have real, symmetric Green's functions that the real Hartley multiplier diagonalizes exactly, and HNO is favored there. Time-dependent operators carry phase, from oscillation in the wave equation to transport in advection, Burgers, and Navier-Stokes, which a real diagonal multiplier cannot represent, so FNO is favored there, and increasingly so with the operator's phase content, leaving the phaseless heat equation as the borderline case. Training both operators identically and benchmarking across PDE classes, initial-condition families, and boundary conditions, we find an elliptic-versus-time-dependent split that is monotone in operator phase content and matches the Green's-function theory we develop. Rather than a universal winner, our findings give a predictive rule: match the spectral basis to the symmetry of the solution operator.

AI.tldr: HNO rivals FNO as the real-valued counterpart to Fourier Neural Operators by using the Discrete Hartley Transform instead of the FFT, enabling real-valued learned multipliers and preserving more spectral information. For self-adjoint elliptic operators, HNO diagonalizes real symmetric Green's functions exactly; for time-dependent operators, FNO's complex phase representation is advantageous. The authors propose a predictive rule: match the spectral basis to the operator's symmetry to choose between HNO and FNO.
AI.motivation: Eliminate conjugate-symmetry redundancy in real-valued PDE solutions and exploit operator symmetry to improve efficiency and accuracy. Provide a theory-grounded comparison between real and complex spectral bases, guided by Green's function properties, to inform neural operator design.
AI.method: Replace the complex FFT with the real Discrete Hartley Transform and learn a single real multiplier per retained spectral mode, avoiding complex arithmetic. Maintain equal width (iso-parametric) across HNO and FNO so they can be fairly compared. Train both architectures identically across PDE classes, initial-condition families, and boundary conditions to benchmark performance relative to operator symmetry.
AI.result: HNO retains twice as many frequency corners as FNO (due to lack of conjugate symmetry halving) while using one real weight where FNO uses a complex pair; when applied to self-adjoint elliptic operators (Poisson, biharmonic), the real Green's function is diagonalized exactly by the Hartley multiplier. Time-dependent operators involving phase (wave, advection, Burgers, Navier–Stokes) favor FNO, with the advantage increasing with phase content; the heat equation (little phase) is a borderline case. Across training and benchmarks, an elliptic-vs-time-dependent split emerges monotone with operator phase content, supporting a predictive rule about basis choice.
AI.conclusion: There is no universal winner between HNO and FNO. The optimal spectral basis should be matched to the operator's symmetry: HNO for real, self-adjoint (elliptic) operators; FNO for phase-rich, time-dependent dynamics. This provides a practical guideline for designing neural operators by aligning the spectral basis with operator Green’s-function properties.

title: CineCap: Structured Reasoning with Spatio-Temporal Anchors for Cinematographic Video Captioning
summary: Cinematographic captioning aims to describe how a video is filmed using professional film-language concepts such as camera movement, shot size, depth of field, composition, and shooting angle. This capability is important for fine-grained video understanding and controllable movie-quality video generation, yet remains underexplored in existing multimodal large language models. Unlike question-answering-based evaluation of cinematic understanding, cinematographic captioning requires a unified open-form description over multiple cinematographic dimensions. This task is challenging for two main reasons: the model must infer professional cinematographic concepts from subtle visual evidence, and it must generate captions that are both comprehensive and accurate. Accordingly, we propose CineCap, a framework that combines structured reasoning with spatio-temporal anchors and reinforcement learning with comprehensiveness, accuracy, and gated coverage rewards. The former grounds professional cinematographic descriptions in explicit visual evidence and organizes them into compact atomic reasoning for supervised fine-tuning, while the latter improves the balance between descriptive completeness and factual correctness. In addition, we construct CineCap Bench, a benchmark of 472 manually annotated video-caption pairs for systematic evaluation. Extensive experiments show that CineCap consistently outperforms strong proprietary and open-source baselines, establishing a new state of the art for cinematographic captioning. The code, model checkpoint, and benchmark are publicly available in https://github.com/Hectormxy/CineCap.git.

AI.tldr: CineCap introduces a cinematographic captioning framework that grounds professional film-language descriptions in visual evidence using structured reasoning and spatio-temporal anchors, optimized via reinforcement learning for comprehensiveness and accuracy. It includes CineCap Bench (472 pairs) and achieves state-of-the-art results, with code and data released.
AI.motivation: Cinematographic captioning requires unified open-form descriptions across multiple film-language dimensions and is underexplored in multimodal LLMs. The task is challenging because models must infer professional concepts from subtle visuals and generate captions that are both comprehensive and accurate, enabling fine-grained video understanding and controllable movie-quality generation.
AI.method: CineCap grounds professional cinematographic descriptions with explicit visual evidence using compact atomic reasoning for supervised fine-tuning and uses reinforcement learning with rewards for comprehensiveness, accuracy, and gated coverage to balance detail with factual correctness. It also constructs CineCap Bench (472 manually annotated video-caption pairs) for evaluation and provides code, model checkpoints, and benchmark publicly.
AI.result: Experiments show CineCap consistently outperforms strong proprietary and open-source baselines, achieving new state-of-the-art performance on cinematographic captioning across evaluated metrics.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively grounds cinematographic language in verifiable visual evidence, and the RL objective improves the trade-off between descriptive completeness and factual correctness. The publicly released benchmark and code support reproducibility and future research in fine-grained cinematic captioning and controllable video generation.

title: Ill-Posed by Design: Probing Evidence Use in VLMs
summary: Counterfactual analysis is widely used to study evidence use in vision-language models, but its diagnostic value is limited on well-posed tasks: when several cues independently support the same answer, removing one may not change the prediction. We propose monocular metric object-size estimation as an ill-posed diagnostic setting for evidence selection: because physical size cannot be determined from a single uncalibrated image, models must rely on imperfect cues category priors, target appearance, local context, apparent image size, and scene geometry. We assemble Metric VQA ($10{,}813$ dimension queries from Objectron and $331$ tape-measured in-the-wild scenes) and evaluate $12$ open-weight VLMs ($3$--$397$\,B parameters) with counterfactual analysis decomposing six visual and language evidence channels. Even the largest VLMs tested (Qwen3-VL-235B, Qwen3.5-397B, InternVL3.5-241B) trail a text-only frontier LLM on the in-the-wild split. The diagnostic analysis shows: target identity is the most load-bearing cue, target pixels and local context help only some models, apparent size shifts predictions without a directional readout, and global scene geometry is largely unused. We analyze LoRA fine-tuning as an actionable intervention specific to metric estimation: while the task is learnable, the models do not learn to leverage scene geometry.

AI.tldr: Proposes monocular metric object-size estimation as an ill-posed diagnostic for evidence use in vision-language models, building Metric VQA and evaluating 12 open-weight VLMs; finds current models struggle with metric reasoning and rely minimally on scene geometry, with a text-only LLM baseline outperforming them on the in-the-wild split.
AI.motivation: Counterfactual analyses are limited on well-posed tasks where multiple cues align; an ill-posed diagnostic (metric estimation from a single uncalibrated image) can reveal how models leverage cue priors, target appearance, context, apparent size, and scene geometry.
AI.method: Create Metric VQA dataset (10,813 dimension queries from Objectron + 331 tape-measured in-the-wild scenes); evaluate 12 open-weight vision-language models (3–397B parameters) with counterfactual analysis decomposing six evidence channels; explore LoRA fine-tuning as an intervention for metric estimation.
AI.result: Largest models still trail a text-only frontier LLM on the in-the-wild split; target identity is the dominant cue; target pixels and local context help only some models; apparent size biases predictions without a consistent directional readout; global scene geometry is largely unused.
AI.conclusion: The ill-posed metric estimation setting reveals limited utilization of scene geometry by current VLMs; LoRA can help but overall models do not robustly learn geometric cues; the findings suggest targeted directions to improve evidence integration and metric reasoning in VLMs.

title: LaGO: Latent Action Guidance for Online Reinforcement Learning
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures. Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Large Language Models for Planning (LM4Plan)
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown strong potential for planning and sequential decision-making, but prior work often relies on using them as direct controllers, which requires precise action generation and can be unreliable in practice. This paper proposes Latent Action Guidance for Online Reinforcement Learning (LaGO), a framework that uses a pretrained LLM as a latent action prior to softly guide online policy optimization, rather than treating the LLM as an explicit planner or controller. Experiments on both a discrete-control benchmark, CLEVR-Robot, and a continuous-control benchmark, Meta-World, demonstrate that LaGO consistently improves both reward and success rate over Vanilla PPO. In particular, LaGO increases the average success rate from 15.1% to 27.2% on CLEVR-Robot and from 2.7% to 15.2% on Meta-World. Our analysis further shows that stronger pretrained LLMs provide more effective guidance, suggesting that LLM knowledge can improve planning and online decision-making.

AI.tldr: LaGO uses a pretrained LLM as a latent action prior to softly guiding online RL (PPO), rather than acting as an explicit planner, improving performance on discrete and continuous tasks.
AI.motivation: To address the unreliability and brittleness of LLMs when used directly as controllers, by leveraging their knowledge as a prior to guide online reinforcement learning.
AI.method: Incorporates a pretrained LLM as a latent action prior to softly bias the online policy optimization (not as an explicit planner). Evaluated by comparing against Vanilla PPO on both discrete-control (CLEVR-Robot) and continuous-control (Meta-World) benchmarks.
AI.result: LaGO improves both reward and success rate over Vanilla PPO: CLEVR-Robot success rate 15.1% -> 27.2%; Meta-World 2.7% -> 15.2%. Stronger pretrained LLMs yield more effective guidance.
AI.conclusion: LLM knowledge can enhance planning and online decision-making when used as a latent action prior; larger or stronger LLMs provide more effective guidance.

title: TIGER: Taming Identity, Geometry, and Generative Priors for High-Quality Face Video Restoration
summary: Face Video Restoration (FVR) aims to recover high-fidelity facial videos from degraded input while preserving identity and semantic consistency across frames. Existing methods often struggle to simultaneously address three key challenges: identity shift, viewpoint-entangled guidance, and perceptual realism. To tackle these issues, we propose TIGER, a structured tri-prior fusion framework that Tames Identity, Geometry, and gEnerative pRiors for high-quality FVR. Specifically, an Identity Prior is first established by injecting subject-discriminative embeddings into the latent space, effectively anchoring the subject's identity against severe degradations. Then, to provide temporally consistent structural guidance for dynamic videos, TIGER constructs a Geometry Prior by lifting 2D reference cues into a disentangled 3D parameter space, creating a geometric anchor through cross-source parameter fusion. Moreover, to achieve maximum efficiency without compromising realism, we harness the video generation model's Generative Prior through a one-step rectified flow. We further design a progressive three-stage training optimization strategy that refines structural fidelity, textural reconstruction, and distribution-level realism to ensure robust optimization. We also construct a large-scale FVR dataset to facilitate robust training and standardized evaluation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TIGER achieves state-of-the-art performance in both identity fidelity and temporal stability, delivering a high-quality, efficient and identity-consistent FVR. Project page: https://yzhoulv.github.io/Tiger/.

AI.tldr: TIGER introduces a tri-prior fusion framework for Face Video Restoration that fuses Identity, Geometry, and Generative priors to improve identity fidelity, temporal consistency, and efficiency, with a three-stage training strategy and a large-scale dataset.
AI.motivation: Current FVR methods struggle to simultaneously maintain identity across degradations, decouple viewpoint-guidance from dynamics, and achieve perceptual realism, leading to identity drift, temporal inconsistency, and artifacts.
AI.method: 1) Identity Prior: inject subject-discriminative embeddings into latent space to anchor identity. 2) Geometry Prior: lift 2D reference cues into a disentangled 3D parameter space to provide temporally consistent geometric guidance via cross-source parameter fusion. 3) Generative Prior: use a one-step rectified flow leveraging a video generation model for realistic outputs. 4) Training: progressive three-stage optimization for structural fidelity, texture reconstruction, and distribution-level realism. 5) Data: construct a large-scale FVR dataset for robust training and evaluation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments report state-of-the-art performance in identity fidelity and temporal stability, delivering high-quality, efficient, and identity-consistent FVR.
AI.conclusion: TIGER offers a robust FVR framework that effectively integrates identity anchoring, geometric guidance, and generative priors to achieve superior fidelity, consistency, and efficiency, supported by a tailored training regime and dataset.

title: Cost-Optimal Decision Diagrams for Stochastic Boolean Function Evaluation
summary: In many decision-making scenarios, acquiring information incurs different costs. We consider the problem of constructing a deterministic evaluation strategy that minimizes the expected cost of evaluating a propositional formula under variable costs and a probability distribution over truth assignments. We present a branch-and-bound algorithm with variable-selection heuristics, pruning, and caching. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first practical exact algorithm for this level of generality. Experiments on random instances demonstrate scalability and quantify the efficiency-quality trade-off of a greedy beam-search variant. We additionally evaluate a structured heart-disease diagnosis instance. Finally, we prove that the problem is $\#P$-hard and contained in $\mathrm{PSPACE}$.

AI.tldr: First practical exact algorithm for optimal evaluation of propositional formulas with variable costs and a truth-distribution, using a branch-and-bound approach with heuristics, pruning, and caching; includes a greedy beam-search variant; demonstrates scalability; proves the problem is #P-hard and in PSPACE; validated on random instances and a structured heart-disease diagnosis case.
AI.motivation: Decision-making with information gathering incurs costs; need to minimize expected evaluation cost of a propositional formula under variable costs and a distribution over truth assignments.
AI.method: Deterministic evaluation strategy via a branch-and-bound framework incorporating variable selection heuristics, pruning, and caching. Also explores a greedy beam-search variant to trade solution quality for speed. Empirical evaluation on random instances and a structured heart-disease diagnosis problem.
AI.result: The paper presents the first practical exact algorithm for this level of generality, showing scalability on random instances and characterizing efficiency-quality trade-offs of the greedy beam-search variant. It also demonstrates applicability to a real-world structured diagnosis problem.
AI.conclusion: establishes practical feasibility for broad problem class, highlights computational hardness (#P-hard) while remaining in PSPACE, and suggests directions for improved heuristics, approximations, or tractable subcases for larger-scale problems.

title: Open-Vocabulary BEV Segmentation with 3D-Aware Geometric Constraints
comment: This paper has been accepted by ECCV 2026
summary: Bird's-eye view (BEV) perception fuses multi-camera images into a unified top-down representation for autonomous driving. Despite recent progress, state-of-the-art methods remain confined to closed-set scenarios, making them vulnerable to unpredictable real-world environments. In this work, we introduce open-vocabulary BEV segmentation (OVBS), which leverages vision-language models (VLMs) to recognize categories beyond the training set while maintaining precise BEV perception and real-time efficiency. A key challenge in OVBS lies in the 3D geometric inconsistency inherent in the ill-posed lifting of 2D VLM semantics into BEV. To address this, we propose OVBEVSeg, a geometry-aware OVBS framework that enhances efficient Gaussian splatting (GS)-based unprojection by leveraging robust 3D geometric constraints across three progressive stages: (1) 2D-to-BEV pseudo-labeling via reliable 3D projection for OV generalization; (2) joint 2D-BEV per-scene optimization with BEV structural constraints for 3D geometric consistency; and (3) 3D geometric distillation for online efficiency. On the nuScenes dataset, OVBEVSeg achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming closed-set methods by 15.3 mIoU on unseen categories. Remarkably, even with no novel-class ground-truth labels, it remains competitive with self- and semi-supervised baselines trained with up to 40% of ground-truth annotations. Furthermore, it achieves 2.5x faster inference with only 0.22x the memory consumption of projection-based methods. Project page: https://hchoi256.github.io/projects/ovbevseg/.

AI.tldr: A geometry-aware open-vocabulary BEV segmentation framework (OVBEVSeg) that uses vision-language models and a three-stage workflow to enforce 3D geometric consistency, achieving state-of-the-art open-set BEV performance with real-time efficiency on nuScenes.
AI.motivation: To enable BEV perception to recognize categories beyond the training set (open vocabulary) while preserving accurate BEV geometry and real-time performance, addressing the ill-posed 2D-to-BEV lifting and 3D inconsistency.
AI.method: OVBEVSeg augments efficient Gaussian splatting (GS) unprojection with three progressive stages: (1) 2D-to-BEV pseudo-labeling via reliable 3D projection for open-generalization; (2) joint 2D-BEV per-scene optimization incorporating BEV structural/3D geometric constraints; (3) 3D geometric distillation to maintain efficiency online.
AI.result: On nuScenes, achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming closed-set methods by 15.3 mIoU on unseen categories; competitive with self-/semi-supervised baselines trained with up to 40% of GT; 2.5x faster inference with only 0.22x memory of projection-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating 3D geometric constraints and multi-stage distillation enables robust open-vocabulary BEV segmentation with strong accuracy and efficiency, surpassing closed-set limits and offering practical real-time performance.

title: Decentralised AI Training and Inference with BlockTrain
summary: Frontier AI training is increasingly shaped by access to dense, centrally controlled accelerator clusters. This creates a structural advantage for hyperscalers and large centralized laboratories, and makes open or independent AI efforts depend on scarce capital, privileged infrastructure, and data-center geography. We present Spheroid BlockTrain, a decentralized training protocol in which a model is partitioned into independently trainable blocks, each optimized on a local objective derived from the same global target and composed at inference into one model. On byte-level WikiText, BlockTrain reaches cross entropy 1.359 (perplexity 3.89), within about 0.04 CE of a same-setup end-to-end Transformer reference, while each active worker trains only one block and avoids full-model optimizer state. A shared six-worker block training run reaches CE 1.385 by averaging same-block updates into one assembled model. HTTP/TCP transport experiments move real serialized checkpoints and updates, including a public-IP three-host run that improves CE from 5.580 to 1.811 while moving 15.22 GB. For inference, the current BlockTrain path uses one block-stack traversal per full output and serves over direct TCP across three public-network GPU hosts up to a 75.80B-parameter logical fp16 shape, outperforming a matched plain-autoregressive TCP pipeline baseline because it emits a full sequence per WAN pipeline traversal rather than one token per traversal.

AI.tldr: Decentralized training by partitioning a model into independently trainable blocks (BlockTrain) achieves near-end-to-end Transformer performance with reduced single-node state, enabling WAN-friendly, multi-host training and inference.
AI.motivation: Address the centralization of AI training infrastructure by hyperscalers; democratize access to high-performance models and reduce reliance on capital-intensive data-center resources.
AI.method: Partition the model into independently trainable blocks each trained on a local objective derived from the global target; blocks are composed at inference into a single model. Evaluations include byte-level WikiText cross-entropy (CE) and perplexity, multi-worker block training, HTTP/TCP transport of checkpoints, and WAN inference across public-network GPUs.
AI.result: On byte-level WikiText: CE 1.359 (perplexity 3.89), within ~0.04 CE of an end-to-end Transformer baseline. In a six-worker setup, CE 1.385 by averaging block updates into a assembled model. HTTP/TCP tests moved 15.22 GB; public-IP three-host run improved CE from 5.580 to 1.811. Inference uses one block-stack traversal per full output, across three public-network GPUs up to 75.80B logical fp16, outperforming a baseline plain-autoregressive TCP pipeline by emitting a full sequence per WAN traversal.
AI.conclusion: BlockTrain demonstrates that decentralized block-wise training can approach centralized training performance with notable network efficiency gains, offering a pathway toward democratized, scalable AI deployment; considerations include coordination of local objectives and the quality of assembled models across blocks during inference.

title: SignNet-1M: Large-Scale Multilingual Sign Language Video Dataset with Downstream Benchmarks
summary: Sign language models are typically trained on datasets captured under constrained conditions, with limited viewpoint, background, and signer-identity diversity, leading to poor robustness under real-world distribution shifts. We introduce SignNet-1M, a large-scale augmented dataset spanning ASL, CSL, and German Sign Language (DGS). SignNet-1M synthesizes realistic variations along three axes: (i) novel-view rendering (rotation and zoom) via 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), (ii) scene/identity editing via diffusion models for background replacement and signer substitution while preserving sign motion and linguistic content, and (iii) post-rendering augmentations that emulate capture and compression artifacts (e.g., pose/temporal perturbations and video-level corruptions) to better match in-the-wild recordings. Beyond data release, we provide a unified benchmark suite across downstream tasks (e.g., translation and recognition) and ablations that isolate each augmentation component. Experiments across backbones show that training with SignNet-1M consistently improves generalization under cross-view, cross-background, cross-identity, and post-rendering shifts, while maintaining strong in-distribution performance. The dataset, full augmentation pipeline, and benchmark are available at https://signnet.chatsign.ai/.

AI.tldr: A large-scale augmented dataset, SignNet-1M, for sign language tasks (ASL, CSL, DGS) using three augmentations: novel-view rendering via 3D Gaussian Splatting, scene/identity editing with diffusion models, and post-rendering artifacts; accompanied by a unified benchmark and ablations.
AI.motivation: Sign language models trained on constrained datasets often fail under real-world distribution shifts; need robust generalization across viewpoint, background, identity, and artifact conditions.
AI.method: Three-part augmentation pipeline: (i) novel-view rendering (rotation/zoom) via 3D Gaussian Splatting to simulate different viewpoints; (ii) scene and identity editing using diffusion models for background replacement and signer substitution while preserving sign motion and linguistic content; (iii) post-rendering augmentations to mimic capture and compression artifacts (pose/temporal perturbations, video-level corruptions); plus unified benchmarks and ablations.
AI.result: Training with SignNet-1M yields consistent generalization improvements across cross-view, cross-background, cross-identity, and post-rendering shifts, while preserving strong in-distribution performance across multiple backbones.
AI.conclusion: SignNet-1M provides a scalable augmentation pipeline and benchmark for robust sign language models; data and pipeline are publicly available at the given URL.

title: Scaling Laws for Task-Specific LLM Distillation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across a growing range of domains, yet their scale poses deployment challenges in applications where latency and cost constraints are critical. This paper derives empirical scaling laws for domain-specific LLM compression, quantifying how in-domain and general knowledge performance scale with dataset size, compression ratio, supervision format, and iterative pruning schedule. Using quantitative finance as our application domain, we compare logit-based and LoRA-based distillation under iterative structural pruning, introducing a blended chain-of-thought supervision loss that stabilizes KL-divergence distillation over reasoning traces. In-domain task quality degrades predictably under compression while general-knowledge benchmarks collapse well before the same point; supervision format is the key driver of this tradeoff, with chain-of-thought supervision actively recovering general knowledge that pruning erases. We release the headline dataset FinHeadlineMix, scaling law results, and practical recommendations to provide a reusable framework for domain-specific compression decisions.

AI.tldr: Empirical scaling laws for domain-specific LLM compression reveal predictable tradeoffs between in-domain and general knowledge as dataset size, compression ratio, supervision format, and pruning schedule vary; logit-based vs LoRA distillation differ under iterative pruning; chain-of-thought supervision mitigates general-knowledge loss; FinHeadlineMix dataset released for benchmarking.
AI.motivation: Enable efficient deployment of domain-adapted LLMs under latency and cost constraints by understanding how compression impacts both domain-specific performance and general knowledge, using quantitative finance as a case study.
AI.method: Empirical study deriving scaling laws for domain-specific compression across dataset size, compression ratio, supervision format, and iterative pruning schedules; compare logit-based distillation with LoRA-based distillation under iterative structural pruning; introduce a blended chain-of-thought supervision loss to stabilize KL-divergence distillation over reasoning traces; evaluate on in-domain tasks and general knowledge benchmarks; release FinHeadlineMix and scaling-law results.
AI.result: In-domain task quality degrades predictably with compression while general-knowledge benchmarks collapse earlier; supervision format is the key driver of the tradeoff, with chain-of-thought supervision actively recovering general knowledge that pruning erases; logit-based and LoRA distillation show differing resilience under iterative pruning; a blended chain-of-thought loss improves KL-distillation stability; headline dataset FinHeadlineMix and scaling-law results released, along with practical recommendations.
AI.conclusion: Provides a reusable framework for domain-specific compression decisions and practical guidelines for practitioners, backed by FinHeadlineMix and scaling-law results.

title: Structural Kolmogorov-Arnold Convolutions: Learnable Function on the Values or the Filter Shape as Parameter-Efficient Alternative to Per-Edge Convolutional KANs
summary: Convolutional Kolmogorov--Arnold Networks (KANs) replace the fixed weights of a convolutional kernel with learnable univariate functions. The dominant formulation attaches one such function to every kernel entry and lets it act on pixel values, expressive but parameter-heavy and prone to overfitting. We argue that the learnable functions are better placed in the \emph{structure} of the convolution than on each edge, and we organise the design space along a single axis: whether the function acts on the pixel \emph{values} or on the filter \emph{shape}. We study three realisations. SV-KAN applies one shared univariate function to the values and leaves the spatial filter free and static, aa classical convolution with a single learnable shared activation. AG-KAN keeps the shared value function but supplies the spatial structure through a content-adaptive Gaussian gate. RF-KAN instead moves the learnable functions onto the filter shape, building each filter from oriented ridge profiles expanded in a localised oscillatory (Morlet) wavelet basis with content-adaptive amplitudes. Under a matched four-layer protocol with in-run references and three seeds, RF-KAN and SV-KAN reach $88.47\pm0.10\%$ and $88.20\pm0.31\%$ on CIFAR-10 and $64.40\pm0.19\%$ and $64.57\pm0.30\%$ on CIFAR-100, at about $0.4$M parameters.
