At this matched scale the shape model and the simplest value model meet at the top, both above a plain convolution and every per-edge KAN we tested, including the official Gram variant, at roughly a fifth of the parameters. A controlled study attributes the RF-KAN gain to an intrinsically localised oscillatory basis and to content adaptivity, and an ablation that removes the learned shape entirely, leaving only the shared value function, collapses accuracy by over forty points, identifying the learned shape as the load-bearing ingredient at this scale.

AI.tldr: KANs replace per-edge parameters with learnable univariate functions, explored by placing functions either on pixel values (SV-KAN, AG-KAN) or on filter shape (RF-KAN); RF-KAN yields strong results with a localized Morlet basis and content adaptivity, and learned shape is essential at moderate parameter budgets.
AI.motivation: Mitigate parameter explosion and overfitting in per-edge KANs by rethinking where to place the learnable functions, and assess whether functions on values or on the filter structure better leverage convolutional inductive biases.
AI.method: Three realizations along the value-vs-shape axis: SV-KAN applies a shared univariate function to pixel values with a static spatial filter; AG-KAN keeps the shared value function but adds a content-adaptive Gaussian gate to encode spatial structure; RF-KAN moves the learnable functions onto the filter shape by constructing each filter from oriented ridge profiles expanded in a localized Morlet wavelet basis with content-adaptive amplitudes. Evaluation under a four-layer protocol with in-run references and three seeds; comparisons against plain convolution and per-edge KAN variants (including Gram variant); parameter budget ~0.4M.
AI.result: RF-KAN achieves 88.47±0.10% on CIFAR-10 and 64.40±0.19% on CIFAR-100; SV-KAN reaches 88.20±0.31% and 64.57±0.30% respectively; both at ~0.4M parameters, outperforming plain conv and all per-edge KAN variants at this scale; Gram variant underperforms relative to these models. At the matched parameter budget, the shape-based model and the simplest value model converge at the top tier. A controlled study attributes RF-KAN gains to the localized oscillatory basis and content adaptivity; ablation removing the learned shape collapses accuracy by over 40 points, signaling learned shape as the load-bearing component at this scale.
AI.conclusion: Structured placement of learnable components matters: learning on filter shape (RF-KAN) delivers strong gains and critical dependence on the learned shape, while value-based variants can match performance at this scale. The top-performing models surpass plain convolution and per-edge KANs with a substantially smaller parameter count (~0.4M). The results highlight the importance of a localized, content-adaptive basis for the filter structure in CNNs.

title: Can Scale Save Us From Plasticity Loss in Large Language Models?
summary: The loss of plasticity - the ability of a network to learn new information after having already learned older information - is a fundamental challenge in creating artificial neural networks capable of continual learning. Although this phenomenon has been known for decades, it has mostly been studied in older, relatively small architectures and rarely in natural-language domains. To determine whether loss of plasticity remains a problem in the modern transformer-based LLM paradigm, we study plasticity loss in GPT-style Transformer models trained on a multilingual continual learning problem. Consistent with prior work, we find evidence of plasticity loss across models ranging from 5M to 314M non-embedding parameters, as measured by deterioration on a held-out Vietnamese probing task. We further find that the onset of plasticity loss follows a predictable scaling law, growing sublinearly with model size. These results suggest that larger models may delay the measurable effects of plasticity loss, but that increasing parameter count alone is likely to be insufficient to completely prevent it. We also find evidence of plasticity loss under stationary multilingual training, challenging the view that the phenomenon is exclusive to continual learning with abrupt task changes. Overall, our results suggest that even large Transformer language models trained on natural-language will eventually lose the ability to efficiently adapt to new data after sufficiently long training, in both continual and stationary settings.

AI.tldr: Plasticity loss persists in GPT-style transformers trained on multilingual tasks, with onset following a sublinear scaling law relative to model size. Larger models delay but do not prevent plasticity loss; the phenomenon also appears under stationary multilingual training, not just abrupt task changes.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether plasticity loss (catastrophic forgetting of new information after prior learning) persists in modern transformer-based LLMs and multilingual continual learning, and whether increasing model size mitigates it or if stationary training also induces it.
AI.method: Train GPT-style Transformer models on a multilingual continual learning setup across languages, with non-embedding parameter counts ranging from 5M to 314M. Evaluate plasticity by probing held-out Vietnamese tasks and examine both continual and stationary multilingual training to assess the onset and scaling of plasticity loss.
AI.result: Plasticity loss is observed across model sizes; its onset scales sublinearly with model size, implying larger models delay but do not eliminate plasticity loss. Loss is present under stationary multilingual training, challenging the view that plasticity loss only arises from abrupt task changes.
AI.conclusion: Increasing parameter count alone is insufficient to prevent plasticity loss in language models. Even large Transformers will eventually lose efficient adaptability with long training, in both continual and stationary settings, motivating development of mitigation strategies.

title: MATCH: Flow Matching for Multi-View Anomaly Detection
summary: Detecting anomalies in industrial objects is an important topic for increasing production efficiency. More complex objects often require the analysis of several view points, which has led to the field of multi-view anomaly detection. We present MATCH, the first multi-view anomaly detection method based on Flow Matching (FM). With the ODE formulation of Flow Matching, we can estimate likelihoods and thereby derive an anomaly score to detect anomalies in multi-view image data at object, image, and pixel-level. The architectural flexibility of FM models allows us to efficiently transform features of different spatial sizes to the normal distribution. We evaluate thoroughly on the already established Real-IAD data set and are also the first to provide a comprehensive evaluation of popular anomaly detection methods for the MANTA-Tiny data set. MATCH achieves state-of-the-art performance in both anomaly detection and segmentation, all while running on consumer-level hardware. By omitting the costly divergence term needed for likelihood estimation, we ensure that MATCH is usable in real-time production scenarios. Lastly, several ablation studies are conducted to validate the methodological choices.

AI.tldr: MATCH is a multi-view anomaly detector based on Flow Matching (FM) with an ODE likelihood estimation, enabling object-, image-, and pixel-level anomaly scoring; it achieves state-of-the-art results on Real-IAD and MANTA-Tiny and runs in real-time on consumer hardware.
AI.motivation: Industrial anomaly detection requires handling complex, multiview objects and operating under real-time constraints; existing methods often struggle with multi-view fusion and efficiency.
AI.method: Utilizes Flow Matching with an ODE formulation to estimate data likelihoods. The FM architecture maps features of varying spatial sizes to a common normal distribution, enabling likelihood-based anomaly scoring at object, image, and pixel levels. The divergence term is omitted to improve real-time applicability. Evaluations include extensive ablations and datasets Real-IAD and MANTA-Tiny.
AI.result: MATCH achieves state-of-the-art performance for both anomaly detection and segmentation on the Real-IAD dataset and provides a comprehensive evaluation on MANTA-Tiny. It runs on consumer hardware, demonstrating real-time compatibility.
AI.conclusion: The work validates Flow Matching as a practical framework for multi-view anomaly detection, with design choices such as multi-scale feature alignment and omission of the divergence term supported by empirical ablations and strong benchmark results.

title: BluTrain: A C++/CUDA Framework for AI Systems
summary: Progress in deep learning is, at scale, more a matter of systems engineering than of modelling: the behaviour of a model in training (its throughput, its memory footprint, and the numerical fidelity of the result) is determined less by the architecture itself than by how that architecture is expressed on the hardware. To achieve absolute control over this hardware expression while abstracting away systems complexity to make modelling seamless and eliminating the need for repetitive orchestration logic, BluTrain was architected from first principles as a robust, lightweight, and architecture-general training framework in standard C++ and the core CUDA programming model. Every layer is implemented natively: a typed tensor module with reverse-mode autograd, a linear-algebra library, a caching allocator, a multi-mode distributed-execution module, and an MLIR-based deep-learning compiler. In formal evaluations training a 124M-parameter GPT-2 baseline in FP32 on an 8-GPU 6000 Ada system, BluTrain outperforms industry-standard baselines in both throughput (sustaining an average of 407K tokens/s versus PyTorch's 395K tokens/s) and memory efficiency (achieving up to a 22% footprint reduction), while strictly preserving numerical fidelity and converging to a marginally lower final validation loss. With every layer explicitly open to native tuning, the performance ceiling is the framework's own to raise.

AI.tldr: BluTrain introduces a low-level, architecture-general training framework designed in C++/CUDA to exert precise control over hardware expression, outperforming PyTorch on a 124M GPT-2 baseline in throughput and memory footprint without sacrificing numerical fidelity.
AI.motivation: Address the observation that model performance at scale is dominated by systems engineering and hardware expression rather than architecture alone; aim to remove orchestration complexity and maximize hardware utilization by providing a native, end-to-end training stack.
AI.method: A native, typed-tensor, reverse-mode autograd framework with a linear-algebra library, a caching allocator, a multi-mode distributed-execution module, and an MLIR-based deep-learning compiler, all implemented in standard C++/CUDA. The system exposes every layer for native tuning to maximize hardware efficiency across devices.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on a 124M-parameter GPT-2 baseline in FP32 on an 8-GPU 6000 Ada system shows BluTrain delivering higher throughput (407K tokens/s vs PyTorch 395K tokens/s) and up to 22% memory footprint reduction, while preserving numerical fidelity and achieving a marginally lower final validation loss.
AI.conclusion: With explicit native access to every layer, BluTrain sets a performance ceiling defined by the framework's own capabilities; the design emphasizes hardware-expression control and modular components to realize high performance gains without compromising model accuracy.

title: Modality-Aware Out-of-Distribution Detection for Multi-Modal Action Recognition
summary: The incorporation of additional modalities into action recognition models increases their performance across a wide range of settings. However, how this additional information can contribute to making the models more robust remains underexplored, particularly for the case of multi-modal out-of-distribution (OOD) detection. While methods exist that regularize the multi-modal training process with OOD detection in mind, they still apply off-the-shelf OOD detectors designed for the uni-modal case during inference, discarding important information. Based on an interesting relationship we find between the multi-modal and uni-modal predictions, we propose to use this signal to build a post-hoc detector explicitly designed for the multi-modal scenario. We combine this new source of information with a feature-space score, which detects off-manifold samples in the multi-modal space, and normalize them by the multi-modal logits. In doing so, the proposed hybrid detector is compatible with existing training-time approaches and consistently improves performance. Experiments on a wide range of established datasets from the MultiOOD benchmark show that, on average, our approach outperforms the state of the art. Our results show the importance of explicitly considering the different modalities at inference time for multi-modal OOD detection.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc multi-modal OOD detector leveraging cross-modal prediction signals and a multi-modal feature-space score, improving robustness beyond uni-modal detectors and training-time methods on MultiOOD.
AI.motivation: Despite accuracy gains from multi-modal inputs, OOD robustness in multi-modal settings is underexplored; existing detectors use uni-modal OOD detectors during inference, discarding cross-modal information.
AI.method: Introduce a post-hoc detector that uses the relationship between multi-modal and uni-modal predictions; combine with a feature-space score for off-manifold multi-modal samples; normalize by multi-modal logits; results compatible with existing training-time OOD approaches.
AI.result: On MultiOOD benchmark datasets, the hybrid detector outperforms state-of-the-art averaging across datasets; demonstrates the value of modality-aware inference for multi-modal OOD detection.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling inference-time modality information yields robust multi-modal OOD detection and complements training-time methods.

title: Assessing Distribution Shift in Human Activity Recognition for Domain Generalization
summary: While the field of Human Activity Recognition (HAR) continues to draw interest from researchers and advance in important ways, some key challenges remain. One of the most difficult aspects of building HAR models that show good performance in real-world settings is dealing with data diversity from device and sensor heterogeneity, and contextual changes that are intrinsic to real-world applications. While data diversity in HAR has been well-acknowledged in the literature, there remains a gap in understanding the effect of various types of distribution shifts on HAR models and the domain generalization problem that arises. Towards that end, this paper systematically evaluates 4 different types of distribution shifts, including variations in device type, sensor placement, sampling rate, and user behavior. Quantifying their effects, we illustrate that diversity shifts predominantly define all types of shifts, indicating the existence of unique features that are not shared across different domains. We then introduce a uniform HAR-based distribution shift benchmarks and conduct a comprehensive evaluation of up to 28 domain generalization methods. Our analysis exposes the limitations of current domain generalization algorithms in achieving model generalizability, marginally outperforming the empirical risk minimization baseline. This work represents the first systematic exploration of domain generalization and adaptation concerning specific distribution shifts in sensor-based HAR, offering an open-source benchmark platform and datasets to spur further research.

AI.tldr: Systematic study of distribution shifts in sensor-based HAR; four shift types (device type, sensor placement, sampling rate, user behavior); introduces a uniform HAR-based distribution shift benchmark and evaluates 28 domain generalization methods, finding shifts largely domain-specific and DG methods offer limited gains over ERM; provides open-source datasets for future research.
AI.motivation: HAR models under real-world heterogeneity face distribution shifts due to devices, sensor placements, sampling rates, and user behavior. There is a gap in understanding how these shifts affect domain generalization in HAR; a benchmark is needed to facilitate systematic evaluation.
AI.method: Quantitatively evaluate four distribution shift types (device type, sensor placement, sampling rate, user behavior). Develop a uniform HAR-based distribution shift benchmark. Perform comprehensive evaluation of up to 28 domain generalization methods against an empirical risk minimization baseline. Provide open-source benchmark platform and datasets.
AI.result: Diversity shifts predominantly define all types of shifts, suggesting domain-specific features not shared across domains. Current domain generalization algorithms struggle to achieve strong generalization, marginally outperforming ERM. An open-source benchmark and datasets are released to spur further research.
AI.conclusion: First systematic exploration of domain generalization and adaptation for distribution shifts in sensor-based HAR. Highlights limitations of current DG methods and provides a benchmark platform and datasets to advance future work.

title: EgoSAT: A Comprehensive Benchmark of Egocentric Streaming Interaction Understanding
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Project page: https://leiyj23.github.io/EgoSAT/
summary: We introduce EgoSAT, the first comprehensive benchmark for egocentric video reasoning in streaming settings, designed to evaluate the capabilities of modern vision-language models (VLMs). The benchmark targets streaming interaction understanding, where video frames arrive sequentially and models must continuously interpret evolving visual context. EgoSAT unifies several previously distinct tasks within a single streaming framework. In this formulation, queries about completed events correspond to retrospective reasoning, queries about ongoing activities require online understanding, and queries about future actions involve prospective anticipation. This unified setting requires models to reason about the past, present, and future while operating under the constraint that only previously observed frames are available. EgoSAT contains 1,997 unique videos spanning 165 hours of egocentric footage and around 4,800 high-quality question-answer pairs, carefully designed to probe reasoning across varying temporal contexts. Using this benchmark, we evaluate a diverse set of both open-weight and closed-weight VLMs, providing a systematic assessment of their ability for streaming interaction understanding. By distinguishing answerability and conducting diagnostics on confidence of models, we find existing models not only struggle with prospective and retrospective modeling, but also exhibit severe mis-calibration: confidence often fails to track inherent answerability, leading to dangerous "confidently wrong" behaviors. Project page: https://leiyj23.github.io/EgoSAT/

AI.tldr: EgoSAT is a streaming egocentric video reasoning benchmark for vision-language models, unifying retrospective, online, and prospective tasks and revealing calibration issues.
AI.motivation: To assess VLMs' ability to reason over evolving, streaming video frames and to consolidate previously separate tasks into a single framework.
AI.method: Construct EgoSAT with 1,997 videos (~165 hours) and ~4,800 QA pairs; design tasks across past/present/future reasoning under observed-frame constraints; evaluate diverse open-weight and closed-weight VLMs; analyze answerability and confidence calibration.
AI.result: Models struggle with prospective/retrospective reasoning and exhibit severe mis-calibration, often being confidently wrong; streaming understanding remains challenging.
AI.conclusion: EgoSAT provides a unified, challenging benchmark for streaming interaction understanding and highlights calibration and reasoning gaps in current VLMs; encourages development of better models and reliable confidence estimation.

title: Solving Inverse Problems of Chaotic Systems with Bidirectional Conditional Flow Matching
summary: Modeling chaotic systems is crucial yet challenging. Inverse problems in chaotic dynamics, namely inferring initial conditions from final states, remain largely unsolved because of ill-posedness, non-uniqueness, instability, and potentially chaotic time-reverse dynamics. We address this open problem with Bidirectional Conditional Flow Matching (Bi-CFM), which learns bidirectional mappings between distributions of initial and final states to capture the stochasticity of chaotic evolution and mitigate exponential error accumulation over time. Furthermore, for systems with conservation laws, we extend it to Conservation-constrained Bi-CFM (CBi-CFM). Across the classic Lorenz, Circuit, and high-dimensional Lorenz 96 systems, Bi-CFM improves five distribution-level metrics over baselines while achieving a speedup of more than two orders of magnitude. In the three-body planet-planet scattering problem in planetary dynamics, CBi-CFM better respects conservation laws, with conservation errors comparable to those of the ground truth. Finally, on real observations of globular clusters, collisional million-body systems shaped by $\sim 10^{10}$ years (10 Gyr) of evolution, our method represents an advance in accuracy, establishing a scalable route to solving inverse problems of long-timescale real-world chaotic dynamics.

AI.tldr: Bi-CFM learns bidirectional distribution mappings to address ill-posed inverse problems in chaotic dynamics; CBi-CFM enforces conservation laws. It achieves strong distribution-level performance and conservation accuracy on standard chaotic systems and real astrophysical data, with substantial speedups.
AI.motivation: Inverse problems in chaotic dynamics are ill-posed due to non-uniqueness, instability, and potential time-reversal chaos. Inferring initial conditions from final states remains largely unsolved; there is a need for methods that capture stochasticity and stabilize long-time inference.
AI.method: Bi-CFM learns bidirectional conditional flow mappings between distributions of initial and final states to capture stochastic evolution and mitigate exponential error accumulation. For systems with conservation laws, CBi-CFM adds conservation constraints to the bidirectional framework. Evaluations are conducted on classic Lorenz, Lorenz-96, high-dimensional Lorenz-96, three-body planet-planet scattering, and globular cluster observations.
AI.result: Bi-CFM improves five distribution-level metrics over baselines and achieves >100× speedup. CBi-CFM further respects conservation laws with conservation errors comparable to ground truth. Real-world globular cluster data show accuracy gains for long-timescale chaotic dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Bi-CFM and its conservation-enhanced variant CBi-CFM offer scalable, effective solutions to inverse problems in long-timescale chaotic dynamics, delivering both improved distributional inference and physical-consistency, with applicability to synthetic benchmarks and real-world astronomical systems.

title: Transformation Behavior of Images in Latent Space
summary:
Training of neural networks for histopathology classification tasks typically relies on data encoding into latent space, which reduces complexity and improves performance. There are several encoder networks available, either pretrained on general image datasets such as ImageNET, or specifically on histopathological images. Training of encoder networks should be adapted to downstream tasks, allowing encoding of biologic/diagnostic content while rendering networks invariant to label-irrelevant transformations.
  This paper investigates the effect of classical image transformation on the latent space, using networks provided by Lunit Inc. and Bioptimus, both focusing on pathological images, and by Meta Research Team. We assess variance of embeddings resulting from standard data transformations by comparing original and transformed image embeddings and by contrasting them with random, unrelated embeddings, using image tiles from hematoxylin/eosin-stained sections available in a colorectal tissue dataset and the publicly accessible TCGA dataset.
  Our findings show that embeddings of original and transformed images are closer to each other than to random embeddings, indicating robustness to transformations. However, they are not fully invariant, revealing that the encoder networks do not completely neutralize transformation effects in latent space, explaining why transformation-mediated augmentation of datasets can improve performance. Significant differences were observed between general and histopathology-specific encoder networks.

AI.tldr: Latent-space embeddings from histopathology encoders show partial invariance to standard transformations; embeddings are more similar across original/transformed images than to random, with notable differences between general and histopathology-specific encoders, suggesting augmentation benefits.
AI.motivation: Assess how classical image transformations affect latent representations in histopathology encoders and compare general vs domain-specific models.
AI.method: Compute and compare distances between embeddings of original and transformed image tiles from colorectal (H&E) and TCGA datasets, using encoders from Lunit, Bioptimus, and Meta Research Team; contrast with random embeddings.
AI.result: Original and transformed embeddings cluster together more than with random embeddings, indicating robustness but not full invariance; transformation-based augmentation can improve performance; encoder type influences transformation handling.
AI.conclusion: Encoders do not fully neutralize transformation effects in latent space; leveraging transformation-aware augmentation and selecting domain-specific encoders can improve histopathology classification performance.

title: Difference-Making without Making a Difference
summary: Over a series of seven papers, Andreas & Günther have introduced seven definitions of actual causation and have classified them as belonging to three different, competing, types of accounts: factual difference-making, counterfactual difference-making, and regularity-based. I show that their most recent - factual difference-making - definition instantiates all three types, thereby proving that these are distinctions without a difference. I further compare their novel account to the other six accounts on several crucial examples, revealing that this undermines all seven of their accounts.

AI.tldr: The abstract argues that the latest factual difference-making (FDM) account of actual causation collapses the three-way taxonomy (factual, counterfactual, regularity-based) into non-distinct distinctions, and that this undermines all seven accounts proposed by Andreas & Günther via cross-type instantiation and critique on key examples.
AI.motivation: Evaluate the robustness and usefulness of a seven-definition taxonomy of actual causation and whether the alleged distinctions are substantive or mere artifacts of framing.
AI.method: Compare the seven definitions, show that the latest FDM instantiates all three account-types, and critique their differences through several crucial examples to claim a wholesale undermining of all seven.
AI.result: Claim that the three-way classification is illusory because FDM covers factual, counterfactual, and regularity-based intuitions; and that the seven accounts, including all the distinctions among them, do not survive unified testing.
AI.conclusion: If valid, the taxonomy collapses; researchers should reconsider the framework for actual causation, potentially unifying under a single robust account or refining criteria; further, the examples used should be formalized and tested across models to assess generalizability.

title: MedPCFM: Improving Medical Point Cloud Completion by Integrating Point Transformers and Flow Matching
summary: Medical point cloud completion is important for anatomical reconstruction and downstream clinical workflows, yet generative modeling in this setting remains insufficiently studied. We investigate completion through continuous-time generative modeling and introduce PCFM, a PTv3-backed flow matching approach for medical point cloud completion. We evaluate on SkullFix and SkullBreak, and additionally on the more recent Mandibular Defect dataset. We build strong baselines by adapting PTv3 to a deterministic encoder-decoder completion model and by instantiating diffusion completion (PCDiff) with both PVCNN and PTv3 denoisers. PCFM with PTv3 is competitive with the deterministic PTv3 baseline and achieves state-of-the-art generative performance across datasets, while requiring substantially fewer sampling steps than diffusion. At the best operating points, PTv3 also yields clear throughput gains, providing up to a 7$\times$ speed-up for PCFM compared to a PVCNN backbone. Finally, we study empirical scaling trends by varying model size and point cardinality, showing consistent gains with higher point resolution and informative trade-offs across model scales.

AI.tldr: PCFM (PTv3-backed flow matching) for medical point cloud completion; achieves competitive generative performance with state-of-the-art results across SkullFix, SkullBreak, and Mandibular Defect datasets, while offering substantial speedups over diffusion and clear throughput gains at optimal points (up to 7x vs PVCNN).
AI.motivation: Medical point-cloud completion needs efficient, high-quality generative models; diffusion-based methods are often slow. A continuous-time flow-matching approach with a strong backbone (PTv3) may deliver comparable or superior results with fewer sampling steps and better throughput.
AI.method: Introduce PCFM, a continuous-time flow-matching model leveraging PTv3 as backbone for medical point cloud completion. Compare against a deterministic encoder–decoder completion baseline (adapted PTv3) and diffusion-based PCDiff with PVCNN or PTv3 denoisers. Evaluate on SkullFix, SkullBreak, and Mandibular Defect datasets. Analyze scaling by model size and point cardinality.
AI.result: PCFM with PTv3 is competitive with the deterministic PTv3 baseline and achieves state-of-the-art generative performance across datasets. It requires markedly fewer sampling steps than diffusion. At best operating points, PTv3 yields up to 7× speed-up for PCFM compared to PVCNN. Scaling analyses show consistent gains with higher point resolution and informative trade-offs across model scales.
AI.conclusion: PTv3-backed continuous-time flow matching provides efficient, high-quality medical point cloud completion, delivering strong generative performance with substantial speedups and favorable scaling behavior.

title: Accuracy and Satisfaction in Multi-Turn LLM Dialogues for NFR Assessment
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures. Accepted to SIGDIAL 2026 (27th Annual Meeting of the Special Interest Group on Discourse and Dialogue)
summary: LLM-based dialogue assistants have become mainstream tools for software developers, yet current evaluation benchmarks focus exclusively on functional correctness. This leaves a critical gap in assessing the quality and accuracy of these conversations when handling Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs), which are inherently vague, context-dependent, and involve many parts of a program. Evaluating how well these systems support collaborative reasoning about NFRs requires methods that go beyond single-turn accuracy to capture both the correctness of the system's outputs and the quality of the multi-turn interaction. In this paper, we investigate the accuracy and quality of multi-turn conversations between developers and an LLM-based agent in the domain of Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) regulatory compliance. We hired 49 programmers to interact with GitHub Copilot to assess 148 HIPAA-derived NFRs against the iTrust codebase, a system designed to comply with HIPAA regulations, across three dimensions: requirement satisfaction level, reasoning, and code localization. We find that developers tend to agree with LLM assessments, but accuracy against expert ground truth is low. We model user satisfaction and find that longer system responses and more information-providing turns negatively affect user satisfaction, whereas proactive interactions positively affect it. Our findings provide insights for designing LLM-based dialogue systems that support NFR assessment.

AI.tldr: Evaluates multi-turn LLM dialogue for HIPAA-related non-functional requirements (NFRs): accuracy vs. expert ground truth is low, but developers often align with LLMs; longer, information-rich turns reduce user satisfaction, while proactive interactions improve it; implications for designing NFR-focused LLM assistants.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in evaluating LLM-based dialogue beyond single-turn correctness by focusing on NFRs, which are vague and context-dependent, in a regulated domain (HIPAA) and analyzing multi-turn collaborative reasoning.
AI.method: Human study with 49 programmers interacting with GitHub Copilot to assess 148 HIPAA-derived NFRs against the iTrust codebase across three dimensions: requirement satisfaction level, reasoning, and code localization.
AI.result: Developers tend to agree with LLM assessments but accuracy against expert ground truth is low. User satisfaction decreases with longer, more information-rich responses; proactive interactions increase satisfaction.
AI.conclusion: Insights for designing LLM-based dialogue systems to support NFR assessment, emphasizing concise interactions, proactive negotiation, and alignment with expert judgments to improve reliability in regulated domains.

title: S1-Omni-Image: A Unified Model for Scientific Image Understanding, Generation, and Editing
summary: We present S1-Omni-Image, an open-weight unified multimodal model for scientific image understanding, generation, and editing. Unlike general-purpose image generation models, scientific image tasks require not only high-fidelity synthesis, but also robust understanding of scientific semantics, structural relations, domain knowledge, and task intent. To this end, S1-Omni-Image builds on the scientific multimodal reasoning backbone S1-VL-32B and couples its understanding capability with an image generation module under a unified think-before-generate paradigm. Given a user instruction, the model first produces a task-oriented reasoning trace, a textual answer, and a task special token; their hidden states are then injected into the generation module to condition image generation or editing. S1-Omni-Image supports scientific image understanding, generation, and editing in a unified framework. For generation, it focuses on scientific illustrations and text rendering, including logical diagrams, relational comparisons, data charts, and realistic scientific visualizations. For editing, it casts segmentation and other domain-specific vision tasks as native image editing problems, enabling multi-turn illustration editing, medical and geographic image segmentation, medical image translation, and scientific image super-resolution. We construct SciGenEdit, a 314K-sample training dataset, and release the model weights, inference code, and SciGenEdit-10K. Experiments show that S1-Omni-Image substantially improves scientific image generation and editing while preserving the scientific image understanding capability inherited from S1-VL-32B. It outperforms open-source models on GenExam and TechImage-Bench, achieves state-of-the-art results on four editing benchmarks including MSD, cigRockSEM, SynthRAD2025, and IXI, and maintains stable performance on scientific image understanding evaluations.

AI.tldr: A unified open-weight multimodal model, S1-Omni-Image, for scientific image understanding, generation, and editing, using a think-before-generate paradigm and SciGenEdit data; surpasses baselines on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Scientific images demand robust semantic understanding, domain knowledge, and task planning; general models struggle to integrate understanding with generation/editing; unify tasks in one framework.
AI.method: Build on S1-VL-32B; during inference produce a task-oriented reasoning trace, a textual answer, and a task token; inject hidden states into generation module to condition image synthesis/editing; create SciGenEdit 314K-sample dataset; release model and code.
AI.result: Significant improvements in scientific image generation/editing; maintains understanding; outperforms open-source models on GenExam and TechImage-Bench; achieves state-of-the-art on editing benchmarks (MSD, cigRockSEM, SynthRAD2025, IXI).
AI.conclusion: S1-Omni-Image demonstrates a viable unified framework for scientific image understanding, generation, and editing with open weights; potential to advance scientific visualization tasks.

title: EERLoss: A Novel Loss Function for Training Deep Biometric Models. A Case Study in Keystroke Dynamics
summary: Deep learning approaches to biometric verification are commonly trained by optimizing indirect objectives, creating a misalignment between the optimization process and the primary evaluation metric, typically the Equal Error Rate (EER). This paper introduces EERLoss: a subdifferentiable, arbitrarily accurate approximation to EER for training deep biometric models. Furthermore, this framework has the potential to be adapted to optimize any specific operating point on the DET curve, enhancing its generalizability. To validate this approach, EERLoss is evaluated on a particularly demanding behavioral biometric modality: keystroke dynamics verification. This task is characterized by its high intra-class and low inter-class variability. Experiments are conducted on the large-scale KVC-onGoing benchmark, incorporating data from over 185,000 subjects across different scenarios. A comprehensive ablation study initially demonstrates the superiority of EERLoss in comparison to existing state-of-the-art loss functions. It also converges substantially faster compared to other losses, reducing the overall training cost. Additionally, a comparison is made between the proposed loss and the KVC-winning architecture by re-training it with EERLoss, demonstrating that the proposed approach significantly outperforms the original SoTA, achieving a relative EER reduction of up to approx. 30\%. This improvement on a challenging, large-scale benchmark validates the effectiveness of EERLoss as a task-aligned training objective specifically suited for high-variance biometric traits.

AI.tldr: Introduces EERLoss, a subdifferentiable, accurate approximation to EER for training deep biometric models; shows improved performance and faster convergence, notably on keystroke dynamics with a large-scale dataset; achieves up to ~30% relative EER reduction over SoTA.
AI.motivation: Align the training objective with the evaluation metric (EER) to reduce misalignment in biometric verification, and extend to optimize any operating point on the DET curve for better generalization to high-variance biometric traits.
AI.method: Define EERLoss as a subdifferentiable, arbitrarily accurate approximation to EER that can be used as a differentiable training objective. It is designed to be adaptable to target any DET operating point. Applied to keystroke dynamics verification on the KVC-onGoing benchmark, with ablations and re-training of a SoTA architecture using EERLoss.
AI.result: EERLoss outperforms existing loss functions in ablations, converges faster (lower training cost), and, when used to re-train a SoTA architecture, yields up to ~30% relative reduction in EER on a large, challenging dataset of 185k+ subjects.
AI.conclusion: EERLoss provides a task-aligned training objective for biometric verification, particularly suited for high-variance traits, and offers potential to optimize any DET operating point, improving generalization and efficiency on large-scale benchmarks.

title: Grading the Grader: Lessons from Evaluating an Agentic Data Analysis System
summary: Agentic data analysis systems produce rich outputs, including code, numerical results, and verbal diagnostics. This makes them more challenging to evaluate than single-turn LLM responses. It is therefore necessary to distinguish genuine disagreement between an agent's output and a ground-truth answer from grading artifacts. We investigate how reliably automated graders assess such a system and what strategies improve grading quality by applying LAMBDA, a multi-agent data-analysis system, on 153 numerical QRData tasks from DSGym. We develop and evaluate a three-layer human-AI grading cascade: strict regex matching, LLM-based lenient grading, and snippet-based human inspection, which combines non-GenAI and GenAI strategies with different failure profiles. Both automated graders achieve 100% observed precision (0/70 false positives). The lenient grader's recall is 97% against human labels. A keyword-anchored extraction pipeline raises the strict grader's recall by 60 percentage points over a last-number heuristic; the lenient grader is architecturally parser-independent. An iterative nudge mechanism raises grading run success from 36% to 97% and lenient-pass rates from 16% to 46%; comparing nudging with and without original-question re-injection shows that re-injection offers no benefit, confirming the nudge as an answer template cue. We further observe in this case study that variable type is the task metadata field most consistently associated with grading pipeline dynamics and observed outcome grades.

AI.tldr: A three-layer automated+LLM+human grading cascade (strict regex, LLM-based lenient grading, snippet-based human inspection) is evaluated on 153 DSGym QRData tasks using the LAMBDA system. Results show 100% observed precision for automated graders, 97% recall for the lenient grader against human labels, and substantial improvements via a keyword-anchored extraction pipeline and iterative nudges. Re-injection of original questions offers no additional benefit. Task metadata, especially variable type, correlates with grading dynamics and outcomes.
AI.motivation: The challenge is evaluating agentic data-analysis outputs (code, numeric results, diagnostics) beyond single-turn LLM responses, distinguishing genuine disagreement from grading artifacts, and improving grading quality in multi-agent data analysis.
AI.method: Apply LAMBDA to 153 numerical QRData tasks from DSGym. Develop a three-layer grading cascade: (i) strict regex matching, (ii) LLM-based lenient grading, (iii) snippet-based human inspection. Use a keyword-anchored extraction pipeline to boost strict grader recall. Employ an iterative nudge mechanism to increase grading run success and lenient-pass rates. Analyze the influence of task metadata (especially variable type) on grading dynamics.
AI.result: Automated graders achieve 100% observed precision (no false positives). The lenient grader attains 97% recall vs human labels. The keyword-anchored extraction raises strict grader recall by 60 percentage points over a last-number heuristic; the lenient grader is parser-independent. Nudging raises grading run success from 36% to 97% and lenient-pass from 16% to 46%; re-injection of original questions yields no additional benefit. Variable type metadata consistently associates with grading dynamics and observed grades.
AI.conclusion: A robust, multi-layer grading pipeline can achieve high precision and substantial recall in evaluating agentic data-analysis outputs. Metadata such as variable type is a key determinant of grading dynamics; nudging serves as an effective cue, and parser-independent lenient grading reduces reliance on strict parsing. The approach supports reliable evaluation of complex agent outputs but should be validated on broader datasets to ensure generalizability.

title: P-MTP: Efficient Document Parsing via Multi-Token Prediction with Progressive Depth Scaling
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have revolutionized document parsing by enabling end-to-end mapping from images to structured text, imposing a significant latency bottleneck, particularly for token-dense documents. While Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) has emerged as a promising approach for accelerating inference, its potential is constrained by optimization instability when scaling to deeper look-ahead depth. In this paper, we propose \textbf{P-MTP}, a framework that leverages \textbf{Progressive Multi-Token Prediction} with a lightweight MTP module to scale the look-ahead depth for high-throughput document parsing. Specifically, we introduce Progressive Curriculum Loss that adaptively re-weights different look-ahead depths using cumulative path reliability and retrospective target consistency. By effectively suppressing gradient noise in long-range predictions, P-MTP, facilitates an automated easy-to-hard optimization transition, enabling the model to master increasingly distant look-ahead depths. Furthermore, we propose Confidence-Gated Dynamic Drafting to maximize the effective look-ahead depth and acceptance rate by adaptively calibrating speculative length during inference, thereby minimizing computational waste and further pushing the boundaries of inference speedup. Experimental results across multiple benchmarks and architectures demonstrate that P-MTP, achieves up to a $5\times$ speedup with negligible loss in accuracy, providing the first successful validation of extensive look-ahead MTP in the document parsing domain.

AI.tldr: Progressive Multi-Token Prediction (P-MTP) enables scalable look-ahead in Vision-Language Model-based document parsing by combining Progressive Curriculum Loss and Confidence-Gated Dynamic Drafting, achieving up to 5x speedups with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: MTP accelerates inference but deep look-ahead suffers optimization instability and gradient noise, limiting scalability for token-dense, high-throughput document parsing.
AI.method: Introduce Progressive Curriculum Loss to adaptively re-weight predictions at different look-ahead depths using cumulative path reliability and retrospective target consistency; add Confidence-Gated Dynamic Drafting to adapt speculative length during inference; deploy a lightweight MTP module to scale look-ahead depth.
AI.result: Empirical results across benchmarks and architectures show up to 5x speedup with negligible accuracy loss; validates extensive look-ahead MTP in document parsing.
AI.conclusion: P-MTP provides a scalable, stable framework enabling deep look-ahead in MTP for document parsing, balancing speed and accuracy and reducing gradient noise during training.

title: Matching Tasks to Objectives: Fine-Tuning and Prompt-Tuning Strategies for Encoder-Decoder Pre-trained Language Models
summary: Prompt-based learning has emerged as a dominant paradigm in natural language processing. This study explores the impact of diverse pre-training objectives on the performance of encoder-decoder pre-trained language models across generation and question answering tasks, with a focus on commonsense knowledge retrieval and completion. We highlight the benefits of incorporating multiple objectives during both pre-training and fine-tuning stages. We introduce the Match Task to Objective (MTO) framework and methods for determining the appropriate objective for a given task. This framework offers automated methods to prepare task-related data for adaptation through unsupervised training, based on the identified objective. In the fine-tuning stage, we design novel templates that align with the objectives of the pre-training and adaptation stages. When aligned with task requirements, these strategies can achieve a performance gain of over 120\% compared to conventional methods in few-shot settings. They significantly outperform related works in few-shot settings and exceed the baseline even in full-dataset scenarios. Furthermore, we extend this approach to include prompt-tuning methodologies, providing guidance for more effective soft prompt engineering and optimization. Our strategies significantly enhance prompt-tuning performance as well. These insights hold substantial value, precisely guiding the selection and optimization of models customized for specific tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/puraminy/MTO/

AI.tldr: MTO: A framework linking task objectives with pre-training and prompting strategies to boost encoder-decoder LMs for generation, QA, and commonsense knowledge tasks; shows large gains in few-shot and full-data regimes; includes automated data prep and templates; extends to prompt-tuning.
AI.motivation: To understand how diverse pre-training objectives affect encoder-decoder models for generation and question answering, particularly for commonsense knowledge retrieval/completion, and to enable automated objective-task alignment for better adaptation.
AI.method: Introduce Match Task to Objective (MTO) framework that maps tasks to suitable pre-training objectives and adapts data via unsupervised preparation. Develop task-aligned templates for fine-tuning and extend to prompt-tuning with guidance on soft prompt optimization. Provide automated data preparation methods and tooling to select best objective.
AI.result: Empirical results show gains up to >120% over conventional methods in few-shot settings; outperform related works in few-shot and surpass baseline even with full datasets. Prompt-tuning strategies also significantly improved. Code released at GitHub: https://github.com/puraminy/MTO/
AI.conclusion: Diversity of pre-training objectives and careful alignment of pre-training/adaptation with downstream task requirements yield substantial performance gains. The MTO framework provides automated, data-driven guidance for selecting and optimizing models and templates tailored to specific tasks, benefiting both full-data and few-shot scenarios.

title: SENTRY: SAM2-Enhanced Neighbor-Aware and Temporally Reasoned Memory for Visual Tracking
comment: Accepted for publication at the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV 2026)
summary: We revisit the memory update mechanism in SAM2-based visual object tracking and identify confidence-only mask selection as the dominant cause of drift under occlusion, rapid motion, and distractors. We introduce SENTRY, a training-free, plug-and-play, refine-before-write module that validates each memory update for short-horizon temporal consistency before committing it. SENTRY aggregates diverse segmentation hypotheses per frame, backtracks them into short tracklets, and uses neighbor-aware cycle-consistent matching against recent trajectories to favor temporally and geometrically consistent masks. It leaves the base architecture untouched, replacing confidence-driven writes with consistency-validated ones. For fair evaluation, we re-evaluate major open-source SAM2-based trackers across all available scales and datasets, filling gaps in prior reports. Integrated into five strong baselines, SENTRY delivers consistent gains across nine benchmarks, achieving new zero-shot SOTA on LaSOT, LaSOT_ext, GOT-10k, VOT20, VOT22, and DiDi. Despite these checks, the SAM2-L version runs at 32.8 FPS on an A100, and across compatible hosts adds only about 0.4--0.6 GB VRAM. Our results provide the first unified all-scale evaluation of SAM2-based trackers and show that enforcing temporal validity at write time stabilizes memory-augmented tracking without retraining.

AI.tldr: A training-free, consistency-validated memory-write module (SENTRY) mitigates drift in SAM2-based trackers by replacing confidence-only writes with short-horizon temporal consistency checks, yielding state-of-the-art zero-shot results across nine benchmarks with modest compute overhead.
AI.motivation: Confidence-driven mask selection causes drift under occlusion, fast motion, and distractors in SAM2-based visual tracking. There is a need for a memory update mechanism that ensures temporal consistency without retraining.
AI.method: SENTRY is a plug-and-play, refine-before-write module. It validates each memory update for short-horizon temporal consistency, aggregates multiple segmentation hypotheses per frame, backtracks them into short tracklets, and uses neighbor-aware, cycle-consistent matching against recent trajectories to favor temporally and geometrically consistent masks. It replaces confidence-driven writes with consistency-validated ones without altering the base architecture.
AI.result: Re-evaluated major open-source SAM2-based trackers across scales/datasets. Integrated into five strong baselines, SENTRY yields consistent gains across nine benchmarks and achieves new zero-shot SOTA on LaSOT, LaSOT_ext, GOT-10k, VOT20, VOT22, and DiDi. The SAM2-L variant runs at 32.8 FPS on an A100 and adds only ~0.4–0.6 GB VRAM. This work provides the first unified all-scale evaluation and demonstrates that write-time temporal validation stabilizes memory-augmented tracking without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing temporal validity at write time reliably improves memory-augmented SAM2-based tracking with no retraining, offering consistent performance gains and a practical, training-free enhancement that complements existing trackers.

title: World Models in Pieces: Structural Certification for General Agents
summary: In the big-world regime, agents cannot be universally capable and their ability is inevitably specialized across a world model in pieces. Consequently, standard uniform guarantees fail to distinguish between the understanding of critical bottlenecks and irrelevant failures. We first formalize this limitation by proving that general agents are not universal, rendering standard worst-case analysis uninformative. To overcome this, we introduce structural certification, a transition-local framework that maps bounded goal-conditioned performance to entry-wise guarantees on the agent's internal world model. Our main contribution is constructive. We provide algorithms that filter specific transitions using deep compositional goals and prove that a general agent on these goals has a structural world model with a $\mathcal{O}(1/n) + \mathcal{O}(δ)$ error bound. Conversely, this bound is tight in the small-$δ$ regime, whose existence is explicitly guaranteed by our certification. These results enable the certifiable deployment of general agents by localizing the specific transitions where long-horizon planning is reliable.

AI.tldr: Proposes structural certification to localize transitions and provide provable guarantees for general agents; achieves an O(1/n) + O(δ) error bound on the internal world model, enabling certifiable deployment.
AI.motivation: In large-scale, real-world settings, agents are inherently specialized across a world model, making universal guarantees ineffective. Standard worst-case analyses fail to distinguish critical bottlenecks from irrelevant failures. A transition-local, certifiable framework is needed to bound performance on key transitions and enable reliable long-horizon planning.
AI.method: Introduce structural certification, a transition-local framework that maps bounded goal-conditioned performance to entry-wise guarantees on the agent's internal world model. Develop algorithms that filter specific transitions using deep compositional goals. Prove that a general agent on these goals has a structural world model with an O(1/n) + O(δ) error bound; show the bound is tight in the small-δ regime, with certification guaranteeing its existence.
AI.result: Constructive contribution: algorithms for transition filtering and theoretical proofs yield an O(1/n) + O(δ) error bound on the structural world model. The bound is tight for small δ, and the certification ensures its applicability, enabling deployment by localizing where long-horizon planning is reliable.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables certifiable deployment of general agents by localizing reliability to specific transitions, linking bounded performance to guarantees on the agent's internal world model and identifying the transitions where long-horizon planning remains trustworthy.

title: Lite Any Stereo V2: Faster and Stronger Efficient Zero-Shot Stereo Matching
summary: Recent advances in stereo matching have achieved remarkable accuracy, but often rely on large models, heavy computation, or additional foundation-model priors, making them difficult to deploy on resource-constrained platforms. In contrast, efficient stereo models offer faster inference but are commonly considered less capable of strong zero-shot generalization. In this paper, we challenge this assumption by introducing Lite Any Stereo V2 (LAS2), an ultra-fast model series designed for efficient zero-shot stereo matching. LAS2 is developed from both architecture and training perspectives. Architecturally, we revisit efficient stereo design under practical deployment settings and propose a 2D-only cost aggregation framework, optimized for real inference latency rather than theoretical MACs alone. For training, we develop a three-stage strategy that combines synthetic supervision, self-distillation, and real-world knowledge distillation. To improve the reliability of real-world pseudo supervision, we further introduce pseudo-label filtering and an error-clamping operation, enabling smoother synthetic-to-real transfer. We instantiate LAS2 as a family of models, including feed-forward variants for different efficiency budgets and an iterative variant for higher accuracy. Extensive experiments show that LAS2 achieves state-of-the-art accuracy among efficient stereo methods while maintaining significantly lower latency. Specifically, LAS2-H achieves stronger overall zero-shot performance than the iterative method Fast-FoundationStereo, with 1.8x and 2.7x faster inference on H200 and Orin, respectively. The project page, demos, and code are available at https://tomtomtommi.github.io/LiteAnyStereoV2/.

AI.tldr: LAS2 is an ultra-fast, zero-shot capable stereo model family that uses 2D cost aggregation and a three-stage training pipeline with pseudo-label filtering and error-clamping to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy among efficient stereo methods with low latency.
AI.motivation: Efficient stereo methods often sacrifice zero-shot generalization; the authors aim to close the gap by designing architecture and training strategies that promote strong zero-shot performance while keeping inference latency low.
AI.method: Proposes a 2D-only cost aggregation framework; three-stage training: synthetic supervision, self-distillation, real-world knowledge distillation; pseudo-label filtering; error-clamping; variants: feed-forward and iterative; evaluation on latency and accuracy; comparison to Fast-FoundationStereo.
AI.result: LAS2-H achieves stronger zero-shot performance than Fast-FoundationStereo; 1.8x and 2.7x faster inference on H200 and Orin; state-of-the-art accuracy among efficient stereo methods.
AI.conclusion: LAS2 provides an efficient, scalable solution for zero-shot stereo matching with practical deployment benefits; code and demos available on project page.

title: OpenThoughts-Agent: Data Recipes for Agentic Models
summary: Agentic language models dramatically expand the applications of AI yet little is publicly known about how to curate training data for broadly capable agents. Existing open efforts such as SWE-Smith, SERA, and Nemotron-Terminal typically target a single benchmark, leaving open the question of how to train models that generalize across diverse agentic tasks. The OpenThoughts-Agent (OT-Agent) project addresses this gap with a fully open data curation pipeline for training agentic models. We conduct more than 100 controlled ablation experiments to systematically investigate each stage of the pipeline, yielding insights on the importance of task sources and diversity. We then assemble a training set of 100K examples from our pipeline and fine-tune Qwen3-32B on this dataset, which yields an average accuracy of 44.8% across seven agentic benchmarks and a 3.9 percentage point improvement over the strongest existing open data agentic model (Nemotron-Terminal-32B, 40.9%). Moreover, our training data exhibits strong scaling properties, outperforming alternative open datasets at every training set size in compute-controlled comparisons. We publicly release our training sets, data pipeline, experimental data, and models at openthoughts.ai to support future open research on agentic model training.

AI.tldr: OpenThoughts-Agent (OT-Agent) proposes an open data curation pipeline for training broadly capable agentic LMs. Using 100K curated examples and fine-tuning Qwen3-32B, it achieves 44.8% average accuracy on seven agentic benchmarks, outperforming Nemotron-Terminal-32B by 3.9 points. Over 100 ablations show task source and diversity are crucial. The dataset and pipeline are publicly released, with strong scaling properties across training sizes.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of generalizable data strategies for training broadly capable agentic LMs. Existing open efforts target single benchmarks and may not generalize across diverse agentic tasks; provide a fully open, scalable data curation pipeline and validate across multiple benchmarks.
AI.method: Develop a fully open data curation pipeline; conduct 100+ controlled ablations across pipeline stages to assess the impact of sources and diversity; assemble a 100K-example training set; fine-tune Qwen3-32B; evaluate on seven agentic benchmarks; compare against Nemotron-Terminal-32B; perform compute-controlled scaling comparisons; publicly release data, pipeline, and models.
AI.result: Average accuracy of 44.8% across seven agentic benchmarks; 3.9 percentage point improvement over Nemotron-Terminal-32B (40.9%). Data exhibits strong scaling properties, outperforming alternative open datasets at every training size under compute-controlled conditions.
AI.conclusion: An open, diverse data curation pipeline can yield generalizable agentic models, with reproducible improvements over existing open baselines. Public release of data and methods facilitates future research in agentic AI.

title: Boosting Text-Driven Video Segmentation via Geometry-Aware Distillation
summary: Text-driven Referring Video Object Segmentation (RVOS) aims to locate and segment target objects in videos given natural language. However, existing models are typically trained on 2D image or video datasets with naive segmentation losses, which overlooks the geometric consistency across frames and leads to weak spatial understanding. In this paper, we propose Geometry-enhanced Language-guided Video segmentation (GeoLaV), a two-stage framework that distills 3D geometric knowledge from images to enhance text-driven video segmentation. In the first stage, we perform monocular geometry pretraining with monocular novel-view synthesis, enabling the model to acquire geometry-consistent visual representations via spatial alignment on large-scale single-image datasets. In the second stage, we introduce geometry-aware distillation and fine-tune the model on video segmentation datasets, transferring 3D structural knowledge from a general 3D prior model. This process reinforces 3D awareness and improves both spatiotemporal coherence and language grounding in segmentation. Extensive experiments show that our method using only image segmentation data already provides notable zero-shot generalization in RVOS. When combined with geometry-aware distillation for fine-tuning on videos, our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple RVOS benchmarks. The code is available at https://github.com/Tony1882880/GeoLaV.

AI.tldr: GeoLaV introduces a two-stage framework that injects 3D geometric knowledge into text-driven video segmentation: monocular geometry pretraining from single images, followed by geometry-aware distillation and video fine-tuning, achieving zero-shot RVOS capability and state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of geometric consistency and 3D awareness in RVOS models trained primarily on 2D data, by leveraging monocular 3D priors and cross-frame alignment to improve spatiotemporal coherence and language grounding.
AI.method: Stage 1: monocular geometry pretraining with monocular novel-view synthesis on large-scale single-image datasets to learn geometry-consistent representations via spatial alignment. Stage 2: geometry-aware distillation and fine-tuning on video segmentation datasets, transferring 3D structure knowledge from a general 3D prior model to reinforce 3D awareness.
AI.result: Using only image segmentation data, GeoLaV achieves notable zero-shot generalization in RVOS. When augmented with geometry-aware distillation for video fine-tuning, it attains state-of-the-art performance on multiple RVOS benchmarks. The code is available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: The approach validates that incorporating 3D priors and geometry-aware distillation significantly improves 3D awareness, spatiotemporal coherence, and language grounding in text-driven video segmentation.

title: video-SALMONN-R$^3$: Learning to ReWatch, ReAsk, and ReAnswer for Efficient Video Understanding
summary: Video large language models (LLMs) are often constrained by computation and memory budgets, leading them to use reduced frame rates and spatial resolutions, which may cause them to miss critical information for question answering (QA). A practical and efficient solution is a two-stage paradigm: first perform coarse video understanding to localize relevant segments, and then re-watch these segments at higher temporal or spatial fidelity. In this paper, we present video-SALMONN-R$^3$, the first end-to-end video-LLM that enables re-watch through reinforcement learning without relying on chain-of-thought (CoT) cold-start. This design removes the need for costly CoT data annotations and avoids CoT-based supervised fine-tuning (SFT), which can otherwise degrade the pretrained video understanding abilities. To address the mismatch between the reasoning-first behavior induced by re-watch and the answer-first tendency of pretrained video-LLMs, we propose a re-answer strategy, in which the model first produces a direct answer in the first watch and then refines it after re-watching. Finally, to improve question adherence during re-watching, we propose a re-ask mechanism that re-injects the query when revisiting localized segments. Experimental results show that video-SALMONN-R$^3$ consistently outperforms both the base model and the QA-SFT baseline, while surpassing prior re-watch-based approaches with significantly lower computational cost. Code, models, and data will be publicly released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Proposes video-SALMONN-R^3, an end-to-end video-LLM enabling a re-watch via reinforcement learning without chain-of-thought; uses re-answer and re-ask strategies to balance answer-first tendencies; achieves better QA with lower computation.
AI.motivation: Video LLMs face computation/memory constraints that force lower frame rates and resolutions, risking missed information for QA. A two-stage approach (coarse localization followed by targeted high-fidelity re-watching) can improve efficiency and accuracy without excessive costs. The proposed method avoids CoT data and SFT that can degrade pretrained understanding.
AI.method: An end-to-end video-LLM (video-SALMONN-R^3) that enables re-watch through reinforcement learning without relying on chain-of-thought data. Introduces a re-answer strategy to address the mismatch between re-watch (reasoning-first) and the pretrained model's answer-first tendency, and a re-ask mechanism to re-inject the query during revisits. Evaluates against base models and QA-SFT baselines, and against prior re-watch approaches, with a focus on computational efficiency.
AI.result: Consistent performance gains over base models and QA-SFT baselines; outperforms prior re-watch methods while incurring significantly lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers the first end-to-end re-watch capable video-LLM, offering an efficient, CoT-free RL-based framework augmented with re-answer and re-ask mechanisms to improve QA accuracy under tight compute constraints.

title: MambaRaw: Selective State Space Modeling for Efficient 4K Raw Image Reconstruction
summary: In-camera JPEG previews are ubiquitous in raw image formats and provide an sRGB reference at negligible storage cost. Although existing metadata-based reconstruction frameworks can exploit this side information when recovering raw images, their context models often become computationally expensive especially at high resolution, eg, 4K raw image, given that attention mechanisms scale quadratically with feature maps, hindering its practical application. To address these limitations, we propose MambaRaw, a JPEG-conditioned metadata-based raw image reconstruction framework that uses State Space Models (SSMs) to estimate entropy parameters efficiently. Our key contribution comprises a Spatial-Energy Coupled Context Modeling mechanism with two lightweight modules: (1) TileMambaBlock, which performs Mamba-style selective scanning only on information-dense tiles to improve the efficiency; and (2) Energy-Aware Refinement (EAR), an identity-initialized residual module that enhance feature representation to match the long-tail energy distribution of raw signals. Extensive experiments on three camera datasets (Sony, Olympus, Samsung) show consistent improvements over strong metadata-based baselines and set a new state of the art for JPEG-guided raw reconstruction with great efficiency. Notably, at low metadata bitrates, MambaRaw increases PSNR by 1.2--1.4 dB and reduces end-to-end coding latency by about 9%. Code is released at https://github.com/Peizeli1/MambaRaw.

AI.tldr: Introduces MambaRaw for JPEG-guided raw reconstruction using State Space Models to estimate entropy parameters; features TileMambaBlock and Energy-Aware Refinement for efficiency and accuracy; achieves state-of-the-art performance with notable PSNR gains and latency reductions.
AI.motivation: In-camera JPEG previews provide a cheap sRGB reference for raw reconstruction, but existing metadata-based methods rely on costly, high-resolution context models (e.g., attention) whose complexity scales poorly with resolution; a more efficient entropy-parameter estimation and context model is needed.
AI.method: Propose Spatial-Energy Coupled Context Modeling with two lightweight modules: (1) TileMambaBlock performs Mamba-style selective scanning only on information-dense tiles to improve efficiency; (2) Energy-Aware Refinement (EAR) is an identity-initialized residual module that enhances feature representation to match the long-tail energy distribution of raw signals. The framework uses State Space Models (SSMs) to estimate entropy parameters for metadata-based raw reconstruction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Sony, Olympus, and Samsung datasets show consistent improvements over strong metadata-based baselines and set a new state-of-the-art for JPEG-guided raw reconstruction. At low metadata bitrates, PSNR improves by 1.2–1.4 dB, and end-to-end coding latency is reduced by about 9%. Code is released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: MambaRaw delivers an efficient, scalable solution for JPEG-conditioned raw reconstruction, combining SSM-based entropy estimation with lightweight context modeling. It achieves competitive, state-of-the-art performance across multiple datasets while reducing computational latency; the introduced TileMambaBlock and EAR contribute to both accuracy and efficiency.

title: Advancing WordArt-Oriented Scene Text Recognition: Datasets and Methods
summary: WordArt (artistic text) features highly customized fonts, textures, and layouts, making WordArt-oriented scene TExt Recognition (WATER) substantially more challenging than general Scene Text Recognition (STR). Existing STR datasets and methods, typically built around regular scene text and fixed-template inputs, struggle to scale to WATER. Thus, we aim to advance this task from both data and model perspectives. On the data side, we construct a 2M synthetic dataset, WATER-S, with the scale improved by hundreds of times compared to existing artistic text data. WATER-S consists of two complementary subsets. One rendered by an upgraded rendering pipeline (SynthWordArt), which provides highly accurate and controllable synthetic WordArt data. The other is generated by combining Qwen3-VL for prompt mining and Z-Image for image synthesis, which improves the coverage of realistic and diverse data. On the model side, we propose WATERec. It adopts an visual encoder supporting arbitrary-shaped inputs and an autoregressive decoder to model complex layouts, structurally breaking the bottleneck of fixed-template STR on WordArt. Experiments show that this architecture outperforms prior STR methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance on irregular texts such as WordArt. Together with WATER-R, carefully reorganized from existing real STR data, our strong baseline with the new synthetic data and model design reaches 90.40% accuracy on WordArt-Bench, surpassing both general-purpose and OCR-specialized vision-language models by a large margin. Code and data are available at https://github.com/YesianRohn/WATER.

AI.tldr: WATER introduces a large-scale WordArt Text Recognition framework with WATER-S (2M synthetic samples) and the WATERec model, plus WATER-R real data baselines, achieving SOTA (90.40% on WordArt-Bench).
AI.motivation: WordArt text exhibits extreme variability in fonts, textures, and layouts, making irregular scene text recognition far more challenging than standard STR. Existing STR datasets/methods are inadequate for WordArt; there is a need for both scalable data and flexible models to handle arbitrary-shaped inputs.
AI.method: Data: 2M WATER-S synthetic data consisting of two subsets. SynthWordArt renders highly controllable WordArt data; a second subset uses Qwen3-VL for prompt mining and Z-Image for image synthesis to improve realism/diversity. Model: WATERec with a visual encoder that supports arbitrary-shaped inputs and an autoregressive decoder to capture complex layouts, breaking the fixed-template STR bottleneck. Additionally, WATER-R reorganizes real STR data as a strong baseline.
AI.result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance on irregular WordArt text, with 90.40% accuracy on WordArt-Bench, outperforming both general-purpose and OCR-specialized vision-language models. Code/data available.
AI.conclusion: Combining large-scale synthetic WordArt data with a flexible, autoregressive recognition model substantially improves WordArt recognition, providing strong baselines and resources for future WATER research.

title: RetiSEM: Generalising Causal Models for Fragmented Biomedical Data
summary: Learning causal models from fragmented biomedical data is challenging because clinical, molecular, and imaging variables are often incomplete or not jointly observed. We propose RetiSEM, a domain-constrained structural equation modelling (SEM) framework for causal graph recovery and mediation analysis under limited multimodal resources. This proposed work organises variables into biologically informed blocks, applies forbidden-edge constraints, and decomposes pathway-level effects into TE, NDE, and NIE components. We evaluate RetiSEM across ten synthetic benchmark scenarios that vary in dimensionality, nonlinearity, causal depth, and pathway structure, together with a fragmented real-world setting that combines NHANES clinical variables with externally derived retinal representations. This approach achieves lower structural error and higher causal accuracy than unconstrained baselines across the synthetic benchmarks. In the real-data analysis, retinal variables behave mainly as downstream biomarker-like indicators, with smaller but detectable indirect effects. These findings support our strategy as an interpretable framework for testing structured causal hypotheses in limited-resource biomedical AI. The code and resources for this work are publicly available at: https://github.com/Inamullah-Colab/ReitSEM.

AI.tldr: RetiSEM offers a domain-constrained SEM framework for causal graph recovery and mediation in fragmented multimodal biomedical data, using biologically informed blocks, forbidden-edge constraints, and pathway-level TE/NDE/NIE decomposition; demonstrated on synthetic benchmarks and NHANES+retinal data with superior structural and causal accuracy; code available.
AI.motivation: Address learning causal models from incomplete, multi-source biomedical data; need interpretable and resource-efficient methods leveraging domain knowledge.
AI.method: Block-structured SEM with domain-informed variable grouping, forbidding specified edges, decomposing pathway effects into TE, NDE, NIE; evaluation on 10 synthetic scenarios varying dimensionality, nonlinearity, causal depth, and pathway structure; real data integration of NHANES clinical variables with external retinal features.
AI.result: Outperforms unconstrained baselines in synthetic benchmarks (lower structural error, higher causal accuracy); real data shows retinal variables primarily as downstream biomarkers with detectable indirect effects.
AI.conclusion: Provides an interpretable, hypothesis-testing framework for structured causal inference in limited-resource biomedical AI; open-source at GitHub.

title: VistaRef: Boosting Visual Spatial Orientation Awareness for Pointing-to-Object Detection
summary: Grounding deictic gestures in natural images is fundamental to AR and human-robot collaboration, providing a basis for seamless spatial interaction. While Transformer-based visual models have achieved significant progress in general object detection, their global attention mechanisms often neglect micro-geometric relationships, degrading orientation accuracy. In pointing tasks, this deficiency manifests as an inability to accurately capture the pointing ray implied by finger poses, which results in pointing drift and localization ambiguity when dealing with distant or densely packed objects. To address this, we propose VistaRef, a framework designed to explicitly enhance spatial orientation awareness. First, we develop the Local Hand Entity Modeling (LHEM) module, which incorporates hand-pose embeddings to strengthen the model's capability to capture subtle finger deviations. Second, drawing inspiration from multi-view geometry, we construct the Geometric Ray Modeling (GRM) module to transform implicit orientation information into explicit spatial geometric features, guiding feature aggregation and deep fusion via attention mechanisms. Furthermore, we introduce a novel Orientation-Consistent Alignment Loss (OCAL) to synergistically supervise hand presence and pointing consistency, ensuring that all architectural improvements collectively serve the core objective of spatial localization. Experimental results demonstrate that VistaRef significantly outperforms the baseline, achieving a 14-point absolute gain in grounding accuracy. Qualitative analysis further confirms that VistaRef effectively models the geometric correlation from hand to target, bridging the spatial perception gap inherent in traditional Transformers for complex scenarios. Code: https://github.com/lingli1724/VistaRef.
