AI.tldr: VistaRef enhances Transformer-based grounding of deictic gestures by introducing Local Hand Entity Modeling (LHEM) and Geometric Ray Modeling (GRM), guided by a novel Orientation-Consistent Alignment Loss (OCAL). It explicitly encodes hand pose and pointing geometry, converting orientation into explicit spatial features, yielding a 14-point absolute improvement in grounding accuracy with strong qualitative evidence; code is released.
AI.motivation: Address the neglect of micro-geometric relationships in global Transformer attention, which impairs orientation accuracy of pointing gestures and deictic grounding, especially in distant or cluttered scenes. The goal is to align hand pose and pointing ray with object targets for robust spatial localization in AR/HRC.
AI.method: 1) Local Hand Entity Modeling (LHEM) injects hand-pose embeddings to capture finger deviations. 2) Geometric Ray Modeling (GRM) translates implicit orientation into explicit spatial geometric features to guide feature aggregation via attention. 3) Orientation-Consistent Alignment Loss (OCAL) jointly supervises hand presence and pointing consistency. The framework integrates these components with a Transformer backbone for improved spatial localization.
AI.result: Significant improvement over baselines: 14-point absolute gain in grounding accuracy. Qualitative analysis indicates better modeling of the hand-to-target geometric correlation, bridging the gap in traditional Transformers for complex spatial scenarios. Code available at provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: VistaRef demonstrates that explicit encoding of hand geometry and pointing rays, coupled with a consistency loss, can substantially enhance orientation-aware grounding in visual Transformers, with potential applicability to AR and human–robot collaboration tasks.

title: GeoIMO: Geometry-Driven Independent Motion Classification for Event Cameras
summary: Existing automotive event datasets rely on appearance-based annotations from frame pipelines, making them poorly suited for motion-aware event perception. We present a geometry-driven, annotation-free framework that classifies detected objects as static or independently moving by exploiting ego-motion structure directly from the event stream. A Focus of Expansion model with yaw compensation estimates global background motion, while objects are labeled as moving when local motion deviates from this prediction, as quantified by a scale-invariant residual. Temporal stabilization improves robustness across consecutive event windows. The method requires no learning, no manual motion labels, and works with any input bounding boxes. Experiments on MVSEC and the Prophesee 1 Megapixel Automotive Detection dataset demonstrate consistent performance across diverse driving scenarios, with yaw compensation improving results during turns and a simple translational local model offering a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off.

AI.tldr: Geometry-driven, annotation-free motion classification for automotive events: use FOE with yaw compensation to separate static vs moving objects from event streams; no learning; validated on MVSEC and Prophesee datasets.
AI.motivation: Automotive event datasets rely on appearance-based frame annotations, which are ill-suited for motion-aware perception. A motion-centric, annotation-free approach is desired to leverage event data and ego-motion directly.
AI.method: A Focus of Expansion (FOE) model with yaw compensation estimates global background motion from the event stream. Objects are labeled moving if local motion deviates from this prediction, quantified by a scale-invariant residual. Temporal stabilization enhances robustness across event windows. The framework is learning-free and works with any input bounding boxes.
AI.result: Experiments on MVSEC and Prophesee 1 Megapixel Automotive Detection datasets show consistent performance across diverse driving scenarios. Yaw compensation improves results during turns, and a simple translational local model provides a favorable accuracy–efficiency trade-off.
AI.conclusion: The proposed geometry-driven, annotation-free framework enables motion-aware object labeling directly from event streams without learning or manual motion labels, demonstrating robust performance across datasets and driving conditions; however, performance may hinge on reliable ego-motion estimates and bounding-box quality.

title: What Do Flow-Based Inverse Solvers Approximate? A Posterior-Transport View
summary: A growing family of training-free solvers -- FlowDPS, FLOWER, PnP-Flow and their diffusion ancestors (DPS, DAPS) -- repurpose a pretrained flow-matching prior to solve imaging inverse problems by adding a measurement-guidance term to the deterministic probability-flow ODE. Despite strong empirical results, what these per-step corrections actually approximate -- and how far the resulting samples are from the true posterior $p(x\mid y)$ -- has not been characterized. We give a posterior-transport account of flow-based inverse problem solving. Our starting point is a simple but consequential fact: for a \emph{deterministic} flow prior, Bayesian conditioning is realized entirely by a \emph{reweighting of the source distribution}, not by a drift correction; pushing the reweighted source through the \emph{unmodified} velocity field yields exact posterior samples. From this we show that trajectory-guidance solvers can be read as the minimum-kinetic-energy \emph{correction} field needed to morph the unconditional source into the posterior, and that FlowDPS / FLOWER / PnP-Flow correspond to distinct zeroth-order / Gaussian / proximal approximations of this single object; we bound the resulting posterior bias in Wasserstein distance. A controlled $2$D study with a closed-form posterior confirms the theory decisively: source reweighting matches the true posterior to the Monte-Carlo floor on every metric, whereas trajectory guidance incurs $200$--$800\times$ larger error and collapses posterior modes, \emph{regardless of guidance strength}. Guided by the analysis we propose a cheap, principled velocity-correction solver that is competitive across two in-domain priors (AFHQ, CelebA) and two out-of-distribution settings while, unlike point-estimate source-space optimizers, producing diverse posterior samples with uncertainty that correlates with reconstruction error.

AI.tldr: Posterior sampling for flow-based solvers is achieved via source reweighting; trajectory-guided corrections are approximations that incur large posterior bias; propose velocity-correction as accurate, efficient alternative yielding diverse posteriors.
AI.motivation: Provide a principled posterior-transport perspective to understand what per-step corrections in FlowDPS/FLOWER/PnP-Flow actually do and how far they are from p(x|y).
AI.method: Develop a posterior-transport framework. Prove that for deterministic flow priors Bayesian conditioning equals reweighting the source; interpret trajectory-guidance as minimum-kinetic-energy correction field; categorize FlowDPS/FLOWER/PnP-Flow as zeroth-order/Gaussian/proximal approximations; bound Wasserstein posterior bias; validate in 2D with closed-form posterior; propose velocity-correction solver.
AI.result: Source reweighting recovers true posterior to Monte Carlo floor; trajectory-guidance methods yield 200–800× larger posterior error and collapse modes; velocity-correction method performs well across in-domain priors (AFHQ, CelebA) and out-of-distribution settings, providing diverse posterior samples with uncertainty tracking reconstruction error.
AI.conclusion: Posterior-transport analysis clarifies Bayesian conditioning for flow-based inverse problems; reweighting is exact under deterministic priors; trajectory-guided corrections are approximate; a principled velocity-correction solver offers accurate, diverse posteriors with practical utility.

title: VisCritic: Visual State Comparison as Process Reward for GUI Agents
comment: 17 pages, 4 figures; ECCV 2026 submission; supplementary material uploaded as ancillary file
summary: GUI agents powered by vision-language models show strong potential for automating digital tasks, yet frequently fail in long-horizon scenarios due to the absence of step-level verification. Existing process reward models verify actions through textual reasoning alone, missing the visual nature of GUI state changes. We introduce VisCritic, a visual process reward framework that verifies agent actions by directly comparing pre-action and post-action screenshots in visual feature space. VisCritic employs a Siamese vision transformer to extract change-aware representations, coupled with an Action-Aware Critic Head that jointly evaluates action success, task progress, and error type. A critic-training data construction pipeline generates weakly supervised samples from existing trajectories without additional human labels for critic training. Experiments and offline analyses across five benchmarks demonstrate that VisCritic serves as a plug-and-play enhancement for diverse GUI agents, generally improving benchmark metrics while providing visual diagnostic cues.

AI.tldr: VisCritic verifies GUI agent actions by visual comparison of before/after screenshots using a Siamese ViT and an Action-Aware Critic Head; trained with weak supervision from existing trajectories; improves performance across five GUI benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon GUI tasks suffer from lack of step-level verification and text-only process rewards fail to capture visual GUI state changes.
AI.method: A Siamese vision transformer extracts change-aware representations from pre-action and post-action screenshots. An Action-Aware Critic Head jointly evaluates action success, task progress, and error type. A critic-training data construction pipeline generates weakly supervised samples from existing trajectories for critic training.
AI.result: Across five benchmarks, VisCritic improves benchmark metrics and provides visual diagnostic cues; it acts as a plug-and-play enhancement for diverse GUI agents.
AI.conclusion: Visual process rewards that operate on visual state changes complement textual reasoning, improving reliability of long-horizon GUI agents; VisCritic can be integrated with existing agents with minimal extra labeling.

title: ForensicsTok: Forensics-Guided Tokenized Modeling for Image Tampering Localization
summary: Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) offer powerful reasoning for forensic tasks, yet existing approaches utilizing exogenous segmentation decoders often suffer from suboptimal localization. The reliance on stitched pipelines introduces information bottlenecks during backpropagation, which dilutes spatial signals and is limited by semantic priors of the segmentor. To address these limitations, we propose ForensicsTok, which reformulates image manipulation localization as an autoregressive sequence generation task. ForensicsTok directly generates spatially grounded token sequences, enabling precise mask prediction without intermediary supervision. Specifically, we introduce a Token Splatting Decoder (TSD) to map tokens to binary masks via codebook-aware code smoothing, which mitigates sharp gradients from deterministic detokenizers. Furthermore, to capture diverse tampering clues, we propose a Hierarchical Expert Fusion (HEF) module that injects multi-scale features from a forensic expert model. This unified architecture effectively compensates for the lack of forensic priors in standard MLLMs. Extensive experiments on six benchmarks show that ForensicsTok substantially improves over existing MLLM-based baselines and slightly improves over strong forensic expert baselines, while exhibiting stronger robustness to perturbations.

AI.tldr: ForensicsTok proposes an autoregressive, end-to-end framework for image manipulation localization in multimodal LLMs, using Token Splatting Decoder and Hierarchical Expert Fusion to generate spatially grounded masks, improving localization and robustness across six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard MLLMs with external segmentation decoders suffer from information bottlenecks, diluted spatial signals, and reliance on forensic priors. An end-to-end, spatially grounded generation approach aims to improve localization accuracy without intermediary supervision.
AI.method: Autoregressive generation of spatial tokens within an MLLM, plus a Token Splatting Decoder (TSD) that maps tokens to binary masks using codebook-aware smoothing, and a Hierarchical Expert Fusion (HEF) that injects multi-scale forensic features from a forensic expert model.
AI.result: Significant improvements over existing MLLM-based baselines and modest gains over strong forensic baselines; enhanced robustness to perturbations; evaluated on six benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unified architecture compensates for lack of forensic priors in standard MLLMs and provides precise, robust localization for image manipulation, highlighting the viability of end-to-end, token-based localization in forensic tasks.

title: PointVG-R: Internalizing Geometric Reasoning in MLLMs for Precise Pointing Localization via Visual Chain of Thought
summary: Pointing-based visual grounding requires models to precisely locate target objects by deciphering complex spatial relationships between the visual scene and pointing gestures. Traditional methods typically encode input images into static feature representations and perform reasoning primarily within the linguistic domain, often overlooking the rich perceptual cues and explicit spatial geometry inherent in images. In this study, we aim to mitigate the cognitive vulnerability of models in interpreting gestural spatial relations by proposing PointVG-R, a reasoning-guided Multi-modal Large Language Model (MLLM). PointVG-R introduces geometric-aware reasoning for pointing-based grounding, enabling the model to think with images through the strategic integration of Reinforcement Learning (RL) and cold-start data. Specifically, we design a novel geometric reasoning pipeline that simulates the iterative cognitive process humans employ when interpreting pointing gestures. Furthermore, we construct EgoPoint-CoT, a high-quality visual Chain-of-Thought (CoT) dataset featuring detailed reasoning trajectories to guide the model via Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and RL. To address the varying quality of learning signals encountered during training, we further propose an Adaptive Importance Weighting strategy based on Group Variance, which dynamically adjusts reward signals to optimize the learning process. Experimental results demonstrate that PointVG-R achieves SOTA performance, outperforming the baseline by $\textbf{15.86}$ points in mIoU. Extensive ablation studies further validate the efficacy of our proposed modules. Code: https://github.com/lingli1724/PointVG-R.

AI.tldr: Introduces PointVG-R, a geometric-aware Reasoning-guided Multimodal LLM for pointing-based visual grounding, with an EgoPoint-CoT dataset and Adaptive Importance Weighting. Achieves state-of-the-art mIoU, +15.86 points over baselines; code released.
AI.motivation: Addresses cognitive vulnerability in interpreting gestural spatial relations. Existing approaches rely on static image features and intra-language reasoning, neglecting perceptual cues and explicit spatial geometry in images. Aims to leverage geometric reasoning, image perception, and gesture signals via RL and curated CoT data to improve robustness and reasoning.
AI.method: Proposes a geometric reasoning pipeline that simulates iterative human cognitive steps in interpreting pointing gestures. Trains with Supervised Fine-Tuning on EgoPoint-CoT and Reinforcement Learning, augmented by Adaptive Importance Weighting based on Group Variance to modulate rewards. Integrates geometric-aware visual perception with language modeling in a PointVG-R framework. Provides code release.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on pointing-based grounding, outperforming baselines by 15.86 points in mIoU. Extensive ablations validate the contribution of each module. Introduces EgoPoint-CoT as a high-quality reasoning trajectory dataset, and demonstrates the benefit of adaptive reward weighting.
AI.conclusion: Geometric-aware reasoning and training signal design substantially bolster pointing-based grounding in multimodal LLMs. The approach highlights the value of structured visual reasoning, curated CoT data, and variance-aware training dynamics for robust cross-modal understanding.

title: Are Text-to-Image Models Inductivist Turkeys? A Counterfactual Benchmark for Causal Reasoning
summary: Text-to-image (T2I) generation models have achieved remarkable progress in producing visually realistic images from natural language prompts. Yet it remains unclear whether their success reflects genuine causal understanding or sophisticated pattern matching over visual-textual correlations. Inspired by Russell's inductivist turkey, we introduce Counterfactual-World (CF-World), a counterfactual benchmark designed to investigate whether text-to-image models can generate images under rules that systematically contradict real-world priors. CF-World organizes each scenario into three progressive levels: factual generation under ordinary world knowledge, explicit counterfactual generation with direct visual instructions, and implicit counterfactual generation requiring causal deduction from altered rules. We evaluate both open-source and closed-source T2I models using a Vision Language Model (VLM)-based evaluator (CF-Eval). Furthermore, we introduce two metrics: Prior Resistance Rate (PRR), which measures a model's ability to overcome entrenched real-world priors, and Reasoning Retention Rate (RRR), which assesses whether models can maintain reasoning-dependent counterfactual generation without explicit visual cues. Experiments show that all models exhibit sharp degradation from factual to counterfactual settings. Further analyses suggest that these failures arise because current T2I models encode world knowledge and visual appearances as tightly coupled patterns. Consequently, their heavy reliance on frequent visual co-occurrences within the training data forces them to default to familiar commonsense priors when tasked with rendering counterfactual worlds.

AI.tldr: Introduce CF-World, a counterfactual benchmark for T2I models to test causal understanding; define PRR and RRR metrics; show current models fail under counterfactuals due to tightly coupled world knowledge and visuals.
AI.motivation: To determine whether text-to-image models exhibit genuine causal understanding or merely pattern-match visual-text correlations by testing performance in counterfactuals that contradict real-world priors.
AI.method: Three-level scenario design: factual generation under ordinary knowledge, explicit counterfactual generation with direct visual instructions, and implicit counterfactual generation requiring deduction from altered rules. Evaluation with a Vision-Language Model (VLM)-based CF-Eval across open- and closed-source models. Introduces two metrics: Prior Resistance Rate (PRR) and Reasoning Retention Rate (RRR).
AI.result: All models show sharp degradation when moving from factual to counterfactual settings. Analyses indicate models encode world knowledge and visuals as tightly coupled patterns, forcing reliance on frequent visual co-occurrences and familiar commonsense priors in counterfactual tasks.
AI.conclusion: CF-World serves as a diagnostic benchmark highlighting the limitations of current T2I models in counterfactual reasoning; addressing these failures requires decoupling world knowledge from visual patterns or incorporating causal reasoning mechanisms; future work should aim to improve PRR and RRR and extend evaluation.

title: Heterogeneous Knowledge Distillation via Geometry Decoupling and Momentum-Aware Gradient Regulation
summary: Heterogeneous Knowledge Distillation (HKD) aims to transfer knowledge across varying architectures (e.g., from Transformer to CNN) but inherently suffers from severe training instability. We reveal that this instability stems from two highly coupled challenges: massive feature norm discrepancies that cause optimization drag, and severe gradient conflicts between the primary and distillation objectives arising from distinct inductive biases. To achieve stable distillation, we propose SPOFA, a framework built upon a novel Feature and Gradient Dual Stabilization mechanism. Specifically, at the feature level, we introduce a LayerNorm-based decoupling projector that explicitly decouples feature magnitude from direction, creating a bounded and stable space for semantic alignment. At the gradient level, we propose a momentum-driven Exponential Moving Average (MEMA) dynamic scaler. By establishing a robust historical baseline of the optimization trajectory, MEMA actively evaluates instantaneous gradient conflicts and adaptively penalizes harmful distillation signals, guaranteeing stable convergence. Importantly, SPOFA achieves this dual stabilization with an extremely lightweight parameter footprint. Extensive experiments on two mainstream benchmarks demonstrate that SPOFA achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, significantly outperforming computationally expensive methods while introducing only minimal computational overhead compared to standard baselines.

AI.tldr: SPOFA offers dual stabilization for heterogeneous knowledge distillation (HKD): feature-level LayerNorm-based decoupling to bound feature magnitude and a gradient-level MEMA-based dynamic scaler to penalize harmful distillation, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: HKD across heterogeneous architectures (e.g., Transformer to CNN) suffers severe training instability due to (1) large feature norm discrepancies causing optimization drag, and (2) gradient conflicts between teacher-student objectives arising from different inductive biases. A stable, lightweight distillation framework is needed.
AI.method: Feature-level: LayerNorm-based decoupling projector that separates magnitude from direction to create a bounded space for semantic alignment. Gradient-level: Momentum-driven Exponential Moving Average (MEMA) dynamic scaler that builds a historical optimization baseline to monitor instantaneous gradient conflicts and adaptively penalize harmful distillation signals, promoting stable convergence. The approach aims for a minimal parameter footprint.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two mainstream benchmarks show SPOFA achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, significantly outperforming computationally expensive methods while adding minimal computational overhead relative to standard baselines.
AI.conclusion: Dual stabilization (feature decoupling and gradient-aware scaling) yields stable convergence for HKD with negligible extra cost, delivering strong accuracy gains over existing methods.

title: Quantum CT via Dynamic Interval Encoding and Prior-Balanced QUBO Reconstruction
summary: Quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO)-based quantum computed tomography (CT) casts reconstruction as a binary quadratic problem for quantum annealing and hybrid quantum--classical solvers. For grayscale CT, however, image encoding is constrained by the binary-variable budget: fixed global bit-plane encodings increase QUBO size and coupling complexity as gray-level precision improves, whereas low-bit encodings introduce quantization error. We propose a QUBO-based grayscale CT reconstruction framework that combines dynamic interval encoding with prior-balanced optimization. Each refinement round encodes active pixels only within local gray-level intervals around the current estimate, and a boundary-hit-guided update rule adaptively switches between search expansion and local refinement. To improve optimization stability, the method balances projection-domain data consistency and an edge-preserving quadratic prior before forming the final QUBO. Sparse-view and limited-angle fan-beam CT experiments show that the proposed method recovers structures and gray-level distributions more faithfully than the evaluated analytic, iterative, variational, and representation-based baselines. Expressivity analysis and ablation studies further indicate that the improvement mainly arises from effective gray-level representation through dynamic local encoding and more stable data-fidelity--prior coupling. Experiments on the D-Wave hybrid binary quadratic model (BQM) solver further demonstrate that the formulation is executable on a hardware-backed hybrid quantum--classical backend.

AI.tldr: A QUBO-based grayscale CT reconstruction using dynamic local encoding and prior-balanced optimization to enable efficient quantum/hybrid solutions; shows superior fidelity and feasible hardware implementation.
AI.motivation: QUBO-based CT methods face a binary-variable budget for grayscale images: fixed global bit-plane encodings explode QUBO size as gray-level precision increases, while low-bit encodings cause quantization error. A compact, flexible grayscale representation is needed to improve reconstruction quality and enable hardware-solvers.
AI.method: Propose dynamic interval encoding with active-pixel selection within local gray-level intervals around the current estimate, plus a boundary-hit-guided update that switches between expansion and refinement. Form a final QUBO that balances projection-domain data consistency with an edge-preserving quadratic prior; iterate with refinement rounds to stabilize optimization.
AI.result: Sparse-view and limited-angle fan-beam CT experiments show improved recovery of structures and gray-level distributions over analytic, iterative, variational, and representation-based baselines. Ablation and expressivity analyses attribute gains to dynamic local encoding and stable data-fidelity--prior coupling. D-Wave hybrid BQM experiments demonstrate executable performance on a hardware-backed quantum–classical backend.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic local grayscale encoding combined with prior-balanced data fidelity yields more faithful grayscale CT reconstructions within a QUBO framework and is compatible with hardware-backed quantum–classical solvers.

title: PatternGSL: A Structured Specification Language for Template-Free and Simulation-Ready 3D Garments
summary: Reconstructing realistic, physically plausible garments from a single image remains a fundamental challenge. Template-free methods capture surface geometry but lack explicit sewing structure for simulation; while programmatic systems are simulation-ready but constrained by predefined templates. This reveals a fundamental representation gap between geometric reconstruction and structured garment construction. We present PatternGSL, a structured garment representation in the form of a template-free and learnable specification language that encodes complete sewing patterns, including panel boundaries, parameterized seams, and explicit stitch topology, in a compact and standardized form. PatternGSL preserves the physical rigor of pattern-based models while removing template dependence, elevating sewing structure as a first-class target for generative modeling. We further propose a vision-language framework that predicts PatternGSL specifications directly from a single image and decodes them into garments using lightweight deterministic validity handling, without optimization-based refinement or manual cleanup. In addition, we introduce PatternGSLData, the first large-scale image-to-GSL paired dataset comprising 300K samples with complete sewing pattern annotations, enabling supervised VLM training for structured garment reconstruction. Experiments demonstrate improved pattern accuracy over prior baselines, explicit sewing-structure recovery, reliable cloth simulation, and pattern-level editing through the same deterministic decoding pipeline. Code and data-processing scripts will be released at https://github.com/PatternGSL/PatternGSL.

AI.tldr: A template-free, learnable specification language PatternGSL encodes complete sewing patterns (panels, seams, stitches) from a single image, with a vision-language model and a 300k-pair dataset; results show explicit sewing-structure recovery and reliable simulation without optimization, enabling editing.
AI.motivation: There is a fundamental gap between geometry-focused garment reconstruction (surface models) and structured, simulation-ready sewing patterns (panels, seams, stitches). Template-based or optimization-driven approaches either constrain flexibility or require heavy refinement. The work aims to unify representation and prediction of complete sewing structures directly from images.
AI.method: Introduce PatternGSL, a compact, template-free specification language that encodes panels, parameterized seams, and explicit stitch topology. Develop a vision-language framework that predicts PatternGSL from a single image and deterministically decodes it into a garment without optimization. Create PatternGSLData, a large-scale image-to-GSL dataset with 300k paired samples for supervised training. Provide deterministic validity checks during decoding to avoid manual cleanup.
AI.result: PatternGSL enables accurate pattern-level reconstruction, explicit sewing-structure recovery, and reliable cloth simulation. Demonstrates pattern-level editing via the same decoding pipeline and outperforms priors on pattern accuracy.
AI.conclusion: PatternGSL bridges geometric reconstruction and structured garment construction, making sewing structure a first-class target for generative modeling. The approach, along with the 300k PatternGSLData dataset and released code, paves the way for direct image-to-pattern synthesis and editing.

title: Multilevel Stochastic Plug-and-Play for Sparse-View CT Reconstruction
summary: Sparse-view computed tomography (SVCT) reduces radiation exposure and acquisition time, but the limited number of projection views makes the reconstruction problem severely ill-posed and leads to streak artifacts when analytical methods are used. Plug-and-Play (PnP) methods provide an effective way to combine data fidelity with learned image priors, while stochastic PnP methods further improve robustness by matching the denoiser input distribution through re-noising. However, these methods often require many iterations to converge, which limits their practical efficiency. In this work, we propose a multilevel (ML) stochastic PnP method for SVCT that accelerates stochastic PnP reconstruction. We highlight that, in the stochastic setting, directly enforcing prior coherence across levels would require accurately estimating fine-level prior gradients through multiple denoiser function evaluations, which substantially increases the computational cost. Motivated by this observation, we perform the multilevel steps in multiresolution analysis (MRA) approximation spaces. This choice is supported by the structure of the wavelet decomposition, which causes the prior-coherence correction to vanish in expectation, thereby avoiding costly estimation of fine-level stochastic prior gradients for the coarse-level corrections. Experiments on SVCT reconstruction show that our method, called Multilevel Stochastic Plug-and-Play (ML-SPnP), achieves reconstruction quality comparable to state-of-the-art methods while substantially reducing runtime.

AI.tldr: ML-SPnP: an accelerated multilevel stochastic Plug-and-Play method for sparse-view CT that uses multiresolution analysis to avoid costly fine-level prior gradient estimation, achieving similar reconstruction quality to state-of-the-art with substantially reduced runtime.
AI.motivation: Sparse-view CT reconstruction is severely ill-posed and prone to streak artifacts with analytical methods. While PnP and stochastic PnP improve reconstructions by incorporating learned priors and robustness, they can be computationally expensive due to repeated denoiser evaluations and cross-level prior gradient estimation.
AI.method: Perform multilevel updates in multiresolution analysis (MRA) spaces (wavelet-based) so that prior-coherence corrections vanish in expectation. This eliminates the need to accurately estimate fine-level stochastic prior gradients. Reconstruct via a iterative ML-SPnP scheme that couples data fidelity with denoising across coarse-to-fine levels.
AI.result: Experimental results show reconstruction quality on par with state-of-the-art methods, while achieving substantially reduced runtime.
AI.conclusion: The multilevel stochastic PnP in MRA spaces provides a computationally efficient framework for SVCT without compromising reconstruction quality, by exploiting the wavelet structure to simplify cross-level prior corrections.

title: Jolia: Concept-Level Vision-Language Alignment for 3D CT Contrastive Learning
summary: Vision-language contrastive pretraining has become the dominant recipe for 3D medical foundation models, leveraging the large volumes of paired scans and reports produced in clinical practice. However, medical images usually span dozens of organs, and radiological reports are much longer than typical natural image captions and are composed of multiple structured sections. CLIP-style pretraining compresses this structure by encoding each modality into a single global token, at the risk of losing important details. We introduce ConQuer (Concept Queries), an image-text pretraining method that augments CLIP's global alignment with a set of localized alignments, one per concept. ConQuer splits the report into concept-specific sections and learns cross-attention queries that pool the matching image features without using any segmentation mask or spatial supervision. Contrastive learning is then applied independently for each concept. Concepts can be any unit of semantic localization; here, they are anatomical regions, one query per organ or gross body region. As a byproduct, each query learns attention maps focused on its concept, providing built-in spatial interpretability. We use ConQuer to train Jolia, a 3D CT foundation model on chest and abdominal CT. Jolia consistently outperforms a CLIP baseline on findings classification, report generation, and cross-center transfer, and sets a new state of the art across multiple public benchmarks. Jolia's weights will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Augments CLIP-style pretraining with concept-specific localized alignments for medical 3D CT, via cross-attention queries per organ, enabling per-concept contrastive learning and interpretable attention maps.
AI.motivation: CLIP-style pretraining compresses multi-organ medical imagery and long reports into a single global representation, risking loss of region-level details crucial for medical diagnosis and report generation.
AI.method: Introduce ConQuer: concept queries—learned cross-attention queries for each concept (anatomical region) that pool matching image features without segmentation masks; apply independent contrastive losses per concept; provide attention maps for interpretability; train a 3D CT foundation model Jolia on chest/abdomen; evaluate on findings classification, report generation, cross-center transfer.
AI.result: ConQuer outperforms a CLIP baseline on multiple tasks and benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art results across several public benchmarks; yields built-in spatial interpretability via per-concept attention maps; weights to be released upon acceptance.
AI.conclusion: Local concept-level alignment complements global CLIP alignment, improving accuracy and interpretability for 3D medical foundation models; the approach shows strong cross-center transfer and potential for broader adoption.

title: ViTexQA: A Multi-Frame Temporal Perception Dataset for Video Text Question Answering
summary: Despite remarkable progress in multimodal understanding, current MLLMs still exhibit limitations in video text understanding, particularly when semantics emerge through the integration of temporally distributed textual cues across multiple frames. This perception challenge fundamentally differs from static image text understanding, yet existing datasets fail to capture: the vast majority of questions remain answerable from single frames, inadequately reflecting real-world video text comprehension demands. To address this, we present ViTexQA, a large-scale video-text QA dataset, and FrameThinker for robust multi-frame temporal reasoning. We build ViTexQA via a quality-controlled Chain-of-Thought (CoT) annotation pipeline boosted with temporal constraints; all its QA pairs demand cross-frame text fusion to solve, enforcing true temporal reliance. FrameThinker adopts two-stage training for explicit temporal modeling: CoT-Guided Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) generates frame-aware reasoning chains, followed by Temporally-grounded Reinforcement Learning (RL) optimized with multi-frame coherence rewards. Evaluations show our method outperforms SOTA baselines on ViTexQA, lifting ROUGE-L by 6.3%.

AI.tldr: ViTexQA introduces a large-scale video-text QA dataset emphasizing cross-frame text fusion for temporal reasoning, paired with FrameThinker, a two-stage model (CoT-guided SFT + temporally-grounded RL) that leverages multi-frame coherence; they report state-of-the-art gains on ViTexQA (ROUGE-L +6.3%).
AI.motivation: Current multimodal language models (MLLMs) underperform in video text understanding where semantic cues are distributed across time. Most existing datasets allow answering from single frames, failing to capture real-world video text comprehension demands.
AI.method: ViTexQA is built via a quality-controlled Chain-of-Thought (CoT) annotation pipeline with temporal constraints to enforce cross-frame fusion. FrameThinker uses a two-stage training: (1) CoT-Guided Supervised Fine-Tuning to produce frame-aware reasoning chains, and (2) Temporally-grounded Reinforcement Learning with multi-frame coherence rewards to optimize temporal reasoning.
AI.result: Evaluation shows FrameThinker outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on ViTexQA, achieving a 6.3% improvement in ROUGE-L.
AI.conclusion: The dataset and two-stage model address the gap in temporal reasoning for video text understanding, enhancing cross-frame textual fusion capabilities and robust multi-frame reasoning in video QA tasks.

title: Agentic Collaborative Cognition for Zero-Shot 3D Understanding
summary: Recent advancements have explored agentic zero-shot 3D understanding by reformulating it as video keyframe understanding with Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). However, existing methods face an intrinsic bottleneck due to the finite observation perspectives inherent in videos and the implicit perception of 3D scenes. In this paper, we propose a collaborative multi-agent framework that assigns a Planning Agent to handle high-level viewpoint planning and supplement novel perspectives, and a Perception Agent to explicitly summarize the 3D scene into a structured holistic cognitive map. Specifically, Planning Agent first analyzes this cognitive map to determine query-relevant viewpoints and supplements missing critical perspectives to ensure comprehensive observation. Subsequently, Perception Agent documents object-level attributes from these views by assigning consistent instance identifiers across viewpoints, thereby integrating fragmented observations into the holistic cognitive map. In parallel, it provides feedback to filter out mismatched candidate objects and guide subsequent viewpoint planning. Through this closed-loop iterative process, two agents collaboratively figure out candidates until Perception Agent determines that sufficient information has been captured to complete the task. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on 6 benchmarks, with improvements of 11.1\% Acc@0.5 on ScanRefer, 14.6 BLEU-1 on 3D-assisted dialog, and 2.1 EM on SQA3D.

AI.tldr: A collaborative multi-agent framework for agentic zero-shot 3D understanding reformulated as video keyframe understanding with MLLMs. It introduces Planning and Perception agents that iteratively plan viewpoints, generate a holistic cognitive map, and maintain consistent object IDs across views to fuse observations, achieving state-of-the-art results on six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limited observation perspectives and implicit 3D perception in video-based 3D understanding by explicitly planning viewpoints and constructing a structured cognitive map, enabling better integration of multi-view observations.
AI.method: A Planning Agent analyzes a structured cognitive map to select query-relevant viewpoints and fill gaps, while a Perception Agent extracts object-level attributes across views and assigns consistent instance identifiers to build a holistic cognitive map. The agents operate in a closed-loop, with feedback to prune mismatches and guide subsequent viewpoint planning until information suffices.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on six benchmarks, including 11.1% absolute improvement in Acc@0.5 on ScanRefer, 14.6 BLEU-1 on 3D-assisted dialog, and 2.1 EM on SQA3D.
AI.conclusion: A tightly coupled, iterative two-agent system effectively converts fragmented multi-view observations into a coherent 3D understanding, significantly improving performance and addressing the observation bottleneck in video-based 3D tasks.

title: Evaluating the Interpretability of Sparse Autoencoders with Concept Annotations
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are increasingly used to extract interpretable concepts from vision and vision language models, yet existing evaluation methods largely rely on proxy metrics or qualitative inspection rather than measuring semantic correspondence. We present a human-grounded evaluation framework that quantifies alignment between SAE latents and human-annotated concepts, without requiring user studies, and validate this matching through targeted attribute perturbations. To enable this intervention-style evaluation in vision, we construct synCUB and synCOCO, synthetic benchmarks of paired images that differ in exactly one attribute. We introduce Fully-Binary Matching Pursuit (FBMP), a coalition-based matching procedure that supports many-to-one mappings between SAE latents and annotated concepts, and consistently outperforms one-to-one baselines. For functional validation, we propose a Targeted Attribute Perturbation Alignment Score (TAPAScore), which tests whether matched concepts respond selectively and in the expected direction under targeted image-level attribute perturbations. Under sanity checks, our matching and TAPAScore are the only evaluated metrics that reliably distinguish trained SAEs from untrained ones. Across SAEs trained on CLIP and DINOv2 embeddings, we find that increased overcompleteness can reduce perturbation alignment, indicating a reduction in interpretability. Our evaluation framework suggests that moderate dictionary sizes provide the best trade-off, yielding the most interpretable SAEs. Code and datasets are available at https://github.com/JonasKlotz/sae-concept-eval.

AI.tldr: A human-grounded, intervention-style evaluation framework for sparse autoencoders (SAEs) that aligns latent concepts with human-annotated concepts using synthetic benchmarks, a new many-to-one matching (FBMP), and a targeted perturbation score (TAPAScore); findings show overcompleteness can hurt interpretability and moderate dictionary sizes are optimal; code available.
AI.motivation: Current SAE evaluations rely on proxy metrics; there is a need to quantify semantic correspondence between SAE latent concepts and human-annotated concepts in vision and vision-language models.
AI.method: 1) Construct synCUB and synCOCO synthetic benchmarks with exactly one attribute difference between image pairs. 2) Propose Fully-Binary Matching Pursuit (FBMP) to support many-to-one mappings from latents to concepts and outperform one-to-one baselines. 3) Define TAPAScore (Targeted Attribute Perturbation Alignment Score) to test selective, directionally correct responses under targeted image-level perturbations. 4) Sanity checks showing matching and TAPAScore distinguish trained from untrained SAEs. 5) Empirical evaluation of SAEs trained on CLIP and DINOv2 embeddings, analyzing overcompleteness and dictionary size effects.
AI.result: FBMP consistently outperforms one-to-one baselines; TAPAScore alignment is selective and in the expected direction under perturbations; matching and TAPAScore reliably distinguish trained vs untrained SAEs; increasing overcompleteness can reduce perturbation alignment, signaling reduced interpretability; moderate dictionary sizes yield the best interpretability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed evaluation framework provides a reliable, scalable approach to quantifying semantic alignment of SAEs, reveals interpretability trade-offs with representation capacity (overcompleteness, dictionary size), and offers practical guidance for SAE design; code and datasets are publicly available.

title: SER: Learning to Ground Video Reasoning with Semantic Evidence Rewards
summary: Video MLLMs often struggle with fine-grained spatio-temporal reasoning, sometimes generating correct answers based on irrelevant frames or objects. Although outputting spatio-temporal evidence during reasoning is a promising direction, existing RL frameworks typically rely on geometry-only (IoU) rewards, which can be sensitive to boundary perturbations and overlook semantic alignment. To address this, we propose Semantic Evidence Reward (SER), which reformulates spatio-temporal evidence grounding as a constrained verification task. Instead of computing pixel-level overlap, SER uses a referee VLM as a local checker to evaluate model-generated evidence claims across two dimensions: relevance and localization quality, combined with a temporal penalty. This design reduces the reliance on dense box annotations and enables training directly on standard video QA data. On the V-STAR benchmark, SER achieves 49.6% mLGM, improving by 3.0 points over the strong evidence-grounded baseline Open-o3-Video, demonstrating its potential in enhancing both answer accuracy and evidence grounding.

AI.tldr: SER introduces a semantic evidence reward using a referee VLM to evaluate evidence across relevance and localization with a temporal penalty, reformulating grounding as verification and training on standard video QA data; achieves notable improvement on V-STAR.
AI.motivation: Address misalignment between spatio-temporal evidence grounding and geometry-only rewards in RL for video QA; reduce reliance on dense box annotations and improve both answer accuracy and grounding.
AI.method: SER uses a constrained verification task where a referee vision-language model assesses model-generated evidence for two aspects—relevance and localization quality—plus a temporal penalty. This replaces pixel-level IoU with semantic checks and enables training directly on standard video QA data.
AI.result: On the V-STAR benchmark, SER achieves 49.6% mLGM, improving by 3.0 points over the Open-o3-Video baseline, showing gains in both answer accuracy and evidence grounding.
AI.conclusion: Semantic evidence grounding via a verification-based reward reduces dependence on dense annotations and demonstrates the potential to improve both QA accuracy and spatio-temporal evidence grounding in video MLLMs.

title: VSANet: View-aware Sparse Attention Network for Light Field Image Denoising
summary: Light field (LF) image denoising is challenging due to the high-dimensional structure of LF data. While noise is independent across sub-aperture images, scene content exhibits strong cross-view correlations. We introduce VSANet, a view-aware sparse attention network for LF denoising. Specifically, we propose a view-aware sparse attention (VSA) block that represents the 4D LF feature map as a unified spatial-angular token space and performs cross-view aggregation via locality-sensitive hashing-based sparse attention. This enables global feature interactions with linear complexity, effectively exploiting LF correlations across views and spatial locations. In addition, we design a feature refinement (FR) block to emphasize informative features in spatial, angular, and epipolar subspaces. The VSA and FR blocks are integrated within a sequential attention refinement module, forming the core of VSANet. Experiments demonstrate VSANet outperforms stateof-the-art LF denoising methods.

AI.tldr: VSANet introduces a view-aware sparse attention network for light field denoising, using a VSA block and FR block to capture cross-view and spatial-angular correlations with linear-complexity sparse attention, achieving superior results over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Light field data exhibit strong cross-view correlations despite per-view noise; efficient denoising requires aggregating information across views and spatial locations without incurring quadratic complexity.
AI.method: Propose a view-aware sparse attention (VSA) block that maps the 4D LF features to a unified spatial-angular token space and applies locality-sensitive hashing–based sparse attention for cross-view aggregation with linear complexity; introduce a feature refinement (FR) block to enhance informative features across spatial, angular, and epipolar subspaces; integrate VSA and FR into a sequential attention refinement module within VSANet.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation shows VSANet outperforms state-of-the-art LF denoising methods.
AI.conclusion: VSANet effectively leverages cross-view and spatial correlations in light field data via efficient sparse attention and feature refinement, delivering superior denoising performance while maintaining scalable complexity.

title: BioMedVR: Confusion-Aware Mixture-of-Prompt Experts for Biomedical Visual Reprogramming
comment: Accepted at ECCV 2026. 19 pages, 6 figures. Project page: https://jxliu-ai.github.io/biomedvr-page/
summary: Recent advances in vision-language models (VLMs) such as CLIP have demonstrated strong generalization across natural-image domains. However, adapting these models to biomedical imaging is non-trivial: full-model fine-tuning is computationally expensive, while medical data are often scarce and exhibit subtle, fine-grained inter-class differences, making parameter-efficient adaptation particularly critical. Visual Reprogramming (VR) offers a parameter-efficient alternative by injecting learnable perturbations into the input space, but existing VR approaches for VLMs mainly focus on positive class prompts and overlook confusing negatives, leading to miscalibrated predictions in fine-grained medical scenarios. We present BioMedVR, the first VR-based framework for biomedical imaging, enabling few-shot adaptation of pretrained VLMs through compact learnable VR modules. To mitigate class confusion, we introduce a Confusion Minimization Mechanism that leverages LLM-generated confusion-aware attributes together with a Confusion-Suppression Loss to explicitly reduce false-positive alignment. Moreover, the designed Mixture-of-Prompt Experts combines a positive expert for main-class discrimination and a negative expert for confusion suppression, balanced via adaptive gating. Extensive experiments on 18 datasets, including 11 biomedical datasets and 7 natural image benchmarks, demonstrate that BioMedVR achieves superior accuracy and generalization, effectively bridging VR and VLMs in biomedical domains.

AI.tldr: BioMedVR introduces a parameter-efficient VR-based adaptation of pretrained vision-language models for biomedical imaging, addressing class confusion via a confusion-minimization mechanism and a mixture-of-prompt-experts, achieving strong cross-domain performance.
AI.motivation: Adapting vision-language models to biomedical imaging is challenging due to scarce data and subtle, fine-grained inter-class differences. Existing VR methods focus on positive prompts only, leading to miscalibration when negatives are confusing.
AI.method: BioMedVR enables few-shot adaptation with compact VR modules injected into the input space. It adds a Confusion Minimization Mechanism that uses LLM-generated confusion-aware attributes and a Confusion-Suppression Loss to reduce false-positive alignments. It also introduces a Mixture-of-Prompt-Experts with a positive expert for main-class discrimination and a negative expert for confusion suppression, balanced by adaptive gating.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 18 datasets (11 biomedical, 7 natural) show superior accuracy and generalization, demonstrating effective cross-domain adaptation and improved handling of class confusion in biomedical imaging.
AI.conclusion: BioMedVR demonstrates that VR-based adaptation can be effectively bridged with vision-language models in biomedical domains, enabling accurate, data-efficient, and robust performance in fine-grained classification tasks.

title: Adaptive Hebbian Memory Routing in Vision Transformers for Few-Shot Learning
summary: Few-shot image recognition requires models to adapt to new classes from a small labeled support set. Hebbian fast-weight memory can provide temporary associative information during an episode, but fixed memory behavior may not be appropriate for every few-shot task. In this work, we propose Adaptive Hebbian Routing for few-shot Vision Transformers. The method uses a lightweight MLP router to control the contribution of Hebbian memory, the strength of memory updates, and the retention of previous memory from support-set features. We study Adaptive Placement, Adaptive Plasticity, and Fully Adaptive Hebbian Routing. Experiments use ViT-Small, DeiT-Small, and Swin-Tiny under 5-way 1-shot evaluation on Omniglot, CIFAR-FS, and cross-domain transfer from CIFAR-FS to Omniglot. In the direct Swin comparison, fixed and adaptive Hebbian variants use the same memory location. Adaptive Plasticity improves the fixed Hebbian result from 96.74\% to 96.92\%, while Fully Adaptive Routing achieves the best result at 96.94\%. The fully adaptive Swin model also reduces inference time from 16.51 ms to 14.05 ms relative to fixed Hebbian Swin. On CIFAR-FS, adaptive variants improve performance across all three backbones, and the multi-shot evaluation shows that these gains remain useful as the number of support examples increases. These results show that adaptive plasticity and adaptive memory activation can improve few-shot Transformer representations beyond fixed Hebbian behavior.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Hebbian Routing adds a trainable MLP router to Hebbian memory in few-shot Vision Transformers, enabling adaptive placement, plasticity, and memory activation to improve performance and reduce inference time across Omniglot and CIFAR-FS.
AI.motivation: Fixed Hebbian memory in few-shot learning can be suboptimal across tasks; introducing adaptive control over memory contribution, update strength, and retention can tailor memory behavior to each episode.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight MLP router that modulates (i) the contribution of Hebbian memory, (ii) memory update strength, and (iii) retention of previous support-set features. Study Adaptive Placement, Adaptive Plasticity, and Fully Adaptive Hebbian Routing. Evaluate on ViT-Small, DeiT-Small, and Swin-Tiny under 5-way 1-shot on Omniglot, CIFAR-FS, and cross-domain transfer from CIFAR-FS to Omniglot.
AI.result: Adaptive Plasticity improves fixed Hebbian from 96.74% to 96.92%, Fully Adaptive Routing achieves 96.94%. Inference time for Swin decreases from 16.51 ms to 14.05 ms under full adaptation. On CIFAR-FS, adaptive variants improve performance across all three backbones, with gains persisting in multi-shot settings.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive plasticity and adaptive memory activation enhance few-shot Transformer representations beyond fixed Hebbian memory, yielding consistent gains across backbones and datasets and enabling faster inference for fully adaptive routing.

title: UniDrive: A Unified Vision-Language and Grounding Framework for Interpretable Risk Understanding in Autonomous Driving
summary: Recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown strong potential for autonomous driving scene understanding, yet existing methods still face a fundamental trade-off between temporal reasoning and spatial precision. Models that rely on single-frame or low-resolution inputs often miss small, distant, or partially occluded hazards, while language-centric driving models frequently provide limited grounded evidence for their explanations. To address this gap, we propose UniDrive, a unified visual-language and grounding framework for interpretable risk understanding in autonomous driving. UniDrive combines a temporal reasoning branch that models scene dynamics from multi-frame visual input with a high-resolution perception branch that preserves fine-grained spatial details from the latest frame. The two branches are integrated through a gated cross-attention fusion module, enabling dynamic context to be aligned with precise spatial evidence. Based on the fused representation, UniDrive jointly generates natural-language risk descriptions and grounded bounding-box outputs for risk objects. Experiments on the DRAMA-Reasoning benchmark show that UniDrive outperforms representative image-based and video-based baselines in both captioning and risk-object grounding. In particular, UniDrive achieves the best overall performance on the validation split and demonstrates clear advantages in small-object localization, zero-shot generalization to NuScenes and BDD100K, and human-rated interpretability and trustworthiness. These results suggest that explicitly combining temporal semantics and high-resolution perception provides a stronger foundation for interpretable and safety-oriented autonomous driving systems. The code is available at https://github.com/pixeli99/unidrive-dev.

AI.tldr: UniDrive fuses multi-frame dynamics and high-resolution perception via gated cross-attention to generate grounded risk descriptions and bounding boxes, achieving state-of-the-art performance on DRAMA-Reasoning with strong zero-shot generalization and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Addresses the fundamental trade-off between temporal reasoning and spatial precision in multimodal driving models, seeking interpretable, grounded risk explanations.
AI.method: Two-branch architecture: a temporal reasoning branch over multi-frame input; a high-resolution perception branch on the latest frame; a gated cross-attention fusion module to align context; jointly outputs natural-language risk descriptions and grounded risk-object bounding boxes; trained/evaluated on DRAMA-Reasoning with zero-shot tests on NuScenes/BDD100K.
AI.result: Outperforms image- and video-based baselines in captioning and risk-object grounding; best overall on validation; improved small-object localization and zero-shot generalization; favorable human interpretability/trustworthiness.
AI.conclusion: Explicit combination of temporal semantics and high-resolution perception yields more interpretable and safety-oriented autonomous driving models; UniDrive demonstrates strong performance and provides a code release.

title: Revealing Training Data Exposure in Vision Language Large Models via Parameter Gradients
summary: Vision-Language Large Models (VLLMs) trained on massive crawled corpora raise pressing copyright and data-provenance concerns. These concerns are particularly acute in healthcare, where patient medical images paired with clinical reports demand rigorous privacy safeguards. However, existing training data detection methods either fail in cross-modal scenarios or rely on superficial output signals with insufficient discriminative power. We introduce GradAudit, a gradient-based auditing framework that examines internal optimization dynamics rather than treating VLLMs as black boxes. Our approach builds on a key observation: model parameters converge to regions where gradients on training samples become stable and well-aligned, whereas gradients on non-training samples remain noisy and inconsistent. By analyzing these gradient signatures, GradAudit achieves strong separability and detects genuine image-text associations learned during training, not merely individual modality membership. Empirically, across both medical and general-domain datasets, GradAudit substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both pretraining and fine-tuning VLLMs. In a case study employing copyrighted content, we show that existing training data detection methods not only underestimate the extent of unauthorized data usage, but that this underestimation becomes more pronounced as models become more recent and more advanced.

AI.tldr: GradAudit leverages gradient dynamics to audit VLLMs for training-data provenance, achieving robust cross-modal detection of genuine image-text associations; outperforms baselines in pretraining and fine-tuning; reveals underestimation of data usage by existing methods, especially for newer models.
AI.motivation: Address copyright/privacy concerns in Vision-Language LMs trained on large crawled datasets, emphasizing cross-modal data provenance in healthcare where patient data is sensitive.
AI.method: Analyze internal optimization dynamics by examining gradients; training samples produce stable, aligned gradients; non-training samples yield noisy gradients; derive gradient-based signatures to separate training data from non-training data; apply to medical and general-domain datasets; evaluate in pretraining and fine-tuning phases; case study on copyrighted content.
AI.result: GradAudit achieves strong separability between training and non-training samples, outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both medical and general-domain tasks; demonstrates better detection of learned image-text associations rather than mere modality membership; highlights greater underestimation by existing methods as models scale.
AI.conclusion: Gradient-based auditing provides a robust, practical tool for data provenance verification in VLLMs, addressing privacy and copyright issues and exposing limitations of prior detection methods.

title: AerialFusionMapNet: Online HD Map Construction with Aerial-Onboard BEV Fusion
comment: Accepted at the IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC) 2026
summary: High-resolution aerial imagery has recently emerged as a complementary modality for automated driving perception and has shown potential to improve birds-eye-view (BEV) scene understanding when fused with onboard sensors. Prior work demonstrated performance gains for online high-definition (HD) map construction through aerial-onboard fusion; however, conventional end-to-end fusion does not fully exploit the structural information contained in aerial representations. In this work, we introduce AerialFusionMapNet, a fusion-based mapping framework with a structured two-stage training strategy that explicitly enhances the contribution of aerial features within a unified pipeline. The proposed training scheme enables more effective integration of structural aerial priors. On the nuScenes geographic split, AerialFusionMapNet achieves up to 54.7 mAP, improving over prior aerial-onboard fusion baselines from 48.8 mAP by +5.9 absolute and +12.1% relative. The results suggest that structured training design, rather than increased architectural complexity, plays a more decisive role in unlocking the full potential of aerial imagery for online HD map construction. Code and trained models are available at https://github.com/DriverlessMobility/AerialFusionMapNet.

AI.tldr: AerialFusionMapNet introduces a structured two-stage training framework for fusion of aerial imagery with onboard sensors to improve online HD map construction, showing that training design, not architectural complexity, drives gains; achieves 54.7 mAP on nuScenes geographic split, outperforming aerial-onboard baselines.
AI.motivation: Leverage structural priors in aerial imagery to enhance BEV mapping, addressing underutilization of aerial information in end-to-end fusion for HD map construction.
AI.method: A fusion-based mapping framework (AerialFusionMapNet) with a two-stage structured training strategy that explicitly enhances the contribution of aerial features within a unified pipeline, enabling better integration of aerial priors.
AI.result: Achieves up to 54.7 mAP on nuScenes geographic split, exceeding the previous aerial-onboard baseline of 48.8 mAP by 5.9 absolute and 12.1% relative; demonstrates effectiveness of structured training design.
AI.conclusion: Structured training design is more decisive than merely increasing architectural complexity for unlocking the full potential of aerial imagery in online HD map construction; code and models are released.

title: Counting Trees from Satellite Imagery with Noisy Supervision
summary:
Counting individual trees is a fundamental task for environmental monitoring, yet remains largely unexplored with satellite imagery. At these resolutions, isolated trees may still be identifiable, but crown boundaries become ambiguous in dense forests, making the notion of an individual tree inherently ill-defined. Moreover, large-scale manual annotations of individual trees are prohibitively expensive. While scalable supervision can be derived from airborne LiDAR, the resulting annotations are noisy and difficult to exploit effectively.
  We address these challenges by formulating tree counting as a spatial density matching problem supervised through Unbalanced Optimal Transport. This formulation naturally accommodates both precise localization of isolate trees and robust density estimation in dense forests. We further introduce a self-correction mechanism that leverages transport residuals to progressively refine noisy supervision during training.
  We evaluate our approach on TinyTrees, a new benchmark spanning three continents and three satellite sensors, comprising over 215 million tree annotations (including 773K manually verified instances) across 23,000 sq.km. Our method consistently outperforms detection-based, regression-based, and transport-based distribution-matching baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of unbalanced transport and reliability-aware supervision for large-scale tree counting from satellite imagery. Code, data and models are available at https://github.com/dgominski/treematch.

AI.tldr: A density-matching approach using Unbalanced Optimal Transport (UOT) for counting trees in satellite imagery, with a self-correction mechanism to refine noisy LiDAR supervision, achieving strong results on TinyTrees and releasing code/data.
AI.motivation: Counting individual trees in coarse-resolution satellite imagery is challenging due to ambiguous crown boundaries, costly manual annotations, and noisy LiDAR-based supervision; scalable and reliable counting is needed for environmental monitoring.
AI.method: Frame tree counting as a spatial density-matching problem supervised by Unbalanced Optimal Transport. Incorporate a self-correction mechanism that uses transport residuals to progressively refine supervision during training; evaluate on TinyTrees (215M annotations, 773K manual verifications, across 23k km^2, three continents and three sensors).
AI.result: The proposed method consistently outperforms detection-based, regression-based, and transport-based distribution-matching baselines, demonstrating robustness in both isolate-tree localization and dense-forest density estimation; code, data, and models are released.
AI.conclusion: Unbalanced transport with reliability-aware supervision is effective for large-scale satellite-based tree counting, enabling precise localization and robust density estimation while mitigating noisy supervision; the approach scales across sensors and regions.

title: Pocket-SLAM: Rendering-Area-Aware Pruning for Memory-Efficient 3DGS-SLAM
comment: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation(ICRA)
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has garnered significant attention in Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) due to its advances in capturing fine-grained geometry features and synthesizing novel views. For SLAM in large-scale scenes, such as autonomous driving, 3DGS-SLAM faces a critical limitation: memory consumption increases continuously over time as Gaussian points accumulate, leading to poor memory efficiency and limiting its applicability. In this work, we propose a rendering-area-aware pruning strategy that selectively removes Gaussians based on their contribution to the effective rendering area, rather than solely relying on Gaussian-level heuristics such as opacity or gradient magnitude. This perspective directly targets the sources of memory redundancy, effectively reducing the peak memory footprint of 3DGS-SLAM during runtime. Evaluations on the EuRoC and KITTI datasets demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms existing pruning approaches in large-scale outdoor scenes, achieving over 60% memory reduction and more than 2 times FPS improvement while preserving localization and mapping accuracy. These results highlight rendering-area-aware pruning as a promising direction for scaling 3DGS-SLAM to real-world autonomous driving scenarios. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/UMN-ZhaoLab/Pocket-SLAM.git.

AI.tldr: Proposes a rendering-area-aware pruning strategy for 3D Gaussian Splatting SLAM (3DGS-SLAM) to curb memory growth in large-scale scenes, achieving significant memory savings and speedups while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Memory consumption in 3DGS-SLAM grows over time as Gaussian points accumulate in large-scale environments (e.g., autonomous driving), limiting practicality.
AI.method: Introduce a pruning mechanism that evaluates Gaussian contributions based on their impact on the effective rendering area rather than traditional Gaussian-level heuristics (opacity/gradient). By removing low-contribution Gaussians, the method reduces memory redundancy and the peak memory footprint during runtime.
AI.result: On EuRoC and KITTI datasets, the approach consistently outperforms existing pruning methods in large-scale outdoor scenes, delivering over 60% memory reduction and more than 2x FPS improvement, with preserved localization and mapping accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Rendering-area-aware pruning is an effective strategy to scale 3DGS-SLAM to real-world autonomous driving, reducing memory demands without sacrificing performance; code is publicly available.

title: EG-VQA: Benchmarking Verifiable Video Question Answering with Grounded Temporal Evidence
summary: Recent advances in Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) have yielded promising performance on video question answering (VideoQA). Nevertheless, existing benchmarks are predominantly evaluated through answer correctness, while the grounding of predictions in relevant video evidence remains largely unexamined. This disconnect between answer generation and evidence understanding motivates the construction of the Evidence-Grounded Video Question Answering Benchmark (EG-VQA), an open-ended evaluation protocol in which each QA pair is explicitly annotated with supporting temporal evidence, thereby requiring joint reasoning and precise evidence localization. EG-VQA is comprised of 2,067 videos and 11,838 QA pairs with fine-grained evidence annotations. To evaluate predicted evidence, Evidence-Grounded F1 (EG-F1) is introduced as a unified metric in which temporal alignment and semantic consistency against ground-truth evidence are jointly measured. Experimental evaluation reveals that even strong proprietary models struggle to accurately ground their predictions, exposing a fundamental discrepancy between answer correctness and faithful evidence localization. To bridge this gap, EG-Reasoner, an evidence-grounded reasoning model trained with explicit supervision, is proposed. State-of-the-art performance is achieved among open-source models, with results competitive against proprietary systems, particularly pronounced gains are observed on reasoning-intensive tasks such as counterfactual questions. These findings demonstrate that scaling alone is insufficient for robust video understanding and that structured evidence supervision is essential for the development of more reliable and interpretable VideoQA systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes EG-VQA, an evidence-grounded video QA benchmark with annotated temporal evidence and a unified EG-F1 metric; shows current models struggle to ground answers; introduces EG-Reasoner to improve evidence grounding, with strong open-source results and competitive performance to proprietary systems, especially on reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing VideoQA benchmarks emphasize answer accuracy but rarely assess whether predictions are grounded in corresponding video evidence. There is a need for joint reasoning and precise evidence localization to improve reliability and interpretability of VideoQA models.
AI.method: Construct EG-VQA dataset with 2,067 videos and 11,838 QA pairs with fine-grained temporal evidence annotations. Introduce Evidence-Grounded F1 (EG-F1) to jointly assess temporal alignment and semantic coverage with ground-truth evidence. Propose EG-Reasoner, an evidence-grounded reasoning model trained with explicit supervision. Evaluate both open-source and proprietary models, with emphasis on reasoning-intensive tasks such as counterfactual questions.
AI.result: EG-VQA enables evaluation of evidence grounding; strong proprietary models underperform in grounding despite good QA accuracy. EG-Reasoner achieves state-of-the-art among open-source models and approaches proprietary performance, with notable gains on reasoning-intensive tasks. Demonstrates a gap between answer correctness and faithful evidence localization and the benefit of explicit evidence supervision.
AI.conclusion: Structured evidence supervision is essential for robust, interpretable VideoQA. Scaling alone is insufficient; models must be trained with explicit evidence supervision to improve grounding and reliability.

title: OrbitForge: Text-to-3D Scene Generation via Reconstruction-Anchored Video Synthesis
summary: Generic text-to-video models can be used as rich open-world scene priors. Despite the high quality of today's generated videos, they do not directly yield reliable 3D assets: camera motion is difficult to control, view coverage is partial, and frames often contain inconsistencies across time. We introduce OrbitForge, an adapter built from frozen video priors and per-prompt Gaussian Splatting reconstruction optimization that converts a single text-generated video into a canonical closed-orbit 3D Gaussian Splatting scene. We use 3D reconstruction as an anchor to improve the 3D consistency of the generated video. We obtain a preliminary 3D reconstruction from a first generated video via Deformable Gaussian Splatting with a robust MedianGS proxy. We render views from a prescribed orbit to detect missing viewpoints. OrbitForge uses the text-to-video model to complete only the missing views, and reconstructs the completed orbit into a final Gaussian Splatting scene. This design requires no task-specific video or multiview fine-tuning, avoids per-prompt score-distillation optimization, and does not progressively generate views one step at a time. We further argue that this setting demands coverage-aware evaluation: local smoothness alone rewards methods that never attempt a full orbit. On a frozen 300-prompt T3Bench-derived audit, OrbitForge reconstruction attains a 359.0-degree measured median span, raises originally unsupported-bin Q10 ImageReward from 8.07 to 16.36 relative to MedianGS-only reconstruction, while remaining competitive with VideoMV on the coverage-quality.

AI.tldr: OrbitForge converts a single text-generated video into a canonical closed-orbit 3D Gaussian Splatting scene by anchoring to a 3D reconstruction, filling missing views with the T2V model, and avoiding per-prompt fine-tuning or progressive view generation.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-video models yield high-quality videos but fail to produce reliable 3D assets due to unstable camera motion, partial view coverage, and temporal inconsistencies. A strong 3D prior and reconstruction-based anchoring can enforce 3D consistency and complete orbit coverage.
AI.method: Use frozen text-to-video priors and per-prompt Gaussian Splatting reconstruction. Generate initial 3D using Deformable Gaussian Splatting with MedianGS proxy. Render an orbit to identify missing viewpoints. Use the T2V model to fill missing views and reconstruct the completed orbit into a final Gaussian Splatting scene. No task-specific video or multiview fine-tuning; no per-prompt score-distillation optimization; no sequential view generation.
AI.result: On a frozen 300-prompt T3Bench audit, OrbitForge achieves a 359.0-degree median orbit coverage. It raises the Q10 ImageReward from 8.07 (MedianGS-only) to 16.36 and remains competitive with VideoMV in coverage and quality.
AI.conclusion: The method provides improved 3D consistency and full-orbit coverage from a single text-generated video, without extra tuning, and introduces coverage-aware evaluation as a necessary metric for 3D-text-to-video systems.

title: DDStereo: Efficient Dual Decoder Transformers for Stereo 3D Road Anomaly Detection
summary: Stereo-based 3D object detection still faces two critical safety challenges: real-time performance and open-set generalization. Existing stereo 3D methods typically achieve twice the accuracy of monocular methods but suffer from significantly lower inference speeds, making them unsuitable for real-time applications. Meanwhile, recent advances in open-world detection have introduced open-set and open-vocabulary algorithms in monocular 2D and 3D settings, yet stereo-based open-set detection remains largely unexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose DDStereo, a novel Dual-Decoder Stereo Transformer for real-time open-set 3D object detection. DDStereo features two lightweight decoder branches: one for open-set foreground 2D detection and the other for 3D attribute regression. These decoders share object-level queries to achieve unified target-level alignment. To enhance inference efficiency, we designed a compact disparity feature extractor and a streamlined decoder architecture. Experiments on public stereo 3D benchmarks demonstrate that DDStereo achieves state-of-the-art accuracy under both closed-set and open-set protocols. Notably, our method surpasses existing stereo 3D detectors in inference speed and, for the first time, achieves real-time performance comparable to monocular approaches.

AI.tldr: DDStereo introduces a Dual-Decoder Stereo Transformer for real-time open-set 3D object detection, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on stereo benchmarks with real-time performance comparable to monocular methods.
AI.motivation: Addresses two critical safety challenges in stereo-based 3D detection: real-time performance and open-set/generalization. Stereo methods are accurate but slow, and open-set stereo detection is underexplored.
AI.method: A dual-decoder architecture with shared object-level queries: one lightweight branch for open-set foreground 2D detection and another for 3D attribute regression; a compact disparity feature extractor and a streamlined decoder to enhance efficiency; unified target-level alignment.
