AI.result: On public stereo 3D benchmarks, DDStereo achieves state-of-the-art accuracy under both closed-set and open-set protocols, and its inference speed surpasses existing stereo detectors, achieving real-time performance comparable to monocular approaches.
AI.conclusion: DDStereo delivers real-time open-set stereo 3D detection with an efficient dual-decoder transformer and shared queries, enabling practical deployment and highlighting potential for further improvements in speed and open-set capabilities.

title: High-Fidelity Synthetic Transmission Electron Microscopy Image Generation Using Diffusion Probabilistic Models for Data-Limited Semiconductor Metrology
comment: To be presented at the 2026 International Symposium ELMAR, published by IEEE in the conference proceedings
summary: Advanced semiconductor nodes drastically increased demand for Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), yet destructive sample preparation, slow imaging and high costs severely limit the availability of diverse datasets needed for downstream machine learning (ML). Synthetic data generation is becoming essential, but current generative models often miss TEM-specific noise, structural detail, and stochastic variability crucial for evaluation. We present a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) framework for synthetic TEM image generation under extreme data scarcity. A progressive patch-based training strategy scales from low-resolution patches to full images, enabling from-scratch training with only 15 samples. We integrate a custom TrivialAugment adaptation, cross-process domain transfer, classifier guidance, and RePaint-style inpainting, culminating in full-image generation that preserves global structural and spatial relationships in compliance with FAB metrology requirements. Beyond synthesis, we repurpose DDPM feature representations for segmentation, partitioning encoder feature maps to obtain coherent region masks. Our synthetic images achieve up to MS-SSIM > 0.98 and qualitative expert assessment consistent with structural similarity results, facilitating downstream ML training for defect detection, segmentation, and metrology while preserving statistical and physical realism.

AI.tldr: A DDPM-based framework for synthetic TEM image generation from extremely scarce data, integrating progressive patch-based training, domain-adapted augmentations, cross-process transfer, classifier guidance, and inpainting, plus DDPM-based segmentation; achieves high similarity and realism to support defect/metrology ML tasks.
AI.motivation: Advanced semiconductor TEM workflows demand large, diverse, realistic datasets, but TEM data collection is destructive, slow, and costly. Current generative models miss TEM-specific noise, structure, and stochasticity, necessitating a method capable of learning from very small samples.
AI.method: Progressive patch-based DDPM training from low-resolution patches to full images with only ~15 samples. Includes a custom TrivialAugment adaptation, cross-process domain transfer, classifier guidance, and RePaint-style inpainting for full-image generation. Also repurposes DDPM features for segmentation by partitioning encoder feature maps into coherent region masks.
AI.result: Synthetic TEM images achieve MS-SSIM > 0.98 with qualitative expert validation. Generated data preserves global structure, noise characteristics, and spatial relationships in FAB metrology-compliant form, enabling downstream ML tasks (defect detection, segmentation, metrology). DDPM representations also support segmentation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables high-fidelity, physics-consistent synthetic TEM data under extreme scarcity, expanding ML training resources for TEM. It demonstrates dual utility—image synthesis and feature-based segmentation—while honoring metrology constraints. Potential limitations include domain specificity and the need for further validation across TEM modalities and real-world variability.

title: GeoT2V-Bench: Benchmarking 3D Consistency in Text-to-Video Models via 3D Reconstruction
summary: Camera-prompted text-to-video (T2V) models are increasingly used to synthesize virtual camera captures, such as orbiting objects or moving through static scenes. For these outputs, visual plausibility is insufficient: the generated frames should also provide coherent multi-view evidence for a single static 3D scene. We introduce GeoT2V-Bench, a reconstruction-based diagnostic benchmark for evaluating whether camera-prompted T2V clips can support explicit rigid 3D reconstruction. Our pipeline estimates per-frame camera intrinsics and poses with VGGT-style geometry estimation, fits DeformableGS, derives a static MedianGS proxy by temporal-median aggregation, and renders this proxy along the estimated camera path. Instead of producing a pass/fail label or a single scalar score, GeoT2V-Bench reports a continuous reconstruction profile covering apparent image motion, estimated trajectory behavior, MedianGS static rendering error, static-render flow agreement, and the gap between flexible and static fits. On a fair-format four-seed evaluation with 3,840 completed reconstructions from 12 open-weight model configurations and 80 GeCo-Eval static-scene prompts, we find that visible motion, static rendering error, flow agreement, and flexible-vs-static behavior often disagree. GeoT2V-Bench therefore captures complementary failure modes that emerge when generated videos are tested as global static-scene acquisitions.

AI.tldr: Introduces GeoT2V-Bench, a continuous reconstruction-based benchmark for camera-prompted text-to-video, assessing multi-view consistency and explicit rigid 3D reconstruction rather than simple plausibility.
AI.motivation: Current T2V evaluations focus on perceptual realism and appearance but rarely test whether generated clips support coherent multi-view geometry and static-scene reconstruction; a diagnostic that quantifies reconstructionability across a camera path is needed.
AI.method: Estimate per-frame intrinsics and poses with VGGT-style geometry estimation; fit DeformableGS for flexible geometry; derive a static MedianGS proxy via temporal median aggregation; render the proxy along the estimated camera path; output a continuous reconstruction profile across metrics: apparent image motion, trajectory behavior, MedianGS static rendering error, static-render flow agreement, and the gap between flexible and static fits.
AI.result: Evaluated on a fair-format four-seed setup with 3,840 reconstructions from 12 open-weight model configurations and 80 GeCo-Eval static-scene prompts; found that visible motion, static rendering error, flow agreement, and flexible-vs-static behavior often disagree, indicating complementary failure modes when videos are treated as global static-scene acquisitions.
AI.conclusion: GeoT2V-Bench provides richer, multi-dimensional diagnostics beyond binary pass/fail, enabling deeper insight into when camera-prompted T2V outputs can support reliable 3D reconstruction and highlighting diverse failure modes across motion, rendering, and geometry fit.

title: Bridging the Manifold Gap: Riemannian Residual Line Search for One-Step Image Editing
summary: One-step diffusion editors are fast because they avoid inversion and iterative optimization, but a single transport update must be aggressive enough to realize the target prompt and conservative enough to preserve the source image--and no fixed update strength satisfies both demands across edit types. We treat this tension as a post-hoc candidate-selection problem on top of energy-field transport rather than as a new editing model. Our proposed method, Riemannian Residual Line Search, first builds a stronger edit by estimating the local time curvature of the prompt-delta field and projecting the corrected direction back onto the update norm of the original first-order energy-field transport estimation. It then forms a small residual path from the source image to this strong edit, retains the original first-order output as one candidate, and picks the final image by maximizing target-prompt CLIP alignment. On a 700-sample PIE-Bench++ evaluation across 10 edit type IDs, our method achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance among current one-step update algorithms.

AI.tldr: One-step diffusion editors trade off aggressiveness vs preservation; the proposed Riemannian Residual Line Search (RRLS) uses post-hoc candidate selection on energy-field transport to achieve better edits.
AI.motivation: Address the speed-versus-fidelity tension in one-step diffusion edits by avoiding model changes and instead selecting stronger edits and a residual path that maximize alignment with the prompt.
AI.method: Compute a stronger edit by estimating local time curvature of the prompt-delta field, project the direction back onto the original update norm, create a small residual path from source to this strong edit, keep the original first-order output as a candidate, and select the final image by maximizing target-prompt CLIP alignment.
AI.result: On a 700-sample PIE-Bench++ evaluation across 10 edit types, achieves state-of-the-art performance among current one-step update algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Post-hoc candidate selection via RRLS improves one-step diffusion editing by combining a stronger curvature-informed edit with a residual path, yielding superior CLIP-aligned results while retaining source content.

title: Spherical-to-ERP Epipolar Rectification for Single-Axis Disparity in 360 Stereo
summary: Omnidirectional stereo images provide full-surround perception but violate the geometric assumptions of classical disparity estimation: in spherical or fisheye views, epipolar correspondences follow curved great-circle paths, producing two-dimensional displacements that cannot be treated as single-axis disparity before geometric rectification. In this work, we adopt a standard spherical-to-equirectangular (ERP) projection as a preprocessing step, which straightens epipolar curves and restores a one-dimensional disparity structure - horizontal for left-right rigs and vertical for top-bottom rigs. Building on our previously introduced RAFT + Epipolar-Aligned Channel Selection (EACS) framework, originally developed for rectilinear and ERP stereo, we examine whether the same modular pipeline remains accurate when the input originates from spherical stereo imagery. After ERP projection, dense optical flow from RAFT is reduced to disparity by retaining only the baseline-aligned flow component. Experiments on synthetic fisheye stereo datasets show that this spherical-to-ERP-to-RAFT+EACS pipeline produces accurate, smooth, and structurally consistent disparity maps at real-time speed. These findings confirm that established ERP preprocessing can be effectively combined with our earlier RAFT+EACS method to enable practical, interpretable, and efficient disparity estimation from spherical stereo, providing a straightforward pathway for extending conventional stereo pipelines to 360 imaging.

AI.tldr: ERP projection straightens epipolar curves in spherical stereo, restoring 1D disparity (horizontal for LR rigs, vertical for TB rigs) and enabling a RAFT+EACS pipeline to estimate disparity in real time from spherical imagery.
AI.motivation: Omnidirectional stereo violates classical disparity geometry because epipolar correspondences curve on spherical/fisheye views. A preprocessing step that converts to ERP can realign disparity to a single axis, allowing established rectilinear stereo pipelines to be reused for 360 imaging.
AI.method: Project spherical images to the equirectangular (ERP) domain to straighten epipolar curves, then apply RAFT-based dense optical flow. Convert flow to disparity by keeping only the baseline-aligned component. Build on the RAFT+EACS framework previously developed for rectilinear and ERP stereo, and evaluate on synthetic fisheye datasets.
AI.result: The spherical-ERP-RAFT+EACS pipeline yields accurate, smooth, and structurally consistent disparity maps at real-time speeds, demonstrating that ERP preprocessing can effectively extend conventional stereo to spherical imagery.
AI.conclusion: ERP preprocessing combined with the RAFT+EACS framework provides a practical, interpretable, and efficient pathway for disparity estimation from spherical stereo, enabling straightforward extension of traditional stereo pipelines to 360 imaging.

title: IV-CoT: Implicit Visual Chain-of-Thought for Structure-Aware Text-to-Image Generation
summary: Unified multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved strong text-to-image generation quality, but still struggle with structure-aware prompt following, where object counts, spatial relations, attribute bindings, and coarse layouts must be preserved. We attribute this limitation in part to the entanglement of structural planning and appearance rendering within a single conditioning stream. To address this issue, we propose Implicit Visual Chain-of-Thought (IV-CoT), a latent visual reasoning framework for query-conditioned image generation. IV-CoT decomposes the visual conditioning queries into a structural-to-semantic cascade, where structural queries first form a latent visual plan and semantic queries then render appearance conditioned on this plan. To guide the structural queries, we introduce training-only sketch supervision, which encourages them to capture structure from sketches without requiring sketch extraction or intermediate decoding at inference time. IV-CoT performs implicit CoT reasoning in a single forward pass and achieves superior results on GenEval and T2I-CompBench. Visualizations and analyses demonstrate that the learned structural and semantic queries play complementary roles in structure-aware generation.

AI.tldr: A latent visual-CoT framework that decouples structure and appearance in MLLMs via a latent structural plan and sketch-guided training, enabling better structure-aware image generation in a single forward pass.
AI.motivation: Unified MLLMs struggle to preserve object counts, spatial relations, and layouts due to entangled structure and rendering; a separate structural conditioning path is needed.
AI.method: IV-CoT introduces a structural-to-semantic cascade: structural queries produce a latent visual plan; semantic queries render appearance conditioned on this plan. Training-only sketch supervision guides structure extraction; inference uses no sketch decoding or extraction; reasoning occurs in a single forward pass.
AI.result: Achieves superior results on GenEval and T2I-CompBench, with visualizations showing the learned structural and semantic queries play complementary roles.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling structure from appearance via a latent plan enables more robust structure-aware generation in MLLMs; implicit CoT in a single pass is effective and benefits from sketch-based supervision.

title: FLUX3D: High-Fidelity 3D Gaussian Generation with Diffusion-Aligned Sparse Representation
summary: Sparse voxel representation has emerged as a scalable foundation for image-to-3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) generation, yet current methods struggle to preserve high-frequency visual details of input images due to two structural bottlenecks. First, they adopt discriminative 2D features optimized for semantic abstraction to construct sparse voxel latents, which suppress reconstructive cues and induce a representation bottleneck. Second, in the generation stage, standard diffusion transformers lack effective mechanisms to align dense 2D image tokens with sparse 3D voxel latents, resulting in a cross-modal correspondence bottleneck. To address these issues, we propose FLUX3D, a scalable image-to-3DGS framework that boosts both representation learning and cross-modal alignment during generation. We first revisit 2D feature selection for sparse-voxel-based 3D representation learning, propose Diffusion-Aligned Structured Latents (DA-SLAT) and couple it with a decoder-only architecture to improve 3DGS reconstruction fidelity. We also design a sparse-structure-aware diffusion framework, which integrates the Sparse-structure Multimodal Diffusion Transformer (SMDiT) and Modal-Aware Rotary Positional Embedding (MARoPE) to achieve geometry-agnostic 2D-3D alignment. Extensive benchmark experiments demonstrate that FLUX3D yields substantial improvements in appearance fidelity and significantly outperforms all state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in generating high-quality 3DGS assets.

AI.tldr: FLUX3D introduces diffusion-aligned structured latents and a sparse-structure diffusion framework to overcome bottlenecks in sparse voxel-based 3D Gaussian Splatting, achieving better 3D appearance fidelity and SOTA performance.
AI.motivation: Current image-to-3DGS methods sacrifice high-frequency details due to (1) representation bottlenecks from semantically-focused 2D features, and (2) cross-modal alignment bottlenecks between dense 2D tokens and sparse 3D latents during diffusion-based generation.
AI.method: Propose Diffusion-Aligned Structured Latents (DA-SLAT) with a decoder-only architecture to improve reconstruction fidelity; design Sparse-structure Multimodal Diffusion Transformer (SMDiT) with Modal-Aware Rotary Positional Embedding (MARoPE) to enable geometry-agnostic 2D-3D alignment; integrate these into a scalable FLUX3D framework for image-to-3DGS generation.
AI.result: Extensive benchmark experiments show substantial improvements in appearance fidelity and that FLUX3D significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in generating high-quality 3DGS assets.
AI.conclusion: FLUX3D provides a scalable solution that enhances both representation learning and cross-modal alignment for image-to-3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling higher fidelity 3D reconstructions and superior SOTA performance.

title: FLAT: Feedforward Latent Triangle Splatting for Geometrically Accurate Scene Generation
summary: Generating explorable 3D scenes from a single image requires strong generative priors and accurate geometric representations suitable for downstream use. Current video diffusion models offer high-quality generation and implicitly encode multi-view geometric structure in latent space. However, existing feedforward latent scene decoders typically output volumetric 3D Gaussians that lack a well-defined surface, limiting their use in simulation or standard graphics pipelines. This motivates decoding surface-aligned primitives that are not only renderable but also closer to explicit geometric assets. We ask whether compressed video diffusion latents can be mapped directly to explicit surface primitives in a single pass. To this end, we introduce FLAT and, for the first time, show that triangle splats can be decoded directly from video diffusion latents. Compared with decoding 3D Gaussians, predicting flat primitives is notoriously more challenging due to high sensitivity to primitive orientations, oftentimes leading to poor gradient flow. FLAT solves with two key ingredients: a ray-centered rotation parameterization for triangle regression and a novel product window function that improves gradient flow during differentiable triangle rendering. On standard benchmarks, FLAT achieves significantly better geometric accuracy while maintaining competitive visual quality compared to state-of-the-art feedforward baselines. We further show that a lightweight test-time refinement step converts the predicted triangle soup into a fully opaque, game-engine-ready representation that supports real-time rendering. By evaluating 3DGS, 2DGS, and triangle splatting variants under an identical training setup, we provide the first systematic analysis of representation tradeoffs in feedforward scene generation. The project page is available at https://flat-splat.github.io

AI.tldr: Decodes triangle splats directly from video diffusion latents (FLAT), enabling explicit surface primitives from latent scenes; introduces a ray-centered rotation parameterization and a product window to improve gradient flow; achieves better geometry than 3D Gaussian baselines with comparable visuals and a lightweight test-time refinement for game-engine use; provides a first systematic tradeoff analysis across representations.
AI.motivation: Existing feedforward latent scene decoders output volumetric 3D Gaussians lacking explicit surfaces, hindering downstream graphics/simulation; need surface-aligned primitives that are compatible with standard pipelines; leverage compressed video diffusion latents for direct primitive decoding.
AI.method: Propose FLAT to decode triangle splats from video diffusion latents in a single pass; adopt ray-centered rotation parameterization for robust triangle regression; introduce a product window function to stabilize gradient flow during differentiable triangle rendering; add lightweight test-time refinement to produce opaque, real-time rendering-friendly representations; evaluate across 3DGS, 2DGS, and triangle splatting under identical training setups.
AI.result: Significant improvements in geometric accuracy over 3D Gaussian baselines while maintaining competitive visual quality; test-time refinement yields game-engine-ready opaque representations suitable for real-time rendering.
AI.conclusion: This work provides the first systematic analysis of representation tradeoffs in feedforward scene generation and demonstrates that triangle-based primitives can be effectively decoded from video diffusion latents, enabling practical, render-ready 3D assets from latent models.

title: BenchX: Benchmarking AI Models for Cancer Detection and Localization with Demographic and Protocol Biases
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) has achieved remarkable success in medical imaging, but it is widely recognized that these models often perform inconsistently across real-world clinical settings. Such inconsistencies occur when patient demographics and imaging protocols vary, for example, in detecting small tumors, analyzing scans from different contrast phases, or evaluating patients of different ages or sexes. To quantify these inconsistencies, we develop a large-scale, open benchmark of 85,355 CT scans that systematically evaluates 12 tumor-detection AI models across tumor size, location, patient subgroup, and imaging protocol. We leverage large language models (LLMs) to extract and organize subgroup information from clinical data, which makes the analysis both scalable and reproducible. Our benchmark reveals that current state-of-the-art AI models, optimized for average accuracy, perform poorly in rare or underrepresented subgroups, such as young, female African Americans. However, collecting sufficient annotated data for these rare cases is often impractical. The benchmark provides a foundation for building more reliable and robust AI models for tumor detection and highlighting the need for rigorous, subgroup-level evaluation in medical imaging and computer vision. Datasets, code

AI.tldr: A large-scale open CT benchmark evaluates 12 tumor-detection models across subgroups and imaging protocols, revealing performance gaps in underrepresented groups and highlighting the need for subgroup-aware evaluation; LLMs enable scalable extraction of subgroup information.
AI.motivation: To quantify and address inconsistencies of AI tumor-detection models across real-world clinical settings caused by patient demographics and imaging protocols, moving beyond average-accuracy assessment.
AI.method: Assemble 85,355 CT scans to benchmark 12 tumor-detection AI models across tumor size, location, patient subgroup, and imaging protocol. Use large language models to extract and organize subgroup information from clinical data, enabling scalable, reproducible analysis. Datasets and code released.
AI.result: State-of-the-art models optimized for average accuracy underperform in rare or underrepresented subgroups (e.g., young, female African Americans). Collecting annotated data for these cases is impractical, underscoring the value of subgroup-level evaluation. The benchmark provides a foundation for robust, subgroup-aware tumor-detection models.
AI.conclusion: Subgroup-focused evaluation is essential for reliable medical-imaging AI. The benchmark and open resources offer a path toward more robust models and greater transparency in performance across diverse patient populations and protocols.

title: DiffusionBench: On Holistic Evaluation of Diffusion Transformers
summary: Diffusion transformer (DiT) research on image generation has converged to a single evaluation setup: class-conditional generation on ImageNet. While methods improve the FID and related metrics, it is increasingly unclear whether they reflect real progress in generative modeling. The natural alternative, i.e., text-to-image (T2I) generation, is perceived as too costly or inconvenient to train and evaluate and is often skipped. We argue that this perception no longer holds. We introduce NanoGen, a unified DiT training and evaluation framework. NanoGen matches state-of-the-art DiT baselines on ImageNet and, with 12 lines of configuration change, also trains competitive text-to-image models. It currently supports RAE, VAE, pixel-space, and MeanFlow diffusion methods under both ImageNet and T2I setups. Under NanoGen, training T2I requires comparable compute to ImageNet. After training 21 latent diffusion models with NanoGen, we observe that method ranking shows no strong correlation between ImageNet and T2I generation: Pearson correlation is between -0.377 and -0.580 across three metrics. This suggests that a method which improves class-conditional ImageNet FID may show no corresponding improvement on T2I, clearly indicating the necessity of evaluating DiTs on both tasks. To this end, we summarize ImageNet and text-to-image results, which yields DiffusionBench, a holistic benchmark for DiT research. We recommend reporting DiffusionBench in place of ImageNet alone: methods that improve DiffusionBench are more likely to reflect broader progress.

AI.tldr: NanoGen unifies DiT training/evaluation and reveals that ImageNet class-conditional improvements do not reliably translate to text-to-image (T2I) gains, advocating DiffusionBench as a holistic DiT benchmark.
AI.motivation: Current Diffusion Transformer (DiT) work is predominantly evaluated on ImageNet, potentially misrepresenting true generative progress. T2I is underexplored due to perceived cost, so a unified, efficient framework is needed to compare methods across tasks.
AI.method: Introduce NanoGen, a unified DiT training/evaluation framework supporting RAE, VAE, pixel-space, and MeanFlow diffusion methods for both ImageNet and T2I setups. It enables T2I training with comparable compute to ImageNet, demonstrates switching to T2I via 12 configuration changes, and analyzes 21 latent diffusion models to compare rankings across tasks.
AI.result: NanoGen matches state-of-the-art DiT baselines on ImageNet and, with minimal configuration changes, trains competitive T2I models. Across 21 latent diffusion models, ImageNet and T2I method rankings show weak to negative Pearson correlations (-0.377 to -0.580), indicating that improvements on ImageNet FID do not reliably translate to T2I gains. DiffusionBench emerges as a holistic benchmark summarizing ImageNet and T2I results.
AI.conclusion: Evaluations should cover both ImageNet and T2I tasks; reporting DiffusionBench provides a better gauge of genuine progress in DiT research than ImageNet alone.
